For the master pane's banding sub-question, middle posture (row dividers, no alternating shading) was chosen over banded (which is appropriate for high-density spreadsheet-like data — v2's master panes show ~22 rows at default density on 4K, low enough that the eye does not lose its place) and over fully minimal with no dividers (which would feel under-organized for a structured data table).
after_payload.title: Selected-state visual vocabulary: 3px left accent bar plus tinted background; consistent across sidebar and master pane

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Apply design system with no special treatment (Option A) — rejected as under-leveraging the natural identity moment. Full showcase with custom brand mark (Option C) — rejected because brand-mark authoring is a separate design problem unsuitable for remote conversation resolution, and v2 has no established mark to evolve; introducing a brand mark mid-styling-pass would derail the pass without producing a deliberately-considered mark. Modest showcase wins as the threading-the-needle option.
after_payload.consequences: The existing QFormLayout in about_dialog.py is restructured to a QVBoxLayout with a header block (wordmark + tagline labels) followed by a vertical list of metadata rows followed by the close button row. The header block is a small custom widget combining the two labels with specific font tokens. Metadata rendering shifts from form-style (label-left-of-value) to two-line list-style (label above value). Path/URL values use JetBrains Mono via the monospace font token. About dialog version-reading logic (importlib.metadata with __version__ fallback per CLAUDE.md v2 version-source convention) is unchanged. The styling Conversation 2 may revisit the tagline wording at build time without re-opening Conversation 1; the design pass commits the wordmark + tagline structure but explicitly notes the tagline as refineable. About dialog becomes a natural early build slice in §3.3's suggested order.
after_payload.context: The styling Conversation 1 kickoff named the About dialog as a 'small showcase' surface — the smallest, most-contained dialog, well-suited to demonstrating the design system end-to-end. Today the About dialog (about_dialog.py) is a pure `QFormLayout` with bold labels (Application / Version / API base URL / Database path / Snapshot directory) and selectable value labels, rendered with Qt defaults — no design intent. Min width 440px. The styling pass needed to settle how much to elevate the About dialog visually. Three options: apply design system tokens but no special treatment (under-leverages the natural identity moment), modest showcase with wordmark + tagline header but no logo (visible identity without brand-mark authoring), full showcase with custom brand mark in addition to wordmark + tagline (requires authoring a logo).
after_payload.decision: **Modest showcase.** Standard dialog chrome (per §2.7) plus a header block at the top of the dialog body containing the application wordmark 'CRMBuilder v2' in font.size.heading_2 (22px), font.weight.semibold, color.neutral.900, followed by a tagline below the wordmark (`space.1` of vertical separation) in font.size.small (13px), color.neutral.500, regular weight. Proposed tagline (refineable at build time): 'Declarative CRM deployment and methodology authoring.' Alternatives noted in §2.8 as 'CRM deployment and methodology tooling for consultants' and 'End-to-end CRM implementation tooling.' Metadata table restructured from the existing QFormLayout to a vertical two-line-per-row list: line 1 is the label in font.size.small font.weight.medium color.neutral.500 (sentence-cased, not bold-capitalized); line 2 is the value in font.size.body color.neutral.800 (paths and URLs in font.family.mono / JetBrains Mono at font.size.small color.neutral.700). Values remain selectable so users can copy paths — preserves current behavior. Close button at the bottom-right of the dialog body, single secondary button per §2.5. Minimum width preserved at 440px.
after_payload.rationale: The About dialog is the natural identity moment in the application — what someone sees when they ask 'what is this app?' Treating it like every other dialog (Option A — no showcase) feels under-leveraged. But authoring a brand mark (Option C — full showcase with logo) is a separate design problem with its own subjective tradeoffs: logos are notoriously hard to settle by remote conversation, and v2 has no established mark to evolve. Option B threads the needle: the wordmark plus tagline gives the dialog visible identity without requiring brand-mark authoring, and the wordmark is already the application's name set in plain Inter Semibold (no additional design subtask needed). The shift from QFormLayout to a two-line-per-row vertical list also exercises the design system end-to-end (label-above pattern from §2.4, monospace token usage from §1.3, neutral text colors from §1.2.3) on the smallest contained surface — making the About dialog a natural early build slice.
after_payload.title: About dialog modest-showcase treatment: wordmark plus tagline header, retiring the QFormLayout default

after_payload.artifacts_produced: Nine deliverables committed to origin/main in one commit by Claude per the in-sandbox close-out convention: (1) PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ui-PRD-v0.5.md (release PRD, 425 lines, draft pending approval); (2) PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ui-v0.5-implementation-plan.md (slice breakdown, 437 lines, draft pending approval); (3) PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.5-A-foundation-and-dogfood-migration.md (slice A build prompt: meta DB + ActiveEngagementContext + two-database API + lazy migration + dogfood migration + sidebar Engagements group + refresh service extension + .gitignore extensions); (4) -B-engagement-schema-and-api.md (slice B: engagement repository + eight REST endpoints + validation + JSON snapshot hook + client extensions); (5) -C-engagement-management-panel.md (slice C: management panel + CRUD dialogs + forbid-active-delete with last-engagement edge case + refresh service registration + sidebar entry); (6) -D-engagement-switching.md (slice D: top-strip widget + picker dropdown + activation overlay + 12-step ActivationWorker + single-gesture NewEngagementDialog + slice-C placeholder rewiring); (7) -E-closeout.md (slice E: version 0.5.0 bump + README release note + end-to-end integration smoke + final regression pass + manual smoke checklist); (8) PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_029.json (this payload); (9) PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-029.md (apply prompt for this payload).

Produced ui-PRD-v0.5.md, ui-v0.5-implementation-plan.md, and the five slice build prompts at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.5-{A..E}-*.md. after_payload.title: v0.5 Conversation 2 — build planning (release PRD, implementation plan, five slice build prompts)
after_payload.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/v0.5-conversation-2-kickoff.md against the project default ARCHITECTURE operating mode.

Pre-flight. Read crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md (684 lines; particular attention to the API envelope contract {data, meta, errors} added at commit 9b6fcdc and the prefixed-identifier client-side-computation rule); PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/multi-engagement-architecture.md from Conversation 1 (the 12-step activation sequence in §4 and the two-database API server detail in §3.10 plus the five Conversation-2-deferred questions in §6.1); PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-schema-specs/engagement.md (the engagement entity schema and the five Conversation-2-deferred questions in §3.8.1); PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/v0.5-engagement-management-workstream-plan.md (the four-slice strawman in §5 and the alternative slicings); ui-PRD-v0.4.md and ui-v0.4-implementation-plan.md (shape templates for the v0.5 release deliverables); the User Process Guide / Engagement Playbook v0.1 at PRDs/process/v2-user-process-guide.md committed at 7885cfe (data layer described as a single SQLite file v2.db; v0.5 introduces multi-engagement routing, so v0.2 update is needed but deferred to v0.6 per the in-flight workstream).

Surfaced numbering-collision context. The parallel PI-001 styling workstream's Conversation 1 (SES-027) closed earlier the same day at 05-16-26; its close-out payload at close-out-payloads/ses_027.json claims SES-027 plus DEC-087 through DEC-094 (eight decisions covering density, color palette, typography, theme keying, modal elevation, icon library Lucide, selected-state vocabulary, and the styling design pass approval). The styling Conversation 2 ran in parallel and committed ui-PRD-v0.6.md plus ui-v0.6-implementation-plan.md mid-way through this conversation; styling Conversation 2 claims SES-028 plus DEC-095-097 (version-bundling as v0.6 separate from v0.5, six-slice structure A-F, per-slice screenshot + WCAG check acceptance pattern). This conversation's payload therefore claims SES-029 plus DEC-098-104 — collision resolution applied at close after the styling apply landed.

Q1+Q2 (consequential decision, paired). UI affordance placement for switching and for management. Three options framed: A — top-strip switching widget + Engagements sidebar group with one entry + dual paths to management; B — sidebar-only with everything in the Engagements group; C — modal-only switching with sidebar group for management. Option A approved. Rationale: switching is a frequent action that needs always-visible affordance distinct from the sidebar's content-navigation function; the Engagements sidebar group entry is the discoverability path for management; dual paths to the management panel (sidebar entry + 'Manage engagements...' picker footer) reduce friction without creating navigational ambiguity. The picker dropdown lives below the top-strip with engagements ordered by engagement_last_opened_at descending, paused/archived sorted to bottom in muted color, active marked with Lucide check icon; footer item opens management panel. Sidebar group positioned above Governance per the v2-install-level scope distinction.

Q3 (consequential decision). Single-gesture engagement creation+activation. Two options: A — one click runs POST + DB file creation + activation behind the scenes with graceful failure recovery; B — two-gesture path with explicit 'Create' then 'Activate' affordances. Option A approved. The New Engagement dialog runs three sequential operations on Submit (POST /engagements → desktop creates per-engagement DB file with Alembic at head → desktop initiates 12-step activation). Three-label progress indicator advances through the operations. Activation failure after both creates succeed surfaces inline with 'Try switching now' / 'Stay in <previous>' affordances; engagement record persists. Validation errors at each stage roll back appropriately. Rationale: matches user mental model (creating an engagement implies starting work in it); the failure-recovery affordances handle the one realistic partial-success state cleanly; the alternative two-gesture flow would force every creator to choose 'now or later' twice which is friction without payoff.

Q9 (consequential decision, refined from kickoff strawman). Slice breakdown. Workstream-plan strawman had four slices (foundation+migration combined, engagement entity panel, switching, closeout); alternative granular framing had six slices (config foundation, dogfood migration, schema+access+REST, panel UI, switching, closeout). Decided five slices: A foundation+migration combined (because foundation alone is non-functional intermediate state), B engagement schema+access layer+REST API, C engagement management panel UI, D switching mechanism+top-strip+picker+single-gesture creation+activation flow, E closeout. Slice A monitored for size at execution time; A1/A2 split fallback documented. Rationale for splitting B from C: B's REST API surface (eight standard endpoints with validation) plus C's panel UI (master/detail panel plus CRUD dialogs plus forbid-active-delete) are each substantial; combining them produces an oversize prompt. Rationale for D as one slice rather than splitting picker from activation worker: the picker and activation worker are tightly coupled (picker click triggers activation worker; activation overlay binds to worker progress signals; the single-gesture flow uses the activation worker as its third step); splitting would require defining a fake activation API in slice C/D and a backfill in the next slice.

Decide-and-announce decisions. Q4 — Forbid soft-delete on active engagement: delete dialog refuses with inline redirect to switch first; last-engagement edge case uses 'create another first' wording. The drift-recovery path in spec §3.4.5 remains as safety net. Q5 — Null default for engagement_export_dir on new engagements: dialog field empty by default with 'Optional — leave blank to disable auto-export' placeholder; null disables auto-export with log warning at activation; the dogfood migration explicitly sets a non-null value for CRMBUILDER (the engine-repo db-export/ path computed at migration time).

Operational decisions (just decided per the two-part test outcome). Q6 — engagement_last_opened_at PATCH path during the switching dance: Option B (defer PATCH until after new API subprocess is up) chosen over Option A (desktop opens meta DB directly during the kill-relaunch dance). Respects DEC-019's API-only principle for recurring operations (the dogfood migration's direct-DB-access exception is a one-shot first-launch thing; activation happens every switch). The PATCH lagging by the few hundred milliseconds between API health-check success and signal emission is imperceptible; PATCH is idempotent so health-check failure leaves the timestamp unchanged with no other consequence. Architecture doc §4 step ordering amended (step 7 moves to between steps 9 and 11; renumbered 12-step sequence documented in PRD §3 architecture summary). Q10 — Meta DB export location: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/meta/engagements.json; the meta/ subdirectory lives parallel to the dogfood's engagement-content exports at db-export/, making the scope distinction visible (install-level routing metadata vs engagement content) while keeping related artifacts together. Q7 — engagement panel refresh trigger: file-watch on db-export/meta/engagements.json per the standard v0.3+ pattern, plus subscription to active_engagement_changed Qt signals for refreshing the active-engagement marker when switching occurs without underlying engagement records changing. Q8 — activation sequence retry/timeout values: adopt architecture doc §4 defaults as written (step 4 port-release timeout 5s before SIGKILL escalation; step 9 health-check exponential backoff starting 100ms, max delay 5s, total timeout 30s; step 3 Alembic pre-flight no explicit timeout with progress indicator). Tune in slice D or post-build if observation reveals problems.

PRD authoring. Drafted PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ui-PRD-v0.5.md at 425 lines, density-matched to v0.4's 378 lines (v0.5 architecture-summary section richer because of routing infrastructure). Thirteen sections: §1 Overview (purpose, background, source decisions including DEC-039/046/068/075-077 plus DEC-078-086 plus DEC-098 through DEC-104 forthcoming, renumbering note recording the three-way collision resolution); §2 Scope (10 in-scope plus 16 out-of-scope items); §3 Architecture summary; §4 Schema summary; §5 Functional Requirements (5.1 management panel; 5.2 switching affordance; 5.3 single-gesture creation; 5.4 dogfood migration; 5.5 lazy migration; 5.6 forbid-active-delete); §6 Cross-Cutting Concerns including §6.5 User Process Guide drift acknowledgement deferring v0.2 to v0.6; §7 Acceptance Criteria including slice E closeout criteria E1-E8; §8 Implementation Plan Reference; §9 PI-001 Coordination (rewritten at closeout after styling Conversation 2 committed ui-PRD-v0.6.md mid-conversation; v0.5 ships first per DEC-095; v0.6 retrofits engagement panel as the 13th panel covered by v0.6 slice C; v0.5 surfaces ship with Qt-default styling plus TODO-for-v0.6-retrofit inline placeholder values); §10 Constraints (storage layer additive; API-only access except at activation and migration boundaries; per-engagement DB schemas unchanged; no engagement-aware MCP tools in v0.5); §11 Risks and Mitigations (10 risks each with likelihood/impact/mitigation); §12 Open Questions (5 questions deferred to build time); §13 Decisions to Be Recorded (DEC-098 through DEC-104 with renumbering note recording the collision resolution).

Implementation plan authoring. Drafted PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ui-v0.5-implementation-plan.md at 437 lines, density-matched to v0.4's 450 lines. Linear five-slice dependency chain (unlike v0.4's partial independence). New module directory layout fully specified including: access/meta_db.py, access/engagement_models.py, access/engagement.py, api/routers/engagements.py, migration/dogfood_v0_5.py, migration/lazy_migration.py, migration/meta_alembic.py, ui/active_engagement_context.py, ui/activation_worker.py, ui/widgets/engagement_top_strip.py, ui/widgets/engagement_picker.py, ui/widgets/activation_overlay.py, ui/panels/engagement_panel.py, ui/dialogs/engagement_crud.py, ui/dialogs/engagement_delete.py, ui/dialogs/new_engagement_dialog.py. Two Alembic chains: meta DB at crmbuilder-v2/migrations/meta/ (new in slice A), per-engagement chain unchanged. Test counts estimated ~165-225 new across the five slices.

Slice build prompt authoring. Drafted five prompts: CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.5-A-foundation-and-dogfood-migration.md (slice A); -B-engagement-schema-and-api.md (slice B); -C-engagement-management-panel.md (slice C); -D-engagement-switching.md (slice D); -E-closeout.md (slice E). Each prompt follows the v0.4 prompt shape (purpose, project context, pre-flight, reading order, step-by-step implementation guidance, acceptance verification, commit instructions with single-commit-Doug-pushes convention, what-NOT-to-do guardrails). Slice A includes the A1/A2 split fallback if size becomes unmanageable at execution. Slice C includes inert TODO placeholders for the 'Switch engagement' / 'Create engagement' buttons in the forbid-active-delete dialog state, with slice D rewiring documented. Slice D documents the 12-step activation sequence with the question-6 amendment (PATCH deferred to step 10). Slice prompts reference styling-design-pass.md as a reading and inheritance point but include the inline-placeholder fallback (which is the actual expected posture given v0.6's separate-release sequencing per DEC-095).

Close-out path chosen. Path 1 (Claude Code apply prompt) mirroring SES-026's precedent — the apply prompt POSTs the payload via apply_close_out.py. Per in-sandbox close-out convention from recent_updates: Claude commits AND pushes all nine deliverables (PRD + implementation plan + five slice prompts + close-out payload + apply prompt) in one commit at conversation close; Doug pulls; the apply prompt itself runs at Doug's terminal under the 'Doug pushes' convention for local-clone work.

Context compaction event. The conversation transcript exceeded the active context window during slice A prompt authoring; the conversation history was compacted to a structured summary. Post-compaction work proceeded against the summary: slice A prompt already drafted before compaction; slices B-E prompts plus close-out payload plus apply prompt drafted after compaction. Mid-way through closeout authoring, a pull from origin/main brought in the styling Conversation 2 deliverables (ui-PRD-v0.6.md, ui-v0.6-implementation-plan.md) plus the SES-027 styling apply; the payload and PRD §9 and PRD renumbering notes were updated to reflect the three-way numbering collision and v0.6's separate-release posture before commit.

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option 1: four slices per the workstream-plan strawman (foundation+migration in one, engagement entity panel in another, switching, closeout). Rejected because the 'engagement entity panel' in the strawman bundled both the REST API and the panel UI, producing an oversize prompt mixing access-layer/API/validation work with UI/CRUD-dialog work. Option 2: six slices per kickoff §5.3's granular framing. Rejected because separating the dogfood migration into its own slice creates a non-functional intermediate state at slice A's completion (the foundation infrastructure exists but the dogfood is still at the old path; running v0.5 in that state requires a fallback path that adds code complexity for a state that exists for only one slice). Option 3: eight slices splitting the activation worker, the picker, and the single-gesture flow into separate slices. Rejected because the tight coupling among these components would require multiple fake-coupling-point slices.
after_payload.context: v0.5 Conversation 2's slicing question (Q9 in the kickoff). Workstream-plan strawman from §5 had four slices (foundation+migration combined, engagement entity panel, switching, closeout). Alternative granular framing from kickoff §5.3 had six slices (config foundation, dogfood migration standalone, schema+access+REST, panel UI, switching+mechanism, closeout). Slice count and content directly affect build-time cadence: too tight makes each prompt oversize and increases the cost of any one slice's failure mode; too granular increases prompt-and-merge overhead and creates non-functional intermediate states. The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact (build cadence and acceptance-gate granularity over the next several days of execution) AND two viable options producing meaningfully different outcomes (four vs six vs five-with-different-cuts). Decided via the consequential-decision template.
after_payload.decision: Five slices: A — foundation infrastructure (meta DB, ActiveEngagementContext, two-database API wiring, lazy-migration helper, sidebar Engagements group container, refresh-service extension) PLUS dogfood migration combined in one slice; B — engagement schema (dataclass, status enum), access layer (eight standard repository methods with validation), and REST API (eight standard endpoints with envelope shape, JSON snapshot hook); C — engagement management panel UI (ListDetailPanel subclass, master/detail panes, CRUD dialogs, forbid-active-delete with last-engagement edge case, refresh-service registration); D — switching mechanism (top-strip widget, picker dropdown, 12-step ActivationWorker with question-6 amendment, activation overlay) plus single-gesture creation+activation flow (NewEngagementDialog extending slice C's EngagementCreateDialog with file creation and activation steps); E — closeout (version 0.5.0 bump, README release note, end-to-end integration smoke test, final regression pass, manual smoke verification). Slice A monitored for size at execution time; A1 (foundation only) / A2 (dogfood migration) split is the documented fallback if the slice A prompt exceeds healthy size.
after_payload.rationale: Five slices balance build cadence with acceptance-gate granularity. Combining foundation with dogfood migration in slice A is justified because foundation alone is a non-functional intermediate state (the meta DB exists but no engagement records and dogfood data is still at the old path; the desktop cannot operate against this state without falling back to the old single-DB path or failing); separating them would require either a non-functional intermediate slice or a hidden fallback path in slice A's code, both of which are worse than combining. Splitting slice B (schema+API) from slice C (panel UI) is justified because each is substantial on its own — B's eight REST endpoints with full validation logic plus the JSON snapshot hook is a complete subsystem; C's master/detail panel plus three CRUD dialogs plus the forbid-active-delete edge cases is a complete UI surface — and combining them produces an oversize prompt with mixed concerns. Keeping the switching mechanism (slice D) as one slice rather than splitting picker from activation worker is justified because the picker and activation worker are tightly coupled: the picker click triggers the activation worker, the activation overlay binds to worker progress signals, and the single-gesture flow uses the activation worker as its third step; splitting would require defining a fake activation API in slice C/D and a backfill in the next slice. The A1/A2 split fallback is documented but not pre-committed; the decision is made at slice A's prompt-drafting time if size signals warrant.
after_payload.title: v0.5 slice breakdown: five slices with foundation+migration combined

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option B: sidebar-only with everything in the Engagements group. Rejected because switching becomes a multi-click sequence (sidebar → engagement panel → row → 'Activate' action) when it should be a single-click affordance. Option C: modal-only switching invoked from the sidebar with no top-strip. Rejected because modal-only switching breaks the user's mental model — switching is a transient action, not a configuration dialog, and forcing a modal interrupt for every switch is friction without payoff. Option D: top-strip-only without a sidebar group. Rejected because management UI discoverability suffers; new users wouldn't know where to find the panel. Option E: place the Engagements group below the existing sidebar groups instead of above. Rejected because v2-install-level scope is structurally above per-engagement scope; placement below would suggest the opposite hierarchy.
after_payload.context: v0.5 Conversation 2's two UI-placement questions (Q1 and Q2 in the kickoff), settled together because the affordances are coupled. Q1: where does the switching affordance live? Q2: where does the management affordance live? The engagement entity is structurally different from the four v0.4 methodology entity types (v2-install-level routing metadata vs domain-scope methodology content), so it doesn't fit the Methodology sidebar group introduced in v0.4. Switching is a frequent action requiring always-visible affordance; management is a less-frequent action requiring discoverable affordance. The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact (every user interaction with v0.5 touches one or both affordances) AND two viable options producing meaningfully different outcomes (top-strip vs sidebar-only vs modal). Decided via the consequential-decision template.
after_payload.decision: Three coordinated affordances: (1) always-visible top-strip switching widget positioned above the sidebar entries inside the sidebar container; renders the active engagement's name with code in parentheses and a Lucide chevron-down caret; clicking opens the picker dropdown. (2) Picker dropdown anchored below the top-strip; live engagements ordered by engagement_last_opened_at descending; paused and archived rendered in color.neutral.500 and sorted to bottom within their bucket; active engagement marked with a Lucide check icon; footer item 'Manage engagements...' separated by hairline divider, opens the management panel. Empty state ('No engagement selected' top-strip; picker contains only the footer item) handled. (3) Engagements sidebar group positioned above Governance with one entry titled 'Engagements' that opens the management panel — same panel that the picker footer opens. The management panel is a ListDetailPanel subclass per the v0.4 pattern: master pane with five columns (Identifier, Code, Name, Status, Last Opened); detail pane with form fields per engagement.md §3.6.3; standard CRUD operations including the forbid-active-delete behavior per DEC-101.
after_payload.rationale: The top-strip placement gives switching an always-visible affordance distinct from the sidebar's content-navigation function. Switching is potentially frequent (multiple times per work session if a consultant is working across multiple engagements); requiring a sidebar click would add friction. The Engagements sidebar group entry is the discoverability path for users who treat the sidebar as their primary navigation; without the entry, the management UI would be reachable only through the picker's footer item which is less discoverable. Dual paths to the management panel (sidebar entry + picker footer) reduce friction without creating navigational ambiguity — both paths arrive at the same panel. The sidebar group's position above Governance reflects the v2-install-level scope distinction (engagement records are routing metadata across all engagements, structurally above the per-engagement governance and methodology content). Picker ordering rules (live first by last-opened-at descending; non-live at bottom; active marked) match user expectations for an MRU-style picker. The empty-state handling is required for the fresh-install case where the picker has nothing to select from.
after_payload.title: Engagement UI affordance placement: top-strip switching widget plus Engagements sidebar group plus dual paths to management panel

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option B: two-gesture flow with explicit Create then Activate affordances. Rejected because every creator would have to choose 'now or later' twice (Create dialog and then a separate Activate action), which is friction without payoff for the dominant use case (create-then-immediately-work-in). Option C: single-gesture with all-or-nothing rollback (any failure rolls back to no-engagement-created state). Rejected because activation failure after both creates succeed is a realistic transient state (port-release timeout, MCP launch failure, etc.) where rolling back the engagement record forces re-data-entry; preserving the record with a retry-or-stay choice is better UX. Option D: single-gesture with implicit retry on activation failure. Rejected because hidden retry behavior produces opaque latency and obscures real failures from the user; explicit retry is better.
after_payload.context: v0.5 Conversation 2's engagement-creation flow question (Q3 in the kickoff). Creating a new engagement requires three operations: POST /engagements (creates meta DB row), desktop creates per-engagement DB file with Alembic at head, desktop initiates 12-step activation. The question is whether to expose these as a single user gesture (one click triggers all three) or as separate gestures (user clicks 'Create', then later clicks 'Activate'). The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact (creation is a recurring action; the choice shapes every engagement-creation user experience) AND two viable options producing meaningfully different outcomes (single-gesture vs two-gesture). Decided via the consequential-decision template.
after_payload.decision: Single-gesture creation+activation: the New Engagement dialog runs all three operations behind one user click on Submit. Three-label progress indicator advances through 'Creating engagement record...' → 'Initializing database...' → 'Switching to <name>...' Each label transitions to a Lucide check icon on success or a Lucide circle-x in color.danger.default on failure. Validation errors at each stage roll back appropriately: POST failure stays in the dialog with inline error per the v0.3 validation-error pattern; file-creation failure DELETEs the meta DB row to roll back; activation failure after both creates succeed surfaces inline with 'Try switching now' and 'Stay in <previous engagement>' affordances and the engagement record persists. The slice-C EngagementCreateDialog (which creates the meta DB row only) remains in the codebase as a parent class for NewEngagementDialog; the slice-D NewEngagementDialog is the primary path for engagement creation after slice D lands, but EngagementCreateDialog is still available for scripted use cases (e.g., creating a record without activation for testing or recovery scenarios).
after_payload.rationale: Single-gesture creation matches the user's mental model: creating an engagement implies wanting to work in it; forcing a separate 'now activate it' gesture adds friction without payoff. The three-label progress indicator gives a coherent narrative for the few-second sequence; the labels advance fast enough to read as confirmation of progress rather than as a slow operation. The graceful failure-recovery affordances handle the one realistic partial-success state (engagement record and file exist but activation failed) cleanly without silent rollback; the user explicitly chooses whether to retry or to stay in the previous engagement. The slice-C dialog remaining in the codebase as a parent class enables the slice-D extension via inheritance rather than duplication, and supports edge-case use through direct invocation.
after_payload.title: Single-gesture engagement creation+activation flow with graceful inline failure recovery

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option B: accept the operation and auto-switch to another engagement before completing the soft-delete. Rejected because the auto-switch target is ambiguous (most-recent? alphabetical? what if there's only one other? what if it's soft-deleted?); making the user choose via the picker is cleaner. Option C: hard error with no redirect (just refuse). Rejected because the message would leave the user wondering what to do next; the redirect affordance is small additional cost for substantial UX gain.
after_payload.context: v0.5 Conversation 2's active-engagement-soft-delete question (Q4 in the kickoff). When the user attempts to soft-delete the engagement that is currently active, two postures are possible: (a) refuse the operation and redirect to switching first, or (b) accept the operation and auto-switch to another engagement before completing the soft-delete. The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact is minor (the operation is uncommon by use-case design) AND only one option is genuinely defensible (refusing is cleaner; auto-switch introduces choosing-which-engagement-to-switch-to as a hidden decision). Decided as decide-and-announce.
after_payload.decision: The engagement panel's Delete dialog detects whether the target row is the currently-active engagement (via comparison against ActiveEngagementContext.engagement_identifier). When active, the standard edge-text confirmation field is replaced with an inline message: '<engagement_name> is currently active. Switch to a different engagement first, then soft-delete this one.' The Delete button is replaced with a 'Switch engagement' button that opens the picker dropdown. Last-engagement edge case (the active engagement is the only one on the install): message changes to '<engagement_name> is the only engagement on this install. Create another engagement before soft-deleting this one.' The button changes to 'Create engagement' opening the New Engagement dialog (single-gesture variant in slice D, slice-C Create dialog before slice D lands). The next-launch drift-recovery path in engagement.md §3.4.5 remains as a safety net for cross-restart desync (e.g., user soft-deletes the active engagement via direct API call or external script, then restarts the desktop; current_engagement.json points at a soft-deleted engagement; in-memory context starts empty; picker prompts for selection).
after_payload.rationale: Refusing the operation is cleaner than auto-switch: the user explicitly chooses which engagement to switch to (via the picker) rather than the system silently picking 'next most recent' or similar. Inline redirect (replacing the Delete button with 'Switch engagement') keeps the user in the same UI context, avoiding modal-stacking. The last-engagement edge case wording handles the corner case where switching isn't possible, redirecting to creation. The drift-recovery safety net handles out-of-band deletion that bypasses the dialog. The operation is uncommon by use-case design (consultants don't typically delete the engagement they're working in), so the additional friction of refuse-and-redirect is acceptable.
after_payload.title: Forbid soft-delete on the currently-active engagement with inline redirect to switch first

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option B: pre-fill with a computed path like ~/Documents/crmbuilder/{engagement_code}/db-export/. Rejected because the path would be wrong for the dominant use case (client-repo-based) and right for an edge case (consultant-local-only). Option C: pre-fill with an absolute path computed from a configuration setting. Rejected because v0.5 has no such configuration setting and adding one for a default isn't justified.
after_payload.context: v0.5 Conversation 2's export-dir-default question (Q5 in the kickoff). The engagement_export_dir field on the engagement record is nullable per engagement.md §3.2; when set, it points at an absolute filesystem path where the engagement's JSON snapshots are auto-written. The New Engagement dialog needs to handle the default state: pre-fill with a computed path (e.g., a subdirectory of the engagement-code-named home directory)? Leave blank? The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact is minor (this is a configuration default, not a behavior change) AND only one option fits the Q8-of-Conversation-1 framing where null disables auto-export. Decided as decide-and-announce.
after_payload.decision: The New Engagement dialog's export-dir field is empty by default with placeholder text 'Optional — leave blank to disable auto-export.' and tooltip 'Where this engagement's JSON snapshots will be written. Recommend a path inside the client repo so exports travel with the engagement documents.' Null is a first-class value (disables auto-export; the access-layer validates this case in slice B and the activation-time export check in slice D logs a warning if null is the value). The dogfood migration in slice A explicitly sets a non-null value for CRMBUILDER (the engine-repo db-export/ path computed at migration time); the migration's behavior is divergent from the dialog default because the dogfood engagement has a definite and computable export location while user-created engagements typically need explicit configuration of where exports should land.
after_payload.rationale: Null default avoids guessing what the user wants (any pre-filled path would either need to be a sensible default for most use cases or would mislead users into accepting a value they hadn't actually chosen). The placeholder text makes the optionality explicit. The tooltip provides discoverability for the recommended pattern (path inside the client repo) without forcing it. The CRMBUILDER divergence from the dialog default is justified because the dogfood has a known export location (engine-repo db-export/) that the migration knows about; user-created engagements don't have an analogous always-correct default.
after_payload.title: Null default for engagement_export_dir on new engagement records

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option B: no JSON export for the meta DB; engagement panel refreshes via explicit API polling or via signals fired by access-layer writes only. Rejected because it breaks uniformity with the v0.3+ pattern and creates a special-case refresh mechanism for one panel. Option C: bundle the meta DB export into the dogfood's db-export/ as a single engagements.json at the parent level. Rejected because the dogfood's db-export/ is scoped to CRMBUILDER's content; mixing in install-level metadata produces a confusing artifact and a coupling between the dogfood and the meta DB that doesn't otherwise exist. Option D: place the meta DB export outside db-export/ entirely. Rejected because doing so departs from the established v0.3+ JSON-snapshot pattern without justification.
after_payload.context: v0.5 Conversation 2's meta DB export location question (Q10 in the kickoff, tightly coupled to Q7's engagement panel refresh trigger). The meta DB hosts the engagements registry table; v0.3's JSON-snapshot pattern (DEC-022, DEC-008) makes writes regenerate snapshot files under db-export/ that panels file-watch for refresh. The question is whether the meta DB participates in this pattern and where its snapshot lands. The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact is moderate (the snapshot location affects how the engagement panel refreshes and where the artifact lives in the repo) AND there are two viable options producing meaningfully different outcomes (bundled into the dogfood's db-export/ vs separate location). Decided as an operational refinement.
after_payload.decision: Meta DB has its own JSON export at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/meta/engagements.json. The db-export/meta/ subdirectory lives parallel to the dogfood's engagement-content exports at db-export/ (which are sessions.json, decisions.json, planning_items.json, etc. — the CRMBUILDER engagement's content). Writes to the meta DB regenerate engagements.json via the standard access-layer hook synchronously within the same transaction. The path is fixed at the engine-repo level (not configurable per engagement, because the meta DB is install-scope routing metadata, not engagement-scope content). The engagement panel registers with the refresh service and refreshes on file change per the standard v0.3+ pattern.
after_payload.rationale: Uniformity with the v0.3+ JSON-snapshot pattern: the meta DB participates in the same render-on-write discipline as per-engagement DBs. The meta/ subdirectory makes the scope distinction visible (install-level routing metadata vs engagement content) — a future reader of the db-export/ directory tree can distinguish 'this is CRMBUILDER's content' from 'this is install-level engagement registry' without reading the file contents. Bundling the meta DB export into the dogfood's db-export/engagements.json is conceptually wrong because the dogfood's per-engagement DB doesn't contain engagement records; engagement records live in the meta DB; mixing them produces a confusing artifact. Placing the meta DB export outside db-export/ entirely (e.g., at crmbuilder-v2/data/exports/) would require a separate file-watch pattern outside the v0.3+ convention.
after_payload.title: Meta DB JSON exports at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/meta/engagements.json

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Alternative scope shapes were not explicitly evaluated as alternatives — the scope was iteratively refined during the conversation through the ten settled questions and the consequential-decision presentations. The PRD shape (13 sections vs the kickoff's suggested 8-section shape) was chosen to match v0.4's shape per the kickoff's 'Shape follows ui-PRD-v0.4.md' direction.
after_payload.context: v0.5 Conversation 2's PRD approval gate (Q12 implicit in the kickoff's deliverables list). The PRD at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ui-PRD-v0.5.md and the companion implementation plan at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ui-v0.5-implementation-plan.md and the five slice build prompts at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.5-{A..E}-*.md were drafted in this conversation and presented for review. The PRD opens 'Draft — pending approval'; this decision records the transition to Approved.
after_payload.decision: v0.5 UI PRD v0.5 approved: 10 in-scope items (multi-engagement routing infrastructure; dogfood migration; engagement entity type with eight standard endpoints; 12-step activation sequence with question-6 amendment; Engagements sidebar group and management panel; switching affordance with top-strip and picker; single-gesture engagement creation; forbid-active-delete with last-engagement edge case; meta DB JSON exports with file-watch refresh; About-dialog version bump and README release note), 16 out-of-scope items (multi-tenant API+MCP per PI-017; engagement-level access control; v1 Client import; cross-engagement queries; optional engagement_db_path override; engagement rename; hard delete with file removal; additional engagement-level fields; User Process Guide v0.2; auto-switch on active-engagement soft-delete; right-click 'Activate' on engagement panel; full styling design pass execution; methodology entity types beyond v0.4's four; methodology entity renderers; catalog FK integration; v1 NOT_SUPPORTED reimplementation workstreams), five-slice plan (A foundation+migration combined; B engagement schema+access+REST; C panel UI; D switching+single-gesture; E closeout), 10 acceptance criteria for slice E closeout plus the 13 architecture-level and 12 entity-level acceptance criteria distributed across slices A-D. Implementation plan companion approved at the same time. Five slice build prompts approved at the same time. PRD §9 coordination section rewritten at closeout to reflect the styling Conversation 2 v0.6 PRD that landed mid-conversation: v0.5 ships first per DEC-095's separate-release sequencing; v0.6 retrofits engagement panel and all other surfaces as the 13th panel covered by v0.6 slice C. PRD transitions from Draft to Approved.
after_payload.rationale: The PRD and implementation plan and five slice prompts together cover everything needed for v0.5 execution: requirements (PRD), build sequencing (implementation plan), step-by-step execution guidance (slice prompts). The release scope matches the workstream-plan and Conversation 1's architectural foundation; the slice plan optimizes for build cadence while preserving non-trivial acceptance gates; the deferred items have justified rationales (cited PIs, future versions, scope discipline). The PRD's 11 risks and mitigations and 5 open questions are appropriate for a build-time-tunable release. The §9 rewrite captures the actual coordination posture with v0.6 (which crystallized mid-conversation when the styling Conversation 2 deliverables landed on origin/main) rather than the pre-clarification posture that the original draft assumed.
after_payload.title: v0.5 UI PRD approval — release scope and slice plan accepted

The PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-v0.5-build-execution-closeout.md prompt remains pending. Its plan assigned SES-030..SES-035 to the six v0.5 build-execution conversations (slices A-E plus launcher-wiring follow-up); this session claims SES-030 ahead of that pass and folds in the slice D follow-up as scope expansion within the same Claude Code conversation. When Doug runs the closeout, its dynamic identifier assignment will compute max+1 (SES-031 after this record) and renumber its planned writes accordingly; the launcher-wiring follow-up's coverage moves from the closeout's planned SES-035 to this SES-030 (which additionally covers the slice D follow-up the closeout's plan did not anticipate). The closeout prompt also includes the status entity update from 'v0.4 complete' to 'v0.5 complete'; that work remains unaffected by this record and still belongs to the closeout pass.

Follow-on technical work not yet scheduled: per-request engagement routing per DEC-080's committed multi-tenant migration trajectory remains the eventual proper fix for the per-process db_path coupling. The data_dir() shim makes the current one-process-per-engagement model robust; the longer-term shift would let one API process serve multiple engagements without env-var dancing or kill+relaunch.
after_payload.summary: Two-commit Claude Code session. Commit one (a4c8b35) executed the slice A launcher-wiring prompt as written: bootstrap_meta_db() at API startup, dogfood-migration trigger at desktop startup with QProgressDialog and hard-fail UX, three regression tests. Discovered a SQLite race during full-suite run (create_all(checkfirst=True) not atomic against eight parallel test-client TestClients) and absorbed it with a narrow OperationalError catch at the call site, preserving slice A's helper untouched per the prompt's 'do not modify slice A helpers' guardrail. Manual smoke surfaced a second defect: per-engagement endpoints 500 against Doug's running API. Diagnosed as a slice D gap — initial UI launch never sets CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH because activation only fires on user-driven engagement switches. Investigation surfaced a third latent bug upstream: access/meta_db.meta_db_path() and migration/lazy_migration.engagement_db_path() derive their locations from s.db_path.parent, which silently produces wrong nested paths when db_path is in the post-activation engagements/{code}.db shape (the slice E end-to-end test masks this with no-op subprocess managers; the activation worker would have hit it in production). Commit two (3467764) closes both: ui/app.py.main now routes the API at the active engagement before lifecycle.start(), and a new data_dir() helper in access/meta_db.py normalises both layouts back to data/ so the meta DB and sibling engagement DBs always resolve correctly regardless of which file db_path names. Both commits pushed; operator API restarted with the env var explicitly set; UI verified green.
after_payload.title: v0.5 slice A follow-up (launcher wiring) + slice D follow-up (route API at active engagement, fix latent data_dir derivation) — two-commit Claude Code session
after_payload.topics_covered:
Pre-flight per the prompt: git pull (already up to date at start), confirmed working tree clean, identity Doug Bower <doug@dougbower.com> (prompt's noted expectation of dbower44022@users.noreply.github.com was stale against the actual recent-commit convention; matched the convention). Reading order: CLAUDE.md v2 section; UI PRD v0.5 §5.4 (dogfood migration at first launch); slice A api/main.py, ui/app.py, access/meta_db.py, migration/dogfood_v0_5.py; slice E end-to-end test for fixture pattern. Pre-flight noted Doug's local data dir still contained v2.db (prompt expected it absent post-manual-migration); proceeded per the 'reasonable call and continue' direction.

Step 1 — API startup hook (api/main.py). Added bootstrap_meta_db() call before init_meta_db_pool(), keeping the prior run_meta_migrations() try/except in place. First pass shipped bootstrap_meta_db() unguarded.

Step 2 — desktop startup migration trigger (ui/app.py). Introduced _run_dogfood_migration_if_needed(log) helper invoked between build_application() and splash.show(). Wraps the synchronous migration in an indeterminate QProgressDialog with no cancel affordance per slice A spec; on failure surfaces a critical QMessageBox naming the .pre-v0.5-backup recovery path and returns _MIGRATION_FAILED_EXIT_CODE (=1).

Step 3 — integration test (tests/crmbuilder_v2/integration/test_launcher_wiring.py, new file). Three regression tests: test_api_startup_creates_meta_db drives create_app() and asserts engagements.db materialises; test_desktop_launcher_runs_migration_when_needed drives the helper against a freshly-seeded v0.4-state fixture and asserts the post-migration filesystem shape; test_desktop_launcher_is_noop_when_already_migrated drives a second invocation and asserts no duplicate row or file rewrite. Fixture pattern adapted from tests/crmbuilder_v2/integration/test_v0_5_end_to_end.py (inline v0.4-shape v2.db seeding rather than the absent fixtures/v0_4_state_v2.db file the prompt sketched).

First full-suite run regressed two previously-passing tests: tests/crmbuilder_v2/api/test_domains_api.py and test_entities_api.py — both test_concurrent_posts_get_distinct_identifiers. Isolated repro confirmed 4/5 to 6/10 failure rate when run together, never in isolation. Traceback fingered create_all(checkfirst=True) racing past has_table on SQLite when eight TestClient(create_app()) instances spin up in parallel — the loser hits 'table engagements already exists'. Narrow OperationalError catch around bootstrap_meta_db() in api/main.py absorbed the race; subsequent 10/10 isolated reruns confirmed clean and the full suite returned 1232 passed (+3 vs. baseline 1229). Lint via ruff --fix sorted imports in api/main.py. Stashed local changes, git pull --rebase origin main (one intervening v0.6 slice E prompt commit, unrelated), restored, committed as a4c8b35.

Manual smoke against Doug's machine surfaced a separate failure: UI launched, lifecycle adopted the externally-running API at port 8765, and per-engagement endpoints (/decisions, /charter/versions) returned HTTP 500 while /engagements and /health returned 200. Inspection showed Doug's data dir had three files in place — v2.db (empty: no alembic_version, no decisions, no charter), engagements/CRMBUILDER.db (alembic head 0010_v0_4_create_crm_candidates_table, 101 decisions, 2 charter), engagements.db (meta DB with the CRMBUILDER row). The running API had no CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH set in its environment, so Settings.db_path defaulted to data/v2.db and SQLAlchemy lazy-create had materialised an empty file there at first connection. Confirmed not caused by the just-shipped launcher-wiring fix (the running API was started before the commit and inherits no env var management from it).

Root cause investigation. Slice D wired engagement switching through ActivationWorker, whose build_lifecycle_managers._launch_api correctly sets CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH to engagement_db_path(target.engagement_code) before lifecycle.start(). But the initial UI launch never triggers activation — it loads ActiveEngagementContext from current_engagement.json, hands the UI a populated context, then ServerLifecycle.start() either adopts an externally-running API or QProcess-spawns a fresh one. Neither path touches the env var. So a clean install or any session-restart presents the same symptom; Doug's case was the externally-running-API variant.

While sketching Option A (set CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH in ui/app.py from active_engagement.engagement_code()), surfaced a second latent defect upstream: access/meta_db.meta_db_path() and migration/lazy_migration.engagement_db_path() both compute their location as s.db_path.parent / 'engagements.db' (or '.../engagements/{code}.db'). Correct when db_path is data/v2.db (parent = data, derives correctly) but silently wrong when db_path is data/engagements/CRMBUILDER.db (parent = data/engagements, derives data/engagements/engagements.db for the meta DB and data/engagements/engagements/CRMBUILDER.db for sibling engagement DBs). The activation worker has been setting db_path to that shape since slice D landed; the slice E end-to-end test masks the bug with no-op subprocess managers (the real _launch_api is never invoked). A real production spawn after activation would have hit it. Verified with a one-liner probe (CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH=/tmp/x/data/engagements/CRMBUILDER.db → meta_db_path resolves wrong).

Operator gate. Presented three options: narrow Option A only (trades /decisions 500 for /engagements 500 on fresh spawn — net zero on Doug's case); recommended Option A + fix data_dir derivation (closes the latent activation-worker bug as a side benefit); or hold off and design a proper fix (per-request engagement routing per DEC-080's committed multi-tenant migration trajectory). Operator chose option two.

Implementation. (1) access/meta_db.py — new public data_dir() helper that returns parent.parent when parent.name == 'engagements', else parent; meta_db_path() delegates to it. Backward-compatible against every existing test. (2) migration/lazy_migration.py — engagement_db_path() imports data_dir() and uses it the same way; layout-invariant. (3) ui/app.py — new _route_api_at_active_engagement(active, log) helper invoked between active_engagement.load_from_disk() (which still reads current_engagement.json under the default data-dir resolution) and lifecycle.start(). On non-None engagement_code, sets the env var and resets settings + engine caches so this process and any QProcess.start child see the new value. No-op for fresh-install or post-deactivation. Removed an unused get_settings import flagged by ruff.

Tests. Added test_data_dir_normalises_both_layouts (parametrized across v2.db and engagements/CRMBUILDER.db shapes) to tests/crmbuilder_v2/access/test_meta_db_connection.py. Added test_route_api_at_active_engagement_sets_env (drives the helper against the freshly-migrated workspace and asserts CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH + get_settings + meta_db_path post-state) and test_route_api_at_active_engagement_noop_without_active (asserts no env mutation for the empty-context case) to test_launcher_wiring.py. Full suite returned 1236 passed (+4 vs. prior commit, +7 vs. baseline). Lint clean. Stashed, rebased (one intervening v0.5 build-execution closeout prompt commit, unrelated), restored, committed as 3467764.

Pushed both commits together (edae295..3467764). Killed the misconfigured running API (auto-classifier blocked the first attempt because the agent did not spawn the process; succeeded after operator authorization). Restarted with CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH explicitly set against engagements/CRMBUILDER.db. Verified: /decisions → 101 rows, /charter/versions → 2 rows, /engagements → 1 row, all 200. Operator confirmed UI working again.

Engagement panel (ui/panels/engagements.py): master pane with five sortable columns per PRD §5.1 — Identifier (mono 80px), Code (mono 100px), Name (body flexible), Status (body with status-aware coloring active/paused/archived), Last Opened (relative-date formatted via format_relative_date helper handling 'N minutes/hours/days ago' / 'YYYY-MM-DD' / '—' cases). Default sort Last Opened descending with soft-deleted rows sorting to the bottom. Active-engagement marker — left accent bar plus prepended Lucide check icon on the Identifier column — compares against ActiveEngagementContext.engagement_identifier(). Soft-deleted row treatment in neutral.500 text with prepended Lucide trash-2 icon, gated behind a 'Show soft-deleted' checkbox that toggles the panel's client.list_engagements(include_deleted=True) call. Right-click context menu with New / Edit / Delete / Restore (Restore appears only on soft-deleted rows). The menu does NOT include Activate — the picker (slice D) is the switching gesture per PRD §5.2. Detail pane with form fields per engagement.md §3.6.3.

Field schema sourced from a new shared _engagement_schema.py module.

Delete dialog (ui/dialogs/engagement_delete.py): EntityCrudDeleteDialog subclass implementing PRD §5.6's forbid-active-delete. When the row to delete is the active engagement, the dialog presents a special state with two inert TODO-placeholder buttons (Switch engagement / Create engagement) that slice D will rewire. The last-engagement edge case is handled — when the only existing engagement is the active one, the dialog presents the Create-engagement affordance as the recovery path.

after_payload.title: v0.5 slice D — engagement switching, top-strip, picker, single-gesture creation+activation
after_payload.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.5-D-engagement-switching.md. Five categories of work executed on top of slices A through C: the top-strip widget at the top of the sidebar container (always visible, shows active engagement's name with code in parentheses, clicking opens the picker); the picker dropdown (live engagements ordered by engagement_last_opened_at descending, paused and archived rendered muted and sorted to the bottom, active marked with a check icon, footer 'Manage engagements...' item); the activation worker as a QThread implementing the 12-step activation sequence per multi-engagement-architecture.md §4 with the PRD §3 question-6 amendment (engagement_last_opened_at PATCH deferred until after new API subprocess is up); the activation overlay centered widget binding to the worker's progress signals with retry/stay affordances on failure; and the single-gesture creation+activation flow (NewEngagementDialog extending slice-C's EngagementCreateDialog with three sequential operations: POST /engagements → create per-engagement DB file → activate via worker; three-label progress indicator; graceful failure recovery per PRD §5.3).

Activation worker (ui/activation_worker.py, 406 lines): 12-step orchestration of the kill-relaunch dance across the API and MCP subprocesses. Amended order per PRD §3 q6: reachability check → confirm-target → kill MCP → kill API → write current_engagement.json → update in-memory ActiveEngagementContext → run engagement migrations → set CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH env var → launch new API subprocess → launch new MCP subprocess → PATCH engagement_last_opened_at via new API → emit done signal → UI restore. Each step emits progress signals to the overlay. Failure at any step transitions the overlay to error state with retry/stay affordances.

Top-strip widget (ui/widgets/engagement_top_strip.py, 109 lines): subscribes to active_engagement_changed and re-renders. Content row with engagement_name body text, code in parentheses small neutral.500, right-aligned Lucide chevron-down. Background neutral.100, height 48px, 1px hairline neutral.200 below. Handles the no-engagement-set fresh-install case with 'No engagement selected ▾' rendering.

Picker dropdown (ui/widgets/engagement_picker.py, 220 lines): popup widget with two-tier ordering (live engagements first ordered by last_opened_at descending; non-live engagements next in muted neutral.500). Active-engagement indicator via leading Lucide check icon. Footer 'Manage engagements...' item separated by hairline that closes the picker and navigates the sidebar to the Engagements entry. Soft-deleted hidden from the picker (the management panel's job).

Activation overlay (ui/widgets/activation_overlay.py, 175 lines): centered widget binding to worker progress signals. Converts to error state with retry/stay affordances on failure per PRD §5.3.

Single-gesture creation+activation flow (ui/dialogs/new_engagement_dialog.py, 354 lines): NewEngagementDialog extending EngagementCreateDialog. Three sequential operations with a three-label progress indicator: POST /engagements creates the meta DB row → create per-engagement DB file with Alembic at head → activate via worker. Graceful inline failure handling at each stage per PRD §5.3 — meta-DB-write failures clear the row before retry; per-engagement-DB-file-creation failures soft-delete the meta DB row and surface the error; activation failures leave the rows in place and surface the standard activation error state.

Wiring: migration/lazy_migration.py extended for the activation worker's step-3 invocation. ui/app.py instantiates the top-strip and docks it above the sidebar group entries. ui/main_window.py extended (+135 lines) to host the picker, overlay, and the single-gesture dialog launch. ui/dialogs/engagement_delete.py +63 lines — rewires the slice-C inert 'Switch engagement' / 'Create engagement' buttons to open the picker and the NewEngagementDialog respectively. ui/panels/engagements.py +22 lines to integrate the picker.

Test suite: 4 new test files added — test_activation_worker.py (317 lines covering the 12-step sequence with all failure paths), test_engagement_picker.py (157 lines), test_engagement_top_strip.py (76 lines), test_new_engagement_flow.py (180 lines covering the three-stage single-gesture flow with all failure recovery paths).

After this slice the full engagement-switching UX works end-to-end. Doug can switch between CRMBUILDER and any other engagement record via the picker; the kill-relaunch dance executes per spec; the active-engagement state persists across restart via current_engagement.json. Single-gesture engagement creation works: one click creates the meta DB row, creates the per-engagement DB file with Alembic at head, and activates the new engagement, with graceful inline failure handling at each stage. Per DEC-081, the v0.5 model is one-process-per-engagement — the activation worker spawns one API per engagement and kills the prior; PI-017 (Migrate API and MCP servers to multi-tenant model) tracks the eventual shift to one API serving multiple engagements.

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option A — Bundle into v0.5.** Slice prompts go under CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.5-{X}-*.md and interleave with engagement-management slices (estimated v0.5 slices: A-D engagement management + E-I styling = nine slices). One v0.5 release ships when BOTH workstreams complete — the slower one gates the release. One README section. One closeout. Rejected on navigability grounds: bundling permanently blurs the release-version index that v2 uses for git log, README, decisions navigation, and MCP status reporting. The marginal savings (one fewer closeout cycle, ~half day) didn't compensate for the permanent blurring cost.
after_payload.consequences: Slice prompts named under CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.6-{A..F}-*.md. PRD at ui-PRD-v0.6.md; implementation plan at ui-v0.6-implementation-plan.md. __version__ in crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/__init__.py bumped to '0.6.0' at slice F closeout (slice F handles the bump). Two README release-note sections — v0.5 entry plus v0.6 entry. Two closeouts — v0.5 closes out independently when its slices land; v0.6 closes out via slice F + the post-slice-F operator steps (status versioned-replace, SES-030 application). Engagement-panel coupling: v0.5 ships first; v0.5's engagement panel ships with Qt-default styling; v0.6 slice C picks up the engagement panel alongside the other 12 existing panels in the omnibus retrofit slice. Build-planning decisions taken in this conversation (DEC-106, DEC-107) are recorded at this same close-out (SES-030).
after_payload.context: The styling workstream's build planning needed to settle a question the workstream plan §6 named explicitly: whether the styling work ships bundled into v0.5 (one release containing engagement management + visual design system) or as a separate v0.6 release that lands shortly after v0.5. Both releases were in flight at the time of this conversation — v0.5's engagement management was in architectural planning, the styling design pass had just closed (SES-027), and Conversation 2 (this conversation) was opening to plan the styling build. The decision had to be made before slice file naming, PRD file naming, or implementation plan file naming could be settled — those depend on whether the styling work is v0.5 slices E-I or v0.6 slices A-F. The workstream plan author's weak prior leaned toward separate v0.6.
after_payload.decision: **Ship as a separate v0.6 release.** v0.5 ships engagement management alone (slices A-D, four slices) and closes out independently when engagement work completes; __version__ bumps to '0.5.0' and v0.5 closes via its own session-record + apply prompt. Styling builds in parallel under CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.6-{X}-*.md with its own A-F six-slice ordering. When styling completes, __version__ bumps to '0.6.0' and v0.6 closes out. Two README sections, two closeouts. Engagement-panel coupling resolved per workstream plan §4 'v0.5 ships first' branch — slice C's panel retrofit work picks up the engagement panel alongside the other 12 existing panels. Follow-on detail committed in the same decision turn: styling release PRD at ui-PRD-v0.6.md; implementation plan at ui-v0.6-implementation-plan.md; slice prompts under prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.6-{A..F}-*.md; __version__ after v0.6 closeout '0.6.0'; release order v0.5 first then v0.6; v0.5 engagement panel inherits whatever design tokens have shipped at slice-land time; design-pass decisions (DEC-087..DEC-094) stand as-is.
after_payload.rationale: Release versions are v2's primary navigation index — git log, README, decisions table, __version__, MCP-exposed status all index by version. To date v2 has held one clean functional theme per release: v0.1 storage system, v0.2 base CRUD, v0.3 references + sessions write surface, v0.4 polish + methodology panels. Bundling makes v0.5 the first v2 release with two unrelated themes welded together, which permanently blurs the project's navigability — anyone (including Doug in a year) asking 'what changed in v0.5' gets a two-track answer where the simple cases have been giving one-track answers. Methodology rule at stake: the workstream plan §6 already named 'release timing' and 'closeout coordination' as the considerations, and the author's weak prior leaned toward separate. The design pass document didn't pick either way. The bundling cost (two unrelated themes under one version) is permanent in the project's history; the separate-release cost (one additional closeout cycle, roughly half a day of administrative work) is real but bounded. Functional independence of the two workstreams is load-bearing.
after_payload.title: Version bundling for the styling work: ship as separate v0.6 release, not bundled into v0.5

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option A — Stay close to the workstream plan five-slice strawman (Foundation / Governance retrofit / Methodology retrofit / Dialog polish / Closeout).** Rejected on three grounds: master-pane delegate buried in panel-retrofit slices creates artificial governance/methodology coupling; redundant review work in two parallel retrofit slices doing the same pattern; status and crash banner work muddled into dialog-polish slice. **Option B — Use the design pass §3.3 eight-step ordering more directly (Foundation, Sidebar, Master pane delegate, Governance retrofit, Methodology retrofit, Dialog polish, Status/warning + crash banner, Closeout).** Rejected because the design pass author already acknowledged the ordering as approximate; collapsing it to six slices reduces closeout overhead without losing material structure. **Option C — Hybrid with reserved override mechanism.** Rejected on YAGNI grounds at decision time (later partially rewalked back: the Topics-panel tree-view delegate override in slice B used a class-attribute override pattern, justified pragmatically because Topics is a concrete v0.6 caller, not speculative).
after_payload.consequences: Slice prompts authored at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.6-{A..F}-*.md, lengths 456 / 369 / 398 / 495 / 369 / 304 lines respectively. Per-slice acceptance criteria specified in ui-PRD-v0.6.md §6 (A1-A7, B1-B7, C1-C7, D1-D6, E1-E6, F1-F7) plus cumulative roll-up. The strictly sequential dependency chain means slice F cannot start until slice E lands; slice E cannot start until slice D lands; etc. The engagement panel coupling between v0.5 and v0.6 is resolved in slice C: by the time slice C runs, v0.5 has shipped per DEC-105 and v0.5's engagement panel is part of the omnibus retrofit. Per-slice screenshot capture protocol per DEC-107 applied across slices A-E; slice F adds the automated WCAG contrast verification.
after_payload.context: After DEC-105 settled the version-bundling question (separate v0.6), the styling build needed a slice structure. The workstream plan §5.3 had sketched a five-slice strawman (Foundation / Governance retrofit / Methodology retrofit / Dialog polish / Closeout). The design-pass document §3.3 had a more granular eight-step suggested order. These needed reconciliation, but the design pass already conceded 'approximate; Conversation 2 may restructure slice boundaries based on file-conflict topology with v0.5 work running in parallel.' The decision was about how to package the work — same surfaces, same tokens, same components either way, but different review-burden distribution and different file-conflict risk profiles.
after_payload.decision: **Six slices A-F.** Slice A: Foundation + About dialog (token module, font and icon asset bundling, loader helpers, modal elevation infrastructure, base widget hooks, About dialog re-skin as the canary surface). Slice B: Sidebar + master-pane delegate (the shared selected-state vocabulary per DEC-093, applied via custom QStyledItemDelegate on the sidebar QListWidget and via a shared QStyledItemDelegate subclass registered on every panel's master view). Slice C: Panel retrofits + ReferencesSection (omnibus retrofit covering all 12 existing panels plus v0.5's engagement panel; label-above form layout EVERYWHERE via QFormLayout.WrapAllRows; ReferencesSection sub-sectioned plain-list rewrite). Slice D: Dialogs + form controls (five button categories with full state coverage via dynamic buttonCategory property; internal context strip on record-editing dialogs; combo dropdown popup + checkbox custom indicator; delete-confirm body treatment). Slice E: Status, error, warning + crash banner (WarningCallout widget; Processes panel retoken from danger-red to warning-amber; error dialog header retoken; crash banner folded into the design system). Slice F: Closeout (version bump, README, WCAG contrast test, regression pass, integration smoke). Strictly sequential dependency chain A→B→C→D→E→F; no parallelism within v0.6 because each slice depends on tokens, hooks, or categories established by prior slices.
after_payload.rationale: Three reconciliation differences from the workstream plan §5.3 strawman: (1) the master-pane delegate is pulled to its own slice B rather than buried in panel-retrofit slices — the delegate is shared across all 12+ panels; putting it in panel-retrofit slices would artificially couple governance and methodology retrofit work (one of them would land the delegate and the other would inherit it); pulling it forward to its own slice lets slice C be a single omnibus retrofit instead of two; (2) governance and methodology retrofits collapsed into one slice C — the retrofit pattern is identical across all 12 panels; splitting it repeats the same review work twice without buying anything once slice B has landed the shared delegate; (3) status surfaces + crash banner promoted to their own slice E — the workstream plan would have folded these into 'dialog polish' (slice D), but the crash banner currently lives outside the design system and the inline-error treatment cuts across many panels; bundling with dialogs muddles two different concerns. The sequencing also accounts for the engagement panel coupling: v0.5 ships first per DEC-105, so by slice C's land time the engagement panel exists and is picked up by the omnibus retrofit alongside the other 12 panels.
after_payload.title: Slice structure for v0.6: six slices A-F reconciling the workstream-plan strawman

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Automated pixel-measurement or QSS-introspection checks.** Rejected as overkill at v0.6's scale — building snapshot test infrastructure or QSS-property assertions adds significant code without buying meaningful regression catch beyond what eyeball + screenshots already provide. **Eyeball-only without screenshots.** Rejected on history-loss grounds — if no screenshots are committed, retrospective work has no concrete reference for 'what changed visually in slice X' beyond the design pass document and the code diff. **Screenshots gitignored.** Rejected: defeats the retrospective purpose. **Multiple WCAG runs (per-slice rather than closeout-only).** Rejected because tokens don't change between slices (read-only after slice A per PRD §8); one run at slice F is sufficient.
after_payload.consequences: PRD §6 acceptance criteria for each visual slice (A1-A7, B1-B7, C1-C7, D1-D6, E1-E6) include the screenshot-commit requirement; PRD §6 slice F includes the WCAG check requirement (F4). Implementation plan §7 specifies the six-step screenshot capture protocol Doug follows after running each slice's Claude Code prompt. Each slice's commit message template instructs Claude Code to 'notify Doug for screenshot capture' as the final step. Slice F's prompt-runner picks the specific WCAG library (wcag-contrast-ratio recommended) and authors tests/crmbuilder_v2/ui/test_token_contrast.py parameterized over the design pass §4.4 combinations. If the warning-on-white combination fails AA at body size (design pass §4.4 flagged it as borderline at caption size), the recommended remediation is a token-value adjustment from #B0731A to #9D6517 — closes PRD Open Question #4.
after_payload.context: v0.6 is visual work with different acceptance signals than functional work. For v0.4 and prior, 'slice done' meant tests pass and new functionality works. For visual surfaces, the test-pass signal is weaker (visual rendering is hard to assert in unit tests without expensive snapshot infrastructure), and eyeball verification by Doug is more meaningful. The styling Conversation 2 needed to settle the per-slice and per-release acceptance pattern.
after_payload.decision: **Per-slice after-state screenshots plus eyeball verification, plus a single automated WCAG AA contrast check at slice F closeout.** Each visual slice (A through E) commits after-state screenshots of each affected surface to PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-screenshots/slice-{X}/. Screenshots captured by Doug after running the slice's Claude Code prompt; committed in a separate operator commit immediately after the prompt's commit lands. Filename convention {surface-name}.png (e.g., about-dialog.png, sidebar.png, master-pane-table.png, master-pane-tree.png, master-pane-soft-deleted.png, sessions-panel.png, etc.). Total expected count across v0.6: ~15-20 PNGs at ~100-300 KB each, ~3-5 MB committed to git. Slice F adds an automated WCAG AA contrast verification test at tests/crmbuilder_v2/ui/test_token_contrast.py using the wcag-contrast-ratio Python library (with hand-rolled fallback if the library is unavailable), parameterized over the text-on-background combinations from design pass §4.4 (A9). The WCAG check is a build gate per this decision — failures are not tolerated; if any combination fails, a token-value adjustment lands in ui/styling.py and slice F re-runs.
after_payload.rationale: For a solo developer with the right aesthetic eye and Doug's three-4K-monitor workstation context, eyeball verification is the appropriate primary signal for visual work — automated pixel-measurement or QSS-introspection checks would be overkill at this scale. Screenshots become project history and release-note material: a year from now, asking 'what did the sidebar look like at the end of slice B?' has a concrete answer in styling-screenshots/slice-B/sidebar.png. Per-slice (rather than only release-final) screenshots also support retrospective work — if a v0.7+ regression is suspected, the slice-by-slice progression is visible in git history. The single automated check (WCAG contrast) is the one accessibility commitment v0.6 makes per design pass A9; running it once at slice F closeout is sufficient because token values are read-only after slice A merges (per PRD §8 constraint). The git-tracked storage cost (~3-5 MB) is the intended trade for project navigability.
after_payload.title: Slice acceptance pattern for v0.6 visual work: per-slice screenshots + closeout WCAG check
