after_payload.title: Multi-tenancy routing fix — planning: seven architectural decisions, two-slice build plan, slice prompts authored
after_payload.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/multi-tenancy-routing-fix-planning-kickoff.md — the planning conversation kickoff produced earlier the same day. The kickoff names the workstream's purpose (fix Bug 1 + Bug 2), the read-first companion (multi-tenancy-routing-investigation-report.md), seven specific Phase 1 architectural questions to surface via the eight-element template, and Phase 2 deliverables (slice plan, per-slice Claude Code prompts, planning item PI-021 [originally PI-018, rebased at apply time — see end-of-topics note], close-out artifacts).

Pre-flight. Loaded crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md via github:get_file_contents (sparse-clone constraint: Claude.ai sandbox has no local clone access, so all repo reads went through the GitHub API with the userMemories' stored PAT). Loaded the investigation report similarly. Confirmed the report's sections A–G layout: §A API startup DB resolution, §B export hook render-target resolution, §C shared resolver gap, §D runtime engagement switch capability, §E proposed fix paths, §F shared workstream framing, §G seven additional findings.

Decision 1 — Fresh-install behavior with no current_engagement.json (DEC-108). Surfaced via full eight-element template per profile preferences (real downstream impact + multiple viable options with meaningfully different outcomes). Three options weighed: (a) auto-pick if exactly one engagement; (b) fail loud always; (c) hybrid first-time-only auto-pick. Doug chose (b) fail loud always. Reasoning: the bug being fixed is silent fallback to v2.db; the fix shouldn't introduce a new silent fallback under a different name. (c)'s 'first invocation only' rule creates state operators can't reason about from filesystem inspection. (a) is least-worst of the auto-pick options but still silent at exactly the moment loudness is wanted. Follow-on detail captured: error message text 'No active engagement. Activate one via the desktop UI's Engagements panel, or pass --engagement <code> when running the API standalone.' Exit code 2 (configuration error). Marker-points-at-missing-code message names the missing code: 'Active engagement \'X\' not found in meta DB.' Error written to stderr via print(..., file=sys.stderr) AND stdout so the UI subprocess capture sees it. Before uvicorn binds the port.

Decision 2 — Missing engagement_export_dir behavior (DEC-109). Surfaced via full eight-element template. Two options: (a) refuse the write; (b) per-engagement scratch dir under data/engagements/{code}/db-export/. Doug chose (a) refuse the write. Reasoning: same as Decision 1 — the bug being fixed is silent fallback, and a scratch dir is silent fallback by a different name. Operators who legitimately don't know where they want exports yet can either set the export_dir to a temp path now and update later, or hold off on commits until they decide — both are deliberate acts. The Open Engagement flow is the right place to surface unconfigured export_dirs; the UI can warn at activation time so the operator sees it before their first commit. Follow-on detail: the gate goes in the new route_settings_to_engagement(code) helper; helper sets CRMBUILDER_V2_EXPORT_DIR to literal sentinel '__UNCONFIGURED__' when meta DB has null; new exception EngagementExportDirNotConfigured raised at write time by the gate helper; access/db.py:session_scope and force_export raise before write_staging; UI Open Engagement flow surfaces yellow warning band when activating engagement with no export_dir. Empty string treated as null throughout.

Decision 3 — Env var vs direct Settings override (DEC-110). Surfaced via full eight-element template. The deepest architectural decision in the bunch. Two options: (a) env var + cache reset (matches existing CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH pattern); (b) Settings becomes engagement-aware directly with active_engagement_code field and computed properties. Doug chose (a). Reasoning: smaller diff (~5 files vs ~10+); matches existing pattern operators and the codebase already know; doesn't dig into the chicken-and-egg between Settings.db_path → data_dir() → meta_db_path(); option (b) ends up being option (a) plus a property-based facade because subprocess inheritance still requires an env var or CLI flag to communicate active engagement across the process boundary. Option (b) is the right shape for a v0.6+ architecture-cleanup workstream if Settings' role expands further; premature for a bug-fix slice. Follow-on detail: helper signature route_settings_to_engagement(code: str) -> None; raises UnknownEngagementError if code not in meta DB; cache resets in order reset_settings_cache → reset_engine_cache → reset_meta_engine_cache (last only when data_dir() would resolve differently); CRMBUILDER_V2_EXPORT_DIR added to Settings as sibling of CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH via the same env_prefix mechanism (config.py:24); empty-string treated as unset; sentinel '__UNCONFIGURED__' set when null in meta DB; session_scope checks for sentinel and raises before write_staging.

Decision 4 — --engagement <code> CLI flag (DEC-111). Surfaced via full eight-element template (operationally lower-stakes than 1–3 but a real scope decision). Two options: (a) land in slice A; (b) defer to follow-on. Doug chose (a). Reasoning: cost is small (~20 LOC + tests); makes Decision 1's error message honest as written (the error references the flag); eliminates one operationally-awkward step from every apply-prompt pre-flight from now on; already in workstream scope per Investigation §F. Deferring would mean either reworking Decision 1's error message now or shipping an error that points at a not-yet-existing flag. Follow-on detail: positional value 'crmbuilder-v2-api --engagement CRMBUILDER'; unknown code → exit 2 with 'Unknown engagement \'<code>\'. Available: CRMBUILDER, CBM.' (enumerated from meta DB); flag-vs-marker disagreement → flag wins, yellow log line emitted '--engagement <flag> overrides current_engagement.json (<marker>)'; agreement → no log line; flag is ephemeral (does not persist to current_engagement.json); tests: happy path, unknown code, flag-overrides-marker, marker-without-flag.

Decision 5 — /active-engagement and/or /admin/runtime-info API endpoints (DEC-112). Surfaced via full eight-element template. Three options: (a) add /admin/runtime-info in slice A; (b) narrower /active-engagement in slice A; (c) defer entirely. Doug chose (c) defer entirely. Reasoning: Investigation §G.6 explicitly flagged it as 'quality-of-life follow-on, not load-bearing'; nothing in the bug fix needs it; pre-flights have a working pattern via filesystem reads of current_engagement.json; the follow-on PI is cheap; the diagnostic surface can be designed properly when there's a real need driving the schema rather than committing to a response shape speculatively. Decision 4 is different because Decision 1's error message references the flag (forcing function); no forcing function here. Follow-on detail: file a follow-on quality-of-life PI (PI-022 or whatever's next-available when the need actually arises); slice A close-out adds this as a NOTE in the slice plan's Deferred section. NOTE: PI numbering for that follow-on deferred to whenever it is actually authored; not opened in this session's payload.

Decision 6 — Catalog exports, force_export, bootstrap/migrate scope (DEC-113). Surfaced via full eight-element template. Three options: (a) centralized gate applied to all active write paths via assert_export_dir_ready helper; (b) defensive per-site inline gates; (c) active-path only, no shared helper. Doug chose (a). Reasoning: today's export_dir bug is one class of bug with four manifestations (session_scope, force_export, catalog exporter, possibly bootstrap/migrate); if we only gate session_scope, three latent regressions remain in the codebase; the cost of the centralized helper is one new function (~10 LOC) + three call sites — strictly less than the cost of three separate inline gates; option (c) is the 'minimum slice surface' instinct, option (a) is the 'fix the class of bug' instinct. Catalog exports specifically MUST be fixed because they're on the active path (Investigation §G.1 was explicit); force_export is free to add since the helper exists; bootstrap/migrate gets a 10-minute read-the-code pass and a one-line conditional fix per branch (engine-scoped → hardcode path; engagement-scoped → apply gate). Follow-on detail: helper signature assert_export_dir_configured(s: Settings) -> None (later widened to assert_export_dir_ready(s) carrying both responsibilities from Decisions 2 and 7); lives in runtime/engagement_routing.py; call sites session_scope before write_staging, force_export before write_staging, catalog exporter's path-return site; about_dialog reads s.export_dir raw with display-mapping for sentinel and missing-path states (does NOT gate); bootstrap/migrate.py path-shape read during slice A.

Decision 7 — Fail-loud vs auto-create at write time (DEC-114). Surfaced via full eight-element template. Three options: (a) fail loud if engagement_export_dir doesn't exist on disk; (b) continue auto-creating full depth; (c) auto-create leaf only. Doug chose (a). Reasoning: aligns with the philosophy of Decisions 1, 2, 6 — no silent fallbacks, no silent rogue-tree creation; today's mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) is Investigation §G.7's 'doubly insidious' finding (writes go to wrong place AND wrong place gets auto-created on demand); Settings-route fix corrects the routing logic but leaves silent-mkdir-on-typo behavior untouched without this decision. The configured engagement_export_dir is supposed to point at an actual existing directory in a real client repo — it's a target operators chose, not a target the system invents. (c) is over-engineered — the parent-vs-leaf rule is one more thing to explain. Operators handle the one-time mkdir-per-engagement setup cost. Follow-on detail: exception EngagementExportDirMissing; message 'Configured engagement_export_dir does not exist on disk: <path>. Either create the directory or update the engagement via Edit Engagement.'; gate consolidated into assert_export_dir_ready(s) carrying both Decision 2 and Decision 7 checks; subdirectory creation below the root keeps using mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) which is safe and useful; about_dialog growth (✓/✗ indicator) deferred to future polish, not slice B.

Phase 2 — slice count. After Phase 1 settled, the slice count question collapsed to '2 slices' rather than requiring its own decision: Decisions 4, 6, 7 all fit in slice A (backend helpers, gate, CLI, tests); Decision 5 deferred; no slice C required. Investigation §F estimated 2 slices; the planning conversation confirmed.

Phase 2 — slice plan authored. PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/multi-tenancy-routing-fix-slice-plan.md authored end-to-end (≈10 sections + ASCII directory tree). Slice A scope: extract helpers, gate, CLI routing, fail-loud, catalog/force_export/bootstrap consumer-site fixes, tests — 13 acceptance criteria (A1–A13). Slice B scope: UI refactor of _route_api_at_active_engagement and ActiveEngagementContext to use shared helpers, warning bands on Open Engagement flow, Edit Engagement dialog visual emphasis, about_dialog (not configured) / (missing) display, error-dialog handling for new exceptions, pytest-qt integration tests across two-engagement state — 9 acceptance criteria (B1–B9). Strictly sequential A → B; slice A independently shippable.

Phase 2 — slice prompts authored. PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-multi-tenancy-routing-fix-A-helpers-cli-gate.md and CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-multi-tenancy-routing-fix-B-ui-refactor-affordances.md authored as DETAIL-mode pure-execution prompts following the established slice-prompt pattern (pre-flight, numbered workflow steps each with an acceptance check, acceptance gate, commit message scaffold, done). Slice A prompt has 9 workflow steps. Slice B prompt has 7 workflow steps.

Phase 2 — close-out artifacts. ses_044.json (this payload) and CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-044.md authored alongside. Identifier check at close-out artifact authoring time: latest applied SES is SES-043 per the user's kickoff (recovery sequence pre-flight 2), so SES-044 is next-available; latest DEC at conversation open per Investigation references was DEC-107 (the SES-043 payload references it), so DEC-108..114 is next-available for seven decisions; PI-017 was latest per userMemories ('Pending PRDs: Dues, SME Request' line context plus tracking), so PI-018 was next-available at close-out authoring time. The apply prompt's pre-flight verifies these are still uncontested at run time, following the same TOCTOU mitigation pattern as the SES-036 reconciliation precedent. NOTE — rebased at apply time: parallel CBM file-split planning work later the same day claimed PI-018 (oneToOne YAML support), PI-019 (cross-file category resolution), PI-020 (cross-file layout aggregation). TOCTOU pre-flight detected the collision; this payload rebased its planning item from PI-018 to PI-021 before applying. Sessions and decisions remained uncontested.

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (a) — Auto-pick when unambiguous.** API enumerates engagements via the meta DB; if exactly one exists, route to it, persist to current_engagement.json, log prominently, start. If zero or two-plus, fail loud. Rejected on no-silent-fallback grounds. **Option (c) — Hybrid first-time-only.** Auto-pick once on the very first install when no marker exists AND exactly one engagement exists, then persist; from then on require explicit marker; if deleted later, fail loud rather than re-derive. Rejected as too clever — operators can't tell from current filesystem state whether the next API start will auto-pick or fail without knowing marker history.
after_payload.consequences: Fresh-install workflow gains one step: open the UI once or pass --engagement once to seed the marker before the standalone shell-launch path works. Slight ops-doc burden; doesn't affect normal operation since the UI handles marker creation automatically on engagement activation. The slice A prompt implements the fail-loud path in cli.py:run_api(); the error message text references --engagement <code> (DEC-111) which lands in the same slice, so the message is honest as written. Doc note: the desktop UI's Engagements panel writing current_engagement.json on activation is the canonical seed mechanism for fresh installs.
after_payload.context: The slice D multi-tenancy work in v0.5 introduced per-engagement DBs at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/{engagement_code}.db and a marker file at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/current_engagement.json that the desktop UI writes to record the active engagement. The CLI entry point crmbuilder-v2-api (cli.py:run_api) never reads the marker — it reads Settings defaults plus any CRMBUILDER_V2_* env vars present in the shell. Post-v0.5-migration, the legacy v2.db has been deleted and SQLAlchemy+SQLite auto-create an empty file on connection; the result is the API silently lands on an empty file and every request 500s on missing tables (Investigation Bug 1, surfaced during the SES-001 paper-test apply attempt on 05-19-26). The fix extracts a resolve_active_engagement() helper that reads the marker; the question is what to do when the marker is absent (fresh install, marker deleted, marker points at a missing code).
after_payload.decision: **Fail loud always.** The API refuses to start without a valid current_engagement.json. Operator must open the desktop UI's Engagements panel to activate an engagement (which writes the marker as a side effect), or pass --engagement <code> as a CLI flag (DEC-111). Exit code 2 (configuration error). Error message: 'No active engagement. Activate one via the desktop UI's Engagements panel, or pass --engagement <code> when running the API standalone.' If the marker exists but points at a code not in the meta DB, the message names the missing code: 'Active engagement \'X\' not found in meta DB. Activate a valid engagement via the desktop UI or pass --engagement <code>.' Errors are written to stderr via print(..., file=sys.stderr) and also to stdout for UI subprocess capture, before uvicorn binds the port.
after_payload.rationale: The bug being fixed is silent fallback to v2.db; the fix should not introduce a new silent fallback under a different name. Auto-picking when exactly one engagement exists (Option a) is silent at exactly the moment loudness is wanted — if a second engagement is later added in a way that bypasses the UI (e.g., a script-created restore, a migration that runs in the background), auto-pick suddenly routes to whichever engagement happens to be first in enumeration order. The hybrid first-time-only auto-pick (Option c) creates state operators cannot reason about from filesystem inspection — they can't tell whether the next start will auto-pick or fail without knowing the marker's history. The system already has a clean mechanism for declaring active engagement (the marker file) and the desktop UI writes it correctly; the question is whether to require operators to use that mechanism or paper over its absence in narrow cases. The kickoff was explicit that this passes the two-part test for consequential decisions; the eight-element template applied.
after_payload.title: Fresh-install behavior when no active engagement marker exists: fail loud always

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Per-engagement scratch dir fallback.** Route helper computes crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/{code}/db-export/ as a default when engagement_export_dir is null and sets CRMBUILDER_V2_EXPORT_DIR to that path. Writes succeed; snapshots land in the scratch dir. Logged prominently at route-time. Rejected because silent fallback to a scratch dir is silent fallback by a different name; the warning-band affordance in slice B is the right UX for forcing intent rather than auto-inventing a path.
after_payload.consequences: New engagements created without engagement_export_dir cannot be written to until the operator configures one (probably one Edit Engagement click for a new engagement). The UI's Open Engagement flow needs to surface this clearly — yellow warning band per slice B's affordance scope — so operators see why their commit failed before they hit the failure. Test fixtures that create engagements need to either supply an export_dir or expect the new exception. The gate uses the sentinel '__UNCONFIGURED__' as the env-var value when null in meta DB; about_dialog renders this as '(not configured)' for human consumption (slice B affordance). Empty string in the meta DB treated as null throughout.
after_payload.context: Investigation Bug 2 surfaced that the export hook writes snapshots against Settings.export_dir which resolves engagement-blindly to the engine-repo default. When CBM was the active engagement during the SES-001 paper-test apply attempt, the CBM-content snapshot wrote to PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/ (CRMBUILDER's path) instead of CBM's configured engagement_export_dir. CBM.db retained the records correctly; only the snapshot side was misrouted. The Settings-route fix (DEC-110) makes Settings.export_dir resolve per-engagement, but the question of what to do when the active engagement has engagement_export_dir = null in the meta DB is a separate behavior decision. The schema allows null because operators may create an engagement without immediately committing to a snapshot target.
after_payload.decision: **Refuse the write.** When route_settings_to_engagement(code) finds the active engagement's engagement_export_dir is null/empty in the meta DB, the helper sets CRMBUILDER_V2_EXPORT_DIR to a literal sentinel value '__UNCONFIGURED__'. The new gate helper assert_export_dir_ready(s) checks for this sentinel at every active export-write site (session_scope, force_export, catalog exporter — see DEC-113) and raises EngagementExportDirNotConfigured if present. The exception message: 'Engagement \'{code}\' has no engagement_export_dir configured. Set engagement_export_dir for the active engagement via the desktop UI's Edit Engagement dialog, or activate a different engagement.' Reads (GET endpoints, the UI sidebar, the panels) work normally; only the export-write path is gated. The UI's Open Engagement flow surfaces a yellow warning band at activation time so the operator sees it before their first write.
after_payload.rationale: Same class of reasoning as DEC-108. The bug being fixed is silent fallback to the wrong path; the safest replacement for the bug is not 'silent fallback to a different path' (the option-b scratch-dir approach), it's loudness. The option-b argument (silent fallback to a per-engagement scratch dir prevents cross-engagement clobbering) is real but it solves a different problem: it prevents Bug 2's specific cross-engagement clobbering, while leaving in place the silence that masks misconfigurations. Operators who legitimately don't know where they want exports yet have two deliberate options post-fix: (1) set engagement_export_dir to a temp path now and update later; (2) hold off on commits until they decide. Either is a deliberate act; neither requires the system to invent a scratch location. The Open Engagement warning band (slice B affordance) is the right place to surface unconfigured engagements; it triggers at activation, before the operator's first write attempt. Investigation §E explicitly recommended this option.
after_payload.title: Missing engagement_export_dir in meta DB: refuse the write

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Settings becomes engagement-aware directly.** Add Settings.active_engagement_code; make db_path and export_dir @property methods consulting meta DB based on that field. route_settings_to_engagement(code) becomes 'set Settings.active_engagement_code, reset caches.' Subprocess inheritance handled via either CRMBUILDER_V2_ACTIVE_ENGAGEMENT env var (Settings reads on init) or explicit CLI flag. Existing CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH / _EXPORT_DIR env vars become test-only or deprecated overrides. Rejected on diff-size grounds plus the observation that even option (b) ends up needing an env var or CLI flag for subprocess inheritance, so it's really option (a) plus a property-based facade.
after_payload.consequences: Settings stays formally engagement-blind. Reading 'what engagement is active?' from code requires either reading current_engagement.json or inspecting env vars rather than asking Settings (slice B's affordances do this via resolve_active_engagement() the helper, not via Settings inspection). Tests that exercise routing manipulate env vars (with monkeypatch.setenv and cleanup) rather than constructing Settings with an engagement code. Some duplication of meta-DB-lookup logic between the route helper and the UI's existing ActiveEngagementContext — slice B's refactor of ActiveEngagementContext to use resolve_active_engagement() fixes this. The route helper's apply order at runtime is exact: meta DB lookup → env var set → reset_settings_cache → reset_engine_cache → reset_meta_engine_cache (conditional) → re-init pools.
after_payload.context: The Settings-route fix from Bug 2 (DEC-109) requires Settings.export_dir to resolve per-engagement. Two architectural plumbing models are available: (a) env var + cache reset — the route helper sets os.environ['CRMBUILDER_V2_EXPORT_DIR'] and calls reset_settings_cache(), and Settings re-resolves on next get_settings() via the existing env_prefix mechanism that already wires CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH; (b) Settings becomes engagement-aware directly — add an active_engagement_code field to Settings, make db_path and export_dir computed @property methods consulting the meta DB based on that field. The env var pattern matches what's already in place for CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH; the direct-Settings pattern eliminates the env-var-roundtrip but requires a deeper refactor.
after_payload.decision: **Env var + cache reset.** The new route_settings_to_engagement(code) helper reads the engagement record from the meta DB, computes per-engagement db_path via migration.lazy_migration.engagement_db_path, sets os.environ['CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH'] and os.environ['CRMBUILDER_V2_EXPORT_DIR'] (the latter to the actual path if non-null, else to the '__UNCONFIGURED__' sentinel per DEC-109), then calls reset_settings_cache() + reset_engine_cache() (+ reset_meta_engine_cache() when data_dir() would resolve differently). Next get_settings() re-resolves with the new env vars. Settings stays a plain config object. CRMBUILDER_V2_EXPORT_DIR is added to Settings as a sibling of CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH (same env_prefix mechanism, config.py:24) — the env var binding is the only Settings change required.
after_payload.rationale: Smaller diff (~5 files vs ~10+ for the direct-Settings refactor) and avoids deepening the chicken-and-egg coupling between Settings.db_path → data_dir() → meta_db_path(). Subprocess inheritance is a forcing constraint: the desktop UI spawns the API as a subprocess and needs to communicate the active engagement across the process boundary; env vars are the natural carrier. Any 'Settings is engagement-aware' model still has to deal with the bootstrap question of how the API subprocess learns which engagement is active before Settings is first read — which lands back at 'an env var' or 'a CLI flag.' Option (b) ends up being option (a) plus a property-based facade, so the marginal benefit is small and the diff cost is much higher. Option (b) is the right shape for a v0.6+ architecture-cleanup workstream if Settings' role expands further; today, premature.
after_payload.title: Plumbing model for engagement-aware Settings: env var + cache reset (matches existing CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH pattern)

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Defer to a follow-on workstream.** Ship the bug fix using env vars only; reword DEC-108's error to reference CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH=... uv run crmbuilder-v2-api instead of --engagement; file a small follow-on planning item for the flag. Rejected: the marginal cost is small, the flag's value compounds across every apply prompt from now on, and DEC-108's error message integrity is more important than minimizing slice-A surface area.
after_payload.consequences: cli.py:run_api() gains an argparse step that today is parameter-less. Slight broadening of slice A's surface (~20 LOC + ~4 tests). The flag introduces a second way to do the same thing as the env var, which is normally a smell but is well-established in CLI tools (env var and CLI flag as equivalent override surfaces). The flag's documentation lives inline in `crmbuilder-v2-api --help`. From now on, apply-prompt pre-flights can use the cleaner `crmbuilder-v2-api --engagement CRMBUILDER &` form instead of the env-var prefix.
after_payload.context: Pre-flight checks in apply prompts currently use the env-var-direct-set incantation: CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH=<path> uv run crmbuilder-v2-api &. This is awkward in shell pipelines and hard to remember. Investigation §G.5 noted that an explicit --engagement <code> CLI flag would let operators verify routing without manually setting env vars, and would support apply-prompt pre-flight checks more naturally. Additionally, DEC-108's fail-loud error message text references --engagement <code> as the standalone-launch remediation — so the flag's existence affects the honesty of that message. The question was whether to land the flag in slice A (alongside the helper) or defer to a follow-on workstream.
after_payload.decision: **Land in slice A.** Add an argparse step in cli.py:run_api() that parses --engagement <code>, validates the code against the meta DB, and (if present) sets CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH via the route helper before reading Settings. Flag wins over marker when both are present — a yellow log line is emitted: '--engagement <flag> overrides current_engagement.json (<marker>)'. When flag matches the marker, no log line. Flag does NOT persist to current_engagement.json (it's an ephemeral override for one process). Unknown code → exit code 2 with message 'Unknown engagement \'<code>\'. Available: CRMBUILDER, CBM.' (enumerated from meta DB).
after_payload.rationale: The cost is small (~20 LOC + happy-path + unknown-code + flag-overrides-marker tests — maybe 4 tests total). The flag makes DEC-108's error message honest as written (the error references the flag; without it the message points operators at something they can't actually do). The flag eliminates one operationally-awkward step from every apply-prompt pre-flight from now on. The flag is already in the workstream scope per Investigation §F. Deferring to a follow-on would mean either reworking DEC-108's error message now or shipping an error that points at a not-yet-existing flag.
after_payload.title: Land --engagement <code> CLI flag on crmbuilder-v2-api in slice A

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (a) — Add /admin/runtime-info in slice A.** One endpoint returning a small diagnostic blob: active engagement code + name + engagement_export_dir + computed db_path; resolved Settings.db_path and Settings.export_dir; v2 version; data_dir. ~30 LOC + envelope wrapping + tests. **Option (b) — Add narrower /active-engagement in slice A.** Same idea but scoped to just the active engagement row, returning meta-DB fields verbatim. ~15 LOC. Both rejected on no-forcing-function grounds; the design surface is better designed once consumers have a real need.
after_payload.consequences: Pre-flight prose stays slightly clunkier (filesystem read + curl rather than just curl). One follow-on PI to track if/when the need arises. Apply-prompt patterns continue working as they do today. Slice A's close-out documents the deferral in the slice plan's Deferred section.
after_payload.context: Investigation §G.6 noted that the apply prompt assumed an /active-engagement endpoint exists for pre-flight verification; it doesn't. The OpenAPI surface only exposes /engagements and /engagements/{id}. Worth either adding /active-engagement or /admin/runtime-info to surface routing state without needing to read current_engagement.json directly. Marginal — the investigation explicitly framed it as 'quality-of-life follow-on, not load-bearing.' The question was whether to land it in slice A (symmetry with DEC-111's --engagement flag) or defer.
after_payload.decision: **Defer entirely.** Pre-flights continue reading current_engagement.json directly (which is what they do today; the working pattern is established). File a follow-on planning item (PI-022 or whatever's next-available when the need actually arises) titled 'Diagnostic runtime-info API endpoint for ops verification' — not opened in this session, just noted as a future candidate. The slice A close-out adds a NOTE in the slice plan's Deferred section pointing at this decision.
after_payload.rationale: Investigation flagged it as not load-bearing. Nothing in the bug fix needs it. Apply-prompts have a working pattern via filesystem reads of current_engagement.json. Adding an endpoint speculatively now means committing to a response shape before knowing what consumers want from it — better to design the schema when there's a real need driving it. DEC-111 (the --engagement CLI flag) is different because DEC-108's error message references the flag (forcing function); no forcing function here.
after_payload.title: Defer /active-engagement and /admin/runtime-info API endpoints; file follow-on PI if real need arises

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Defensive per-site inline gates.** Each consumer rewrites its own check inline. Verbose; three places to update if the gate logic changes; no shared helper to maintain. Rejected on duplication grounds. **Option (c) — Active-path only, no helper.** Fix session_scope directly with inline gate logic; catalog exporter and force_export inherit Settings re-resolution (so they write to the right place when configured) but don't gate on the unconfigured sentinel; bootstrap/migrate untouched. Rejected because catalog exports are on the active path and inherit the same bug.
after_payload.consequences: One small new helper function in runtime/engagement_routing.py (added alongside resolve_active_engagement and route_settings_to_engagement). Three single-line call sites at session_scope, force_export, and the catalog exporter. about_dialog explicitly does NOT gate; it grows display-side mapping in slice B for the sentinel and missing-path states. bootstrap/migrate.py outcome documented in the slice A commit message. The helper's responsibilities widened to carry both DEC-109's sentinel check and DEC-114's is_dir() check — the function is named assert_export_dir_ready(s) rather than assert_export_dir_configured(s) to reflect both responsibilities.
after_payload.context: Investigation §G.1, §G.2, §G.3 listed three downstream consumers of Settings.export_dir besides session_scope: (1) catalog exporter at access/repositories/catalog/exports.py:57-59 — active path, has the same per-engagement-blindness as Bug 2; (2) force_export at access/db.py:120-140 — same shape as session_scope; not on any active production path today but inherits the same bug; (3) bootstrap/migrate.py:64 — uses settings.export_dir.parent; flagged as 'probably engine-scoped, but worth confirming.' The Settings-route fix from DEC-110 corrects what Settings.export_dir resolves to, but DEC-109's unconfigured-sentinel gate logic needs to live somewhere — either inline at each consumer site, or in a shared helper called from each site.
after_payload.decision: **Centralized gate, applied to all active write paths via a shared assert_export_dir_ready(s: Settings) -> None helper.** The helper lives in crmbuilder_v2.runtime.engagement_routing (the new module from DEC-110), checks for the DEC-109 sentinel and the DEC-114 missing-path condition, raises EngagementExportDirNotConfigured or EngagementExportDirMissing per case, or returns None silently when ready. Call sites: session_scope (before write_staging), force_export (before write_staging), catalog exporter's path-return site. about_dialog reads Settings.export_dir raw with display-side mapping for the sentinel and missing-path states (no gate — it's a display, not a write). bootstrap/migrate.py:64 gets a brief code-read pass during slice A; if engine-scoped, hardcode the path and remove the settings.export_dir.parent indirection; if engagement-scoped, apply the gate.
after_payload.rationale: Today's export_dir bug is one class of bug with four manifestations. If we only gate session_scope, we leave three latent regressions in the codebase that surface the moment someone exercises catalog exports under an unconfigured engagement, or someone wires force_export into a Recovery path. Cost of the centralized helper is one new function (~10 LOC) plus three call sites (one line each) — strictly less than the cost of three separate inline gates that duplicate the same check logic. Catalog exports specifically MUST be fixed because they're on the active path (Investigation §G.1 was explicit). force_export is free to add since the helper exists. bootstrap/migrate gets a 10-minute code-read and a one-line fix per branch.
after_payload.title: Centralized export-write gate applied to all active write paths via assert_export_dir_ready helper

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Continue auto-creating, full depth.** Match today's behavior; mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) runs on the full path; silent on missing trees. Rejected because today's behavior is the bug §G.7 flagged. **Option (c) — Auto-create leaf only.** Check parent of engagement_export_dir exists; if it does, auto-create the leaf if missing; if parent doesn't exist, fail loud. Rejected as over-engineered — the parent-vs-leaf rule is one more thing operators have to internalize; clean fail-loud is simpler.
after_payload.consequences: Operator workflow gains a one-time setup step per engagement: create the engagement_export_dir on disk before (or after) configuring it in Edit Engagement. Edge case: a fresh client repo where the methodology-records subtree exists but db-export/ hasn't been created yet — operators mkdir db-export/ once, then writes work forever. The exception name is EngagementExportDirMissing (sibling to DEC-109's EngagementExportDirNotConfigured under a shared EngagementExportDirError base for catchability). Slice B's Edit Engagement dialog grows save-time validation that prompts on non-existent paths with [Save anyway] / [Cancel]. Slice B's Open Engagement flow grows a red warning band when the active engagement's export_dir is set but missing on disk. about_dialog's display in slice B renders '(missing — <path>)' for this state.
after_payload.context: Investigation §G.7 flagged that access/exporter.py:115 calls export_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) before writing — today's behavior of silently writing to the wrong location is doubly insidious: it not only writes to the wrong place, it auto-creates the wrong directory tree if needed. The Settings-route fix corrects the routing logic but leaves silent-mkdir-on-typo behavior in place. Scenario: operator configures CBM's engagement_export_dir to /home/doug/Dropbox/Projects/ClevelandBusinessMentors/PRDs/methodology-records/db-export, later reorganizes Dropbox and moves the local CBM clone to /home/doug/Repos/ClevelandBusinessMentors/, forgets to update the engagement record. Today: write_staging silently creates an empty Dropbox tree mirroring the old structure; CBM's actual repo gets nothing. The question: fail-loud if engagement_export_dir doesn't exist on disk, or auto-create?
after_payload.decision: **Fail loud if engagement_export_dir doesn't exist on disk.** The gate helper assert_export_dir_ready(s) (from DEC-113) checks export_dir.is_dir() before write. If False, raises EngagementExportDirMissing (new exception, sibling to DEC-109's EngagementExportDirNotConfigured). Message: 'Configured engagement_export_dir does not exist on disk: <path>. Either create the directory or update the engagement via Edit Engagement.' Subdirectories below the configured root (catalog/, snapshots/, future structure) continue to be auto-created with mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) — that's the system's space to manage. The configured root is the operator's choice; the system doesn't invent or create it on demand.
after_payload.rationale: Aligns with the philosophy of DEC-108, DEC-109, DEC-113: no silent fallbacks, no silent rogue-tree creation. The configured engagement_export_dir is supposed to point at an actual existing directory in a real client repo — it's a target operators chose, not a target the system invents. Operator workflow becomes 'create the engagement_export_dir on disk first, then configure it in Edit Engagement.' One-time setup cost per engagement, not per-write. The Edit Engagement dialog (slice B affordance) can grow a 'Create directory' button or validate-at-save in a future polish slice if the manual-mkdir step becomes friction.
after_payload.title: Fail loud if engagement_export_dir doesn't exist on disk; do not auto-create the configured root

after_payload.description: Two bugs surfaced during the 05-19-26 SES-001 paper-test apply attempt are addressed by a single workstream because they share a root cause (v0.5 slice D wired multi-tenancy at the DB-write path but missed two adjacent paths) and a single fix shape (extract resolve_active_engagement + route_settings_to_engagement helpers; centralized assert_export_dir_ready gate at all active export-write sites). Bug 1: API startup ignores current_engagement.json and connects to the post-migration empty v2.db, returning 'no such table: sessions' on every request — cli.py:run_api never reads the marker file; UI-launched API works only because ui/app.py:_route_api_at_active_engagement inlines the env-var-set + cache-reset pattern before spawning the API subprocess. Bug 2: export hook writes snapshots against the engine-repo default Settings.export_dir regardless of active engagement; under any non-CRMBUILDER engagement, writes silently clobber CRMBUILDER's PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/. Same gap exists in force_export() and the catalog exporter. Scope per the slice plan at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/multi-tenancy-routing-fix-slice-plan.md: two slices (A backend, B UI). Slice A extracts the helpers, adds the CRMBUILDER_V2_EXPORT_DIR env var to Settings, adds the --engagement <code> CLI flag, wires cli.py:run_api to fail loud on no active engagement (per DEC-108), applies the assert_export_dir_ready gate at session_scope and force_export and the catalog exporter (per DEC-113), investigates and fixes bootstrap/migrate.py:64 per its scoping. Slice B refactors ui/app.py:_route_api_at_active_engagement and ui/active_engagement_context.py to use the shared helpers, surfaces the new failure conditions to operators via warning bands on Open Engagement (yellow for null export_dir, red for missing-on-disk), Edit Engagement dialog visual emphasis, about_dialog '(not configured)' / '(missing)' display, error-dialog handling for the new exceptions, and pytest-qt integration tests across a two-engagement state. The decisions captured at SES-044 are DEC-108 through DEC-114; they set out fresh-install behavior, missing-export_dir behavior, plumbing model, CLI flag inclusion, /admin/runtime-info deferral, centralized-gate scope, and fail-loud-on-missing-disk-path policy. Deferred from this workstream: cross-engagement references, snapshot replay/restore, engagement deletion, /admin/runtime-info diagnostic API endpoint (DEC-112; possibly opens as a follow-on PI if a real need arises). Discharge: slice B's commit lands; this PI moves to Closed (resolved) via the desktop UI's planning-items panel or a direct API write.
after_payload.title: Complete v0.5 multi-tenancy routing: API startup engagement resolution + per-engagement export_dir
