after_payload.artifacts_produced: Committed at 3aa268c (crmbuilder repo, main): crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/api/routers/admin.py (new — /admin/connection, /admin/version, /admin/active-engagement); crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/migration/version_info.py (new — SchemaVersion + engagement/meta schema readers); crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/ui/connection_info_dialog.py (new — Connection Info dialog); api/main.py (admin router registered; FastAPI version + root version sourced from __version__); ui/activation_worker.py (build_lifecycle_managers re-routes in place with spawn fallback; step descriptions reworded); ui/app.py (client passed to build_lifecycle_managers); ui/client.py (connection_info, version_info, route_active_engagement); ui/main_window.py (Connection Info menu item; post-activation panel refresh + stale-marking). 8 files, +578/-17.
after_payload.conversation_reference: Claude Code conversation on 05-20-26 working directly in the crmbuilder repo (no seed prompt). Driven by an operator bug report: switching engagements via the desktop dropdown did not change the displayed data. Diagnosed root cause, shipped the fix, and added operator introspection surfaces. Code landed at commit 3aa268c on main. No transcript preserved per DEC-025.
after_payload.in_flight_at_end: Docs follow-up: multi-engagement-architecture.md §4 still describes the kill-and-relaunch switch and should be updated to the in-process re-route per DEC-115. No follow-on PI is needed for the diagnostic endpoint DEC-112 deferred — DEC-116 built it. Operator must restart any API/app predating commit 3aa268c to pick up the /admin endpoints (the running dogfood app was already on the new code during this session). UI-side get_settings() is still only routed at startup; the About dialog's db_path can lag after a switch — acceptable since Connection Info queries the API for authoritative state, but a future polish could re-route the UI process too.
after_payload.title: Fix engagement switching (in-process re-route) + connection/version introspection
after_payload.topics_covered:
BUG. Switching engagements via the top-strip picker did not change displayed data. Root cause: the activation worker's switch relied on ServerLifecycle kill-and-relaunch of the API subprocess (per multi-engagement-architecture.md §4), but the API binds to one engagement's DB at spawn (env var CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH + lru-cached Settings). Against an externally-launched API ('crmbuilder-v2-api &', the documented dev workflow) terminate() is a no-op (the UI does not own that process) and start() short-circuits on a successful /health probe — so the switch silently re-attached to the old process on the old DB. A stale old-code API holding port 8765 during testing confirmed the failure mode live.

FIX (DEC-115). Switch now re-routes the live API in place via a new POST /admin/active-engagement, which calls the existing route_settings_to_engagement (DEC-110) to reset the settings + engine caches against the target DB — no subprocess restart, works whether the API is UI-owned or external. build_lifecycle_managers gained a client param; kill_api is a no-op when the client is wired; launch_api POSTs the re-route with a spawn fallback when the API is unreachable. On completion the main window refreshes the visible panel and marks the rest stale so they re-fetch. Verified end-to-end: CRMBUILDER→CBM flipped db_path (3.3 MB → 401 KB) and export_dir.

INTROSPECTION + VERSION SYSTEM (DEC-116). Added GET /admin/connection (bound DB path/exists/size, engagement code derived from the DB filename, export-dir state) and GET /admin/version (api_version from crmbuilder_v2.__version__; engagement + meta DB Alembic schema versions as current-revision vs chain-head + up_to_date flag). The FastAPI app version, previously a hardcoded '0.1.0' in two places, now reads __version__ (0.6.0). New migration/version_info.py reads the stamped revision from each DB's alembic_version table and the head from the chain. New Help → Connection Info… dialog surfaces all of it, querying the API so it reflects the server's live state rather than the UI process's startup get_settings() (which only routes at startup and would lag after a switch — the same reason the About dialog's db_path can be stale).

This discharges DEC-112's deferral of the diagnostic endpoint: the switching bug plus the operator's request to verify the running DB/version were the 'real need' DEC-112 said would justify building it. No schema migration (no new tables); the version surface reads existing Alembic state. ruff clean across all touched files.

after_payload.alternatives_considered: (a) Keep kill-and-relaunch but force-kill the API — impossible for an externally-launched process the UI does not own, which is the documented dev workflow. (b) Reconcile routing to current_engagement.json on every request — mutates global process state (env, caches, pools) under uvicorn's threadpool; hazardous under concurrency. (c) In-process re-route via an explicit endpoint (chosen) — a single controlled call during activation while the UI is blocked by the overlay, no concurrency hazard.
after_payload.consequences: New /admin router. Activation worker keeps a spawn fallback for the down-API case. The UI process's own get_settings() is still only routed at startup, so About-dialog db_path can lag after a switch — Connection Info (DEC-116) queries the API for authoritative state instead. multi-engagement-architecture.md §4 is now stale and should be updated to describe the re-route.
after_payload.context: multi-engagement-architecture.md §4 specified switching engagements by killing the API subprocess and relaunching it bound to the new engagement's DB (the DB is fixed at spawn via CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH + lru-cached Settings). This silently failed against an externally-launched API ('crmbuilder-v2-api &'): ServerLifecycle.terminate() is a no-op for a process the UI does not own, and start() short-circuits when /health already responds, so the switch re-attached to the old process on the old DB. Displayed data never changed — the reported bug.
after_payload.decision: Switch by re-routing the live API in place. New POST /admin/active-engagement calls route_settings_to_engagement (DEC-110) to reset the settings + engine caches against the target DB; no subprocess restart. The activation worker's build_lifecycle_managers takes the StorageClient; kill_api is a no-op when the client is wired; launch_api POSTs the re-route and falls back to the legacy spawn only when the API is unreachable. Works identically for a UI-owned or an externally-launched API.
after_payload.rationale: Re-routing reuses the in-process routing helper DEC-110 already built for exactly this ('in-process re-routing under a long-running UI'). It works regardless of API ownership — the UI cannot kill a process it does not own, so kill-and-relaunch could never fix the external case. It also removes the port-release race between terminate and respawn and is faster (no process start). The spawn fallback preserves recovery when the API is genuinely down.
after_payload.title: Engagement switching re-routes the live API in-process, not via kill-and-relaunch

after_payload.alternatives_considered: (a) One opaque /admin/runtime-info blob as DEC-112 sketched — split into /connection vs /version for clearer semantics and API docs. (b) Maintain a separate human-readable schema_version constant — redundant with and prone to drifting from the Alembic revisions. (c) Read versions UI-side from get_settings() — stale after a switch; rejected in favor of an API query.
after_payload.consequences: DEC-112's deferral is discharged; no follow-on PI for the diagnostic endpoint is needed. Future schema migrations automatically surface as 'migration pending' in the Connection Info dialog until applied. The endpoints are general-purpose operator/diagnostic surfaces other tooling can consume.
after_payload.context: DEC-112 deferred a diagnostic API endpoint (/admin/runtime-info) with 'file a follow-on when a real need arises.' Two needs arrived together: diagnosing the engagement-switch bug (DEC-115) required knowing which DB the live API was actually bound to, and the operator asked to verify which API and database versions are running. There was also no single source of truth for the API version (FastAPI app version was a hardcoded '0.1.0' diverged from __version__ = 0.6.0) and no surfaced notion of a database version.
after_payload.decision: Add GET /admin/connection (bound DB path / exists / size, engagement code derived from the DB filename, export-dir state) and GET /admin/version (api_version from crmbuilder_v2.__version__; the active-engagement DB and meta DB Alembic schema versions as current-revision vs chain-head plus an up_to_date flag). Source the FastAPI app + root version from __version__. Add migration/version_info.py to read each DB's stamped revision and chain head. Add a Help → Connection Info… dialog that queries the API so it reflects live server state.
after_payload.rationale: The database version system already exists — it is the Alembic migration chain, whose revisions are cleanly numbered (0001..0010). Surfacing current-vs-head is the natural 'what version is this DB' answer and flags pending migrations for free. The API version is single-sourced from __version__. Querying the API (not the UI's get_settings(), which only routes at startup) makes the dialog authoritative after an in-process switch.
after_payload.title: Build /admin/connection + /admin/version introspection and a DB/API version surface (the diagnostic endpoint DEC-112 deferred)

after_payload.artifacts_produced: Three files committed to PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ in the crmbuilder repo: (1) governance-entity-schema-workstream-establishing-kickoff.md (new — the kickoff prompt for the new workstream-establishing conversation, committed at e1a0be4 on 05-20-26); (2) close-out-payloads/ses_046.json (new — this payload); (3) prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-046.md (new — the apply prompt for this payload). Six decision records (DEC-117..122) and one planning item (PI-022) and seven references land via this payload's apply prompt. No source code modified; this is a pure planning conversation. No other PRDs touched.
after_payload.conversation_reference: Claude.ai strategic scoping conversation across multiple working sessions in May 2026 (initial scoping early in May, verification and finalization on 05-20-26). The conversation had no formal kickoff prompt — opened ad-hoc as an exploration of how the planning-and-execution workflow functions in the V2 application's governance portion. The exploration surfaced a category-defining gap: the V2 governance database, while it tracks decisions, sessions, planning items, references, charter, status, risks, and topics as first-class records, does not track any of the operational artifacts the planning-and-execution workflow produces or consumes (kickoff prompts, workstream master plans, Product Requirements Documents, implementation plans, methodology guides, schema specifications, per-slice execution prompts, close-out payloads, deposit events, conversations, or workstreams). The gap directly contradicts the stated single-source-of-truth role of the database. The conversation worked through six principle-level questions to resolve how to close it. On 05-20-26, Doug returned to the conversation to verify the original draft kickoff prompt against the now-current state (after v0.5 and v0.6 had shipped and the methodology-schemas paper-test had completed). The verification revealed substantial drift: the draft's premise of pausing the methodology workstream was moot (it had completed); the version sequence assumption was stale; new architectural disciplines had emerged (strict reference vocabulary, envelope unwrap, per-engagement isolation); the draft was never committed. The updated kickoff prompt was authored against current state and committed at e1a0be4. No transcript preserved per DEC-025.
after_payload.in_flight_at_end: The workstream-establishing planning conversation for the governance entity schema-design workstream opens against PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-entity-schema-workstream-establishing-kickoff.md as the next step. That conversation produces the workstream master plan, the schema-spec methodology guide, six per-entity schema-design kickoff prompts, and its own close-out. After the workstream-establishing conversation closes, the six per-entity schema-design conversations run in order (workstream, conversation, reference book, work ticket, close-out payload, deposit event). After all six schema specifications are complete, a build-planning conversation consumes them and produces the integrating Product Requirements Document, implementation plan, and per-slice execution prompts. Target version for the resulting build is open — set by the build-planning conversation in coordination with the active version sequence at that time. The governance workstream operates in parallel to: (a) the multi-tenancy routing fix work in flight (slice B remaining); (b) the paper-test-flagged sub-domain hierarchy amendment (Planning Item 001 in the Cleveland Business Mentors engagement) which is a separate planning conversation; (c) the Cleveland Business Mentors redo Phase 1 conversation which waits on (b). None of these block the governance workstream; the governance workstream does not block any of them. PI-022 (retroactive-migration question) opens for resolution at the workstream-establishing conversation or later — whether to backfill governance entity records for sessions, decisions, and prior workstreams already in the database, or only go forward from the moment the entities land.
after_payload.title: Governance entity schema gap identified; six principle-level decisions reached; workstream-establishing kickoff prompt authored
after_payload.topics_covered:
No seed prompt — this was an ad-hoc strategic scoping conversation opened without a kickoff. Topics worked through:

Gap identification. Doug opened with general questions about how the planning-and-execution workflow functions in V2. Walked through the workflow step by step: kickoff prompt creation, where prompts live, how they relate to sessions, how the workstream concept is captured. At each step, the question was asked whether the artifact in question was first-class in the governance database. The answer was uniformly no. Surfaced first for kickoff prompts (only referenced as free-text mentions in session records' artifacts_produced and topics_covered fields), then for workstreams (only an English word and a Markdown filename), then for the broader set of artifacts the workflow produces. Doug's principal observation: the single-source-of-truth principle is repeatedly violated wherever an operational artifact lives only on the filesystem with at-best a free-text mention in a session record.

Artifact inventory. Catalogued every artifact the planning-and-execution workflow produces or consumes, sorted by relationship to the governance database. Three categories fell out: (1) artifacts already first-class as typed records — the eight existing entity types (charter, status, decision, session, risk, planning item, topic, reference); (2) artifacts filesystem-only with free-text mentions in session records — initially seven types (workstream master plan, kickoff prompt, methodology guide, schema specification, Product Requirements Document, implementation plan, per-slice execution prompt), expanded to ten when the verification framing forced close-out payload, apply script, and the universal session-startup entry document into the same category; (3) artifacts filesystem-only with no database mention — empty after the corrections.

Lifecycle analysis. The ten category-two artifacts grouped into three lifecycle shapes: Shape A multi-consumer versioned reference document (workstream plan, methodology guide, schema specification, Product Requirements Document, implementation plan, session-startup entry document, apply script — seven of the ten), Shape B single-consumer seed document (kickoff prompt, per-slice execution prompt — two of the ten), Shape C single-use state-write package (close-out payload — one of the ten). The verification dimension forced a fourth shape into existence: Shape D apply event, a transactional record linking a payload to its application outcome and to the records it wrote.

Decision space. After lifecycle clarity, the decision space narrowed to two questions: how to handle the workflow artifacts (three purpose-built entity-type families per lifecycle shape, one generalized governed-artifact entity, or a hybrid), and whether to add a separate conversation entity for the unit of conversational work distinct from the after-the-fact session record.

Principle 1 — Three purpose-built entity-type families. Doug chose three purpose-built families over one generalized governed-artifact type. Reasoning: the families have meaningfully different lifecycles (long-lived versioned vs. single-use seed vs. single-use state-write); a single generalized entity would need an optional-field-heavy schema and a complex status enum accommodating all three shapes; per-family schemas are cleaner semantically.

Principle 2 — Two entities within the deposit bucket. The deposit bucket initially proposed as one entity holding both the close-out payload (the slip) and the deposit event (the apply record). Doug's framing about verification ("how do we know if the script/payload met the requirements if they are unavailable to a user using the DB?") forced separation: the payload is one record produced at conversation close; the deposit event is a separate record created at apply-time linking the payload to its outcome, the script version that ran, and the resulting records. Different lifecycles, different timing, different content. Two entities.

Principle 3 — Conversation as a first-class entity. The governance database knows about sessions, which are after-the-fact records. It has no concept of a planned-but-not-yet-happened conversation, a kickoff-drafted-but-not-yet-opened conversation, or an in-flight conversation. Doug chose to add a conversation entity representing the unit of conversational work through its full lifecycle (planned, kickoff-drafted, ready, in-flight, complete, cancelled, superseded). The session record continues to capture what happened at close; the conversation record carries the work unit through every state including the pre-session states.

Principle 4 — Workstream as a first-class entity. The workstream concept appears in filenames and free text but is not modeled. Doug chose to add a workstream entity representing a coherent line of related conversations. Workstreams have their own lifecycle (planned, in flight, complete) and let queries like 'show me every conversation in this workstream and the state of each' become answerable from the database. The nested-workstream question (whether a workstream can contain a sub-workstream) was deferred to the workstream entity's own schema-design conversation; it is a refinement, not a principle.

Principle 5 — Single source of truth coverage extension. Stated as the umbrella principle the four entity-introduction decisions serve: after the new entities land, queries like 'what is queued up next,' 'did this payload's apply succeed,' 'what version of the Product Requirements Document was in force at session NNN' become answerable from the database without filesystem scanning. Captures the design intent the gap violates today.

Principle 6 — Workstream order, governance workstream opens immediately. After the principles landed, the sequencing question: open a governance entity schema-design workstream now, or wait for other in-flight work. The methodology workstream had already completed; v0.5 engagement architecture had shipped; v0.6 styling had shipped; the paper test had completed with one BLOCKING finding (sub-domain hierarchy amendment to domain, recorded as Planning Item 001 in the Cleveland Business Mentors engagement); a multi-tenancy routing fix was in flight (Sessions 044 and 045 plus remaining slices). Doug chose to open the governance entity schema-design workstream immediately, in parallel to the in-flight work. Reasoning: the governance gap exists independently of any of the other workstreams; the Cleveland Business Mentors redo Phase 1 waits on its own amendment regardless; the multi-tenancy fix is unrelated to governance entity scope; the governance workstream operates against the CRMBuilder dogfood engagement and does not touch other engagements' content.

Kickoff prompt drafting. After the six principles landed, Doug asked for the workstream-establishing kickoff prompt to be drafted in the same conversation. Three defaults baked in: workstream name 'governance entity schema-design workstream' (parallels 'methodology entity schema-design workstream'); the deposit bucket as two entities not one (per Principle 2); ordering of the six per-entity schema-design conversations by dependency — workstream first (most independent), then conversation (references workstream), then reference book (independent record but conversations link to it), then work ticket (references conversation), then close-out payload (produced by a conversation), then deposit event (references payload). Doug approved the draft.

Verification step on 05-20-26. After v0.5 and v0.6 completed and the paper test ran, Doug returned to verify the draft against current state. Key findings: the draft kickoff prompt was never committed (lived only in conversation history); the five-or-six original decisions were never recorded as formal database entries (the original conversation never closed out); the 'pause methodology workstream' framing was moot because the methodology workstream completed organically (SES-011 through SES-015 on 05-11/05-12 followed by v0.4 build); the version sequence assumption was stale (v0.5 and v0.6 had shipped); new architectural disciplines had emerged that did not exist or were not enforced at draft time (strict reference relationship vocabulary at crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/access/vocab.py with REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS plus _kinds_for_pair plus Alembic migration on refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint; envelope unwrap pattern in apply scripts per the canonical SES-025 prompt; per-engagement isolation with engagements as a methodology entity introduced in v0.5). The kickoff prompt was rewritten against current state and committed at e1a0be4. The original six principles were preserved as 'six incoming principles' to be formalized as decisions at the close of the new workstream-establishing conversation — but this close-out (SES-046) formalizes them instead, since the new conversation will need them as predecessors.

Close-out artifact authoring. Following Doug's approval of the kickoff prompt and direction to handle the full close-out, this payload (ses_046.json) and the companion apply prompt (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-046.md) were authored. Identifier check at close-out time: latest applied SES is SES-045 (multi-tenancy routing fix slice A build, 05-20-26) so SES-046 is next-available; latest DEC is DEC-116 (the diagnostic introspection decision in SES-045's payload) so DEC-117 through DEC-122 is next-available for six decisions; latest PI is PI-021 (the multi-tenancy routing PI surfaced in SES-044) so PI-022 is next-available for the retroactive-migration planning item. The apply prompt's pre-flight verifies these are still uncontested at run time per the TOCTOU mitigation pattern established by the SES-036 reconciliation precedent.

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — One generalized governed-artifact entity type.** A single entity type with a `kind` discriminator field (eleven values: workstream_plan, methodology_guide, schema_specification, prd, implementation_plan, session_startup_document, apply_script, kickoff_prompt, slice_execution_prompt, close_out_payload, deposit_event) and a status enum supporting the union of all lifecycles. Rejected on schema-complexity grounds — the optional-field count and the per-kind validation logic exceeds the cost of three smaller entities. **Option (c) — A conversation entity ties everything together, and artifacts are subsumed under it.** Rejected because conversations and artifacts are different governance objects; the conversation question is settled separately by DEC-119.
after_payload.consequences: Three new entity types must be designed in the governance schema-design workstream: reference book, work ticket, deposit bucket. Each gets its own per-entity schema-design conversation. The deposit bucket family expands to two entities (close-out payload and deposit event) per DEC-118, raising the workstream's per-entity-schema conversation count from three to four for the file-tracking side, plus two more (conversation, workstream) for the unit-of-work side, totalling six per-entity schema-design conversations. The conventions inherited from the methodology entity schema-design workstream — parent-prefix field naming (DEC-046), source-first relationship-kind naming (DEC-048), three-status lifecycle posture — apply to all six new entity types where compatible. New relationship kinds in the references vocabulary must be added per Decision 006's universal-references-table pattern and per the strict reference-vocab discipline established since.
after_payload.context: The V2 governance database does not track any of the operational artifacts the planning-and-execution workflow produces or consumes (kickoff prompts, workstream master plans, Product Requirements Documents, implementation plans, methodology guides, schema specifications, per-slice execution prompts, close-out payloads, apply scripts, the session-startup entry document). These ten artifact types are governance-meaningful — each can fail to exist, exist in stale state, be superseded, or be in flight — but none is queryable from the database. The gap directly contradicts the stated single-source-of-truth role. The first decision was how to structure the database additions: one generalized entity-type with a discriminator field accommodating all ten kinds, or multiple purpose-built entity types each modeling a distinct lifecycle.
after_payload.decision: **Three purpose-built entity-type families, one per lifecycle shape.** Family 1 (reference book) covers long-lived multi-consumer versioned reference documents — seven of the ten artifacts fit here (workstream master plan, methodology guide, schema specification, Product Requirements Document, implementation plan, session-startup entry document, apply script). Family 2 (work ticket) covers single-consumer seed documents — two of the ten fit here (kickoff prompt, per-slice execution prompt). Family 3 (deposit bucket) covers single-use state-write packages — one artifact fits here (close-out payload), expanded by DEC-118 to also include the apply event as a separate but paired record. Each family becomes its own entity type with a schema fitted to its lifecycle.
after_payload.rationale: The three families have meaningfully different lifecycles. Family 1 has versioning and supersession with many readers across time. Family 2 has produce-and-consume-once semantics with at most one consumer. Family 3 has produce-and-apply-once semantics with a verification linkage to the records its application wrote. A single generalized governed-artifact entity would need to carry the union of all three lifecycles in one schema — optional fields per family, a status enum accommodating all transitions, conditional validation by kind. The cost of three smaller purpose-fitted schemas is strictly less than the cost of one large general schema with three modes lurking inside it, and the per-family schemas are easier to reason about per type at design time and at use time.
after_payload.title: Track workflow files as three purpose-built entity-type families, not one generalized governed-artifact type

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — One combined deposit entity with optional apply-side fields.** Payload fields populated at conversation close; outcome and resulting-record-list populated at apply time. Status enum spans both phases (drafted, applied_success, applied_partial, applied_failure). Rejected because the two phases have meaningfully different timing and immutability semantics — payloads can in principle be revised before apply; deposit events are immutable transactional records.
after_payload.consequences: Six entity types in the governance schema-design workstream total, not five: workstream, conversation, reference book, work ticket, close-out payload, deposit event. A new relationship kind in the references vocabulary expresses payload-to-deposit-event linkage (provisional name `applied_in`, naming finalized at the close-out-payload schema-design conversation). The deposit event entity carries linkages to (a) the close-out payload it applied (one-to-one or possibly one-to-zero-or-one if a payload is authored but never applied), (b) the apply script version that ran it (a reference book record), (c) the conversation whose close produced the payload (a conversation record). The apply outcome enum captures success / partial / failure with sub-fields for partial-failure detail. The resulting-record-list captures the actual identifiers written so 'what did this apply produce' is queryable directly.
after_payload.context: DEC-117 establishes the deposit bucket as one of three artifact-tracking families. The initial framing of the family had it as one entity holding both the structured payload (the file produced at conversation close listing what should be written) and the act of applying it (the record of when the apply ran with what outcome). Doug's question during the scoping discussion — 'how do we know if the script/payload met the requirements if they are unavailable to a user using the DB?' — forced the question of whether one entity is enough.
after_payload.decision: **Two entities within the deposit bucket family.** The close-out payload entity captures the structured package produced at conversation close — the slip declaring what should be written. The deposit event entity captures the application of a payload — the deposit, linking the payload to its application outcome (success, partial, failure) and to the identifiers of the records the apply wrote (session, decisions, planning items, references). The deposit event is a transactional record, not a document, and its lifecycle differs from the payload's: a payload is produced once at conversation close and applied (typically) once; a deposit event is created at apply-time and immutable thereafter.
after_payload.rationale: The verification question Doug raised is a governance question, not an implementation question. To answer 'did the apply do what the planning conversation said it should,' the database needs both the payload (the spec) and a record of the apply (the outcome) as separate first-class records. A single combined entity cannot represent both clearly because the lifecycles are out of phase — the payload exists from the moment it is authored; the deposit event does not exist until the apply runs. A single entity would need optional 'application-side' fields that may or may not be populated, plus a status field whose meaning straddles the two phases. Two entities with a relationship between them keeps each schema clean and lets verification queries express themselves directly.
after_payload.title: Within the deposit bucket family, separate the close-out payload from the deposit event as two entities, not one

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Allow sessions to be created in a 'planned' state and transitioned to 'complete' at close.** Rejected because it violates DEC-013's append-only rule. Sessions are records of fact; the work-unit state lives elsewhere. **Option (c) — Use planning items to track pending conversations.** Rejected because planning items have different semantics (deferred design questions, open issues), and conflating them with planned conversations would muddy both concepts.
after_payload.consequences: A new entity type to design in the schema-design workstream. The conversation entity is the second in the per-entity sequence per the workstream master plan (after workstream, which it references). A new relationship kind for the conversation-to-session linkage. A new relationship kind for predecessor and successor chains between conversations. The work-ticket entity (DEC-117) needs a relationship to the conversation entity (one work ticket is for one conversation). The close-out-payload entity (DEC-117 / DEC-118) needs a relationship to the conversation entity (one payload is produced by one conversation). Retroactive question: backfill conversation records for sessions already in the database (the original 45 sessions from SES-001 through SES-045), or only go forward? Captured as PI-022 in this close-out for resolution later.
after_payload.context: The V2 governance database tracks sessions, which are after-the-fact records of conversations that have happened. Sessions are append-only per DEC-013. The database has no concept of a conversation that is planned but not yet opened, a conversation whose kickoff prompt is drafted but not yet executed, or a conversation that is in flight. Queries like 'what is queued up next' have no answer in the database because the planned-but-not-yet-happened state is not modeled. The work-ticket entity (DEC-117) covers kickoff prompts but not the unit of conversational work itself — a conversation may have no kickoff prompt (the workstream-establishing conversation for the methodology workstream had none), and a kickoff prompt may be superseded before being used.
after_payload.decision: **Add a conversation entity representing the unit of conversational work through its full lifecycle.** Statuses: planned, kickoff_drafted, ready, in_flight, complete, cancelled, superseded. Fields (subject to the conversation entity's schema-design conversation) include identifier (Conversation NNN), purpose, position-in-workstream, optional kickoff-prompt reference (to a work-ticket record), predecessor-conversation reference, successor-conversation reference, and workstream membership. The session record continues to exist as the after-the-fact record per DEC-013's append-only rule; the conversation record links to the session via a new relationship kind (provisional name `recorded_as`, naming finalized at the conversation entity's schema-design conversation) once the conversation completes.
after_payload.rationale: The session entity captures what happened. The conversation entity captures the unit of work. These are different governance objects. A conversation may exist as a planned work item before any session record exists; a session record exists only at the moment of close. Without a conversation entity, the database cannot answer 'what is queued,' 'what is in flight,' or 'what is the predecessor-successor chain of conversations in this workstream.' Adding the conversation entity does not change the session entity or violate the append-only rule for sessions — sessions continue to be after-the-fact records; the conversation entity is the work-unit representation that precedes them and outlives them.
after_payload.title: Add a conversation entity representing the unit of conversational work through its full lifecycle, distinct from the session record

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — No workstream record.** Conversations carry a free-text workstream-name field, or just predecessor and successor links to other conversations; workstreams are inferred from grouping the data rather than modeled directly. Rejected because (i) it conflates the workstream identity with text that may drift across conversations within the same workstream; (ii) workstreams have their own state (planned, in flight, complete) that cannot be inferred from member conversations alone — a workstream is 'complete' only when its scope is satisfied, which is a fact about the workstream not about its conversations.
after_payload.consequences: A new entity type to design first in the per-entity schema-design sequence (workstream is the most independent — it references nothing else in the new set). Conversations relate to workstreams via a new relationship kind (provisional name `belongs_to_workstream`, naming finalized at the workstream entity's schema-design conversation). The nested-workstream question is deferred to the workstream entity's own schema-design conversation. Retroactive question: backfill workstream records for completed workstreams already in the database (methodology entity schema-design workstream, v0.5 engagement management workstream, v0.6 styling workstream, multi-tenancy routing fix workstream, the catalog ingestion workstream) — covered by PI-022 alongside the conversation-backfill question.
after_payload.context: The project uses 'workstream' as a real organizing concept — a sequence or group of conversations that share a goal. Examples: the methodology entity schema-design workstream, the v0.5 engagement-management workstream, the v0.6 styling workstream, the multi-tenancy routing fix workstream. Today the database has no idea workstreams exist; they appear only as English words in text and as Markdown filenames at `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/{name}-workstream-plan.md`. Queries like 'show me every conversation in this workstream and the state of each' have no answer in the database.
after_payload.decision: **Add a workstream entity representing a coherent line of related conversations.** Statuses: planned, in_flight, complete, cancelled. Fields (subject to the workstream entity's schema-design conversation, which is the first in the per-entity sequence) include identifier (Workstream NNN), name, purpose, optional parent-workstream reference if nested workstreams are admitted (deferred to the workstream schema-design conversation), and lifecycle dates. Conversations belong to a workstream via a relationship in the references table. The workstream entity's schema-design conversation also resolves whether nested workstreams are supported (a workstream contained inside another).
after_payload.rationale: The same single-source-of-truth principle. Workstream is a real organizing concept the project uses to scope, sequence, and declare work complete. If that is the unit of organization, it should be queryable. Without a workstream entity, queries about a workstream as a whole — its conversations, its progress, its current state, its outcome — have no answer; the consultant or developer must scan Markdown filenames and read free-text mentions in session records to assemble the picture by hand. Adding the workstream entity makes the organizing concept first-class and queryable.
after_payload.title: Add a workstream entity representing a coherent line of related conversations, as a first-class record

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Per-artifact case-by-case judgment without an umbrella principle.** Each new artifact type evaluated on its own merits when introduced. Rejected because the case-by-case pattern is what produced the current gap — every artifact type in the filesystem-only category was case-by-case-judged into existence without a forcing principle. **Option (c) — Universal first-class coverage including source code and snapshots.** Rejected as over-broad; source code lives in git with its own audit mechanisms, and snapshots are views of the database, not artifacts of the workflow.
after_payload.consequences: Future workstreams that introduce new artifact types include an evaluation of whether the type should be first-class in the database. The methodology guides for per-entity schema-design conversations include a question about artifact tracking. The principle is referenced in the workstream-establishing kickoff prompt and in the methodology guide the workstream produces. The principle does not retroactively force backfill of pre-existing workstreams' artifacts (PI-022 covers retroactive migration separately).
after_payload.context: The first four decisions in this batch (DEC-117 through DEC-120) each introduce one or more new entity types. Stated individually they answer specific questions (how to track files, how to track conversations, how to track workstreams). Stated collectively they serve an umbrella principle: the database is supposed to be the single source of truth for project state, and today it falls short of that role wherever an operational artifact lives only on the filesystem. This decision captures the umbrella principle explicitly so future design choices can refer to it as a constraint.
after_payload.decision: **The database's coverage extends to every artifact the planning-and-execution workflow produces or consumes that is governance-meaningful.** An artifact is governance-meaningful if it can fail to exist, exist in stale state, be superseded, or be in flight. The new entity types from DEC-117 through DEC-120 cover the ten artifact types and two work-unit concepts that fit this definition today. New artifact types introduced in future workstreams are evaluated against the same definition; if governance-meaningful, they get a first-class entity type or an addition to an existing one. The principle does not extend to (a) source code (covered by git), (b) database export snapshots which are views of the database itself (circular), (c) ephemeral working files that exist only during a single tool invocation. After this principle is in force, queries like 'what is queued up next,' 'did this payload's apply succeed,' 'what version of the Product Requirements Document was in force at session NNN,' and 'show me every conversation in this workstream' become answerable from the database without filesystem scanning or grep over Markdown.
after_payload.rationale: The database was introduced explicitly to retire Markdown as the source of truth for governance content. The migration from Markdown to database for charter, status, decisions, and sessions in v0.1 was justified by exactly this argument. As the workflow has grown, new artifact types have accumulated on the filesystem side without parallel additions on the database side, and the original argument applies to each of them. Stating the principle explicitly as a decision lets it function as a constraint at future design choice points — when a new artifact type emerges in a workstream, the question 'should this be first-class in the database' has a default answer (yes, if governance-meaningful) rather than relying on each consultant or developer to re-derive the principle on the spot.
after_payload.title: Single source of truth coverage extension: the new entity types close the gap between the database's stated role and its actual coverage

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Defer the governance workstream until after the Cleveland Business Mentors redo Phase 1 conversation and the multi-tenancy routing fix complete.** Rejected because the gap continues to compound — every conversation that runs without governance entity coverage adds to the retroactive-migration question (PI-022). **Option (c) — Run the governance workstream sequentially after the multi-tenancy routing fix slice B but before Cleveland Business Mentors redo Phase 1.** Rejected because there is no scheduling conflict justifying the sequencing; the work is independent.
after_payload.consequences: The next Claude.ai conversation opens against PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-entity-schema-workstream-establishing-kickoff.md (the kickoff prompt committed at e1a0be4 on 05-20-26 as part of this scoping conversation's output). That conversation produces the workstream master plan, methodology guide, six per-entity schema-design kickoff prompts, its own session record, and any decisions or planning items it surfaces. After it closes, the six per-entity schema-design conversations run in the order specified by the workstream plan. Multi-tenancy routing fix slice B continues in parallel as a separate workstream. Cleveland Business Mentors redo Phase 1 waits on its own sub-domain amendment as a separate planning conversation, also in parallel. Governance workstream's build planning conversation sets its own target version (v0.7+ candidate, to be confirmed at build-planning time).
after_payload.context: At the time of this conversation's close on 05-20-26, the project state included: the methodology workstream complete (since 05-12-26); v0.5 (engagement management) shipped (05-18-26); v0.6 (styling rollout) shipped (05-18-26); the methodology-schemas-cbm paper test complete (05-19-26) with a single blocking finding requiring a sub-domain hierarchy amendment to the domain schema, tracked as Planning Item 001 in the Cleveland Business Mentors engagement; the multi-tenancy routing fix planning conversation complete (SES-044 on 05-19-26); slice A of the routing fix shipped (SES-045 on 05-20-26); slice B of the routing fix pending. The governance entity gap identified in this conversation has been a known omission for the entire interval. The question: open the governance workstream now, or sequence it after the in-flight items?
after_payload.decision: **The governance workstream opens immediately, in parallel to the other in-flight items.** The workstream-establishing planning conversation opens as the next Claude.ai conversation after this close-out lands; it produces the workstream master plan, the methodology guide, and the six per-entity schema-design kickoff prompts. The six per-entity schema-design conversations then run in sequence (workstream, conversation, reference book, work ticket, close-out payload, deposit event). A build-planning conversation closes the workstream by consuming the six schema specifications. The workstream operates against the CRMBuilder dogfood engagement only; the Cleveland Business Mentors engagement is untouched.
after_payload.rationale: The governance gap exists independently of the other workstreams; none of them addresses it. The Cleveland Business Mentors redo Phase 1 conversation waits on the sub-domain hierarchy amendment regardless of governance work. The multi-tenancy routing fix is unrelated in scope (it touches engagement routing in code; the governance work touches schema design in planning). The work the governance workstream does is design (planning conversations producing schema specifications), not implementation; it does not consume the build capacity that the other workstreams need. Parallel execution does not create scheduling conflict.
after_payload.title: The governance entity schema-design workstream opens immediately, in parallel to other in-flight work

after_payload.description:
DEC-117 through DEC-120 introduce six new entity types (workstream, conversation, reference book, work ticket, close-out payload, deposit event). After the schema-design workstream lands the entities and a build implements them in the database, the question is whether to retroactively populate records for pre-existing project state, or only go forward from the moment the entities ship.

Scope of the retroactive question:

(a) **Workstream records for completed workstreams.** Examples: the methodology entity schema-design workstream (SES-011..015 plus the SES-017 build-planning conversation plus the SES-019..024 v0.4 build conversations); the v0.5 engagement management workstream (SES-026..032 spanning multiple conversations); the v0.6 styling workstream (SES-036..043 spanning conversation 1, conversation 2, and seven build-execution conversations); the multi-tenancy routing fix workstream (SES-044..045 plus slice B); the catalog ingestion workstream (SES-016 or wherever it landed). Each has a clear identity, member conversations, and an outcome.

(b) **Conversation records for completed sessions.** All 45 existing sessions (SES-001 through SES-045) had a conversation behind them; each conversation has metadata (purpose, position-in-workstream where applicable, kickoff prompt path where one existed, predecessor and successor).

(c) **Reference book records for existing reference documents.** Workstream master plans, Product Requirements Documents (v0.1 through v0.6), implementation plans, methodology guides, schema specifications (the four methodology entity specs plus the soon-to-arrive six governance entity specs), the session-startup entry document (`crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md`), the apply script (`crmbuilder-v2/scripts/apply_close_out.py`).

(d) **Work ticket records for existing kickoff and execution prompts.** The four methodology schema-design kickoffs (domain, entity, process, crm_candidate), the methodology workstream-establishing planning prompt, the v0.4 through v0.6 planning prompts, all per-slice Claude Code execution prompts under `prompts/`.

(e) **Close-out payload records for existing payloads.** Every `close-out-payloads/ses_NNN.json` file (there are ~38 of them).

(f) **Deposit event records for past applies.** Reconstructable from commit history (git log of the relevant payload-apply commits), but the apply outcomes (success/partial/failure) are not in the commit log; outcome reconstruction would be assumptive (assume success unless commit message indicates otherwise).

Resolution paths to consider at the workstream-establishing conversation or at the per-entity schema-design conversations:

1. **Go-forward only.** No backfill. Record entities exist only for work that happens after the entities ship. Pre-existing project state has no governance entity records. Queries about pre-shipped state continue to work via the existing free-text mentions in session records.

2. **Selective backfill.** Backfill the entity types where reconstruction is high-fidelity (workstream, conversation, reference book, work ticket, close-out payload) but skip deposit event because reconstructing outcomes is assumptive.

3. **Full backfill with reconstructed outcomes.** Backfill all six entity types including deposit events; outcome reconstruction tags assumptive entries explicitly.

Resolution is not blocking for the workstream itself — the schema design proceeds with the entities defined for go-forward use; backfill is a follow-on decision at the build-planning conversation or later.

Discharge: closed when a decision is reached and (if backfill is chosen) the backfill is executed.
after_payload.title: Retroactive migration: whether to backfill governance entity records for sessions, decisions, planning items, references, and prior workstreams already in the database

after_payload.artifacts_produced:
Eight files committed to PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ in the crmbuilder repo over five commits (95b7939, 802bdc4, 369f32e, 0dfcc25, 0aeb5c0):

(1) governance-schema-workstream-plan.md (new, v1.0) — workstream master plan, 301 lines. Establishes the workstream's purpose, references DEC-117..122 as foundation and PI-022 as the backfill question, names six per-entity schema-design conversations in workstream order followed by a build-planning conversation, captures the bootstrap chicken-and-egg framing, lists architectural disciplines each per-entity conversation must apply, glossary.

(2) governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md (new, v1.0) — schema-spec methodology guide, 343 lines. Template every per-entity schema specification follows: nine required sections (identity, fields, relationships, lifecycle, API surface, UI considerations, acceptance criteria, open questions, cross-references), conversation cadence (pre-flight reads, one-question-at-a-time discussion, drafting, governance recording at close), cross-spec consistency conventions, two validation gates.

(3) schema-design-kickoff-workstream.md (new) — kickoff for conversation 1 of 6. Working prefix WS. Includes nested-workstream question that DEC-120 deferred to this entity's schema-design conversation.

(4) schema-design-kickoff-conversation.md (new) — kickoff for conversation 2 of 6. Working prefix CONV. Carries the seven-state lifecycle from DEC-119 as the working starting point.

(5) schema-design-kickoff-reference-book.md (new) — kickoff for conversation 3 of 6. Working prefix RB. Versioning model is the principal design question; references charter and status entities' versioned-replace pattern as the closest existing model.

(6) schema-design-kickoff-work-ticket.md (new) — kickoff for conversation 4 of 6. Working prefix WT. Boundary question against reference book (single-use vs. continued-reference) is the principal design question.

(7) schema-design-kickoff-close-out-payload.md (new) — kickoff for conversation 5 of 6. Working prefix COP. Payload-content representation (JSON column, text column, file-path-plus-metadata, or hybrid) is the principal design question. Append-only after apply is the working assumption.

(8) schema-design-kickoff-deposit-event.md (new) — kickoff for conversation 6 of 6, and the workstream's last schema-design conversation. Working prefix DEP. Back-references to records the apply wrote (references-table edges vs. JSON column vs. dedicated table vs. target-side columns) is the principal design question. Append-only throughout. This conversation also produces an additional artifact at close: the build-planning conversation's kickoff prompt at governance-schema-build-planning-kickoff.md.

after_payload.title: Governance entity schema-design workstream established; workstream master plan, methodology guide, and six per-entity schema-design kickoff prompts produced
after_payload.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Establish the governance entity schema-design workstream and produce its scaffolding artifacts. Four categories of deliverable: (1) Six new decision records formalizing the six incoming principles below (numbers in the Decision 117 range; actual values resolved at conversation close); (2) PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-workstream-plan.md — workstream master plan; (3) PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md — methodology guide; (4) Six kickoff prompts under PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/, one per per-entity schema-design conversation."

Topics worked through:

1. Pre-flight resolution against the live database. The kickoff was authored under the assumption that the predecessor scoping conversation had not yet recorded the six principle-level decisions and the backfill planning item at this conversation's open. Pre-flight resolved that SES-046 had in fact closed out earlier the same day with DEC-117..122 and PI-022 already recorded. Treating those records as the workstream's foundation rather than re-recording reduced the deliverable count from four categories to three (master plan, methodology guide, six kickoff prompts) plus a session record.

2. Master plan drafting. Modeled on methodology-schema-workstream-plan.md (SES-011's analogue). Sections cover purpose, origin and foundation (referencing DEC-117..122 explicitly), scope (six entities under minimum-viable framing, with the six identifier-prefix working assumptions: WS, CONV, RB, WT, COP, DEP), methodology (eight-conversation workstream: this conversation + six schema-design + one build-planning), workstream structure with sequence diagram, independence (per DEC-122, does not gate on CBM PI-001 or multi-tenancy routing fix), bootstrap (the chicken-and-egg about designing workstream and conversation entities while operating inside a workstream and conversations that have no database records — resolved by deferring retroactive population to PI-022, executed after build ships), governance (no new decisions or planning items authored here; refers to DEC-117..122 and PI-022), architectural disciplines (vocabulary discipline per access/vocab.py, API envelope per the SES-025 fix, client-side identifier computation, per-engagement isolation, source-first relationship-kind naming per DEC-048, parent-prefix field naming per DEC-046), open questions, link-outs to companion documents, glossary.

3. Schema-spec methodology guide drafting. Modeled on methodology-entity-schema-spec-guide.md. Inherits parent-prefix field naming (DEC-046) and source-first relationship-kind naming (DEC-048) as policy for new entity types regardless of category — no methodology-only caveat, in contrast to the methodology guide's section 6 forward-only scope. PI-006 retrofit of existing governance entities remains tracked separately and untouched. Adds append-only as a first-class lifecycle option in section 3.4 (deposit event will likely need it; close-out payload may); methodology guide did not have this. Lifecycle posture inherited as 'prefer simple,' but conversation entity gets the full seven-state DEC-119 lifecycle explicitly as a tolerated exception. Section 6 cross-spec consistency table covers identifier prefix style, status field naming, status casing, relationship-kind naming, field naming, timestamp inheritance, soft-delete versus append-only, identifier auto-assignment, lifecycle preference, API envelope.

4. Six per-entity kickoff prompts drafted. Workstream order: workstream → conversation → reference book → work ticket → close-out payload → deposit event. Each follows the schema-design-kickoff-domain.md model from the methodology workstream: task, context (why this entity at this position), what the entity needs to host with real grounding examples from the current project state, read-first list, architectural questions likely to arise, working style, pre-flight, governance at close, what the conversation does NOT do. Grounding examples drawn from real project artifacts: actual workstreams (methodology, v0.5 engagement-management, v0.6 styling, multi-tenancy routing fix, CBM paper test, this one), actual reference books (PRDs v0.1..v0.6, implementation plans, master plans, methodology guides, the conduct framework, the multi-engagement architecture document), actual work tickets (the methodology workstream's per-entity kickoffs and this workstream's seven kickoffs, the Claude Code apply prompts), actual close-out payload files at close-out-payloads/. The deposit-event kickoff (sixth) has one extra deliverable at close — the build-planning conversation's kickoff prompt — called out explicitly so it is not lost in closeout.

5. Identifier prefix working assumptions named but not locked. WS, CONV, RB, WT, COP, DEP carried through the kickoff prompts as working assumptions. Each per-entity conversation has first refusal at lock-in; the spec guide's section 6 collision list will be amended as each conversation closes.

6. Mid-conversation correction (committed separately). Initial drafts of the workstream plan and deposit-event kickoff stated that this conversation authors the retroactive-backfill planning item. Pre-flight had already established PI-022 exists; the claim slipped through into drafting. Five passages in the workstream plan and one passage in the deposit-event kickoff updated to cite PI-022 by identifier and attribute its authorship to SES-046. Two follow-up file changes, one follow-up commit.

7. Bootstrap framing explicit in master plan section 7. This workstream designs the workstream and conversation entities, but the conversations doing the designing are themselves conversations inside a workstream that has no records. Resolution: during the workstream, the session record on the append-only sessions table remains the only governance record per conversation per DEC-013; the workstream itself has no database record during its lifetime; retroactive population is PI-022's responsibility, executed after the build ships. Each schema-design conversation designs without depending on backfill ordering, but the schemas must support retroactive population (records insertable with historical timestamps and complete status).

8. Eight artifacts produced over four local commits, pushed to origin/main after sign-off: governance-schema-workstream-plan.md, governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md, six schema-design-kickoff-{entity}.md files. Plus one corrective commit for the PI-022 framing.
