after_payload.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Design the `workstream` entity type schema for V2's storage layer. Drive a structured architectural discussion that produces one deliverable: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/workstream.md — the complete schema specification per the template in governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md. The directory does not yet exist; this conversation creates it. Cadence matches the methodology workstream's schema-design conversations and the user-interface planning conversations: structured architectural discussion driven one decision at a time, building toward the specification section by section. At conversation close: decisions written via direct API as decision records; any deferred items written as planning items; one session record written by Doug through the V2 desktop New Session dialog per the session-record-at-close pattern."

Topics worked through:

1. Pre-flight orientation. Sparse-cloned the crmbuilder repo and read the eight-item reading list named in the kickoff: crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md, governance-schema-workstream-plan.md, governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md, the six foundation decisions DEC-117..122 from db-export/decisions.json, SES-046 and SES-047 from db-export/sessions.json, multi-engagement-architecture.md, crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/access/vocab.py, and methodology-schema-specs/domain.md as the structural exemplar. API health check (against the conversation's sandbox localhost, not Doug's machine) and pytest run (sparse checkout doesn't include tests/) were flagged as not meaningfully runnable in the conversation environment; design-only conversation, so neither gates.

2. Two concerns surfaced before opening Decision 1. (a) The kickoff's working assumption for workstream-to-conversation linkage was 'direct foreign key on conversation table,' but DEC-120's decision text explicitly says 'Conversations belong to a workstream via a relationship in the references table.' Drift; DEC-120 binds. (b) The kickoff said three statuses (planned, in_flight, complete) with cancelled and superseded flagged as open, while DEC-120 itself names four statuses including cancelled, and DEC-117 references 'three-status lifecycle posture' as the inherited methodology pattern. Drift; DEC-120 binds for cancelled (in by default), superseded genuinely open.

3. Decision 1 — workstream-to-conversation relationship via references-table edge. Eight-element template. Confirmed DEC-120's references-edge mechanism over the kickoff's working-assumption foreign-key. Cross-spec precedent locked: references-edge over foreign-key for parent-child governance relationships. New relationship kind named: `conversation_belongs_to_workstream` (source-first per DEC-048). Approved Option B.

4. Decision 2 — status lifecycle, five values including superseded. Eight-element template. Confirmed cancelled per DEC-120 with no debate. Resolved superseded as in (five statuses total) for parity with DEC-119's conversation entity (which has superseded as a status), with truly-terminal terminal states (no transitions out of any terminal; reactivation is conceptually a new workstream). Supersession reuses the existing generic `supersedes` reference kind for the (workstream, workstream) pair; access-layer rule requires an outgoing supersedes edge when status is superseded. Approved Option A.

5. Identifier prefix — drafted as WS, not surfaced as a separate decision. Two-letter form acceptable per the spec guide's 2-5 letter range; matches PI's short-prefix posture; doesn't conflict with the existing collision list. Downstream conversations have their own first refusal on their working prefixes.

6. Decision 3 — nested workstreams. Eight-element template. Resolved the deferred-from-DEC-120 question: flat catalog, no nesting in this release. Zero observed need in the project's operating history; DEC-120's 'if nested workstreams are admitted' phrasing leans this way; user-interface cost of hierarchical rendering is real; the retrofit path is documented as a references-edge addition (not self-FK column) so the workstream table is not touched if nesting becomes needed later. Cleveland Business Mentors Planning Item 001 (sub-domain hierarchy on the methodology `domain` entity) is NOT pre-decided by this — the methodology workstream's PI-001 conversation makes its own call with this conversation's reasoning available but not binding. Approved Option A.

7. Decision 4 — lifecycle timestamps. Eight-element template. Per-status lifecycle timestamps adopted: workstream_started_at, _completed_at, _cancelled_at, _superseded_at (four columns, all nullable, server-set on the matching transition). No planned_at column (redundant with created_at). Diverges from the methodology entities' base-timestamps-only pattern; the divergence is appropriate because methodology entities have propose-verify lifecycles while workstream has a workflow-shaped lifecycle that benefits from queryable per-status timestamps. Cross-spec precedent locked: per-status lifecycle timestamps for workflow-shaped governance lifecycles (conversation entity likely inherits this). Approved Option B.

8. Routine items drafted and approved as a single batch — master plan linkage (references-edge to a reference_book record with new kind `workstream_planned_in_reference_book`, provisional naming, reference_book conversation may refine, build-planning reconciles); soft-delete posture (default with restore — workstream is organizing metadata not transactional fact, so append-only is inappropriate; soft-delete is distinct from cancelled status, which is lifecycle outcome rather than administrative removal); API surface (standard 8-endpoint set with no deviations); user-interface considerations (default layout with two natural additions documented in the spec — conditional lifecycle-timestamp rendering on the detail pane showing only non-null timestamps, and supersession-requires-edge UX where the user must add the outgoing supersedes edge before committing status to superseded).

9. Spec drafting in one pass per spec guide section 4.3. governance-schema-specs/workstream.md drafted following the structural shape of methodology-schema-specs/domain.md, with all nine required subsections of section 3 substantively filled. 16 testable acceptance criteria captured (spec guide gate requires 10). Section 3.8 categorized by build / backfill / future tags per the spec guide convention. Section 3.9 cross-references DEC-117..122 as foundation decisions and DEC-046, DEC-048, DEC-013, DEC-115/116 as inherited conventions. Committed at b981c1f after a small fix to the acceptance-criterion-1 column count (10 → 13, correcting the field-section sum).

10. Close-out artifacts authored. After Doug's confirmation, this payload (ses_048.json) and the companion apply prompt (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-048.md) were drafted in one pass. Identifier check at close-out: latest applied SES is SES-047 so SES-048 is next-available; latest DEC is DEC-122 so DEC-123..128 is next-available for the six decisions; no new planning items (PI-022 already covers retroactive backfill including this workstream's records).

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — WST, WSTRM, or WORK.** Longer forms; no semantic improvement; would set a soft precedent for three-or-more-letter prefixes that the remaining five conversations might not want to inherit. Rejected because there is no functional reason to prefer length.
after_payload.consequences: Spec guide section 6 collision list extended with WS. The remaining five governance entity conversations have first refusal on their own prefix choices, with no cross-spec length norm locked at this conversation. The workstream entity's spec section 3.1 documents the soft-prefix-length posture explicitly for downstream readers.
after_payload.context: The workstream entity is the first of six new governance entities designed in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Its identifier prefix sets a small precedent for the cross-spec collision list and contributes to the visible-or-not pattern of cross-spec consistency on prefix length. The existing V2 collision list spans two-letter (PI), three-letter (DEC, SES, RSK, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT), and four-letter (PROC, CRMC) prefixes; the governance schema spec guide section 6 admits 2-5 letters without locking a length. The remaining five governance entities have working prefixes spanning two to four letters (CONV, RB, WT, COP, DEP).
after_payload.decision: **Adopt `WS` as the workstream identifier prefix, format `WS-NNN` zero-padded to three digits, server-assigned on POST omission with helper at GET /workstreams/next-identifier.** The two-letter form is affirmed as acceptable; the cross-spec norm is NOT locked at two letters — downstream conversations may choose three or four letters where the two-letter form would be ambiguous or read awkwardly. Spec guide section 6's collision list is extended with WS.
after_payload.rationale: WS is short, mnemonic, and easy to scan. The two-letter form matches PI's existing posture. The collision list has no conflict with WS. The spec guide's 2-5 letter range admits the choice without justification. Locking the cross-spec norm at two letters would constrain the five downstream conversations needlessly; each conversation has its own context for choosing length.
after_payload.title: Workstream entity identifier prefix and format: WS, two-letter form acceptable without locking the cross-spec norm

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Direct foreign-key column `conversation_workstream_id` on conversation table.** Would give a single indexed filter for 'list conversations in a workstream' and schema-level NOT NULL enforcement of the parent relationship. Rejected because DEC-120's decision text explicitly chose references-table edge, and the query/enforcement gains do not constitute cause to override a foundation decision. Methodology workstream's mixed-mechanism pattern (entity_scopes_to_domain as references-edge, process_belongs_to_domain as foreign-key) was considered as precedent for case-by-case mechanism choice; rejected for the governance family because architectural consistency across the family outweighs per-relationship optimization.
after_payload.consequences: All five remaining governance entity schema-design conversations inherit references-edge for analogous parent-child relationships unless they have specific cause to deviate. One new relationship_kind to register in vocab.py; matching Alembic migration on refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint. Access-layer rule enforcing exactly-one parent edge per conversation; tests required. The references-table generic supersedes/is_about/references kinds continue to sit alongside the new typed kinds in the universal references table.
after_payload.context: DEC-120's decision text states explicitly: 'Conversations belong to a workstream via a relationship in the references table.' The kickoff prompt for SES-048 carried a working assumption that the workstream-to-conversation relationship would be a direct foreign-key column on the conversation table (conversation_workstream_id). The discrepancy needed to be resolved at the workstream entity's schema-design conversation — both because the workstream spec must describe how the relationship is modelled and because the choice sets a precedent for analogous parent-child relationships across the remaining five governance entities (close_out_payload to conversation, work_ticket to conversation, deposit_event to close_out_payload, and so on).
after_payload.decision: **Adopt the references-table edge mechanism per DEC-120's binding decision text.** The workstream-to-conversation relationship is modelled as a references-table edge with new relationship kind `conversation_belongs_to_workstream` (source-first per DEC-048: source = conversation, target = workstream). No `workstream_id` column on the conversation table. The access layer enforces 'every conversation has exactly one outgoing conversation_belongs_to_workstream edge'; POSTs that omit the edge or supply multiple edges return HTTP 422. Vocabulary additions: REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS += conversation_belongs_to_workstream; ENTITY_TYPES += workstream, conversation; matching clause in _kinds_for_pair. Cross-spec precedent locked: references-edge over foreign-key for parent-child governance relationships across the six new entities.
after_payload.rationale: DEC-120's text is binding — foundation decisions are overridden only with clear cause, and the kickoff's preference for foreign-key offered convenience rather than cause. The references-edge posture matches the governance entity family's coherent architectural shape: parent-child relationships across the six new entities all use the same mechanism, making the family easier to reason about per type and at use time. The query and schema-enforcement costs of references-edge over foreign-key are real but small at expected scale (10-20 conversations per workstream, dozens of workstreams).
after_payload.title: Workstream-to-conversation relationship via references-table edge per DEC-120, with new relationship kind conversation_belongs_to_workstream

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Four statuses with no superseded.** Supersession via existing supersedes reference edge with terminal status remaining `cancelled`. Rejected because the asymmetry with conversation's superseded status (DEC-119) would invite confusion at the family level. **Option (c) — Reactivatable terminal states (e.g., complete → in_flight on resume).** Rejected because workflow-shape lifecycles work best with terminal-means-terminal discipline; resumption is more accurately modelled as a new workstream record that supersedes the prior one. **Option (d) — Introduce a new typed kind `workstream_supersedes_workstream`.** Rejected as redundant with the existing generic supersedes kind whose semantic is identical.
after_payload.consequences: New vocabulary in vocab.py: WORKSTREAM_STATUSES = frozenset({planned, in_flight, complete, cancelled, superseded}); WORKSTREAM_STATUS_TRANSITIONS dict with all three terminals as empty-successor sets. Access-layer rule enforcing supersession-requires-edge; tests for both the create-with-superseded-status case and the patch-to-superseded case. Cross-spec precedent of truly terminal terminal states for workflow-shaped governance lifecycles; conversation entity inherits it for its seven-state lifecycle. Cross-spec precedent that lifecycles richer than three states are acceptable for governance entities — the spec guide already endorsed this; this decision exercises it.
after_payload.context: DEC-120's decision text names four statuses: planned, in_flight, complete, cancelled. The kickoff prompt for SES-048 carried a working assumption of three statuses (planned, in_flight, complete) with cancelled and superseded flagged as open questions. DEC-117 references 'three-status lifecycle posture' as the convention inherited from the methodology workstream. Three foundation documents named different status counts; the workstream entity's schema-design conversation had to reconcile and decide the lifecycle. DEC-119's conversation entity carries seven statuses including superseded, raising the question of parity between workstream and conversation.
after_payload.decision: **Adopt five statuses: `planned`, `in_flight`, `complete`, `cancelled`, `superseded`.** The default starter status is `planned`. Transitions per spec section 3.4.1: planned admits in_flight, cancelled, superseded; in_flight admits complete, cancelled, superseded; the three terminal states (complete, cancelled, superseded) admit no outgoing transitions — terminal means truly terminal. Access-layer rule: setting workstream_status to `superseded` requires an outgoing `supersedes` reference edge to another workstream record; POSTs and PATCHes that violate the rule return HTTP 422 with `supersession_requires_successor_edge`. The supersession edge reuses the existing generic `supersedes` reference kind (already valid for the (workstream, workstream) pair via _kinds_for_pair's source_type == target_type rule once workstream is in ENTITY_TYPES); no new kind is introduced for the supersession relationship. Cross-spec precedent locked: truly terminal terminal states for workflow-shaped governance lifecycles.
after_payload.rationale: DEC-120's text binds cancelled (already named in the foundation decision); the kickoff's three-status framing drifted and is corrected. Adding superseded as a fifth status provides parity with DEC-119's conversation entity, where superseded is one of seven statuses; the project's operating history demonstrates supersession as a real workflow event (the methodology workstream effectively superseded an originally-planned user-interface version 0.4 kickoff). Truly terminal terminal states preserve timeline semantics — reactivation of a finished workstream blurs the timeline; a workstream that resumes is conceptually a new workstream with an inbound supersedes edge. Reusing the existing generic supersedes kind avoids a redundant typed kind for what is already a well-established cross-entity semantic.
after_payload.title: Workstream lifecycle: five statuses (planned, in_flight, complete, cancelled, superseded) with truly terminal terminals and supersession-requires-edge rule

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — No lifecycle timestamps (methodology parity).** Rely on change_log for transition history. Rejected because workstream's workflow-shape lifecycle has queryable per-status durations as a real use case, and methodology parity does not justify ignoring the use case when the methodology lifecycle is different in kind. **Option (c) — Single `state_changed_at` timestamp.** Tracks the last transition only. Rejected as adding nothing over updated_at and change_log together. **Option (d) — Outcome summary field as required-on-complete.** Rejected because the build-planning conversation's session record carries the outcome narrative and a duplicate field invites drift.
after_payload.consequences: Workstream table has 13 columns (9 substantive + 4 inherited/lifecycle). Server-side enforcement of lifecycle-timestamp population on transitions; tests required. Cross-spec precedent of per-status lifecycle timestamps; conversation entity (with seven states per DEC-119) most likely inherits this. Deposit_event probably just needs applied_at (append-only). Future-release additions (target user-interface version, outcome summary, predecessor-successor reference kinds between workstreams, nested-workstream retrofit) are documented as deferred in spec section 3.8.3.
after_payload.context: Workstream needed a field inventory under minimum-viable scope: enough to host the organizing-unit concept faithfully without over-engineering. The kickoff prompt named several candidate fields (purpose, description, target user-interface version, master plan link, outcome summary) as working assumptions. The methodology entities (domain, entity, process, crm_candidate) carry only base timestamps (created_at, updated_at, deleted_at) and rely on change_log for status-transition history; the kickoff prompt's working assumption for workstream included per-status lifecycle timestamps (planned_at, started_at, completed_at). The divergence between the methodology pattern and the kickoff's per-status pattern needed an explicit decision since it sets a cross-spec precedent for downstream timeline-shaped entities.
after_payload.decision: **Adopt a nine-field schema plus three inherited base timestamps plus four per-status lifecycle timestamps.** Identity fields: workstream_identifier, workstream_name. Content fields: workstream_purpose (required, one-sentence), workstream_description (required, paragraph), workstream_notes (optional, internal). Classification fields: workstream_status. Relationship fields: NONE (parent-child relationships go through refs per DEC-124; no nesting per DEC-127). Timestamp fields: inherited workstream_created_at, workstream_updated_at, workstream_deleted_at, plus four nullable lifecycle timestamps server-set on the matching transition — workstream_started_at (planned → in_flight), workstream_completed_at (→ complete), workstream_cancelled_at (→ cancelled), workstream_superseded_at (→ superseded). No workstream_planned_at column (redundant with workstream_created_at since planned is the default starter). The three terminal-state timestamps are mutually exclusive on terminal records. No storage-level length caps on text fields. Cross-spec precedent locked: per-status lifecycle timestamps for workflow-shaped governance lifecycles.
after_payload.rationale: Workflow-shaped lifecycles benefit from per-status timestamps in a way propose-verify lifecycles don't. Queries like 'average time-to-complete' and 'workstreams that stalled at planned' are first-class under per-status timestamps and indirect joins through change_log under the methodology base-only pattern. Storage cost of four extra nullable columns is negligible. The methodology divergence is appropriate because methodology entities use propose-verify lifecycles (candidate, confirmed, deferred) where per-status timestamps add no value; workstream's lifecycle is different in kind. Deliberately omitted fields (target user-interface version, outcome summary) have weak justifications in the minimum-viable shape: the build-planning conversation's session record carries the outcome story; the build-planning conversation sets the target version; both can grow into the schema additively in a later release.
after_payload.title: Workstream field inventory with per-status lifecycle timestamps for the workflow-shaped lifecycle

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Self-referential foreign-key column workstream_parent_id (topic.parent_topic pattern).** Rejected because the future retrofit would touch the workstream table; references-edge is a lighter retrofit. **Option (c) — References-edge with new parent-child kind in this release.** Rejected because no real need exists and adding an unused mechanism creates dead vocabulary that the build-planning conversation must aggregate without justification.
after_payload.consequences: No nesting infrastructure in this release. Soft precedent for downstream governance entities considering hierarchy: prefer flat unless real need surfaces; if hierarchy is added later, prefer references-edge retrofit over self-FK column addition. Cleveland Business Mentors Planning Item 001 (sub-domain hierarchy on the methodology domain entity) is NOT pre-decided by this decision — that conversation makes its own call with this reasoning available but not binding. Future workstream-shaped initiatives expressing multi-stage organization use free text or (when needed) future predecessor/successor reference kinds rather than nesting.
after_payload.context: DEC-120 explicitly deferred the nested-workstream question to 'the workstream entity's own schema-design conversation,' phrasing the deferral as 'optional parent-workstream reference if nested workstreams are admitted.' The workstream entity's spec needed to resolve whether workstream is a flat catalog or a tree, and if a tree, what mechanism (self-referential foreign key like topic.parent_topic, or references-table edge with a new parent-child kind). The choice sets a soft precedent for downstream governance entities considering hierarchy and informs the Cleveland Business Mentors Planning Item 001 sub-domain hierarchy question on the methodology domain entity, though it does not pre-decide PI-001.
after_payload.decision: **Adopt a flat catalog — no nesting in this release.** Workstream records are siblings. Multi-stage initiatives are expressed via free text in workstream_description or via future predecessor/successor reference kinds (not modelled in this release). The retrofit path, if nesting becomes a real need in a future release, is a references-edge addition with a new relationship kind (provisional name `workstream_parent_of_workstream`) plus an access-layer 'at most one parent edge per workstream' rule plus user-interface tree rendering. The retrofit deliberately uses references-edge rather than self-referential foreign-key so the workstream table is not retroactively modified.
after_payload.rationale: Zero observed need from the project's operating history — all workstreams to date have been flat siblings. DEC-120's 'if nested workstreams are admitted' phrasing leans toward not-admitting. User-interface cost of hierarchical rendering is real (tree widget, expand-collapse state, breadcrumbs, drag-to-reparent); deferring until signal supports it is the minimum-viable posture. The project's actual multi-stage organizing pattern is sequential (predecessor/successor relationships between workstreams) rather than nested (parent/child trees); sequential edges would model the real pattern more accurately than nesting if added later.
after_payload.title: Workstream flat catalog, no nesting in this release; retrofit path is references-edge addition not self-FK column

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Master plan as a foreign-key column workstream_master_plan_reference_book_id** — rejected per Decision 124's references-edge-over-foreign-key precedent. **Append-only posture for workstream** — rejected because workstream records can be created in error or with the wrong name; correction via edit-or-delete-and-restore is the appropriate posture. **Outcome summary as a required-on-complete field** — already rejected in DEC-126. **Tree-widget sidebar instead of flat panel** — out of scope per DEC-127 (no nesting in this release).
after_payload.consequences: Vocabulary additions to aggregate at build-planning: REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS += workstream_planned_in_reference_book; ENTITY_TYPES += reference_book; matching _kinds_for_pair clause for (workstream, reference_book). API endpoint test coverage required across all eight endpoints. Default UI layout with the two natural additions reduces to the existing EntityCrudDialog and ReferencesSection widgets per UI version 0.3 patterns. Sixteen acceptance criteria translate into specific test cases at build-planning time. The spec's per-spec completeness gate (spec guide section 7.1) passes: all nine subsections of section 3 substantively filled, at least ten acceptance criteria, explicit category tags on open questions, cross-references include workstream plan, spec guide, and foundation decisions.
after_payload.context: Routine items batched together at the close of the workstream schema-design conversation: how to model the master-plan-to-workstream linkage, what API endpoints the entity exposes, what the user-interface looks like, soft-delete versus append-only posture, and how many testable acceptance criteria the spec captures. Each item follows defaults from the schema-spec methodology guide; no deviations were warranted, but the choices set precedents and need to be recorded.
after_payload.decision: **(a) Master plan linkage** via references-edge with new relationship kind `workstream_planned_in_reference_book` (source = workstream, target = reference_book; cardinality zero-or-one from workstream side; provisional naming, reference_book conversation may refine, build-planning reconciles). ENTITY_TYPES gains reference_book for vocab registration. **(b) Supersession edge** reuses the existing generic `supersedes` kind for the (workstream, workstream) pair; no new typed kind. **(c) API surface** is the standard 8-endpoint set per spec guide section 3.5 with no deviations: GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE/restore/next-identifier; all endpoints return the {data, meta, errors} envelope. **(d) User-interface** uses the default ListDetailPanel layout per spec guide section 3.6, with two natural additions documented in the spec — conditional lifecycle-timestamp rendering on the detail pane (showing only non-null timestamps) and supersession-requires-edge UX (the user must add the outgoing supersedes edge before committing status to superseded). **(e) Soft-delete** posture is the default — soft-delete with restore — explicitly distinct from cancelled status (administrative housekeeping versus lifecycle outcome). **(f) Acceptance criteria** capture sixteen testable statements in spec section 3.7 (gate requires ten).
after_payload.rationale: Each item follows the spec guide's defaults with reasoning in the spec sections cited. No deviations warranted at this scope. The master-plan kind name workstream_planned_in_reference_book is provisional because the reference_book conversation (third in the workstream order) has not run yet; the kind name may be refined for verb-tense consistency with reference_book's other inbound kinds, and build-planning reconciles. Soft-delete fits workstream because the entity is organizing metadata not transactional fact (append-only fits decisions, sessions, deposit_events; not workstream).
after_payload.title: Workstream master plan linkage via references-edge, standard API and UI defaults, soft-delete posture, sixteen acceptance criteria

after_payload.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Design the `conversation` entity type schema for V2's storage layer. Drive a structured architectural discussion that produces one deliverable: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/conversation.md — the complete schema specification per the template in governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md. Cadence matches the methodology workstream's schema-design conversations and the user-interface planning conversations: structured architectural discussion driven one decision at a time, building toward the specification section by section. At conversation close: decisions written via direct API as decision records; any deferred items written as planning items; one session record written by Doug through the V2 desktop New Session dialog per the session-record-at-close pattern."

Topics worked through:

1. Pre-flight orientation. Sparse-cloned the crmbuilder repo and read the eight-item reading list named in the kickoff: crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md, governance-schema-workstream-plan.md, governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md, governance-schema-specs/workstream.md (the just-completed predecessor specification), the six foundation decisions DEC-117..122 plus the six SES-048 decisions DEC-123..128 from db-export/decisions.json, SES-046, SES-047, and SES-048 from db-export/sessions.json, crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/access/vocab.py, and methodology-schema-specs/domain.md as the structural exemplar. API health check (against the conversation's sandbox localhost, not Doug's machine) and pytest run (sparse checkout doesn't include tests/) were flagged as not meaningfully runnable in the conversation environment per the SES-048 precedent; design-only conversation, so neither gates.

2. Cross-spec precedents inherited from SES-048 surfaced at orientation: references-edge over foreign-key for parent-child governance relationships, per-status lifecycle timestamps for workflow-shaped lifecycles, and terminal-states-are-terminal discipline. Items already pre-committed by workstream.md and not re-decided by this conversation: the kind `conversation_belongs_to_workstream` (its name and direction were settled in workstream.md; this conversation registers it in vocab.py but does not re-decide it); ENTITY_TYPES additions `conversation` and `reference_book` (already listed in workstream.md's additions table).

3. Drift surfaced before opening Decision 1. The kickoff's working assumption for the conversation-to-session linkage was 'add a conversation_id foreign key to session in the build (back-fillable for historical records), keep direct relationship simple.' But workstream.md locked references-edge as the cross-spec precedent for parent-child governance relationships, and the conversation-to-session relationship is structurally parent-child (a conversation produces zero or one sessions per DEC-013). The drift is exactly analogous to the SES-048 drift on the workstream-to-conversation relationship; the locked precedent binds unless clear cause to deviate. The kickoff's explicit 'do NOT modify sessions table shape' boundary reinforces the precedent — the FK alternative would modify the append-only sessions table per DEC-013, the references-edge alternative does not. No other drifts.

4. Decision 1 — conversation-to-session relationship mechanism. Eight-element template. Resolved in favour of references-edge per the SES-048 precedent. New relationship kind named: conversation_records_session (source=conversation, target=session, source-first per DEC-048, past-tense 'records' mirrors workstream_planned_in_reference_book's past-participle posture). Cardinality at-most-one outgoing per conversation per DEC-013. Required-when rule: edge required when conversation_status='complete', enforced at the access layer with the same combined-validation pattern as workstream's supersession-requires-edge rule (HTTP 422 with {error: complete_conversation_requires_session_edge}). Approved Option A (references-edge).

5. Decision 2 — lifecycle granularity. Eight-element template. Resolved to keep all seven states from DEC-119 in full (planned → kickoff_drafted → ready → in_flight → one of complete / cancelled / superseded). The kickoff_drafted-vs-ready distinction is operationally real and was demonstrated by the project's own conversation lineage at the kickoff's authoring time: workstream's kickoff was drafted and ready, conversation's kickoff was drafted but not ready (waiting on workstream to close), reference_book's kickoff was drafted but two conversations away from openable. Collapsing the two states would lose the operationally meaningful distinction. Inherited truly-terminal posture from workstream's precedent (no transitions out of complete/cancelled/superseded, including between terminal states). Added the complete-requires-session-edge rule per Decision 1; supersession-requires-edge rule inherited verbatim from workstream. Forward-only planning lifecycle (no regression within the four non-terminal states either). Six per-status lifecycle timestamps named (one per non-starter status; planned_at collapses into created_at per workstream precedent): conversation_kickoff_drafted_at, conversation_ready_at, conversation_started_at, conversation_completed_at, conversation_cancelled_at, conversation_superseded_at. Approved Option A (full seven states).

6. Decision 3 — conversation-to-kickoff (work_ticket) relationship. Eight-element template. Resolved to references-edge with tentative new kind conversation_opens_against_work_ticket (active verb, present tense, source=conversation). Cardinality at-most-one outgoing per conversation. Required-when rule: edge required when conversation_status is in {kickoff_drafted, ready, in_flight, complete}; optional for {planned, cancelled, superseded}. Kind-name posture: committed tentatively here parallel to workstream.md's tentative workstream_planned_in_reference_book commitment; the work_ticket schema-design conversation may refine for verb-tense consistency with work_ticket's other inbound kinds; build-planning reconciles. ENTITY_TYPES gains work_ticket. Approved Option A.

7. Decision 4 — predecessor/successor chaining between conversations. Eight-element template. Resolved to introduce new kind conversation_succeeds_conversation (source=successor, target=predecessor, source-first per DEC-048). Diverges from workstream.md's defer-sequencing posture on the strength of conversation-sequencing frequency: every conversation in a sequenced workstream has predecessors and successors, captured today only in in_flight_at_end fields of session records (the weak-coupling pattern DEC-121 says to eliminate), while workstream sequencing is comparatively rare. Cardinality many-to-many (a build-planning conversation has six predecessor schema-design conversations; a workstream-establishing conversation has multiple direct successors). No required-when rule. Cycle prevention deferred to build-planning. New cross-spec precedent established: typed sequencing edges are introduced when entity-family frequency justifies, distinct from workstream's defer-sequencing posture. The remaining four schemas inherit the precedent and may deviate with rationale. Approved Option A.

8. After Decision 4 Doug instructed Claude to 'Proceed with making your recommendations final decision and complete the prompt without asking for further input.' Routine items 5-15 from the proposed sequence were resolved as a single batch and folded directly into the spec draft.

9. Routine items resolved without per-decision confirmation: (a) identifier prefix CONV, four letters matching PROC/CRMC precedent; (b) field inventory minimum-viable — no estimated_duration, no priority, no conversation_kind, intended-deliverable description folded into conversation_description; (c) workstream membership mandatory at the access layer, enforced as 'exactly one outgoing conversation_belongs_to_workstream edge'; (d) per-status lifecycle timestamps as enumerated in Decision 2; (e) cross-workstream conversation references use existing generic references/is_about kinds, no new vocabulary; (f) soft-delete with restore matching workstream's posture, distinct from cancelled status; (g) standard 8-endpoint API surface; (h) UI deviations — master pane gains a Workstream column as the fifth column (vs workstream's four), plus workstream_identifier and status filter combos in the panel toolbar realising the new query-string parameters on the list endpoint; (i) eighteen acceptance criteria (parallel to workstream's sixteen, with two added for the workstream-membership-required rule and the new list-endpoint filters); (j) vocabulary additions named in the spec table for build-planning aggregation; (k) cross-references section mirrors workstream.md's structure with the additional six SES-048 decisions cited as related prior decisions.

10. Spec drafting in one pass per spec guide section 4.3. governance-schema-specs/conversation.md drafted following the structural shape of governance-schema-specs/workstream.md, with all nine required subsections of section 3 substantively filled. Eighteen testable acceptance criteria captured (spec guide gate requires 10). Section 3.8 categorized by build / backfill / future tags per the spec guide convention with five build items (sidebar grouping, migration ordering, tentative kind names on both outbound and inbound sides, backfill behavior on create with terminal status, cycle prevention in conversation_succeeds_conversation), five backfill items inheriting from PI-022 plus new items specific to this entity (historical lifecycle timestamps, status assignment for prior conversations, predecessor-successor edges from in_flight_at_end text, kickoff-to-work_ticket edges from seed prompts, mandatory-workstream-membership for ad-hoc conversations like SES-046), and five future items (estimated duration, priority, conversation kind/type, outcome summary, pause/resume states, cross-workstream typed kinds). Committed at 6580a32 in one commit.

11. Close-out artifacts authored. After spec commit, this payload (ses_049.json) and the companion apply prompt (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-049.md) were drafted in one pass. Identifier check at close-out: latest applied SES is SES-048 so SES-049 is next-available; latest DEC is DEC-128 so DEC-129..134 is next-available for the six decisions; no new planning items (PI-022 covers retroactive backfill including this conversation's records).

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — CV.** Two letters, matches PI and WS short-prefix posture. Rejected because the abbreviation reads as 'curriculum vitae' or other domain shorthand and reduces scannability. **Option (c) — CONVO.** Five letters, no semantic improvement over CONV. Rejected because the longer form adds no value. **Option (d) — CHAT.** Four letters, changes the term of art from the project's working language. Rejected because the entity is documented throughout the project's writing as 'conversation,' and the prefix should match.
after_payload.consequences: Spec guide section 6 collision list extended with CONV. The remaining four governance entity conversations (reference book, work ticket, close-out payload, deposit event) have first refusal on their own prefix choices, with no cross-spec length norm locked at this conversation. The conversation entity's spec section 3.1 documents the four-letter posture explicitly.
after_payload.context: The conversation entity is the second of six new governance entities designed in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Its identifier prefix sets a small precedent for the cross-spec collision list. DEC-123 affirmed that the cross-spec norm is not locked at two letters; each downstream conversation makes its own call within the spec guide's 2-to-5-letter range. The kickoff prompt named CONV as a working assumption with CONVO, CHAT, and CV as alternatives. The existing collision list spans two-letter (PI, WS), three-letter (DEC, SES, RSK, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT), and four-letter (PROC, CRMC) prefixes.
after_payload.decision: **Adopt `CONV` as the conversation identifier prefix, format `CONV-NNN` zero-padded to three digits, server-assigned on POST omission with helper at GET /conversations/next-identifier.** The four-letter form matches the existing PROC and CRMC methodology-entity precedents and avoids the ambiguity of two-letter CV. Spec guide section 6's collision list is extended with CONV.
after_payload.rationale: CONV is mnemonic, easy to scan, and unambiguous. The four-letter form aligns with the methodology-entity precedents (PROC for process, CRMC for crm_candidate). Two-letter CV could read as 'curriculum vitae' or other domain shorthand; the longer form removes ambiguity. CONVO and CHAT were considered (the kickoff named both as alternatives); CONVO is one letter longer for no semantic gain; CHAT changes the term of art from 'conversation' (the project's working language) to a different word.
after_payload.title: Conversation entity identifier prefix and format: CONV, four-letter form matching PROC and CRMC precedents

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Foreign-key column `conversation_identifier` on the sessions table (the kickoff's working assumption).** Would give a single indexed filter for 'session for conversation X' and column-level constraint enforcement of the at-most-one-session-per-conversation rule. Rejected because the SES-048 precedent explicitly chose references-edge for analogous parent-child relationships, the kickoff itself forbade modifying the sessions table shape, and DEC-013 establishes sessions as the most delicate table in the governance system (modifying its shape requires strong justification). **Option (c) — Computed via text matching only (no formal relationship; rely on session.conversation_reference descriptive text per DEC-025).** Rejected because DEC-121's coverage extension principle requires machine-resolvable linkages between governance entities; text matching is exactly the weak-coupling pattern DEC-121 says to eliminate.
after_payload.consequences: One new relationship_kind to register in vocab.py (conversation_records_session); matching Alembic migration on refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint. Access-layer rule enforcing at-most-one session edge per conversation plus required-when-status-equals-complete rule; tests required for both. The sessions table shape is unmodified; no migration on sessions; no backfill column populate. The references-edge precedent locked by SES-048 is reinforced for the four remaining governance entity schema-design conversations. PI-022's backfill pass reconstructs the conversation_records_session edges for historical conversations from the descriptive text in session.conversation_reference.
after_payload.context: The kickoff prompt for SES-049 carried a working assumption that the conversation-to-session relationship would be a direct foreign-key column on the sessions table (conversation_identifier, back-fillable for historical records, with the kickoff calling the approach 'keep direct relationship simple'). SES-048 locked the cross-spec precedent of references-edge over foreign-key for parent-child governance relationships (DEC-124). The conversation-to-session relationship is structurally parent-child — a conversation produces zero or one sessions per DEC-013. The kickoff's own 'What this conversation does NOT do' section explicitly forbids modifying the existing sessions table shape, reinforcing the precedent's applicability. The drift between the kickoff's FK working assumption and the locked precedent is exactly analogous to the SES-048 drift on the workstream-to-conversation relationship.
after_payload.decision: **Adopt the references-table edge mechanism per the SES-048 cross-spec precedent (DEC-124).** The conversation-to-session relationship is modelled as a references-table edge with new relationship kind `conversation_records_session` (source-first per DEC-048: source = conversation, target = session, past-tense 'records' mirrors workstream_planned_in_reference_book's past-participle posture). No `conversation_identifier` column on the sessions table; no modification to the append-only sessions table shape per DEC-013. Cardinality: at most one outgoing edge per conversation. Required-when rule: edge required when conversation_status = 'complete', enforced at the access layer with the same combined-validation pattern as workstream's supersession-requires-edge rule (HTTP 422 with {error: complete_conversation_requires_session_edge}). Vocabulary additions: REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS += conversation_records_session; matching clause in _kinds_for_pair for (conversation, session).
after_payload.rationale: The SES-048 precedent is binding — cross-spec precedents are overridden only with clear cause, and the kickoff's preference for foreign-key offered convenience rather than cause. The references-edge posture matches the governance entity family's coherent architectural shape: parent-child relationships across the six new entities all use the same mechanism. The kickoff's own 'do NOT modify sessions table shape' boundary reinforces the choice — the FK alternative would have modified the append-only sessions table per DEC-013, requiring an Alembic migration and a backfill pass to populate historical rows; the references-edge alternative does not touch the sessions table at all. The query and schema-enforcement costs of references-edge over foreign-key are real but small at expected scale (50+ existing conversations, ~10 new per workstream).
after_payload.title: Conversation-to-session relationship via references-table edge per the SES-048 precedent, with new relationship kind conversation_records_session

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Collapse kickoff_drafted and ready into a single state.** Would reduce the status set from seven to six. Rejected because the operational distinction (kickoff exists but not openable vs. kickoff exists and dependencies met) is real and frequent. **Option (c) — Add a paused/hold status for in-flight conversations awaiting external input.** Rejected because no real operational signal supports it at the time of this conversation; deferred to future-release per spec section 3.8.3 parallel to workstream's deferred pause/resume posture. **Option (d) — Allow regression within the planning lifecycle.** Rejected because timeline semantics are clearer with forward-only; the cost of cancelling and re-planning a conversation when scope changes is real but small.
after_payload.consequences: Conversations table has 15 columns (6 substantive + 3 inherited base + 6 per-status lifecycle). Server-side enforcement of forward-only transition map plus truly-terminal posture plus complete-requires-session-edge plus supersession-requires-edge; four access-layer rules to test. Future-release additions (estimated duration, priority, conversation kind, outcome summary, paused/hold states, cross-workstream typed kinds) are documented as deferred in spec section 3.8.3. PI-022's backfill pass populates the six lifecycle timestamp columns for historical conversation records; the backfill policy for which historical dates to use is PI-022's call.
after_payload.context: DEC-119 names seven working statuses for the conversation entity: planned, kickoff_drafted, ready, in_flight, complete, cancelled, superseded. The kickoff prompt for SES-049 asked whether all seven were needed in the user-interface version 0 build, specifically whether kickoff_drafted and ready could collapse into one state. The conversation also needed to decide the truly-terminal posture (SES-048 locked terminal-states-are-terminal for workstream; conversation inherits the precedent by default), the transition map within the four non-terminal states, and the access-layer rules tied to specific statuses (the conversation→session edge from Decision 1 implies a required-when rule analogous to workstream's supersession-requires-edge rule).
after_payload.decision: **Adopt the seven-status set from DEC-119 in full, with the following transition map and access-layer rules.** (a) Status values: planned (default starter), kickoff_drafted, ready, in_flight, complete (terminal), cancelled (terminal), superseded (terminal). (b) Forward-only planning lifecycle: the four non-terminal states form a strict line (planned → kickoff_drafted → ready → in_flight), with no regressive transitions. cancelled and superseded reachable from any non-terminal state. (c) Truly-terminal posture inherited from workstream's DEC-125 precedent: terminal states reject all outgoing transitions including between terminal states. (d) Complete-requires-session-edge rule: setting status to complete requires an outgoing conversation_records_session edge per Decision 1 (DEC-130); HTTP 422 with {error: complete_conversation_requires_session_edge} on violation. (e) Supersession-requires-edge rule: inherited verbatim from workstream's DEC-125; setting status to superseded requires an outgoing supersedes edge to another conversation record. (f) Six per-status lifecycle timestamps (one per non-starter status; planned_at collapses into created_at per workstream precedent): conversation_kickoff_drafted_at, conversation_ready_at, conversation_started_at, conversation_completed_at, conversation_cancelled_at, conversation_superseded_at. Server-set on transition; three terminal timestamps mutually exclusive at the terminal level.
after_payload.rationale: The kickoff_drafted-vs-ready distinction is operationally real and was demonstrated by the project's own conversation lineage at the kickoff's authoring time: workstream's kickoff was drafted and ready, conversation's kickoff was drafted but not ready (waiting on workstream to close), reference_book's kickoff was drafted but two conversations away from openable. Collapsing the two states would lose this operationally meaningful distinction or require a separate computed-readiness signal to recover it. The methodology guide explicitly admits 'Specifications with more than three statuses are expected and do not need special justification, provided the states are operationally meaningful and transitions are well-defined' (section 5). The forward-only planning lifecycle preserves timeline semantics — a conversation that 'regresses' from ready back to kickoff_drafted because an upstream dependency surfaced is conceptually a new conversation (cancel and re-plan, or delay the actual opening). The truly-terminal posture matches workstream's. The complete-requires-session-edge rule realizes Decision 1's references-edge mechanism — a complete conversation without a session edge is in an inconsistent state. The six per-status lifecycle timestamps follow DEC-126's per-status timestamps precedent extended from workstream's four columns to conversation's six (more lifecycle states means more timestamps).
after_payload.title: Conversation lifecycle: seven statuses with truly terminal terminals, forward-only planning lifecycle, complete-requires-session-edge and supersession-requires-edge rules

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Foreign-key column work_ticket_id on conversation.** Rejected per the inherited references-edge precedent from DEC-124. **Option (c) — Foreign-key column conversation_id on work_ticket.** Would model the relationship from work_ticket's side. Rejected for the same precedent reason and because it would commit a column on a not-yet-designed table from this spec's side, which the work_ticket conversation could not change without breaking this spec. **Option (d) — Past-participle kind name (conversation_seeded_by_work_ticket).** Rejected because the active-verb framing matches the project's working language and the source-first convention reads more naturally with the active verb. **Option (e) — Defer the relationship entirely to the work_ticket conversation.** Rejected because the conversation entity's spec must describe its outbound relationships to be considered complete per spec guide section 2; deferring the relationship would leave the conversation entity's spec incomplete.
after_payload.consequences: One new relationship_kind to register in vocab.py (conversation_opens_against_work_ticket); one new ENTITY_TYPES entry (work_ticket); matching Alembic migration on refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint and the ENTITY_TYPES enumeration. Access-layer rule enforcing at-most-one work_ticket edge per conversation plus required-when rule binding the edge to statuses past kickoff_drafted. The work_ticket schema-design conversation (third after this one) may refine the kind name; build-planning reconciles. PI-022's backfill pass reconstructs conversation_opens_against_work_ticket edges from the kickoff-prompt filenames named in historical session records' seed prompts and topics_covered text.
after_payload.context: Every conversation has one kickoff prompt — captured as a work_ticket record per the workstream plan's single-use-seed-document framing. The work_ticket entity is the fourth schema-design conversation in the workstream and has not yet been designed at SES-049's writing. The conversation entity's spec must declare the relationship from conversation to work_ticket; the question is the mechanism (references-edge per the precedent, or foreign-key column on conversation as a deviation) and the kind name (which the work_ticket conversation may refine for consistency with work_ticket's other inbound kinds).
after_payload.decision: **Adopt references-edge with tentative new kind `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket`.** Source = conversation, target = work_ticket. Active verb 'opens against' mirrors how Doug refers to kickoffs in practice ('Opens against PRDs/.../schema-design-kickoff-X.md') and reads as present-tense ongoing relationship between the conversation and its kickoff. Cardinality: at most one outgoing edge per conversation; exactly one inbound edge per work_ticket (the work_ticket's single-use property is enforced from work_ticket's side). Required-when rule: edge required when conversation_status is in {kickoff_drafted, ready, in_flight, complete}; optional for {planned, cancelled, superseded}. Kind name is tentative — the work_ticket schema-design conversation may refine for verb-tense consistency with work_ticket's other inbound kinds (for example, if work_ticket ends up with a uniform consumed-by verb pattern, this kind might shift to conversation_consumes_work_ticket); build-planning reconciles. ENTITY_TYPES gains work_ticket. Vocabulary additions: REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS += conversation_opens_against_work_ticket; ENTITY_TYPES += work_ticket; matching clause in _kinds_for_pair for (conversation, work_ticket).
after_payload.rationale: References-edge inherited from the SES-048 cross-spec precedent (DEC-124) — the conversation-to-work_ticket relationship is structurally parent-child (conversation outlives work_ticket; work_ticket is consumed during one conversation), and parent-child governance relationships use references-edge across the family. Active verb 'opens against' reads naturally in the project's writing context (Doug's commit messages, session records, and kickoff prompts uniformly use this phrasing). Past-participle alternatives (conversation_seeded_by_work_ticket) read more passively and put the work_ticket in the verb role; given conversation is the active organising unit, the source-active framing reads better. Required-when rule mirrors the complete-requires-session-edge rule from DEC-131 and the supersession-requires-edge rule from DEC-125 — the access layer enforces status-relationship consistency uniformly across the conversation entity. Tentative kind-name posture parallels workstream.md's tentative workstream_planned_in_reference_book commitment; build-planning has explicit reconciliation responsibility per the spec guide section 6 cross-spec consistency check.
after_payload.title: Conversation-to-kickoff (work_ticket) relationship via references-table edge with tentative new kind conversation_opens_against_work_ticket

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Defer to signal, parallel to workstream.md's posture.** Rejected because conversation sequencing is observed today on every conversation in this workstream; the signal is already present, not pending. **Option (c) — Foreign-key column predecessor_conversation_id on conversation.** Rejected per the inherited references-edge precedent from DEC-124 and because the many-to-many cardinality (a conversation may have multiple predecessors) does not fit a single-column FK. **Option (d) — Reuse supersedes for both sequencing and supersession.** Rejected because the semantics are distinct — supersession terminates the source's lifecycle, succession does not. **Option (e) — Introduce the kind but without a cycle-prevention rule.** Rejected because circular succession (A succeeds B which succeeds A) is semantically meaningless and would create graph-traversal hazards in any query that walks the DAG; cycle prevention is required, with the specific mechanism deferred to build-planning.
after_payload.consequences: One new relationship_kind to register in vocab.py (conversation_succeeds_conversation); matching Alembic migration on refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint. Access-layer cycle-prevention enforcement at insert time; tests required. The new cross-spec precedent on typed-sequencing-frequency-justification is documented in the spec's section 1 and 3.3.1 and informs the four remaining schema-design conversations: reference_book may have version-sequencing semantics that justify a typed kind, work_ticket has single-use semantics that argue against any sequencing, close_out_payload is produced once per conversation (no sequencing), deposit_event has retry semantics that may justify a typed retry-sequencing kind. Each downstream conversation applies the frequency test. PI-022's backfill pass reconstructs conversation_succeeds_conversation edges from in_flight_at_end fields of historical session records.
after_payload.context: Conversations within a workstream are often sequenced. This conversation's kickoff names a predecessor (the workstream schema-design conversation, SES-048) and a successor (the reference book schema-design conversation, planned). In a strictly sequenced workstream like the governance schema-design workstream, every conversation has explicit predecessor and successor relationships. In other workstreams (like the user-interface version 0.5 engagement-management workstream), conversations may be parallel — the workstream-establishing conversation has multiple direct successors. The chaining is captured today only in the in_flight_at_end fields of session records (free text describing what comes next); DEC-121's coverage extension principle says machine-resolvable linkages should replace free-text correspondence. The question for this conversation: does the schema introduce a typed sequencing kind in this release, or defer to signal as workstream.md did for its parallel workstream_succeeds_workstream question (section 3.8.3 of workstream.md)?
after_payload.decision: **Introduce new relationship kind `conversation_succeeds_conversation` in this release.** Source = successor conversation, target = predecessor conversation, source-first per DEC-048. Cardinality many-to-many (a build-planning conversation has six predecessor schema-design conversations; a workstream-establishing conversation has multiple direct successors). No required-when rule — chaining is optional, captured when meaningfully sequenced. Cycle prevention deferred to build-planning (the working assumption is access-layer DFS at insert time; the error response is HTTP 422 with {error: circular_conversation_succession}). Diverges from workstream's defer-sequencing posture on the strength of frequency. **Establishes new cross-spec precedent: typed sequencing edges are introduced when the entity family's sequencing is structurally frequent.** The remaining four governance entity schemas (reference_book, work_ticket, close_out_payload, deposit_event) inherit the precedent and may introduce parallel typed sequencing edges of their own, or defer, based on the same frequency test. Vocabulary additions: REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS += conversation_succeeds_conversation; matching clause in _kinds_for_pair for (conversation, conversation) — note that the existing _kinds_for_pair rule 'if source_type == target_type: kinds.add(supersedes)' already admits supersedes for this pair; the new clause adds conversation_succeeds_conversation alongside without conflict.
after_payload.rationale: Conversation sequencing is structurally part of every conversation's existence within a workstream — every conversation in a sequenced workstream has predecessors and successors. The existing fallback (filter conversations by workstream_id and status=planned, sort by planned_at) works for strict sequences but fails when a workstream-establishing conversation has multiple parallel successors. The text-matching alternative (parse in_flight_at_end fields to find named-conversation references) is exactly the weak-coupling pattern DEC-121 says to eliminate. Workstream's defer-sequencing posture is appropriate at workstream-entity granularity because workstream sequencing is rare (most workstreams are independent of each other); the frequency argument inverts at conversation granularity. The supersedes kind and the conversation_succeeds_conversation kind capture distinct semantics — supersedes is a directed replacement (one conversation abandoned, another carries forward, source's lifecycle terminates at superseded), while conversation_succeeds_conversation is a directed continuation (both conversations exist as healthy lifecycle objects, source positioned after target in planned sequence). The two kinds never co-occur on the same source-target pair.
after_payload.title: conversation_succeeds_conversation introduced for predecessor-successor chaining; new cross-spec precedent that typed sequencing edges are introduced when entity-family frequency justifies

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Field inventory with conversation_kind enum** — rejected for minimum-viable scope; kind taxonomy emerges from real-use signal. **Workstream membership optional** — rejected because DEC-120's text binds. **API surface without list-endpoint filters** — would force client-side filtering of unfiltered lists; rejected because the filters are small additions with material query-time benefit. **Master pane without Workstream column** — would require detail-pane open to see workstream membership; rejected because workstream membership is the most operationally relevant property of a conversation. **Append-only posture for conversation** — rejected because conversation records can be created with the wrong title or pointed at the wrong workstream; correction via edit is appropriate. **Sixteen acceptance criteria (matching workstream)** — would have skipped the two additional rules-specific criteria; rejected because the workstream-membership-required rule and the list-endpoint filters are conversation-specific and deserve test coverage.
after_payload.consequences: Conversations table has 15 columns (6 substantive + 3 base + 6 lifecycle per DEC-131). Two new query-string filter parameters on the list endpoint; test coverage required for both independently and composed. Master-pane Workstream column requires a small extension to ListDetailPanel's column-rendering (the column derives its value from a references-edge target rather than a direct column read); the build-planning conversation specifies the access-layer helper. Soft-delete is the default UI version 0.3 pattern with no code changes required. Eighteen acceptance criteria translate into specific test cases at build-planning time. The spec's per-spec completeness gate (spec guide section 7.1) passes: all nine subsections of section 3 substantively filled, eighteen acceptance criteria (gate requires ten), explicit category tags on open questions, cross-references include workstream plan, spec guide, workstream.md (the just-completed predecessor specification), foundation decisions DEC-117..122, and SES-048's six decisions DEC-123..128 as inherited precedents.
after_payload.context: Routine items batched together at the close of the conversation schema-design conversation: the field inventory beyond identifier/status/timestamps, the deliberately-excluded fields, the workstream-membership cardinality rule, the API surface, the master pane and detail pane layouts, soft-delete versus append-only posture, and the count and shape of testable acceptance criteria. Each item follows defaults from the schema-spec methodology guide; no significant deviations were warranted, but two natural additions warrant explicit decision recording (the list-endpoint filters for operational queries and the master-pane Workstream column for at-a-glance workstream membership).
after_payload.decision: **(a) Field inventory** is six content/classification fields (conversation_identifier, conversation_title, conversation_purpose, conversation_description, conversation_notes, conversation_status) plus nine timestamps (three inherited base — created_at, updated_at, deleted_at; six per-status lifecycle per DEC-131 — kickoff_drafted_at, ready_at, started_at, completed_at, cancelled_at, superseded_at). Intended-deliverable description is folded into conversation_description rather than carried as a separate column. Deliberately excluded under minimum-viable scope: estimated_duration, priority, conversation_kind, outcome_summary — each is documented as future-release in spec section 3.8.3. **(b) Workstream membership** is mandatory at the access layer — every conversation has exactly one outgoing conversation_belongs_to_workstream edge; POSTs that omit the edge return HTTP 422 with {error: missing_workstream_membership_edge}; PATCH/PUT that removes the edge without replacing it returns the parallel 422. **(c) API surface** is the standard 8-endpoint set per spec guide section 3.5 plus two new query-string filters on the list endpoint: workstream_identifier filters by workstream membership; status filters by status value. The two filters compose. **(d) Master pane** has five columns (vs workstream's four), adding Workstream as the third column showing the workstream_identifier of the membership edge's target. Workstream-selector and status-selector combo boxes in the panel toolbar drive the two new query-string filters. Default sort by Identifier ascending. **(e) Detail pane** uses the default ListDetailPanel layout per spec guide section 3.6 with conditional lifecycle-timestamp rendering on the detail pane (showing only non-null timestamps) and complete-requires-session-edge / supersession-requires-edge UX (the user must add the matching outgoing edge before committing the status change). **(f) Soft-delete** posture is the default — soft-delete with restore — explicitly distinct from cancelled status (administrative housekeeping versus lifecycle outcome). **(g) Acceptance criteria** capture eighteen testable statements in spec section 3.7 (gate requires ten; workstream produced sixteen; two additional criteria added for the workstream-membership-required rule and the new list-endpoint filters).
after_payload.rationale: Field inventory follows minimum-viable per the methodology guide; deferred fields are listed in spec section 3.8.3 with growth-condition language so future signal can justify additions. Workstream-membership-mandatory realizes DEC-120's binding text. List-endpoint filters are operational additions: 'what conversations are in workstream X' and 'what conversations are currently in_flight' are routine consultant questions, materially friction-reducing if the API answers directly rather than the client filtering an unfiltered list of 50+ conversations. Master-pane Workstream column is the at-a-glance complement — without it, the operator must open each conversation's detail to see its workstream. Soft-delete fits conversation because the entity is organizing metadata not transactional fact (append-only fits decisions, sessions, deposit_events; not conversation). Eighteen acceptance criteria fully satisfy the spec guide's per-spec completeness gate (section 7.1) and the cross-spec consistency check (section 7.2).

The state mismatch between the local sandbox clone (one commit ahead of origin) and the V2 database (no SES-050 or DEC-135..140 yet recorded) surfaced the gap. The conversation transitioned from schema-design mode to audit-and-finalize mode: a structured audit pass against the schema spec guide §7.1 completeness gate and §6 cross-spec consistency requirements, against the workstream master plan's position-3-of-6 constraints, against DEC-117's family-1 classification, and against the predecessor specs' (workstream.md, conversation.md) declarations the spec relies on. The audit concluded the spec is substantively complete, methodologically disciplined, and passes every completeness and consistency criterion — with one factual error appearing in three places (the Change Log v1.0 paragraph, the DEC-138 description in §3.9.1, and the DEC-117 entry in §3.9.3) that misidentified the three `reference_book_kind` enum values that are additions beyond DEC-117's seven. after_payload.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Design the `reference_book` entity type schema for V2's storage layer. Drive a structured architectural discussion that produces one deliverable: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/reference_book.md — the complete schema specification per the template in governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md. Cadence matches the methodology workstream's schema-design conversations and the user-interface planning conversations: structured architectural discussion driven one decision at a time, building toward the specification section by section. At conversation close: decisions written via direct API as decision records; any deferred items written as planning items; one session record written by Doug through the V2 desktop New Session dialog per the session-record-at-close pattern."

Topics worked through:

1. Pre-flight orientation. Sparse-cloned the crmbuilder repo state at session open. Confirmed `crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md` is the session-startup entry per Doug's confirmation. On the first `git log` check, found that the local sandbox clone is one commit ahead of origin/main: c1d007e "v2: reference book schema spec — third of six governance entity schemas" dated 05-21-26 14:55 by author Doug, adding a complete 467-line `reference_book.md` spec. Cross-checked the V2 db-export: decisions through DEC-134, sessions through SES-049, no DEC-135..140 or SES-050 yet. Conclusion: a prior Claude.ai sandbox session drafted and committed the spec but never authored a session record or close-out payload; the V2 database is silently out of sync with the local git state. Surfaced this immediately to Doug.

2. Prevention discussion. Doug asked how to prevent the class of issue (sandbox commits spec → never closes out → V2 app shows stale workstream state → next conversation starts from stale assumption). Response framed the root cause as a two-system-of-record gap (git holds committed deliverables; V2 database holds governance state; the bridge — manual close-out apply — is a separate step that can be skipped). Recommended: a workstream-state reconciliation check at every kickoff pre-flight. Concretely a small `tools/workstream_reconcile.py` that takes a workstream identifier, asks the V2 API for that workstream's conversations and their statuses, asks git for commits touching the workstream's deliverable directory, and cross-references — any commit whose deliverable is not referenced by some session record's artifacts_produced is a stale-state signal. Alternatives considered (automatic close-out from the sandbox, V2 desktop app `last_processed_commit_hash` warnings, co-commit hooks) and rejected as either infeasible (sandbox can't reach Doug's local API) or higher-effort with smaller coverage. Flagged at conversation close for Doug's planning-item decision — not added to this payload.

3. Path selection. Three paths presented: (1) adopt the existing c1d007e commit and close out; (2) discard the commit and redo schema design from scratch; (3) audit-first then choose. Recommended Path 3. Doug accepted Path 3.

4. Audit execution. Read crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md, governance-schema-workstream-plan.md, governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md, governance-schema-specs/workstream.md (predecessor 1), governance-schema-specs/conversation.md (predecessor 2), DEC-117 from db-export/decisions.json, sessions SES-048 and SES-049 from db-export/sessions.json, plus the spec file itself in full. Audited against four dimensions: schema-spec methodology guide §7.1 per-spec completeness gate (all nine §3 subsections present and substantively filled, §3.7 ≥10 testable criteria, §3.8 explicit category tags, §3.9 cites required references); §6 cross-spec consistency requirements (identifier prefix style, format, status field naming, status values, default starter, relationship-kind naming, field naming, timestamp inheritance with explicit deviation documented, soft-delete, identifier auto-assignment, API envelope); workstream master plan position-3-of-6 and constraints (RB-NNN working assumption realized, version history and 'in force at' semantics realized); predecessor-spec consistency (workstream.md line 123 declares `workstream_planned_in_reference_book`, verified; conversation.md generic-kinds posture for conversation→reference_book, verified).

5. Audit findings. Conclusion: the spec passes the per-spec completeness gate on every criterion and conforms to the §6 cross-spec consistency requirements with one explicit, justified deviation (base timestamps only for documentary-shaped lifecycles, established as a new cross-spec precedent in §1 and DEC-137). The spec also establishes a well-motivated parent-plus-child versioning model that diverges from charter/status's singleton-with-payload pattern with rationale (file content lives in git/files, database tracks metadata only). One substantive factual error identified: the Change Log v1.0 paragraph at line 21, the DEC-138 description at line 420, and the DEC-117 entry in §3.9.3 at line 438 each named the three `reference_book_kind` enum values that are additions beyond DEC-117 as `workstream_master_plan`, `apply_script`, `session_startup_document`. Per the DEC-117 decision record's verbatim text, those three values are explicitly listed in DEC-117's seven artifacts. The actual three additions beyond DEC-117 — present in the eleven-value enum but absent from DEC-117's seven — are `architecture_document`, `conduct_framework`, `investigation_report`. Doug accepted the audit and authorized Path 1 (correct-and-close-out).

6. Correction execution. Five str_replace operations on `governance-schema-specs/reference_book.md`: (a) update header `Last Updated` to 05-21-26 15:30; (b) bump header `Status` to `Draft v1.1` with annotation; (c) append a v1.1 row to the Revision Control table documenting the correction; (d) prepend a v1.1 paragraph to the Change Log section documenting the correction and update the v1.0 paragraph to be factually correct (architecture_document, conduct_framework, investigation_report); (e) correct DEC-138 description in §3.9.1; (f) correct DEC-117 entry in §3.9.3. All five operations verified — the enum declaration at line 96 (originally line 93 pre-edit) was correct in v1.0 and is unchanged. The two surviving mentions of the wrongly-named triple are now in the v1.1 Revision Control row and v1.1 Change Log paragraph, where they document the correction itself, which is the correct location.

7. Close-out payload and apply prompt authored. This payload (ses_050.json) and the companion apply prompt (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-050.md) drafted in one pass. Identifier check: latest applied SES is SES-049, so SES-050 is next-available; latest DEC is DEC-134, so DEC-135..140 is next-available for the six decisions; no new planning items in this payload (PI-022 covers retroactive backfill including this conversation's records implicitly; the reconciliation-utility planning item is flagged at close-out for Doug's decision but not added).

8. Decisions authored (DEC-135..140 — see the decisions array below):
   - DEC-135: `reference_book` identifier prefix and format — RB-NNN, two-letter form acceptable per DEC-123, kickoff's open-question concern about visual ambiguity resolved by checking remaining-three-conversations' working prefixes (WT, COP, DEP) for confirmed non-collision.
   - DEC-136: `reference_book` versioning model — parent record plus child versions table. Distinct from charter/status's singleton-with-payload pattern because the file content lives in git/files and the database tracks version metadata only. Denormalized current-version pointer fields on the parent stay in sync with child rows via access-layer recompute on any child-table write. Establishes the documentary-entity versioning pattern for the project.
   - DEC-137: `reference_book` documentary-shaped lifecycle with three statuses and base timestamps only. active → archived / superseded transition map with the truly-terminal posture inherited verbatim from workstream and the supersession-requires-edge access-layer rule mirroring workstream's pattern. Establishes new cross-spec precedent: documentary-shaped lifecycles inherit base timestamps only, deviating from DEC-126's per-status-timestamps-for-workflow-shapes precedent. The remaining three governance specs apply the documentary-vs-workflow distinction on their own facts.
   - DEC-138: `reference_book_kind` closed enum with eleven values — covering all seven of DEC-117's named artifact types (product_requirements_document, implementation_plan, workstream_master_plan, methodology_guide, schema_specification, apply_script, session_startup_document) plus three additional types observed in the project's operating history beyond DEC-117 (architecture_document, conduct_framework, investigation_report) plus an `other` sentinel. The seven-versus-three split is documented honestly per the v1.1 correction.
   - DEC-139: `reference_book` field inventory plus file-path semantics plus engagement-scoping plus soft-delete posture. Seven content/classification fields plus three file-pointer fields plus three inherited base timestamps (no per-status timestamps per DEC-137).
