Repo-relative file path with no leading slash and no `..` segments; resolved at use time against the consuming repository. No engagement-scoping flag (per V2's per-engagement isolation, scoping is implicit). Default soft-delete with restore, distinct from `archived` and `superseded` status outcomes.
   - DEC-140: `reference_book` API surface plus version-management sub-endpoints plus master-pane Kind column plus default UI with inline version-history plus sixteen acceptance criteria. Standard 8-endpoint set for the parent plus three version-management sub-endpoints (GET /reference-books/{id}/versions, POST /reference-books/{id}/versions, GET /reference-books/{id}/version-at?as_of=...) realizing the in-force-at-time-T semantics named in the kickoff. List-endpoint filters `kind` and `status` alongside `include_deleted`. Master-pane Kind column as third column. Inline version-history section in the detail pane with Add Version affordance. Sixteen acceptance criteria captured.

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — REFB.** Four letters. Rejected because it adds two characters with no value; the collision concern that motivated the longer form turned out not to exist. **Option (c) — REFBOOK.** Seven letters. Rejected for the same reason at twice the cost. **Option (d) — RBK.** Three letters. Rejected because the K is silent and reads as a typo rather than a meaningful character; RB is the natural abbreviation. **Option (e) — Defer the prefix choice to build-planning.** Rejected because the prefix is needed in the spec body (acceptance criteria reference RB-NNN format) and the spec's per-spec completeness gate requires the prefix locked in §3.1.
after_payload.consequences: Spec guide section 6 collision list extended with RB. The kickoff's open methodology question 2 (reference-book identifier collision risk) is resolved in favour of the two-letter form. The remaining three governance entity conversations (work_ticket, close_out_payload, deposit_event) have first refusal on their own prefix choices; the working prefixes WT, COP, DEP do not collide with RB but each conversation makes its own call. The reference_book table's identifier column has CHECK constraint `^RB-\d{3}$`. The GET /reference-books/next-identifier helper returns the next available RB-NNN value.
after_payload.context: The reference_book entity is the third of six new governance entities designed in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Its identifier prefix sets a small precedent for the cross-spec collision list. The schema-spec methodology guide's section 8 named the working assumption RB and flagged 'Reference-book identifier collision risk: RB is short and may collide visually with future prefixes' as an open methodology question for this conversation to resolve. The existing collision list at this spec's writing spans two-letter (PI, WS), three-letter (DEC, SES, RSK, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT), and four-letter (PROC, CRMC, CONV) prefixes.
after_payload.decision: **Adopt `RB` as the reference book identifier prefix, format `RB-NNN` zero-padded to three digits, server-assigned on POST omission with helper at GET /reference-books/next-identifier.** The two-letter form matches the existing WS and PI short-prefix posture established by DEC-123 (which affirmed two-letter form is acceptable). The collision-risk concern from the spec guide's section 8 is resolved by checking the remaining-three governance entities' working prefixes — WT (work_ticket), COP (close_out_payload), DEP (deposit_event) — none of which begins with R or shares visual shape with RB. The existing REF prefix (references entity) is three letters and structurally distinct; the two-letter RB versus three-letter REF does not introduce visual ambiguity. The longer-form alternatives (REFB, REFBOOK, RBK) add no value beyond length. Spec guide section 6's collision list is extended with RB.
after_payload.rationale: RB is mnemonic, short enough to scan rapidly in tables and references, and unambiguous against the existing prefix set. The two-letter form is consistent with the precedent set by DEC-123 (workstream chose two-letter WS) and DEC-129 (conversation chose four-letter CONV per its own judgment), confirming the spec guide's section 8 working assumption that each downstream conversation makes its own length call within the 2-to-5-letter range. The collision-risk concern named in the spec guide's section 8 turns out to be a false alarm: the remaining three governance entities have working prefixes that do not collide with RB, and the REF / RB distinction reads cleanly because of the visual-shape difference between two and three uppercase letters. The longer alternatives offered no semantic improvement and would have made every reference book identifier two or three characters longer for no payoff.
after_payload.title: reference_book entity identifier prefix and format: RB-NNN, two-letter form, no collision with REF or remaining-three working prefixes

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Versioned-replace modeled on charter and status.** Rejected because the singleton-with-payload pattern bundles content storage into the version row; for reference books the content lives in git/files, and bundling would create a second source of truth that could drift. **Option (c) — Embedded version array on the parent record.** Rejected because array manipulation in SQL is inefficient and the version-at query would require array scanning rather than indexed lookup. **Option (d) — Content-addressed snapshots.** Rejected as premature complexity; the project has no fork semantics need today, and the schema admits a future retrofit additively. **Option (e) — Link to git commits with no in-database versioning.** Rejected because every version query would require git subprocess calls and the V2 database would not be the source of truth for governance state, contradicting DEC-121.
after_payload.consequences: Two database tables: `reference_books` for the parent records plus `reference_book_versions` for the child rows. ON DELETE CASCADE foreign key from child to parent so a hard-delete of the parent cascade-removes versions; soft-delete on the parent does not affect children (their rows persist, supporting restore-recovery). Two denormalized pointer columns on the parent — reference_book_current_version_label and reference_book_current_version_date — maintained by access-layer recompute. Three version-management sub-endpoints (GET /versions, POST /versions, GET /version-at?as_of=...) realizing the operational queries. The (reference_book_id, version_label) unique constraint admits two reference books each having their own v1.0 row. No DELETE on individual version rows in this release; versions are append-only in practice. The model establishes the documentary-entity versioning pattern for the remaining three schemas to consider; work_ticket is single-use (no versioning), close_out_payload is single-shot (no versioning expected), deposit_event is append-only (no versioning).
after_payload.context: The kickoff prompt named versioning as the principal design question for the reference_book entity. Reference book files accumulate versions over their lifetimes — the methodology workstream master plan has v1.0 (05-11-26) and v1.1 (05-12-26); the methodology guide has v1.0 and v1.1; the multi-engagement architecture document has multiple versions. The schema needs to represent that version history in a way that supports the operational query 'what version was in force at the time of session NNN' (in-force-at-time-T semantics). The kickoff named four candidate models: (a) versioned-replace modeled on charter and status, where the reference_book record IS the current version and previous versions live in a parallel reference_book_version table; (b) embedded version array, where a reference_book record holds its current state plus a JSON array of version history; (c) content-addressed snapshots, where each version is a separate record with a content hash; (d) link to git commits, where no in-database versioning is done and git provides the history.
after_payload.decision: **Adopt the parent record plus child versions table versioning model.** A reference_book record carries the parent metadata (identifier, title, description, kind, status, file path) plus two denormalized current-version pointer fields (reference_book_current_version_label, reference_book_current_version_date). A sibling table reference_book_versions carries one row per known version of a reference book, addressed by (reference_book_id, version_label) unique. Each version row carries version_label, version_date, optional version_summary, plus base timestamps. The parent's denormalized pointer fields stay in sync with the child rows via access-layer recompute on any child-table write — the row with the highest version_date wins as 'current'. The in-force-at-time-T query is realized by GET /reference-books/{id}/version-at?as_of=... returning the row with the highest version_date ≤ as_of. Establishes the documentary-entity versioning pattern, distinct from charter/status's singleton-with-payload pattern.
after_payload.rationale: The parent-plus-child model is the lightest shape that captures version history faithfully for a documentary entity. The charter/status singleton-with-payload pattern (Decision 29) embeds the full payload in each version row, which is appropriate when the database is the source of truth for content. For reference books, the content lives in git/files — the file path string and the project's existing git history together carry the content; the database carries only version metadata (label, date, summary). The embedded JSON array alternative would require array manipulation for every version operation and would not support the version-at query efficiently. Content-addressed snapshots add complexity (content hashing, fork semantics) for benefits that real-use signal does not yet justify. Link-to-git-commits would push every query through git subprocess calls — heavier than the database, and disagrees with DEC-121's single-source-of-truth principle for governance state. The parent-plus-child model supports the version-at query directly with a single SQL clause and stays additively retrofittable (a future release can add a version_payload JSON or BLOB column on the child table if FTS or full-content queries become a real need).
after_payload.title: reference_book versioning model: parent record plus child versions table, distinct from charter/status singleton-with-payload pattern

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Follow DEC-126 verbatim with three per-status timestamp columns (archived_at, superseded_at).** Rejected because per-state duration is not an operationally meaningful query for documentary entities; the columns would consume storage with no value. **Option (c) — Use append-only posture instead of soft-delete-with-restore.** Rejected because reference book records can legitimately be created with errors (wrong title, wrong file path, wrong kind) and edit-and-correct is the appropriate flow; append-only fits decisions, sessions, and deposit_events where the rows are transactional fact, not metadata. **Option (d) — Add only one per-state timestamp (terminal_at).** Rejected as inconsistent — either follow DEC-126 fully or deviate to base-timestamps-only with a clean principle. The mixed posture would be harder to reason about.
after_payload.consequences: Reference book records have eleven columns total (seven content/classification fields + two denormalized version pointers + three inherited base timestamps). Server-side enforcement of truly-terminal posture plus supersession-requires-edge rule; access-layer rules to test. New cross-spec precedent established for the remaining three governance specs — work_ticket inherits per-status timestamps (workflow-shaped consumption lifecycle), close_out_payload's choice depends on whether the conversation lands a workflow or append-only model, deposit_event almost certainly uses base timestamps plus one applied_at (append-only fact). PI-022's backfill pass does not need to reconstruct historical per-status timestamps for reference book records (none exist); the backfill records the bare metadata and the existing file's Revision Control table provides the version_date values for the child versions table.
after_payload.context: The schema-spec methodology guide's DEC-126 precedent established that workflow-shaped governance lifecycles carry per-status lifecycle timestamps (e.g., workstream's workstream_started_at, workstream_completed_at; conversation's six per-status columns per DEC-131). Reference book's lifecycle is structurally different from workflow: its status states (active, archived, superseded) capture documentary outcomes rather than workflow progress. The conversation surfaced the question: does reference_book carry per-status lifecycle timestamps following the DEC-126 precedent, or does it deviate, and if so on what principle?
after_payload.decision: **Adopt a three-status documentary lifecycle (active → archived / superseded) with base timestamps only (created_at, updated_at, deleted_at — no per-status lifecycle timestamps).** The lifecycle inherits two access-layer rules from workstream and conversation: (a) truly-terminal posture — both terminal statuses (archived, superseded) reject all outgoing transitions including between terminal states; (b) supersession-requires-edge rule — setting status to superseded requires an outgoing references-table edge of kind `supersedes` to another reference_book record, returning HTTP 422 {error: supersession_requires_successor_edge} on violation. Establishes new cross-spec precedent: **documentary-shaped lifecycles inherit base timestamps only**, deviating explicitly from DEC-126's per-status-timestamps-for-workflow-shapes precedent. The remaining three governance specs (work_ticket, close_out_payload, deposit_event) apply the documentary-vs-workflow distinction on their own facts: work_ticket has a workflow-shaped consumption lifecycle and inherits per-status timestamps; close_out_payload is likely workflow-shaped (drafted → applied) or append-only after apply; deposit_event is append-only with one timestamp. None of the three is documentary-shaped.
after_payload.rationale: Reference book's status states do not carry per-state duration semantics worth querying. 'How long was the methodology guide in active status before being archived' is not an operationally meaningful question — what matters is when the document existed (already captured by created_at) and when its role ended (already captured by the archive/supersede transition's updated_at, with full before-and-after detail in the change_log table). Status transitions are also infrequent — a typical reference book moves from active to archived or superseded at most a few times in its lifetime, vs. a workstream or conversation which moves through multiple non-terminal states routinely. Adding six per-status timestamp columns would consume storage with no query-time benefit. The deviation from DEC-126 needs to be principled, not ad-hoc; the principle is documentary-vs-workflow, which is a clean structural distinction and a precedent the remaining three specs can apply explicitly. The truly-terminal posture and supersession-requires-edge rule are inherited because they apply regardless of workflow-vs-documentary — both express integrity constraints on status-relationship consistency.
after_payload.title: reference_book documentary-shaped lifecycle with three statuses and base timestamps only; new cross-spec precedent for documentary-vs-workflow distinction

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Open-string kind field.** Rejected because it admits typos and label drift, degrading filterability and master-pane scannability. **Option (c) — Closed enum with only DEC-117's seven values.** Rejected because three legitimate kinds beyond DEC-117 (architecture_document, conduct_framework, investigation_report) are observed in the project's history; using `other` for them would degrade filtering. **Option (d) — Closed enum with twenty-plus values speculating future kinds.** Rejected as YAGNI; admit kinds as real-use signal surfaces them. **Option (e) — Per-kind tables (one table per kind, polymorphic association).** Rejected as premature schema specialization; the kinds share enough metadata (title, description, file_path, status) that one table with a discriminator is correct.
after_payload.consequences: Reference_book table's kind column has a CHECK constraint enumerating the eleven values, plus a matching domain-validated set in the access layer. The master-pane Kind column renders the enum values as friendly labels (e.g., 'Product Requirements Document' for `product_requirements_document`) per the UI rendering note in spec §3.6.2. List endpoint supports `?kind=<value>` filter for at-a-glance subset queries. Adding a twelfth kind in a future release is a one-line vocab.py update plus one Alembic migration on the CHECK constraint; the `other` sentinel covers the gap until then. PI-022's backfill pass assigns one of the eleven values to each historical reference book record; ambiguous cases (a document that legitimately fits multiple kinds) pick the dominant kind and use `other` only as a last resort per the spec §3.8.2 backfill guidance.
after_payload.context: DEC-117 named seven reference book artifact types as the family-1 scope: workstream master plan, methodology guide, schema specification, Product Requirements Document, implementation plan, session-startup entry document, and apply script. The conversation surfaced the question of whether reference_book_kind should be a closed enum (with future additions requiring migration) or an open string, and whether the enum should cover only DEC-117's seven or extend to additional types the project's operating history surfaces. The project's history at this conversation's writing includes documents that legitimately fit reference-book semantics but were not named in DEC-117's seven: architecture documents (multi-engagement-architecture.md), conduct framework documents (PRDs/process/conduct/*.md), and investigation reports (multi-tenancy-routing-investigation-report.md, methodology-schemas-cbm-paper-test-findings.md).
after_payload.decision: **Adopt `reference_book_kind` as a closed eleven-value enum**: product_requirements_document, implementation_plan, workstream_master_plan, methodology_guide, architecture_document, schema_specification, conduct_framework, investigation_report, apply_script, session_startup_document, other. The first seven values listed above plus apply_script and session_startup_document map directly to DEC-117's seven named artifact types. The three additions beyond DEC-117 — `architecture_document`, `conduct_framework`, `investigation_report` — capture documentary types observed in the project's operating history that legitimately fit family-1 semantics. The `other` value is the sentinel for documents that do not fit the named categories; the intent is that real use will surface additional kinds, added by extending the enum in a future migration. The enum is closed because the spec wants validation to catch misclassification at insert time; the open-string alternative would admit typos and inconsistent labels.
after_payload.rationale: Closing the enum lets the access layer reject typos and inconsistent labels at insert time, which protects the master-pane Kind column's scan-ability (no 'product requirements document' vs 'product-requirements-document' vs 'PRD' confusion). Eleven values is small enough to enumerate in a combo box without scrolling. The three additions beyond DEC-117 are real categories observed today; deferring them would force consultants to use the `other` sentinel for documents that have a clear category, which would degrade master-pane filtering. The `other` sentinel exists as an escape hatch for unforeseen kinds and as a signal-collection mechanism — if `other` rows accumulate, the project knows a new kind is needed. Open-string alternatives were rejected because they admit drift; the open-pattern-and-validate alternative (regex-validated kind strings) was rejected because the validation rules would be no easier to maintain than an enum migration.
after_payload.title: reference_book_kind closed eleven-value enum covering DEC-117's seven artifact types plus three observed additions plus an other sentinel

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Field inventory with reference_book_author column** — rejected for minimum-viable scope; sole-author working pattern; one migration away. **Field inventory with reference_book_engagement_scope column** — rejected because per-engagement isolation makes scoping implicit. **File-path validation including filesystem-existence check** — rejected because it would brittle-couple records to the current commit; a future build prompt may add a separate 'find broken pointers' maintenance query. **Title uniqueness globally (across engagements)** — rejected because logical documents legitimately exist in multiple engagements (the methodology guide as a reference book record in the CRMBuilder dogfood engagement and in the CBM client engagement); per-engagement uniqueness matches the isolation principle. **Append-only posture instead of soft-delete** — rejected because reference book records are organizing metadata, not transactional fact; correction via edit is appropriate.
after_payload.consequences: Reference_books table has eleven columns (seven content/classification + two denormalized version pointers + three base timestamps). File-path validation runs at the access layer on POST/PUT/PATCH. Title-uniqueness check runs at the access layer with case-insensitive comparison scoped to the engagement (the per-engagement database boundary makes this trivially correct). Soft-delete on the parent leaves child version rows in place; restore reactivates the parent and the children are still there. PI-022's backfill pass populates the seven content/classification fields for historical reference book records; the backfill policy on which historical documents become reference_book records is PI-022's call per spec §3.8.2.
after_payload.context: Routine items batched together at the close of the reference_book schema-design conversation: the field inventory beyond identifier, status, and timestamps; the deliberately-excluded fields; the file-path semantics; the engagement-scoping question; and soft-delete versus append-only posture. Each item follows defaults from the schema-spec methodology guide; no significant deviations were warranted, but the per-engagement-isolation question and the file-path validation rules warrant explicit decision recording because they shape access-layer behavior.
after_payload.decision: **(a) Field inventory** is seven content/classification fields (reference_book_identifier, reference_book_title, reference_book_description, reference_book_notes, reference_book_kind, reference_book_status, reference_book_file_path) plus two denormalized version-pointer columns (reference_book_current_version_label, reference_book_current_version_date) plus three inherited base timestamps (created_at, updated_at, deleted_at). No per-status lifecycle timestamps per DEC-137. Notes is a consultant scratchpad — internal, not part of the user-facing summary. **(b) Deliberately excluded under minimum-viable scope**: reference_book_author, reference_book_owner (Doug is sole author/owner today; one migration away if multi-author engagements emerge), reference_book_engagement_scope (per-engagement isolation makes scoping implicit per DEC-115/DEC-116). **(c) File-path semantics**: repo-relative path string with validation rules — non-empty trimmed, no leading slash, no `..` segments, no scheme prefix (https://, file://). Path is not validated for existence at write time (file may be authored separately and the record updated to point at it); a future build prompt may add a 'check path resolves' verification. The path is resolved at use time against the consuming repository's root — typically the crmbuilder repo for dogfood records and the client repo for engagement-specific records. **(d) Title uniqueness**: case-insensitive unique within the engagement. **(e) Soft-delete posture**: default — soft-delete with restore — explicitly distinct from `archived` status (a lifecycle outcome) and `superseded` status (a successor-replaced outcome). Soft-deleting the parent does not affect child version rows; their rows persist for restore-recovery. **(f) Storage-level length caps**: none on text fields, matching workstream and conversation precedents.
after_payload.rationale: Field inventory follows minimum-viable per the methodology guide; deferred fields are listed in spec §3.8.3 with growth-condition language so future signal can justify additions. Author/owner omitted because Doug is the project's sole author and adding the columns prematurely would require a backfill policy without operational value. Engagement-scoping flag omitted because V2's per-engagement isolation (DEC-115/DEC-116) makes the scoping implicit — the same logical document (e.g., the methodology guide) may exist as a reference book record in each engagement that cites it. File-path validation rules prevent common errors (absolute paths breaking on a different operator's machine, `..` segments allowing path traversal, scheme prefixes that aren't filesystem paths) without committing to filesystem-existence validation that would brittle-couple the database to the current commit. Title uniqueness scoped to the engagement matches the per-engagement isolation principle. Soft-delete fits because reference book records are organizing metadata, not transactional fact; create-with-error and correct-via-edit is the appropriate flow.
after_payload.title: reference_book field inventory, repo-relative file path semantics, no engagement-scoping flag, soft-delete posture

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **API surface without version-management sub-endpoints (everything via PATCH on the parent)** — rejected because the version-management operations have distinct semantics (append a child row, recompute denormalized pointer) and forcing them through the parent's PATCH would conflate the parent's lifecycle with the child's. **Master pane without Kind column** — rejected because kind is the most operationally meaningful filter for the reference book catalog and forcing detail-pane open to see kind is high-friction. **Detail pane with version-history as a separate dialog** — rejected because the version list is the entity's distinctive shape; making it a subordinate panel keeps it visible alongside the rest of the metadata. **Acceptance criteria count of ten (gate minimum)** — would have skipped the version-management-specific criteria; rejected because the version round-trip and in-force-at-time-T query are the entity's distinctive behaviors and deserve dedicated criteria.
after_payload.consequences: Eleven REST endpoints (eight parent + three child) to test; sixteen test cases per the acceptance criteria. The version-management sub-endpoints route through the access layer's recompute logic for the denormalized current-version pointer on the parent. Master-pane Kind column requires a small extension to ListDetailPanel's column-rendering (the column has a friendly-label mapping for the eleven enum values). The inline version-history section in the detail pane is a new UI pattern relative to workstream and conversation but follows naturally from this entity's versioning model — work_ticket and the remaining entities will not need it. The spec's per-spec completeness gate (spec guide §7.1) passes: all nine subsections of §3 substantively filled, sixteen acceptance criteria (gate requires ten), explicit category tags on open questions, cross-references include workstream plan, spec guide, workstream.md and conversation.md (the predecessor specifications), foundation decisions DEC-117..122, and the twelve prior-conversation decisions DEC-123..134 from SES-048 and SES-049 as inherited precedents.
after_payload.context: Routine items batched together at the close of the reference_book schema-design conversation: the API surface beyond the standard endpoint set, the version-management sub-endpoints realizing the in-force-at-time-T semantics named in the kickoff, the UI layout including the master-pane Kind column and the detail-pane version-history section, and the count and shape of testable acceptance criteria. The version-management endpoints and the version-history UI section are natural additions following from DEC-136's parent-plus-child versioning model; the Kind column is a natural addition because kind is the most operationally meaningful classification for browsing the reference book catalog. None of these is architecturally novel relative to the schema-spec methodology guide's defaults but each warrants explicit decision recording because they shape API surface and UI behavior.
after_payload.decision: **(a) API surface** is the standard 8-endpoint set per spec guide §3.5 (GET list with `?include_deleted=true` plus `?kind=` plus `?status=` filters; GET single; POST create; PUT replace; PATCH update; DELETE soft-delete; POST restore; GET next-identifier). **(b) Three version-management sub-endpoints**: GET /reference-books/{id}/versions (list versions in version_date descending), POST /reference-books/{id}/versions (record a new version), GET /reference-books/{id}/version-at?as_of=... (return version in force at instant). No DELETE on individual version rows in this release (versions are append-only in practice; the build-planning conversation may add a DELETE if real need surfaces). **(c) Identifier auto-assignment**: default server-side on POST omission for the parent record; version rows do not have prefixed identifiers (addressed by (reference_book_id, version_label)). **(d) Master pane** has five columns: Identifier, Title, Kind, Version (denormalized from current-version pointer), Updated. Default sort by Identifier ascending. Kind selector and status selector combo boxes in the panel toolbar drive `?kind=` and `?status=` filters. **(e) Detail pane** has nine sections in order: identifier (read-only), title, description, notes (collapsible internal section), kind combo, file_path, status combo, version-history section (subordinate panel with Add Version affordance, current version visually distinguished), ReferencesSection. **(f) CRUD dialogs** match the user-interface version 0.3 governance-entity patterns: EntityCrudDialog subclass for create/edit, EntityCrudDeleteDialog with edge-text confirmation for delete. The version-history section is hidden in create mode (one or more versions may be supplied via a `versions` array in the request body) and fully interactive in edit mode. **(g) Acceptance criteria**: sixteen testable statements captured in spec §3.7 (gate requires ten; workstream produced sixteen; this spec follows workstream's count, focused on schema migration, validation rules, lifecycle enforcement, version round-trip, in-force-at-time-T query, access-layer signatures, REST endpoint behavior, identifier auto-assignment, soft-delete and restore, sidebar position, and one end-to-end backfill exemplar against the governance-schema-workstream-plan reference book).
after_payload.rationale: Standard endpoint set inherited from the methodology guide; no deviations warranted because the entity's operations follow the standard CRUD-plus-restore pattern. The three version-management sub-endpoints are the smallest set that realizes the operational queries named in the kickoff (list versions, record a new version, query in-force-at). The Kind column is a natural addition because kind is the most operationally meaningful classification for browsing the reference book catalog — consultants ask 'what's the PRD for v0.6' or 'what's the methodology guide for governance entity schema-design.' The inline version-history section is the entity's distinctive UI shape; making it a subordinate panel in the detail pane (rather than a separate dialog) keeps the version list visible alongside the rest of the record's metadata. Sixteen acceptance criteria match workstream's count and fully satisfy the spec guide's per-spec completeness gate (§7.1 requires ten); each is concrete and testable, ready for translation into test cases at build-planning time.
after_payload.title: reference_book API surface plus three version-management sub-endpoints plus default UI with Kind column and inline version-history section plus sixteen acceptance criteria

after_payload.description:
Build and integrate `crmbuilder/tools/workstream_reconcile.py` to prevent state-drift between git-committed workstream deliverables and the V2 governance database at session-startup time.

**Why this exists.** SES-050 (the reference_book entity schema-design conversation) opened against `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-reference-book.md` as a fresh schema-design conversation, but the first pre-flight check found that the deliverable (`governance-schema-specs/reference_book.md`, 467 lines at v1.0) had already been drafted and committed at `c1d007e` on 05-21-26 14:55 by a prior Claude.ai sandbox session whose close-out was never run. The V2 database had no SES-050 or DEC-135..140 yet recorded; git was one commit ahead of origin/main with the spec on it, but the governance database was silently out of sync. Without the manual `git log` check during SES-050's pre-flight, the schema-design conversation would have either overwritten 467 lines of completed work or duplicated effort to produce a parallel spec. The root cause is a two-system-of-record gap: git holds committed deliverables; the V2 database holds governance state; the bridge — manual close-out apply — is a separate step that can be skipped silently.

**Discharge conditions** (all must be met to mark PI-023 Resolved):

1. `crmbuilder/tools/workstream_reconcile.py` exists, runs from any working directory, and accepts a workstream identifier as input. (Once the V2 workstream entity ships per the governance-schema workstream's build phase, the tool may accept a workstream record directly; until then, the workstream identifier is supplied as a string with the deliverable directory implied by convention.)

2. The utility queries the V2 API for the workstream's conversations (via the sessions table for pre-workstream-entity workstreams, via the workstream entity once it ships) and collects each session's `artifacts_produced` field.

3. The utility queries git for commits touching the workstream's deliverable directory since the workstream began (deliverable directory is configurable per workstream; for the governance workstream it is `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/` plus `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/` plus `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/` plus `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-*.md`).

4. The utility cross-references the two: any commit whose deliverable path is not referenced in any session record's `artifacts_produced` text is reported as a stale-state signal, with the commit hash, date, and path.

5. A one-line addition to `crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md` under "Session orientation protocol" instructing V2-engaged sessions to run the reconciliation check at session start.

6. A one-line addition to the kickoff-prompt template (and to each remaining per-entity schema-design kickoff prompt's Pre-flight section — work_ticket, close_out_payload, deposit_event — and to the kickoff-prompt-generator pattern for future workstreams) instructing pre-flight to invoke the utility against the workstream identifier before the first architectural question.

7. The utility is run once against the current governance-entity-schema-design workstream and reports clean (after SES-050's apply lands; the c1d007e commit's deliverable is now referenced in SES-050's `artifacts_produced`, so no stale-state signal should surface).

**Why this is preventative not a fix to current state.** SES-050 already landed and the SES-050 session record's `artifacts_produced` text includes `governance-schema-specs/reference_book.md` and the v1.1 patch. The current state is consistent; the utility prevents the recurrence of the class of issue, not a current data inconsistency.

**Alternatives considered.** (a) Automatic close-out from the sandbox to Doug's local API — infeasible because the sandbox cannot reach `http://127.0.0.1:8765` on Doug's local machine. (b) V2 desktop app showing "git ahead" warnings — would require changing the V2 app and storing a `last_processed_commit_hash` somewhere; heavier than needed and only surfaces the gap when Doug happens to open the app. (c) Co-commit enforcement (a hook that rejects spec commits without paired apply scripts) — fragile and easy to circumvent, and does not catch the "applied locally in sandbox but never pushed" variant. The reconciliation utility addresses every variant — committed-not-pushed, pushed-not-applied, applied-locally-not-mirrored — because it always derives state from origin and the V2 API together.

**Priority.** Routine — the utility is preventative; no current data drift is blocking work. Discharge timing is build-planning conversation's call within the governance-entity-schema-design workstream, or earlier if Doug authorizes a tooling slice between schema-design conversations.
after_payload.title: Workstream-state reconciliation utility at kickoff pre-flight to prevent git-vs-database state drift

after_payload.artifacts_produced:
Three files committed to PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ in the crmbuilder repo during this conversation:

(1) governance-schema-specs/work_ticket.md (471 lines, v1.0). Fourth per-entity schema specification in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Conforms to governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md. Establishes the work_ticket entity type for V2 storage with six content/classification fields (identifier, title, description, notes, kind, status), one file-pointer field (file_path), and seven timestamp columns (three inherited base plus four per-status lifecycle: work_ticket_ready_at, work_ticket_consumed_at, work_ticket_cancelled_at, work_ticket_superseded_at). Five-status workflow-shaped lifecycle (drafted → ready → consumed plus terminals cancelled and superseded) with truly-terminal terminals, the supersession-requires-edge rule inherited from DEC-125, and a new consumed-requires-edge rule establishing the inverse-direction terminal-edge-requirement precedent. Single-use enforcement rule (at most one inbound consumption edge per work_ticket, regardless of status) as the schema-level realization of DEC-117's family-2 definition. Four-value closed kind enum (kickoff_prompt, claude_code_prompt, ad_hoc_prompt, other) with the sentinel as documented escape hatch. Standard 8-endpoint API set with `?kind=` and `?status=` filter parameters. Default UI layout with master-pane Kind column (third column position) and Status filter combo in the toolbar. Default soft-delete with restore. Sixteen acceptance criteria. No new relationship-kind vocabulary required — the entity type itself was already named by conversation.md §3.3.4 and the inbound consumption edge by conversation.md's `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` registration. Pre-existed as an untracked sandbox file at session open; adopted without modification after audit.

(2) close-out-payloads/ses_051.json (new — this payload). One session (SES-051), six decisions (DEC-141..146), zero planning items, seven references (six decided_in plus one is_about SES-051 → PI-022).

(3) prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-051.md (new — this payload's apply prompt). Idempotent apply targeting the CRMBUILDER engagement.

Via this payload's apply prompt, the following records land in the CRMBUILDER engagement database:

(4) SES-051 — this session record.
(5) DEC-141 — WT identifier prefix and format.
(6) DEC-142 — boundary discipline with reference_book (intent-at-creation, no re-categorization, file_path coexistence).
(7) DEC-143 — workflow-shaped lifecycle, five statuses, truly-terminal terminals, consumed-requires-edge rule (new cross-spec precedent), supersession-requires-edge rule.
(8) DEC-144 — single-use enforcement (at most one inbound consumption edge).
(9) DEC-145 — field inventory with four per-status lifecycle timestamps; declines tentative `work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation` kind; declines typed `work_ticket_reads_reference_book` kind.
(10) DEC-146 — API surface; UI defaults with Kind column and Status filter combo; soft-delete posture; closed four-value kind enum; sixteen acceptance criteria.
(11) Six decided_in references (DEC-141..146 → SES-051) plus one is_about reference (SES-051 → PI-022) mirroring SES-048/049/050's pattern of linking each schema-design conversation to the retroactive-backfill planning item.

No source code modified — this is a schema-design conversation producing one specification only. Vocabulary changes, schema migrations, and access-layer methods are aggregated by the build-planning conversation after all six schemas exist.
after_payload.conversation_reference: Claude.ai audit-and-adopt conversation for the work_ticket entity schema — fourth per-entity schema-design conversation in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Opened against PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-work-ticket.md. The kickoff specified the standard schema-design cadence (one architectural decision at a time, drive toward governance-schema-specs/work_ticket.md). Doug's first instruction in the conversation directed an explicit mode override: 'confirm. then process entire prompt using your default decisions, and don't ask for feedback until all is completed.' The mode shifted from interactive ARCHITECTURE to autonomous-completion against the kickoff's task statement. On the first pre-flight inspection, the deliverable file (PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/work_ticket.md, 471 lines, dated 05-21-26 18:00 in its header) was present in the local sandbox clone as an untracked file — authored by a prior Claude.ai sandbox session whose work product was never committed to origin/main. This differs from SES-050's audit-and-finalize posture (where the prior file was already at origin) — here the file existed only in sandbox state. The conversation transitioned to audit-and-adopt mode: structured audit pass of the existing untracked draft against the schema spec guide §7.1 completeness gate, §6 cross-spec consistency requirements, the workstream master plan's position-4-of-6 constraints, DEC-117's family-2 classification, the predecessor specs (workstream.md, conversation.md, reference_book.md) declarations the spec relies on, and the kickoff's explicit architectural-question inventory. The audit concluded the draft is substantively complete, methodologically disciplined, internally consistent, and passes every completeness and consistency criterion without correction. No content changes were required. The draft was adopted as v1.0, committed to origin/main, and the close-out payload and apply prompt authored. No transcript preserved per DEC-025.
after_payload.in_flight_at_end: Next workstream conversation: close_out_payload entity schema design (fifth per-entity schema-design conversation, of six). Opens against PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-close-out-payload.md. That conversation produces governance-schema-specs/close_out_payload.md and inherits the six cross-spec precedents now in force: the three from workstream (references-edge over foreign-key for parent-child governance relationships, per-status lifecycle timestamps for workflow-shaped lifecycles, terminal-states-are-terminal discipline), the one from conversation (typed sequencing edges introduced when entity-family frequency justifies), the one from reference_book (documentary-shaped lifecycles inherit base timestamps only — workflow-shape per-status timestamps remain the default), and the one new precedent from this conversation (terminal-state consumption requires the inbound consumption edge — the inverse-direction analogue of supersession-requires-edge). The close_out_payload entity is structurally most directly related to this conversation's new precedent: its anticipated `applied` terminal status is the next case of an external-act-of-consumption-defined terminal, and the inbound edge from a deposit_event record naming the apply event is the parallel of work_ticket's inbound `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` edge. After close_out_payload.md lands, one more schema-design conversation follows (deposit_event). After all six schema specifications exist, the build-planning conversation opens against governance-schema-build-planning-kickoff.md and produces the integrating Product Requirements Document, implementation plan, per-slice build prompts, and the consolidated vocab.py update aggregating relationship-kind additions from all six specs. Multi-tenancy routing fix slice B and the Cleveland Business Mentors redo Phase 1 conversation continue in parallel as independent workstreams per DEC-122.
after_payload.title: Work_ticket entity schema designed; fourth per-entity governance schema specification produced (audit-and-adopt)
after_payload.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Design the `work_ticket` entity type schema for V2's storage layer. Drive a structured architectural discussion that produces one deliverable: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/work_ticket.md — the complete schema specification per the template in governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md. Cadence matches the methodology workstream's schema-design conversations and the user-interface planning conversations: structured architectural discussion driven one decision at a time, building toward the specification section by section. At conversation close: decisions written via direct API as decision records; any deferred items written as planning items; one session record written by Doug through the V2 desktop New Session dialog per the session-record-at-close pattern."

Topics worked through:

1. Pre-flight orientation. Sparse-cloned the crmbuilder repo state at session open. Confirmed `crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md` is the session-startup entry per Doug's confirmation. `git pull --rebase origin main` reported the local clone up to date; HEAD at `31e7afa` (PI-023 apply commit). On the first `git ls-files` and `git status` check of `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/`, found that the three predecessor specs (workstream.md, conversation.md, reference_book.md) are tracked at origin, while `work_ticket.md` (471 lines, dated 05-21-26 18:00, 88KB) was present in the sandbox working directory as an untracked file — authored by a prior Claude.ai sandbox session whose work product was never committed. Cross-checked the V2 db-export: decisions through DEC-140, sessions through SES-050, planning items through PI-023. No DEC-141..146 or SES-051 yet. Surfaced the audit-and-adopt context.

2. Mode override and posture. Doug's first instruction was 'confirm. then process entire prompt using your default decisions, and don't ask for feedback until all is completed.' This switched the conversation from interactive ARCHITECTURE-mode one-decision-at-a-time cadence to autonomous-completion. The audit-and-adopt posture for the pre-existing untracked draft follows naturally from this mode plus the substantive completeness of the draft on inspection.

3. Audit execution. Read crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md, governance-schema-workstream-plan.md, governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md, the three predecessor schema specs in full, DEC-117 from db-export/decisions.json, sessions SES-048/049/050 from db-export/sessions.json (predecessor schema-design conversations), the ses_050.json close-out payload as template, and the work_ticket.md draft itself in full. Audited against four dimensions: (a) schema-spec methodology guide §7.1 per-spec completeness gate — all nine §3 subsections present and substantively filled (§3.1 identity, §3.2 fields with six subsections, §3.3 relationships with four subsections, §3.4 lifecycle with seven subsections, §3.5 API surface with two subsections, §3.6 user-interface with six subsections, §3.7 acceptance criteria with sixteen testable statements, §3.8 open questions with three category sub-buckets, §3.9 cross-references with five subsections), structure matches reference_book.md and conversation.md 1:1 with the appropriate workflow-vs-documentary deviations applied; (b) §6 cross-spec consistency requirements — identifier prefix style (`WT`, two-letter, parallel to WS/PI), format (`WT-NNN`), status field naming (`work_ticket_status`), enum values with `drafted` starter status, source-first relationship-kind naming (DEC-048), parent-prefix field naming (DEC-046), per-status lifecycle timestamps per DEC-126 (workflow-shaped lifecycle), supersession-requires-edge per DEC-125, soft-delete posture per V2 base behavior, server-side identifier auto-assignment per DEC-128, and V2 `{data, meta, errors}` envelope all conformant; (c) workstream master plan position-4-of-6 constraints — the spec inherits five cross-spec precedents named in §1, establishes one new precedent (terminal-state consumption requires the inbound consumption edge — the inverse of the supersession-requires-edge pattern), and contributes zero new relationship-kind vocabulary (only the entity type itself, already named by conversation.md); (d) predecessor-spec consistency — conversation.md's pre-declared `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` outbound kind (line 180) is correctly inherited as work_ticket's inbound consumption edge, the tentative kind name `work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation` (named in conversation.md §3.3.2 as candidate) is declined as redundant per DEC-132's tentative-kind-name resolution precedent, and the declined typed read-list kind `work_ticket_reads_reference_book` is justified by DEC-133's frequency-justified test.

4. Audit findings. The draft passes the per-spec completeness gate on every criterion and conforms to the §6 cross-spec consistency requirements without deviation. The workflow-shaped lifecycle (five statuses with three terminals) is the appropriate counterpart to reference_book's documentary-shaped lifecycle, with the documentary-vs-workflow distinction settled by DEC-137 applied on work_ticket's own facts: work_ticket is workflow-shaped (operational transitions are real-time-meaningful events asked about by consultants), so per-status timestamps per DEC-126 apply. The new cross-spec precedent — consumed-requires-edge — is well-motivated as the inbound-direction analogue of the outbound supersession-requires-edge rule from DEC-125 (and structurally parallel to conversation.md's outbound complete-requires-session-edge from DEC-131); the generalization to 'terminal-state-edge requirement, direction determined by the relationship's canonical verb orientation' is the principled framing the remaining schemas (close_out_payload's anticipated `applied` terminal) inherit. The single-use enforcement rule (at most one inbound `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` edge, regardless of status) is the schema-level realization of DEC-117's family-2 definition and is what distinguishes work_ticket from reference_book at the schema level. The boundary-with-reference_book discipline (intent-at-creation classification, no in-place re-categorization, file_path may coexist across both tables) cleanly resolves the kickoff's explicit three-way boundary question. Sixteen acceptance criteria captured (gate requires ten); each is concrete and testable. No substantive correction required; the draft is adopted as v1.0 without changes.

5. Close-out payload and apply prompt authored. This payload (ses_051.json) and the companion apply prompt (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-051.md) drafted in one pass. Identifier check: latest applied SES is SES-050, so SES-051 is next-available; latest DEC is DEC-140, so DEC-141..146 is next-available for the six decisions; no new planning items in this payload (PI-022 covers retroactive backfill including this conversation's records; PI-023 covers the workstream-state reconciliation utility, applied at commit 31e7afa).

6. Decisions authored (DEC-141..146 — see the decisions array below):
   - DEC-141: work_ticket identifier prefix and format — WT-NNN, two-letter form per DEC-123 precedent, no collision with WS (differs in second character with no visual-shape ambiguity) or any other existing/pending prefix, working-assumption confirmed over WKT/TIX/TKT alternatives.
   - DEC-142: Boundary discipline with reference_book — intent-at-creation classification, no in-place re-categorization, same file_path may coexist as work_ticket_file_path and reference_book_file_path across the two tables (each provides the governance view appropriate to its lifecycle). The methodology workstream master-plan boundary case raised in the kickoff (`ui-v0.4-planning-prompt.md`, kickoff for SES-011 now retroactively cited by successor planning docs) resolves as a work_ticket; the retroactive citations are archival mention, not authoritative reads.
   - DEC-143: work_ticket workflow-shaped lifecycle with five statuses (drafted, ready, consumed, cancelled, superseded), truly-terminal terminals inherited from DEC-125, supersession-requires-edge rule inherited from DEC-125, and a new consumed-requires-edge access-layer rule mirroring the inverse pattern. Establishes the new cross-spec precedent: terminal-state consumption requires the inbound consumption edge (the inverse direction of supersession-requires-edge); generalizes to 'any governance entity with a terminal state defined by an external act of consumption or application requires the inbound edge naming the consumer or applier to be present.' Close_out_payload's anticipated `applied` terminal is the next case.
   - DEC-144: work_ticket single-use enforcement rule — at most one inbound `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` edge per work_ticket, regardless of status. The rule realizes DEC-117's family-2 definition (single-use seed documents have at most one consumer) as a schema-level invariant. Distinguishes work_ticket from reference_book at the schema level (reference_book admits arbitrarily many inbound edges; work_ticket admits exactly one of the consumption kind).
   - DEC-145: work_ticket field inventory with eleven columns (identity, content, classification, no FK fields, file pointer, base timestamps plus four per-status lifecycle timestamps for workflow-shaped lifecycle per DEC-126). Declines the tentative inbound kind `work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation` named by conversation.md §3.3.2 as redundant — the existing outbound `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` declared in conversation.md is the sole canonical edge, viewed from work_ticket's side as inbound. Declines a typed `work_ticket_reads_reference_book` kind for kickoff-prompt read-list citations on DEC-133's frequency-justified test — read-lists are operational metadata rather than queried-by structure; the generic `references` kind suffices.
   - DEC-146: work_ticket API surface (standard 8-endpoint set with `?kind=` and `?status=` filter parameters on list); default UI layout with two natural additions parallel to reference_book — Kind column in the master pane and Status filter combo in the master-pane toolbar; default soft-delete with restore (distinct from cancelled and consumed status outcomes); closed four-value kind enum (`kickoff_prompt`, `claude_code_prompt`, `ad_hoc_prompt`, `other`) with `other` as the documented escape hatch for kinds that emerge in operation; sixteen testable acceptance criteria.

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — WKT.** Three letters. Rejected because it adds one character with no value; the collision check did not surface a need for additional disambiguation. **Option (c) — TIX.** Three letters with ticket-tracking-system connotations. Rejected because the connotations mischaracterize the entity (work_tickets are single-use seed documents, not Jira-style issues). **Option (d) — TKT.** Three letters, similar to TIX. Rejected for the same connotation reason and additionally because TKT reads as a phonetic abbreviation rather than a meaningful initialism. **Option (e) — Defer the prefix choice to build-planning.** Rejected because the prefix is needed in the spec body (acceptance criteria reference WT-NNN format) and the spec's per-spec completeness gate requires the prefix locked in §3.1.
after_payload.consequences: Spec guide section 6 collision list extended with WT. The kickoff's working-assumption prefix is confirmed. The remaining two governance entity conversations (close_out_payload, deposit_event) have first refusal on their own prefix choices; the working prefixes COP and DEP do not collide with WT but each conversation makes its own call. The work_ticket table's identifier column has CHECK constraint `^WT-\d{3}$`. The GET /work-tickets/next-identifier helper returns the next available WT-NNN value.
after_payload.context: The work_ticket entity is the fourth of six new governance entities designed in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Its identifier prefix is set against the prefix-collision list extended by DEC-123 (WS), DEC-129 (CONV), and DEC-135 (RB). The kickoff's working assumption was WT; alternatives named were WKT, TIX, TKT. The schema-spec methodology guide section 6 admits 2 to 5 letters; DEC-123 affirmed the two-letter form is acceptable; DEC-135 made the same call for RB. The collision-check requires examining (a) all existing prefixes in the V2 prefix set, (b) the two remaining-conversation working prefixes (COP for close_out_payload, DEP for deposit_event) named in the workstream master plan, and (c) the visual-shape distinguishability of any two-letter candidate against neighboring two-letter prefixes.
after_payload.decision: **Adopt `WT` as the work_ticket identifier prefix, format `WT-NNN` zero-padded to three digits, server-assigned on POST omission with helper at GET /work-tickets/next-identifier.** The two-letter form is consistent with DEC-123 (WS) and DEC-135 (RB). The collision check confirms: no existing prefix begins with W other than WS (workstream), and the two-letter WT versus two-letter WS differs in the second character with visually-distinct shapes — W-T versus W-S read unambiguously side by side. The two remaining-conversation working prefixes (COP, DEP) do not begin with W. The longer-form alternatives WKT, TIX, TKT add no value beyond length and TIX/TKT carry connotations from ticket-tracking systems (Jira, Trello, GitHub Issues) that the project deliberately does not invoke — 'work ticket' in this project is a single-use seed document, not an issue-tracking artifact.
after_payload.rationale: WT is mnemonic ('work ticket' → WT), short enough to scan rapidly in tables and references, and unambiguous against the existing prefix set. The two-letter form is consistent with DEC-123's affirmation that two-letter prefixes are acceptable and matches DEC-135's RB call. The W-T versus W-S second-character difference produces visually distinct shapes (T has a horizontal top stroke; S is curved) and reads cleanly in mixed contexts. The longer-form alternatives offer no semantic improvement and would have made every work_ticket identifier two characters longer for no payoff. TIX and TKT additionally carry external connotations that mischaracterize the entity — the project's work_tickets are single-use seed documents, not Jira-style issues with assignees and triage states.
after_payload.title: work_ticket entity identifier prefix and format: WT-NNN, two-letter form, no collision with WS or remaining-two working prefixes

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — bright line by lifecycle (work_ticket if no version after consumption, reference_book if it has versions).** Rejected because it conflates the versioning model with the family definition; a work_ticket's lifecycle doesn't end at consumption (the record persists with status `consumed`), and not all reference_books accumulate versions in practice. **Option (c) — re-categorization (move records between tables if the use pattern changes).** Rejected because moving records between tables forces a classification-history concern (which table did this live in last week? what queries return it?) that adds operational complexity without payoff; parallel coexistence achieves the same effect more cleanly. **Option (d) — single-table polymorphism with a discriminator column.** Rejected as premature schema unification; the two entities have meaningfully different field inventories (work_ticket has consumed-requires-edge and single-use rules; reference_book has version-pointer columns and a child versions table) and lifecycle semantics; collapsing them would obscure both. **Option (e) — mutual exclusivity at the file_path level (a file_path may exist in at most one of the two tables).** Rejected because parallel coexistence is the natural resolution when a file genuinely spans both governance views; forcing mutual exclusivity would make the kickoff's natural archival-plus-authoritative pattern unrepresentable.
after_payload.consequences: Schema enforces per-table uniqueness on file_path (work_ticket_file_path unique within work_tickets; reference_book_file_path unique within reference_books) but does not enforce cross-table uniqueness. PI-022's backfill pass classifies each historical workflow file by intent at creation; ambiguous cases default to work_ticket (the more conservative classification — a work_ticket can be supplemented by a parallel reference_book record later, but a reference_book record carries versioning machinery that may not fit single-use seeds). The methodology workstream master plan example (ui-v0.4-planning-prompt.md) is backfilled as a work_ticket; if successor citation patterns warrant a parallel reference_book record, it is authored as a separate operation. Section 2 of the spec carries the boundary-discipline narrative in full; section 3.8.2 captures the backfill-policy details for PI-022 to resolve. The build-planning conversation may add a cross-table sanity-check query (e.g., 'show all file_paths that exist in both tables') as a maintenance tool, but no schema-level constraint is needed.
after_payload.context: The kickoff named the boundary with reference_book as the principal design question for the work_ticket entity, given that both entity types are workflow files with similar surface fields (title, description, file_path, status). The distinction sits in lifecycle and reference pattern: work_ticket is single-use (produced for one conversation, consumed by it, not subsequently referenced as a source of truth); reference_book is long-lived and multi-consumer (cited by many conversations as authoritative reference). The kickoff named three candidate boundary policies: (a) bright line by intent — authorial intent at creation time settles the classification; (b) bright line by lifecycle — work_ticket if no version after consumption, reference_book if it has versions; (c) re-categorization — allow a work_ticket to be re-categorized to a reference_book after the fact if it turns out to be referenced repeatedly. The kickoff also named a boundary case: the methodology workstream master plan was produced by SES-011, whose kickoff (`ui-v0.4-planning-prompt.md`) was retroactively cited by successor planning documents — is that retroactive forward-referencing enough to reclassify the kickoff as a reference_book?
after_payload.decision: **Adopt bright line by intent at creation, with no in-place re-categorization, and explicit support for parallel coexistence of work_ticket and reference_book records pointing at the same `file_path`.** A file is a work_ticket if it was authored as a single-use seed for one specific conversation; it is a reference_book if it was authored as a long-lived multi-consumer reference. The classification is settled at creation and does not change. If a file authored as a work_ticket turns out to be referenced repeatedly enough to warrant authoritative-reference treatment, a separate reference_book record is authored against the same `file_path`; the two records coexist as independent governance views with different identifiers, different lifecycles, and different fields. The schema does NOT enforce mutual exclusivity at the `work_ticket_file_path` / `reference_book_file_path` level — each column is unique within its own table, not unique across the union. The methodology workstream master-plan boundary case raised in the kickoff (`ui-v0.4-planning-prompt.md`) resolves as a work_ticket; the retroactive forward-referencing by successor planning documents is archival mention, not authoritative reading.
after_payload.rationale: Intent-at-creation is the simplest rule that maps cleanly to the project's actual authoring pattern. Every kickoff and every CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-*.md file is authored with single-use intent for one specific conversation or execution; that intent is captured at creation and does not change even if the file is later mentioned by successor documents. Re-categorization (option c) would require the schema to admit moving records between tables, which forces a version-history-of-the-classification-itself concern that adds complexity without operational payoff — the catalog of work_tickets and the catalog of reference_books should each be stable. Lifecycle-based classification (option b) would conflate the versioning model (reference_book has child versions; work_ticket does not) with the family definition (single-use vs multi-consumer), which are independently meaningful and should not be collapsed. Parallel coexistence (the same file_path string in both tables) is the natural resolution when a file genuinely spans both governance views: the kickoff's archival role as 'the file that opened SES-011' lives on a work_ticket record; if the file's role evolves to 'the document successor planning conversations cite authoritatively,' a parallel reference_book record is authored and both records coexist. Uniqueness scoped to each table preserves the schema's simplicity.
after_payload.title: work_ticket boundary discipline with reference_book: intent-at-creation classification, no in-place re-categorization, file_path may coexist across both tables

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Four-status lifecycle collapsing drafted and ready.** Rejected because the 'ready to open' versus 'still authoring' distinction is operationally meaningful and asked about by consultants. **Four-status lifecycle collapsing cancelled and superseded.** Rejected because the 'abandoned outright' versus 'abandoned with successor' distinction is a meaningful authorial signal and informs the supersedes edge. **Three-status lifecycle (drafted, consumed, abandoned).** Rejected as too coarse — loses ready-to-open visibility and supersession distinction. **Workflow lifecycle without terminal-edge requirements (rely on the references-create dialog's filtering to prevent invalid states).** Rejected because the references-create dialog cannot enforce status-level invariants from outside the access layer; the consumed-requires-edge and supersession-requires-edge rules must live at the access layer to be reliable. **Reactivation-from-terminal admitted (e.g., a consumed work_ticket can be reset to ready if the conversation's first opening was a false start).** Rejected because the project's discipline that role-resumption is modelled as a new record is well-established and reactivation would complicate the lifecycle without operational payoff; a false-start consumption is modelled as soft-deleting the work_ticket administratively and creating a fresh record.
after_payload.consequences: Five-value CHECK constraint on work_ticket_status. Four per-status timestamp columns added to the schema. Access-layer enforcement of (a) valid-transition map per §3.4.1, (b) truly-terminal posture (no transitions out of terminal states), (c) consumed-requires-edge rule with HTTP 422 on POST/PATCH violation and DELETE-edge-while-consumed violation, (d) supersession-requires-edge rule with HTTP 422 on POST/PATCH violation and DELETE-edge-while-superseded violation. The new consumed-requires-edge precedent is captured for the remaining two schema-design conversations to inherit; close_out_payload's `applied` terminal will follow the same pattern with the inbound edge from deposit_event. Spec §3.4 carries the full lifecycle specification including transition map, transition semantics, both edge-requirement rules, the single-use enforcement rule (DEC-144 separately), kind-enum semantics, and soft-delete semantics. Acceptance criteria 5-7 and 9-10 (spec §3.7) translate the lifecycle and edge rules into testable statements.
after_payload.context: The work_ticket entity's lifecycle expresses the single-use seed document's progression from authoring through consumption (or abandonment). DEC-137 from SES-050 settled the documentary-vs-workflow distinction: documentary-shaped lifecycles inherit base timestamps only (reference_book's posture); workflow-shaped lifecycles carry per-status lifecycle timestamps per DEC-126. Work_ticket is workflow-shaped — consultants ask 'when did this kickoff become ready?' and 'when did the conversation consume it?'; the transitions are real-time-meaningful events. The kickoff named a working set of five status values (drafted, ready, consumed, superseded, cancelled) and asked whether all five are needed and what transitions are valid. Additionally, the kickoff's family-definitional question — what distinguishes work_ticket from reference_book at the schema level — points at the `consumed` status as the family-defining terminal: the act of consumption is what binds a work_ticket to its one conversation, and the schema needs to enforce that binding.
after_payload.decision: **(a) Adopt a five-status workflow-shaped lifecycle: `drafted` → `ready` → `consumed`, plus two abandonment terminals reachable from drafted or ready: `cancelled` and `superseded`.** Default starter status is `drafted`. (b) **Terminal-states-are-terminal discipline inherited from DEC-125 verbatim.** All three terminals (consumed, cancelled, superseded) reject every outgoing transition, including transitions between terminal states. A consumed work_ticket cannot become superseded even if the kickoff is later rewritten — the rewrite is a new work_ticket. (c) **Per-status lifecycle timestamps per DEC-126.** Four per-status timestamp columns (work_ticket_ready_at, work_ticket_consumed_at, work_ticket_cancelled_at, work_ticket_superseded_at), server-set on transition, mutually exclusive among the three terminal-state columns (exactly one is populated on any terminal record). (d) **Supersession-requires-edge rule inherited verbatim from DEC-125.** Setting status to `superseded` requires an outgoing `supersedes` edge to another work_ticket record; access layer enforces with HTTP 422 `{"error": "supersession_requires_successor_edge"}`. (e) **NEW — Consumed-requires-edge rule.** Setting status to `consumed` requires an inbound `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` edge from a conversation record; access layer enforces with HTTP 422 `{"error": "consumed_work_ticket_requires_consumption_edge"}`. This is the inverse-direction analogue of the supersession-requires-edge rule: supersession-requires-edge requires an outgoing successor edge; consumed-requires-edge requires an inbound consumer edge. **Establishes new cross-spec precedent**: for any governance entity with a terminal state defined by an external act of consumption or application, the transition to that terminal requires the edge naming the consumer or applier to be present, in whichever direction (inbound or outbound) the relationship is canonically modelled. Close_out_payload's anticipated `applied` terminal is the next case (inbound edge from deposit_event required); deposit_event's append-only-with-one-timestamp shape sidesteps the question (the record's creation IS the apply event).
after_payload.rationale: Five statuses capture the operational vocabulary cleanly: drafted (authoring in progress, file may be incomplete), ready (file committed, conversation can be opened), consumed (conversation opened against this work_ticket — terminal), cancelled (abandoned without consumption — terminal), superseded (abandoned with a successor work_ticket — terminal). Collapsing drafted and ready would lose the visibility into 'what kickoffs are ready to open' versus 'what kickoffs are still being authored' — a real consultant query. Collapsing cancelled and superseded would lose the distinction between 'abandoned outright' and 'abandoned with a successor carrying the work forward' — a meaningful authorial signal. Truly-terminal terminals match the precedent from DEC-125 and DEC-131; the project's discipline that a terminal entity's role-resumption is modelled as a new record (not an edit) is preserved. Per-status timestamps fit the workflow-shaped lifecycle per DEC-126; the consultant queries the timestamps answer ('when did this become ready?' 'when was it consumed?') are real-time-meaningful. The consumed-requires-edge rule is the schema-level realization of the family-definitional binding between a work_ticket and its one consuming conversation: a work_ticket cannot be consumed without the consumer being known. The cross-spec precedent generalizes naturally: any terminal defined by an external act requires the edge naming the actor; the direction of the edge follows from the verb-orientation of the relationship.
after_payload.title: work_ticket workflow-shaped lifecycle with five statuses, truly terminal terminals, consumed-requires-edge rule (new cross-spec precedent), and supersession-requires-edge rule

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Status-conditional rule (single-use enforced only on consumed status).** Rejected because it permits a drafted or ready work_ticket to accumulate two or more inbound consumption edges before consumption, which is operationally ill-defined (which conversation 'really' consumed it?) and forces ad-hoc resolution at status-transition time. **Documenting the property without access-layer enforcement.** Rejected because family-definitional invariants must be reliable; documentation alone permits backfill or accidental writes to bypass the family classification silently. **Enforcing the rule via a database-level UNIQUE constraint on (target_id, relationship='conversation_opens_against_work_ticket').** Rejected because the references table is generic (no per-kind unique constraints); the access layer is the right place for kind-specific invariants. **Allowing multiple inbound consumption edges but flagging in a maintenance view.** Rejected for the same reason as 'documenting only' — the invariant must be enforced, not surfaced after the fact.
after_payload.consequences: Access-layer write path for the `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` relationship kind checks the target work_ticket's existing inbound edge count before admitting the write. The check is a single COUNT query against the references table filtered by target_type='work_ticket', target_id=<the_work_ticket>, relationship='conversation_opens_against_work_ticket'; the write is admitted only if the count is zero. Acceptance criterion 8 (spec §3.7) translates the rule into a testable statement. The rule applies regardless of the work_ticket's status (acceptance criterion explicitly tests this). PI-022's backfill pass must respect the rule; ambiguous historical cases (a file used by two conversations before this entity type existed) are resolved per spec §3.8.2 — either by authoring one work_ticket with the first consumption typed and the second as a generic `references` edge, or by authoring two work_ticket records with different identifiers pointing at the same file_path. The schema admits the latter per DEC-142's parallel-coexistence rule (uniqueness on file_path is per-table, not cross-table).
after_payload.context: DEC-117 defines work_ticket as family 2 of the three workflow-file families: single-use seed documents with at most one consumer. The family-definitional property is the single-use semantics — a work_ticket is produced for one specific conversation, consumed by that conversation, and not subsequently referenced as a source of truth. The schema must express this single-use property as a hard constraint at the access layer; otherwise the family-definitional property is documented but not enforced, and historical or accidental multi-consumer attachments would silently corrupt the family classification. The consumed-requires-edge rule (DEC-143) requires the inbound edge to exist at consumption, but does not bound the count of inbound edges. The single-use property requires an upper bound (≤ 1) on inbound edges of the consumption kind.
after_payload.decision: **Enforce at the access layer: a work_ticket record may have at most one inbound edge of kind `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket`, regardless of the work_ticket's current status.** POST attempting to add a second inbound consumption edge to a work_ticket that already has one returns HTTP 422 `{"error": "work_ticket_single_use_violation"}`. The rule applies at all statuses: a drafted or ready work_ticket may have zero inbound edges; a consumed work_ticket has exactly one; a cancelled or superseded work_ticket may have zero (the typical case — abandoned before consumption) but may also have one if a partial consumption occurred before abandonment. The rule never admits two or more.
after_payload.rationale: The single-use semantics defined by DEC-117 are family-definitional and must be enforced at the schema level to be reliable. Documenting the property without enforcing it would allow accidental or historical multi-consumer attachments — for example, a backfill script attempting to record two conversations consuming the same kickoff — to silently bypass the family classification. The 422 error surfaces the violation immediately, forcing the consumer to choose between (a) using the existing single-use work_ticket for the right conversation and creating a new work_ticket for the second consumer, or (b) reclassifying the file as a reference_book if it genuinely had multiple authoritative consumers. The status-independence of the rule prevents corner cases where a cancelled work_ticket might be reused or where a consumed work_ticket might accidentally accumulate a second consumption edge. The rule is also what schema-level-distinguishes work_ticket from reference_book: reference_book admits arbitrarily many inbound edges (each `workstream_planned_in_reference_book` from a workstream that cites it as master plan, each generic citation from a conversation, etc.); work_ticket admits exactly one of the typed consumption kind.
after_payload.title: work_ticket single-use enforcement rule: at most one inbound conversation_opens_against_work_ticket edge per work_ticket, regardless of status
