after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Field inventory with separate work_ticket_target_deliverable column.** Rejected because the target deliverable is almost always named in the first paragraph of work_ticket_description; a separate column duplicates the narrative. **Field inventory with work_ticket_author column.** Rejected for minimum-viable scope; Doug is sole author today. **Adopt the tentative `work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation` kind in addition to the conversation-side `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` kind.** Rejected because it breaks the one-edge-per-relationship pattern and would force consumers to handle two equivalent forms. **Adopt the typed `work_ticket_reads_reference_book` kind in this release.** Rejected because the query frequency does not justify schema-level investment per DEC-133's precedent; the generic `references` kind is adequate. **Adopt a sub-kind taxonomy below the four named work_ticket_kind values (e.g., kickoff_prompt with sub-kinds session_prompt, update_prompt; claude_code_prompt with sub-kinds apply_prompt, build_prompt).** Rejected as premature taxonomy specialization; the four values cover the project's observed work-ticket families and a sub-kind axis can be added in a future release if querying by it becomes routine.
after_payload.consequences: Work_tickets table has eleven columns. CHECK constraints on identifier format (`^WT-\d{3}$`), kind enum (four values), status enum (five values), file_path validation (non-empty trimmed, no leading slash, no `..` segments, no scheme prefix). Per-status timestamp columns are nullable and server-set on transition; the consumed/cancelled/superseded triple is mutually exclusive on any terminal record. No new relationship-kind vocabulary added by this spec; the entity type `work_ticket` is already listed in conversation.md's additions table and the existing generic kinds plus the conversation-side `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` cover every relationship work_ticket needs. PI-022's backfill pass populates the eleven content/classification/file-pointer/timestamp fields for historical work_ticket records; the backfill policy on historical multi-consumer cases and unknown timestamps is PI-022's call per spec §3.8.2.
after_payload.context: Three field-inventory and vocabulary items decided at the close of the work_ticket schema-design conversation: (a) the complete field inventory beyond identifier, status, and base timestamps; (b) whether to introduce the tentative inbound relationship-kind `work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation` named by conversation.md §3.3.2 as a candidate; (c) whether to introduce a typed `work_ticket_reads_reference_book` kind for the kickoff-prompt read-list citation pattern (every kickoff prompt names a 'Read this first' list of reference books). Each item is structurally a vocabulary or schema-shape decision that the spec must lock before the per-spec completeness gate is met.
after_payload.decision: **(a) Field inventory (eleven columns total):** identity fields (work_ticket_identifier, work_ticket_title); content fields (work_ticket_description, work_ticket_notes); classification fields (work_ticket_kind, work_ticket_status); no foreign-key columns (every relationship lives in `refs` per DEC-124's references-edge precedent); file pointer (work_ticket_file_path); three inherited base timestamps (work_ticket_created_at, work_ticket_updated_at, work_ticket_deleted_at); four per-status lifecycle timestamps per DEC-126 for workflow-shaped lifecycle (work_ticket_ready_at, work_ticket_consumed_at, work_ticket_cancelled_at, work_ticket_superseded_at). Deliberately excluded under minimum-viable scope: work_ticket_author and work_ticket_owner (Doug sole today, one migration away), work_ticket_target_deliverable (folded into description), work_ticket_engagement_scope (per-engagement isolation implicit per DEC-115/116), content-blob storage (file lives in git), sub-kind enum (the work_ticket_kind enum is closed at four values for this release). **(b) Decline tentative kind `work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation`.** Conversation.md §3.3.2 named it as a candidate inbound-from-work_ticket-perspective name for the same relationship that conversation.md §3.3.1 already declared as outbound-from-conversation-perspective via `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket`. The existing outbound kind is the sole canonical edge for the relationship, viewed from work_ticket's side as inbound. Introducing a parallel inbound name would create two relationship-kind values for the same edge and break the one-edge-per-relationship pattern enforced by the source-first naming convention (DEC-048). **(c) Decline typed kind `work_ticket_reads_reference_book` for read-list citations.** Every kickoff prompt names a 'Read this first' list of reference books; the citation pattern is structural and recurs across all kickoff prompts. However, applying DEC-133's frequency-justified test: the question 'what work_tickets read reference book RB-042' is operational metadata about authoring, not a queried-by structure (consultants don't run that query at scale). The generic `references` kind covers the citation adequately. If queries by read-list become routine in operation, a future release adds the typed kind with the matching vocab and access-layer support (one-line vocab.py + backfill pass converting existing generic edges).
after_payload.rationale: **(a) Field inventory** follows minimum-viable per the methodology guide; the four per-status lifecycle timestamps fit DEC-126's workflow-shaped lifecycle precedent (work_ticket is workflow-shaped per DEC-143). Deferred fields are listed in spec §3.8.3 with growth-condition language so future signal can justify additions. The target-deliverable description is folded into work_ticket_description because most kickoffs name the target deliverable in the first paragraph of their description; a separate column would duplicate the narrative. **(b) Declining the tentative kind** preserves the one-edge-per-relationship pattern. The source-first naming convention (DEC-048) settles direction: the conversation entity is the actor that 'opens against' the work_ticket; the kind name expresses the conversation's verb, with work_ticket on the receiving side. Introducing a parallel inbound name would force every consumer of the references table to handle two equivalent forms of the same edge. The decline mirrors DEC-132's tentative-kind-name resolution from SES-049 (where conversation.md declined the tentative `conversation_succeeds_conversation` inverse name). **(c) Declining the typed read-list kind** is the application of DEC-133's frequency-justified test: typed sequencing or relationship kinds are introduced when the entity-family frequency of the query justifies the schema-level investment. Read-list citations are authored once at kickoff drafting time and rarely queried-by; the generic kind suffices. The retrofit cost is low (one line of vocab + a backfill pass converting existing generic edges to the typed kind), so deferring is non-prohibitive if the query pattern emerges.
after_payload.title: work_ticket field inventory with four per-status lifecycle timestamps; declines tentative work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation kind; declines typed work_ticket_reads_reference_book kind

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **API surface with version-management sub-endpoints.** Rejected because work_ticket is single-use, not versioned; the sub-endpoints would have no semantics. **Master pane without Kind column.** Rejected because kind is the most operationally meaningful classification for browsing the work_ticket catalog. **Master pane with status-first default sort (per the kickoff's hint).** Rejected in favour of the partial adoption — status is the fourth column (visible without scrolling) and the Status filter combo serves the browse-by-status pattern, but the default sort remains identifier-ascending for cross-spec consistency with workstream/conversation/reference_book. **Detail pane without conditional lifecycle-timestamp rendering (always show all four columns even when null).** Rejected because nullable timestamps cluttered the pane unnecessarily — the conditional rendering matches the underlying data and stays clean. **Open-string kind field instead of closed enum.** Rejected per DEC-138's precedent that closed enums with `other` sentinels are the structural pattern for kind classifications. **Six-value kind enum naming session_prompt and update_prompt as first-class values.** Rejected as premature speciation; the CBM filename conventions are captured as kickoff_prompt with title/description distinction for this release. **Acceptance criteria count of ten (gate minimum).** Rejected because the consumed-requires-edge rule, single-use enforcement rule, and end-to-end backfill exemplar deserve dedicated criteria; sixteen matches the project's working baseline.
after_payload.consequences: Eight REST endpoints to test; sixteen test cases per the acceptance criteria. The list endpoint's `?kind=` and `?status=` filter parameters combine additively (both filters narrow the result set). Master-pane Kind column requires the friendly-label mapping for the four enum values (e.g., 'Kickoff prompt' for `kickoff_prompt`). The detail-pane's conditional lifecycle-timestamp rendering follows the underlying nullable columns; the references-section displays the inbound `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` edge prominently as the family-defining relationship per spec §3.6.3. Edit dialog hint logic for missing consumption/supersedes edges adds a small UX layer atop the access-layer 422 enforcement. Soft-delete is administrative and orthogonal to the lifecycle statuses; the operational vocabulary distinguishes 'archive an erroneous record' (soft-delete) from 'this work_ticket was abandoned' (cancelled status) from 'this work_ticket was consumed' (consumed status). The spec's per-spec completeness gate (spec guide §7.1) passes: all nine subsections of §3 substantively filled, sixteen acceptance criteria, explicit category tags on open questions, cross-references include workstream plan, spec guide, workstream.md and conversation.md and reference_book.md as predecessor specifications, foundation decisions DEC-117..122, and the eighteen prior-conversation decisions DEC-123..140 from SES-048/049/050 as inherited precedents.
after_payload.context: Routine items batched together at the close of the work_ticket schema-design conversation: the API surface beyond the standard endpoint set, the UI layout including the master-pane Kind column and the toolbar Status filter combo, the soft-delete posture relative to the cancelled and consumed status outcomes, the kind enum membership and the `other` sentinel semantics, and the count and shape of testable acceptance criteria. None of these is architecturally novel relative to the schema-spec methodology guide's defaults or the precedents established by DEC-128/134/140, but each warrants explicit decision recording because they shape API surface and UI behavior.
after_payload.decision: **(a) API surface** is the standard 8-endpoint set per spec guide §3.5 (GET list with `?include_deleted=true` plus `?kind=` plus `?status=` filters; GET single; POST create; PUT replace; PATCH update; DELETE soft-delete; POST restore; GET next-identifier). No version-management sub-endpoints (work_ticket is single-use, not versioned). All endpoints return the V2 `{data, meta, errors}` envelope. **(b) Identifier auto-assignment**: default server-side on POST omission; helper at GET /work-tickets/next-identifier. **(c) Master pane** has five columns: Identifier, Title, Kind, Status, Updated. Default sort by Identifier ascending. The Kind column is the third column (between Title and Status) — operationally meaningful classification, parallel to reference_book's pattern. Kind selector and Status selector combo boxes in the panel toolbar drive `?kind=` and `?status=` filters; both default to 'all'. The Status filter combo is the primary operational filter per the kickoff's note that browse-by-status is the work_ticket's natural pattern. **(d) Detail pane** has nine sections in order: identifier (read-only), title, description, notes (collapsible internal section, collapsed by default), kind combo, file_path, status combo, lifecycle timestamps section (read-only labels rendered conditionally for non-null timestamps), ReferencesSection. **(e) CRUD dialogs** match the user-interface version 0.3 governance-entity patterns: EntityCrudDialog subclass for create/edit, EntityCrudDeleteDialog with edge-text confirmation for delete. Edit dialog detects missing consumption edge when transitioning to `consumed` and surfaces an inline hint; same for missing supersedes edge when transitioning to `superseded`. **(f) Soft-delete posture**: default — soft-delete with restore — explicitly distinct from `cancelled` status (a lifecycle outcome — work_ticket was abandoned without being consumed) and `consumed` status (a successful-use outcome — the conversation opened against it). **(g) Kind enum**: closed four-value enum (`kickoff_prompt`, `claude_code_prompt`, `ad_hoc_prompt`, `other`). The `other` sentinel is the documented escape hatch for kinds that emerge in operation. The CBM `SESSION-PROMPT-*` and `UPDATE-PROMPT-*` filename conventions are captured as `kickoff_prompt` for this release with the distinction recorded in title or description; a future release adds first-class enum values if those families warrant. **(h) Acceptance criteria**: sixteen testable statements captured in spec §3.7 (gate requires ten; this spec matches workstream's sixteen, conversation's eighteen, reference_book's sixteen), covering schema migration, identifier and kind and status enum validation, terminal-states-truly-terminal, consumed-requires-edge rule, single-use enforcement rule, supersession-requires-edge rule, lifecycle timestamp server-set behavior, file_path repo-relative validation, access-layer method signatures, REST endpoint behavior with kind and status filter parameters, identifier auto-assignment, soft-delete and restore round-trip, and one end-to-end backfill exemplar against this conversation's own work_ticket.
after_payload.rationale: Standard endpoint set inherited from the methodology guide; no deviations warranted because the entity's operations follow the standard CRUD-plus-restore pattern. No version-management sub-endpoints because work_ticket is single-use (the file is consumed once and not subsequently versioned). The Kind column is a natural addition parallel to reference_book's; consultants ask 'what apply-close-out prompts are queued' or 'what kickoffs are ready,' and kind plus status are the two operational axes. The Status filter combo is the primary operational filter — a consultant browsing the work_ticket catalog typically wants to see what is `ready` to be opened (the kickoff's note explicitly named browse-by-status as the natural pattern); the toolbar combo serves this without committing to a status-first default sort that would diverge from reference_book's identifier-first cross-spec consistency. Conditional rendering of lifecycle timestamps in the detail pane (only non-null timestamps shown as read-only labels) keeps the pane clean across the lifecycle. Soft-delete posture matches workstream/conversation/reference_book precedents and is explicitly distinct from the two lifecycle outcomes (cancelled, consumed). The closed four-value kind enum covers every work_ticket observed in the project's history; the `other` sentinel preserves additive extensibility. Sixteen acceptance criteria match reference_book's and workstream's counts and fully satisfy the gate; each is concrete and testable, ready for translation into test cases at build-planning time.
after_payload.title: work_ticket API surface, default UI with Kind column and Status filter combo, soft-delete posture, closed four-value kind enum, sixteen acceptance criteria

after_payload.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Design the close_out_payload entity type schema for V2's storage layer. Drive a structured architectural discussion that produces one deliverable: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/close_out_payload.md — the complete schema specification per the template in governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md."

The conversation was directed to process autonomously per Doug's opening turn, so the structured-architectural-discussion cadence collapsed into a single drafting pass with one principal-decision pause point.

Pre-flight context gathering. Sparse-cloned crmbuilder; read governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md, workstream.md, conversation.md, reference_book.md, work_ticket.md in full; read DEC-117 and DEC-118 from db-export/decisions.json; inventoried close-out-payloads/ directory (ses_001.json through ses_051.json, all sharing the four-section payload shape: label, session, decisions, planning_items, references); read CLAUDE.md head; verified next-available identifiers (SES-052, DEC-147..152, no new PI — PI-022 covers backfill, PI-023 already applied).

Principal decision surfaced and locked: payload content representation. Four options examined — JSON column for in-DB queryability, text column with on-demand parse, file-path with metadata fields, hybrid file-plus-snapshot. Lock: file-path only, no content column. Justified by the cross-spec precedent locked by DEC-139 (reference_book) and DEC-145 (work_ticket); the payload is structurally a file-tracking entity identical to work_ticket; the JSON content lives authoritatively in git; the apply script reads the file directly; future content-query needs are satisfied by a derived-view endpoint that parses file_path on demand. The two-part test was passed but the cross-spec precedent narrowed the options decisively enough that the question did not warrant a separate interrupt.

Remaining decisions locked without interrupt under the autonomous-processing directive: identifier prefix COP (three letters, matches DEC/SES/REF precedent; two-letter alternatives ambiguous); lifecycle five-status workflow drafted→ready→applied with terminals cancelled and superseded, parallel to work_ticket; applied-requires-edge rule realizing the inverse-pattern precedent locked by DEC-143; production-edge required at all statuses (the family-3 definition that each payload is produced by exactly one conversation); one new relationship-kind close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation; declines close_out_payload_kind enum (single-kind entity) and close_out_payload_schema_version column (structure stable across 50+ historical files); standard API surface with no deviations; default UI plus Status filter combo (parallel to work_ticket's pattern); soft-delete with restore explicitly distinct from cancelled lifecycle status and applied terminal status.

Validation gate per spec guide §7.1 passed: all nine subsections of §3 present, 16 acceptance criteria, 12 categorized open questions (5 build, 2 backfill, 5 future), all required cross-references present (workstream plan, spec guide, foundation decisions DEC-117..122, prior schemas), zero TBDs. Committed and pushed at 87c7c16.

Post-spec reconciliation. Doug flagged the missing commit on GitHub — sandbox commit had not been pushed. Investigation surfaced the contradiction between project-level instruction 'You commit, I push' (unconditional) and userMemories entry 16 (context-dependent split: sandbox=Claude pushes, Claude Code=Doug pushes). Reconciled in three steps: (1) Doug clarified the rule operationally — Claude.ai sandbox is inaccessible to Doug so commits without pushes are lost; Claude Code commits to Doug's local clone where Doug reviews before pushing. (2) userMemories entry 16 was replaced with the tightened wording. (3) Doug updated the Claude.ai project instructions on his side. (4) Claude observed that Claude Code has no visibility into Claude.ai project instructions or userMemories — both halves of the rule must live in repo-level CLAUDE.md to reach Claude Code. New 'Working conventions' section added to both crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md (commit 536df25) and ClevelandBusinessMentoring/CLAUDE.md (commit 00c0bda) with identical text documenting the bifurcation.

Close-out artifact authoring. This payload (ses_052.json) and the companion apply prompt (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-052.md) authored at conversation close. The session record was authored as part of the payload bundle per the operative SES-046-through-SES-051 precedent (the kickoff's 'desktop dialog' language was descriptive of one available mechanism but not prescriptive; the bundle-in-payload mechanism is the proven path).

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — two-letter CO or CP.** Rejected for ambiguity (CO reads as Company; CP is too generic). **Option (c) — four-letter PYLD, CLOSE, CLOUT.** Rejected as length-without-disambiguation; the three-letter form is unambiguous against the existing prefix set. **Option (d) — five-letter CLOSEOUT.** Rejected as visually cluttering when paired with the NNN suffix.
after_payload.consequences: All close_out_payload records use the COP-NNN identifier pattern. The access layer's identifier-validation regex is ^COP-\d{3}$. The next-identifier helper endpoint returns {"next": "COP-NNN"} for the next available number. The cross-spec consistency check before build-planning verifies no collision against any other governance-entity prefix introduced by the six schemas (workstream uses WS, conversation uses CONV, reference_book uses RB, work_ticket uses WT, close_out_payload uses COP, deposit_event chooses its own at its conversation).
after_payload.context: Per the spec guide section 6, identifier prefixes must be 2 to 5 uppercase letters and must not collide with existing prefixes. The kickoff prompt named COP as the working assumption; alternatives examined were two-letter forms (CO, CP) and four-to-five-letter forms (CLOUT, PYLD, CLOSE). The existing prefix set: DEC, SES, RSK, PI, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT, PROC, CRMC, ENG, WS, CONV, RB, WT. DEC-123 affirmed each downstream conversation makes its own prefix-length call within the 2-to-5-letter range without locking the cross-spec norm at any particular length.
after_payload.decision: **Adopt COP as the close_out_payload entity identifier prefix.** Three letters, matching the most common existing prefix length (DEC, SES, RSK, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT). Identifier format: COP-NNN, zero-padded to three digits (COP-001, COP-046, etc.). The two-letter form CO was rejected for ambiguity (reads as Company or Carbon monoxide); CP was rejected as too generic. Four- and five-letter alternatives (CLOUT, PYLD, CLOSE) add length without disambiguation gain. COP survives the collision check against the full existing prefix list.
after_payload.rationale: Three-letter is the dominant precedent length and reads cleanly with the standard NNN suffix. Per DEC-123's affirmation that each downstream conversation chooses its own prefix length, three letters here is appropriate without binding the deposit_event conversation to any particular choice on its own facts.
after_payload.title: close_out_payload entity identifier prefix and format: COP-NNN, three-letter form, no collision with existing prefixes

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (a) — JSON column.** Rejected as denormalization of content that lives authoritatively on the filesystem. Adds a write-time consistency burden (database column may drift from file) and a column-vs-file integrity question on every read. **Option (b) — text column with parse.** Rejected as worst-of-both-worlds: same storage cost as (a) without the in-database query benefit. **Option (d) — hybrid file plus snapshot column.** Rejected as redundancy under git's existing audit guarantees; the snapshot adds maintenance burden (when does the snapshot get refreshed? how does it stay in sync with the file?) for marginal benefit. The derived-view endpoint pattern named in the chosen option supplies content-query convenience without the synchronization burden.
after_payload.consequences: The close_out_payloads table carries one file-pointer column (close_out_payload_file_path) with the same repo-relative validation rules as work_ticket_file_path and reference_book_file_path. The same physical file may legally coexist as a file_path value across all three tables (work_ticket, reference_book, close_out_payload) under the section-2 boundary discipline; uniqueness is within each table only. The apply script's evolution to consume payload content from the file (current behaviour) versus from a database lookup of file_path (alternative behaviour) is a build-planning concern — both are consistent with this schema. The future derived-view endpoint, if shipped, is non-breaking; no table change.
after_payload.context: The kickoff prompt named this as the principal design question — how the structured JSON payload is represented in the database. Four options surfaced: (a) JSON column storing the payload directly for in-database queryability; (b) text column with on-demand parse; (c) file-path-with-metadata fields where the file remains the source of truth; (d) hybrid file-plus-snapshot. The choice has consequences for how the build-planning conversation's apply path interacts with the payload entity. All 50+ historical payloads under PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ live as JSON files; the apply script (apply_close_out.py) reads the file directly.
after_payload.decision: **Adopt option (c): repo-relative close_out_payload_file_path column as the only payload-content representation; no payload_content JSON column on the entity table.** The payload's authoritative content lives in the file under git, exactly as reference_book records' content does. The database carries the governance view (identifier, title, description, status, lifecycle timestamps, references to producing conversation and applying deposit_event). The apply script continues to read the file at apply time; future content-query needs are satisfied by a derived-view endpoint (provisional path GET /close-out-payloads/{identifier}/content) that reads file_path on demand and parses the JSON, not by a stored column. The schema admits the future endpoint additively without requiring a table change.
after_payload.rationale: The cross-spec precedent is decisive. DEC-139 (reference_book) and DEC-145 (work_ticket) both adopted repo-relative file_path with no content column — file-tracking governance entities store the path, not the content. Close_out_payload is structurally identical in shape: a file at a deterministic location under PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/, produced once, applied once, audited via git history. The fact that the content is JSON rather than markdown is a content-format detail, not a governance-shape difference. Denormalizing a 25-to-60 KB JSON blob into a database column per record adds storage cost, mutation-vs-file-integrity questions, and write-time validation complexity for no first-class query need that the file itself does not already satisfy.
after_payload.title: close_out_payload content representation: repo-relative file_path pointer only, no payload_content column

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — pure append-only with no soft-delete.** Rejected because legitimate record-cleanup operations exist (e.g., removing a stale draft authored by mistake). Append-only fits the deposit_event side of the family-3 pair — the apply-outcome record — not the payload side which is mutable in pre-apply states. **Option (c) — three-status simplification (drafted → applied; cancelled and superseded collapsed into a single 'abandoned' terminal).** Rejected because cancelled and superseded carry different governance semantics — cancelled has no successor; superseded names one. Collapsing them loses the distinction between 'discarded' and 'replaced by'. **Option (d) — admit pause/resume by allowing transitions out of cancelled.** Rejected per the truly-terminal precedent established by workstream and conversation. A payload that needs to resume is a new payload that supersedes the prior; the audit trail prefers two records with a supersession edge over one record with a non-monotonic history.
after_payload.consequences: The close_out_payloads table carries four per-status lifecycle timestamp columns (ready_at, applied_at, cancelled_at, superseded_at) in addition to the three inherited base timestamps (created_at, updated_at, deleted_at). The access layer enforces: status-transition validation per the lifecycle map; applied-requires-inbound-edge (HTTP 422 if attempted without the deposit_event edge); supersession-requires-outbound-edge (HTTP 422 if attempted without the supersedes edge); production-edge-required-at-all-statuses (HTTP 422 if attempted without exactly one outbound close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation edge); single-producer rule (HTTP 422 if more than one production edge attempts to coexist). Soft-delete is orthogonal to lifecycle state; a record can be in any of the five lifecycle statuses and additionally soft-deleted. This conversation does NOT introduce a new cross-spec precedent — every rule follows an inherited precedent applied to this entity's facts. The append-only-vs-soft-delete posture for deposit_event is deferred to that conversation.
after_payload.context: The kickoff prompt named the lifecycle questions as four sub-questions: status set, append-only-vs-soft-delete, schema version field, and inbound-edge requirements for terminal states. The cross-spec precedents in force at conversation open: DEC-125 (truly-terminal terminals, supersession-requires-outbound-edge), DEC-131 (multi-state workflow-shaped lifecycle for governance entities that need it), DEC-143 (terminal-state consumption requires the inbound consumption edge — work_ticket's consumed-requires-edge, explicitly named close_out_payload's applied-terminal as the precedent's first downstream application).
after_payload.decision: **Adopt a five-status workflow-shaped lifecycle: drafted → ready → applied, plus terminals cancelled and superseded.** Default starter status is drafted. The applied terminal requires an inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edge (realizing DEC-143's inverse-pattern precedent on this entity's facts). The superseded terminal requires an outgoing supersedes edge to another close_out_payload (mirroring DEC-125's pattern verbatim). The production-linkage edge close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation is required at all statuses regardless of lifecycle state — including drafted — because the family-3 definition (per DEC-117) is that a payload is produced at a conversation's close; the producing conversation is part of the payload's identity. All three terminal states (applied, cancelled, superseded) are truly terminal — no transitions out, including transitions between terminal states. Re-application of a payload is modelled as a new payload that supersedes the prior; reactivation as a status transition is not supported.
after_payload.rationale: The shape parallels work_ticket exactly because the governance roles are structural mirrors: work_ticket has drafted → ready → consumed; close_out_payload has drafted → ready → applied. Both have terminals cancelled and superseded. Both have a single non-starter terminal (the successful-use outcome) requiring a specific external-act edge to be present. The truly-terminal posture inherited from workstream and conversation prevents audit-blurring (an applied payload becoming un-applied would break the deposit_event's referent). The production-edge-required-at-all-statuses rule realizes the family-3 definition cleanly: a payload without a producing conversation is malformed by DEC-117's definition, regardless of how complete the payload file itself is.
after_payload.title: close_out_payload workflow-shaped lifecycle: five statuses with truly terminal terminals, applied-requires-edge rule realizing the SES-051 precedent, supersession-requires-edge rule, production-edge required at all statuses

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — foreign-key column conversation_id on the close_out_payloads table.** Rejected per the references-edge-over-FK cross-spec precedent. Modifying the conversations table or adding columnar coupling between governance entities is the pattern the precedent specifically declines. **Option (c) — typed close_out_payload_succeeds_close_out_payload sequencing kind.** Rejected per DEC-133's frequency-justified test; sequencing in the close_out_payload family is rare enough that the generic supersedes kind suffices and the typed kind would add vocabulary noise without query value. If a real frequency emerges, the typed kind is a one-line vocab.py addition in a future workstream.
after_payload.consequences: One new relationship-kind vocabulary entry close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation is added by the build-planning conversation alongside the other governance schemas' additions, with the matching _kinds_for_pair clause and the Alembic migration on refs.relationship_kind's CHECK constraint. The access layer enforces exactly-one-outbound-edge cardinality at all statuses for this kind. The inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload kind from the next conversation's entity is listed informationally in this spec's section 3.3.2 only; its vocab.py registration and access-layer at-most-one-inbound cardinality enforcement belong to deposit_event.md. Same-type supersession is admitted automatically once close_out_payload is in ENTITY_TYPES because the existing _kinds_for_pair rule for same-type pairs admits supersedes.
after_payload.context: Every close_out_payload is produced at exactly one conversation's close (per DEC-117's family-3 definition). The kickoff prompt named this relationship's mechanism as an open question: foreign-key column on the close_out_payloads table versus references-table edge. The cross-spec precedent locked by DEC-124 and DEC-130 is references-edge over foreign-key for all parent-child governance relationships. Separately, the question of whether close_out_payload sequencing (one payload replaced by another for the same conversation) should be typed (e.g., close_out_payload_succeeds_close_out_payload) or use the generic supersedes kind was governed by DEC-133's frequency-justified test.
after_payload.decision: **Production-linkage lives in the references table with a new relationship kind close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation; cardinality is exactly-one outbound edge per close_out_payload record, enforced at the access layer at every status including drafted.** A conversation may be the target of zero or more such edges (zero if not yet closed; one in the typical single-payload-per-conversation case; more than one if drafts were superseded and replaced). The new relationship-kind vocabulary entry is registered in REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS, with the _kinds_for_pair clause binding it to the (close_out_payload, conversation) pair only. The ENTITY_TYPES set adds close_out_payload (conversation is already in ENTITY_TYPES from SES-049's registration). Same-type supersession reuses the existing generic supersedes kind for the (close_out_payload, close_out_payload) pair — no new typed kind close_out_payload_succeeds_close_out_payload is introduced.
after_payload.rationale: References-edge for parent-child governance relationships is the locked cross-spec precedent. No reason on this entity's facts to deviate. The production relationship is naturally one-edge-one-kind, consistent with conversation's belongs_to_workstream pattern and work_ticket's inbound-from-conversation pattern. Per DEC-133's frequency-justified test, typed sequencing kinds are introduced when the entity-family's sequencing is structurally frequent. Close_out_payload sequencing (one draft replaced by another) is rare in practice — most conversations produce exactly one payload that is then applied. The frequency-justified test points at generic supersedes for the same-type pair, consistent with workstream's posture and unlike conversation's (where the every-conversation-belongs-to-a-predecessor-or-successor frequency justified the typed kind).
after_payload.title: close_out_payload production-linkage via references-edge with new kind close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation; declines typed sequencing kind in favour of generic supersedes

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — add close_out_payload_kind enum with values like 'session_close_out' and 'bulk_import_payload'.** Rejected because no other variants exist or are currently anticipated. Adding the enum now would require populating every record with the same value indefinitely; adding it later (when a second variant actually emerges) is a one-line additive migration. **Option (c) — add close_out_payload_schema_version column with default 1.0 for all historical records.** Rejected as premature; the JSON structure has been stable across 50+ files. **Option (d) — denormalize record-counts-by-kind (decisions_count, planning_items_count, references_count) into columnar fields.** Rejected because the values are easily computed from the file on demand and would require a write-time consistency burden between file content and column values.
after_payload.consequences: The close_out_payloads table has exactly eleven columns. No kind enum. No schema_version column. No counts columns. Future additive migrations remain available for any of these if real-use signal supports them; the schema accommodates them without breaking existing records. The detail pane in the desktop UI does not render a Kind row (since the field does not exist); the master pane's column list does not include a Kind column for the same reason. If a derived-view endpoint exposing payload content is shipped in a future build, it can compute and return record-counts-by-kind from the file at request time.
after_payload.context: The kickoff prompt named the field-inventory question and three specific sub-questions: whether to include a close_out_payload_kind classification enum (since other state-write package variants might emerge), whether to include a close_out_payload_schema_version field tracking payload structural version, and whether to denormalize record-counts-by-kind metadata into columnar fields. The existing 50+ payload files all share the four-section shape (label, session, decisions, planning_items, references) with no version field on the payload content itself.
after_payload.decision: **Adopt the eleven-column shape: close_out_payload_identifier, close_out_payload_title, close_out_payload_description, close_out_payload_notes, close_out_payload_status, close_out_payload_file_path, plus three inherited base timestamps (created_at, updated_at, deleted_at) and four per-status lifecycle timestamps (ready_at, applied_at, cancelled_at, superseded_at).** Decline a close_out_payload_kind enum because every record in this table is the same kind (the single artifact type close-out-payload-at-conversation-close); the kind enum has no taxonomic axis to express. Decline a close_out_payload_schema_version column because the payload's JSON structure has been stable across all 50+ historical files; tracking schema version is premature optimization. Decline denormalized record-counts-by-kind columns; the file_path read at use time supplies the counts when needed.
after_payload.rationale: The eleven-column shape parallels work_ticket's field inventory verbatim (work_ticket has eleven columns too: identifier, title, description, notes, kind, status, file_path, three base timestamps, four per-status lifecycle timestamps). Two structural differences worth noting: work_ticket has a kind enum (four values: kickoff_prompt, claude_code_prompt, ad_hoc_prompt, other) while close_out_payload has no kind enum because no taxonomic axis exists; close_out_payload uses a different status set (drafted/ready/applied vs. work_ticket's drafted/ready/consumed) reflecting the different terminal-state external act (apply vs. consume). The decline of payload_schema_version is principled: the schema admits the column as a one-line additive migration if structure ever evolves, and pre-adding the column on the speculation that structure will evolve violates minimum-viable scope. The decline of record-counts-by-kind columns reflects the same scoping discipline as the file_path-only content-representation decision (DEC-148): the file under git carries the authoritative content; derived views compute counts from it on demand.
after_payload.title: close_out_payload field inventory including four per-status lifecycle timestamps; declines close_out_payload_kind enum (single-kind entity) and close_out_payload_schema_version column (structure stable)

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — pure append-only after POST, no PUT/PATCH/DELETE.** Rejected because legitimate pre-apply mutation cases exist (correcting a typo in description before the payload is finalized as ready; updating notes after the payload is authored but before it is applied). **Option (c) — collapse Status filter combo into the inherited 'show deleted' toggle.** Rejected because the two filters do different work (lifecycle status vs. soft-delete state); combining them would conflate orthogonal concepts. **Option (d) — defer the Status filter combo to a future UI release.** Rejected because the operational pattern is structurally part of the entity's day-one use (the very first reason to browse the close_out_payloads catalog is to see what is queued for apply), and adding the combo at build-time is a small ListDetailPanel filter machinery extension matching work_ticket's pattern.
after_payload.consequences: The build-planning conversation produces a build slice for close_out_payload that mirrors work_ticket's build slice in shape: one Alembic migration creating the close_out_payloads table; one access-layer module with the eight standard methods; one FastAPI router with the eight endpoints plus the next-identifier helper; one desktop panel using ListDetailPanel with the four columns and the Status filter combo toolbar addition; one detail pane rendering the eleven fields plus the ReferencesSection widget with the family-defining production-edge prominently displayed; create/edit/delete/restore dialogs matching the v0.3 pattern; sixteen test cases mapped to the acceptance criteria. Vocabulary additions consolidated with the other governance schemas' additions into one vocab.py update plus one Alembic migration on refs.relationship_kind's CHECK constraint. No deviations from V2's standard API or UI architecture; the close_out_payload panel reads, looks, and behaves like the other governance-entity panels.
after_payload.context: The remaining design questions after lifecycle, relationships, fields, and content representation were settled: which API endpoints to expose, which UI deviations from default the entity needs, what soft-delete semantics apply, and what acceptance criteria define correct implementation. The cross-spec precedents for these questions were locked by DEC-128 (workstream — standard defaults), DEC-134 (conversation — standard defaults with one natural addition), DEC-140 (reference_book — standard API with three version-management sub-endpoints, Kind column and version-history detail pane addition), and DEC-146 (work_ticket — standard API with Kind column and Status filter combo additions).
after_payload.decision: **Adopt the standard V2 endpoint set with no deviations: GET /close-out-payloads (with ?include_deleted and ?status query filters), GET /close-out-payloads/{identifier}, POST /close-out-payloads, PUT /close-out-payloads/{identifier}, PATCH /close-out-payloads/{identifier}, DELETE /close-out-payloads/{identifier}, POST /close-out-payloads/{identifier}/restore, GET /close-out-payloads/next-identifier.** All endpoints use the {data, meta, errors} envelope. Identifier auto-assignment server-side on POST omission. The desktop UI uses the default layout with one natural addition: a Status filter combo in the master-pane toolbar (parallel to work_ticket's addition), because browse-by-status is the operational pattern for payloads — ready to see what is queued for apply, applied to see what has been deposited. No Kind column in the master pane (no kind field exists). Soft-delete is the default behaviour and is explicitly orthogonal to lifecycle state: cancelled is a lifecycle outcome (the payload was abandoned without being applied; the record remains visible in list endpoints), applied is a successful-use outcome (the deposit_event records the apply; the record remains visible), soft-deleted is a record-management operation (the consultant removed the record from default views). Sixteen acceptance criteria captured covering schema migration, identifier validation, status-transition validation, the three terminal-states-truly-terminal cases, the three edge-requirement rules (applied/superseded/production), per-status timestamp server-set behaviour, file-path validation, access-layer method signatures, REST endpoint shape, identifier-auto-assignment race-freedom, soft-delete round-trip, vocabulary registration, sidebar position, master pane columns, detail pane rendering, and end-to-end backfill of a representative historical payload.
after_payload.rationale: Standard defaults plus one natural addition is the established posture for governance entities with operational browse patterns (work_ticket and reference_book both did this on their own facts). The Status filter combo addition is naturally justified by the close_out_payload's operational pattern: a consultant scanning the entity catalog wants to see 'what is queued for apply' (status=ready) or 'what has been applied' (status=applied) at a glance. Soft-delete as the posture for the payload side of the family-3 pair (with append-only naturally fitting deposit_event on the other side, deferred to that conversation) is consistent with DEC-013's framing of when append-only is the right pattern — append-only fits transactional records (a decision, a session, a deposit event), not state-write packages with legitimate pre-apply mutability.
after_payload.title: close_out_payload API surface, default UI with Status filter combo addition, soft-delete posture distinct from cancelled lifecycle status, sixteen acceptance criteria

(2) PRDs/product/UPDATE-PROMPT-yaml-schema-v1.2-category-6.md (new — kickoff prompt for the next-conversation drafting of app-yaml-schema.md v1.2 Category 6 Parts A–E sections; committed at f152181). Plus this close-out payload (close-out-payloads/ses_053.json) and the companion apply prompt (prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-053.md), committed together as the close-out artifacts. Two decision records (DEC-153, DEC-154) and two references land via this payload's apply prompt. No source code modified; this is a planning conversation with a documentation-edits side-track. after_payload.conversation_reference: Claude.ai ARCHITECTURE-mode conversation that opened against the open question of how to express deployment-time security declaratively in CRM Builder's YAML schema. The conversation walked the design space in two consequential decisions: first, the fundamental fork between definitions-only and definitions-plus-assignments scope for the YAML (resolved as Option A, definitions only); second, the reordering of Category 6 of the gap analysis between schema v1.2 and v1.3 (resolved as Option C, scope-level + teams in v1.2, field-level deferred to v1.3). Between decisions the conversation produced a strawman security block grounded in a Mentor role for Cleveland Business Mentors, then ran a code-verification pass against the actual loader and validator in espo_impl/core/config_loader.py to confirm the existing top-level key set and the absence of any security/roles/teams recognition. After the second decision locked, the gap-analysis Section 6 was rewritten end-to-end to express the new five-part v1.2 scope (Parts A–E) and the deferred v1.3 scope (old Parts B and C), with Q1–Q3 amended and Q7–Q8 added. A new UPDATE-PROMPT was then authored as the kickoff for the next conversation (drafting the operator-facing reference sections of app-yaml-schema.md for v1.2 Category 6 Parts A–E). All three documents were committed and pushed; a stale commit-SHA reference in the kickoff prompt was discovered post-rebase and corrected in a follow-up commit. No transcript preserved per DEC-025.
after_payload.in_flight_at_end: The next Claude.ai conversation opens against PRDs/product/UPDATE-PROMPT-yaml-schema-v1.2-category-6.md (committed at f152181, SHA corrected at 4ff2e43). That session drafts the operator-facing reference sections of app-yaml-schema.md covering v1.2 Category 6 Parts A–E (Roles declaration, Teams declaration, scope-level entity access, system permissions, panel/layout role-aware visibility). Per the kickoff, the session is expected to surface three consequential decisions before drafting begins: section placement in app-yaml-schema.md (new top-level section vs. slotted under existing structure), file-type concept (new program-file type for security vs. extension of existing single program-file type to allow optional roles:/teams: top-level keys), and deploy-ordering expression (schema-level constraint vs. pipeline convention vs. loader-discovery rule). Downstream of the schema-spec session, the v1.2 Category 6 implementation prompt series for Claude Code (analogous to v1.1's archived A–H series at PRDs/_archive/yaml-schema-prompts/) covers loader extensions parsing the new top-level keys, validator extensions enforcing whitelist semantics and role-name cross-references, new deploy managers (role_manager.py and team_manager.py) wired into the Configure pipeline ahead of fields and relationships, API client extensions for /api/v1/Role and /api/v1/Team CRUD, and test coverage matching the v1.1 series pattern. The v1.3 deferred field-level + presets work (old Parts B and C of the original Category 6 plan) is queued behind the v1.2 implementation series; the MR-pilot's two access-control items (MR-MC-AC-001/002) remain on the MANUAL CONFIGURATION list through v1.2 release and ship at v1.3.
after_payload.title: YAML schema Category 6 (Role-Based Access Control) reordered across v1.2 and v1.3; gap-analysis Section 6 amended and v1.2 drafting kickoff committed
after_payload.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Lets discuss deploying security with YAML".

Landscape framing. Opened by mapping the EspoCRM security model into its constituent layers (roles, teams, scope-level access, field-level access, assignment permission, portal roles, user-to-role and user-to-team assignments) and naming the one fork that needed to settle before any schema work could begin: whether the YAML owns role and team *definitions* only or *definitions + assignments*. Side-flagged the existing memory note that YAML schema Category 6 was deferred to v1.2, with verification of current status to come once a direction was chosen.

Decision 1 — definitions-only security model (Option A). Doug chose Option A: YAML defines roles and teams; user-to-role and user-to-team assignments are runtime data managed in the target CRM UI. Locked as DEC-153. Rationale: matches the prevailing IAM industry pattern (policy as code, identity membership as runtime state); avoids requiring a YAML edit and Configure run for every new user. The CBM scenario of a new mentor onboarded mid-cycle was the concrete motivator.

Portal and field-level scope confirmation. Confirmed no portal in CBM's plans, so Portal Roles drop from v1.0 scope. Confirmed (by default, not reopened) that the original Category 6 field-level deferral to v1.2 held at this point in the conversation; this would be revisited at Decision 2.

Strawman drafting. Produced a strawman security block grounded in a CBM Mentor role: top-level `security:` containing `teams:` (Mentors, Administrators, Fundraising) and `roles:` with one fully-realized Mentor role showing `scope_access:` per entity (c-Engagement, c-Session, Contact, Account) using the EspoCRM grain (create as yes/no; read/edit/delete/stream as all/team/own/no), plus `system_permissions:` covering assignment_permission, user_permission, export, mass_update, audit_log. Flagged three implementation concerns: YAML boolean coercion on bare `no`; the limit of EspoCRM's role scope levels for expressing linked-record access (Mentor → Contact via Engagement); naming convention for built-in vs. custom entities. Doug approved the structure and noted values can be revised against the final security design.

Code verification — does the YAML processor handle any of this today? Conversation pivoted from PRD/schema work to code investigation per the working pattern that mandates verifying behavior against the actual codebase. Doug confirmed crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md as the reference. Sparse-cloned the crmbuilder repo with the project's standard sparse pattern; read CLAUDE.md head; inspected espo_impl/core/config_loader.py for top-level key recognition; inspected the validate_program() function for unknown-key enforcement; searched for any role or team manager files in espo_impl/core/ and confirmed none exist; located the existing Category 6 plan in PRDs/product/yaml-schema-gap-analysis-MR-pilot.md and read the four-part structure (Parts A–D, with Part D added 05-03-26 covering panel and layout role-aware visibility).

Gap-analysis findings reported to Doug. (1) Loader recognizes exactly five top-level keys: version, description, content_version, entities, relationships. No security:, roles:, or teams: handling. (2) validate_program() applies no top-level key whitelist; an unknown top-level key is silently ignored (no error, no warning, no effect). (3) No role manager, team manager, or API client wrappers for /api/v1/Role or /api/v1/Team exist. Greenfield. (4) EspoCRM's REST API exposes Role and Team as first-class CRUD entities — not in the no-public-write-path list (which contains only saved views, duplicate checks, workflows). (5) The existing v1.2 plan in the gap analysis is field-level-centric; it covers Part A (roles declaration), Part B (field-level permissions), Part C (permission presets), and Part D (panel/layout role-aware visibility). Critically, it covers no teams concept at all and no scope-level entity access (CRUD per role at all/team/own/no). The strawman from earlier in the conversation tackled exactly what was missing from the existing plan. The two scopes are complementary, not competing.

Decision 2 — Category 6 reordering (Option C). Surfaced the gap between the existing v1.2 plan (field-level + presets) and the strawman scope (scope-level + teams + system permissions) as a consequential decision passing the two-part test. Three options presented: (A) narrow v1.2 — ship the existing plan only, scope-level and teams remain manual config; (B) expanded v1.2 — ship both, double the surface area; (C) reorder — ship scope-level + teams as v1.2, field-level shifts to v1.3. Recommended C with one-line rationale that scope-level access is more universal (every role needs entity-level CRUD bounds; only some roles need field-level overrides) and that field-level was acknowledged in the original plan as needing additional pilot input. Doug chose C. Locked as DEC-154.

Gap-analysis Section 6 amendment. With Option C locked, the gap-analysis Section 6 was rewritten end-to-end in a single commit (eb1d7e7). New five-part v1.2 structure: Part A — Roles declaration (largely preserved from original, name-as-identity, persona metadata); Part B — Teams declaration (NEW); Part C — Scope-level entity access (NEW) with whitelist semantics, vocabulary documented, YAML boolean coercion called out; Part D — System permissions (NEW); Part E — Panel and layout role-aware visibility (was original Part D, renumbered, content preserved). New deferred-to-v1.3 section covers old Part B (field-level permissions) and old Part C (permission presets). Q1 amended (still defer to v1.2, with explicit part split); Q2 amended (file renamed from programs/roles.yaml to programs/security.yaml to reflect roles + teams); Q3 amended (presets deferred to v1.3); Q7 added (user-to-role and user-to-team assignments are out of YAML scope, capturing the DEC-153 outcome at the gap-analysis level); Q8 added (whitelist semantics for scope_access: omission denies). Change log entry recorded at the top of the document.

Next-step kickoff prompt. UPDATE-PROMPT-yaml-schema-v1.2-category-6.md authored at PRDs/product/ as the kickoff for the next Claude.ai conversation, which will draft the operator-facing reference sections of app-yaml-schema.md covering v1.2 Category 6 Parts A–E. The kickoff names three decisions likely to surface in that session (section placement, file-type concept, deploy-ordering expression) and constrains the session to documentation drafting only (no code, no Claude Code prompt authoring, no design extension beyond what gap-analysis Section 6 specifies). Initial commit at f152181.

Post-rebase SHA fix. Push of the two pending commits (gap analysis amendment, UPDATE-PROMPT) hit a non-fast-forward error; git pull --rebase incorporated remote changes (originating from a different recent conversation's commits), which rewrote the local commit SHAs. The kickoff prompt's `Created in:` line still referenced the pre-rebase SHA 3d883b9; corrected to eb1d7e7 in a follow-up commit at 4ff2e43.

Close-out artifact authoring. This payload (ses_053.json) and the companion apply prompt (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-053.md) authored at conversation close. The session record is bundled into the payload per the operative SES-046-through-SES-052 precedent. Identifier check at close-out time against the v2 database snapshot (db-export/sessions.json, decisions.json, planning_items.json): latest applied SES is SES-052; next-available is SES-053. Latest applied DEC is DEC-152; next-available is DEC-153 and DEC-154 for the two decisions. Latest applied PI is PI-023; no new planning item is created in this session (the next-conversation kickoff is committed as a work artifact, not deferred as an open question). The apply prompt's pre-flight verifies these heads are still uncontested at run time.

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option B — definitions + assignments in YAML.** The deployment artifact fully describes the running security posture; new users require a YAML edit and a Configure run. Defensible for small, stable user bases where 'redeploy on new user' might even be a feature (auditable user lifecycle in git). Rejected because the operational cost compounds quickly (every CBM mentor recruitment adds churn), the YAML loses its 'structure declaration' purity by mixing in identity data, and the industry pattern is clear that policy-as-code stops at the role boundary rather than extending to membership.
after_payload.consequences: Recorded as Q7 in the amended gap-analysis Section 6 (commit eb1d7e7). The role and team declarations in programs/security.yaml describe structure only — no members:, users:, or assignments: blocks on roles or teams. When the v1.2 implementation lands (Parts A–E per DEC-154), the role_manager.py and team_manager.py deploy managers create and update Role and Team records via /api/v1/Role and /api/v1/Team but do not touch the User entity or any user-to-role / user-to-team linkage tables. Operators manage user lifecycle (creation, role assignment, team membership, deactivation) through the target CRM's standard administrative UI. The definitions-only posture is final for v1.2 and v1.3; revisiting requires a new decision.
after_payload.context: Opening question of how to express deployment-time security in CRM Builder's YAML schema. The fundamental fork: does the YAML own role and team *definitions* (the structures — what each role can access, which teams exist) AND user-to-role and user-to-team *assignments* (the membership state — which users belong to which roles and teams), or only definitions, with assignments treated as runtime data managed in the target CRM UI? Both options are defensible: the definitions-plus-assignments model fully describes the running security posture from the deployment artifact, but requires a YAML edit and Configure run for every new user. The definitions-only model leaves runtime user management in the CRM UI but matches the industry pattern of treating IAM policy as code and identity membership as runtime state.
after_payload.decision: **Adopt definitions-only.** YAML declares role and team structures and their permissions (scope-level access, system permissions, field-level permissions when v1.3 ships). User-to-role and user-to-team assignments are runtime data managed exclusively in the target CRM UI; they are not represented in any YAML file and not touched by the deploy pipeline. Adding a new user (e.g., onboarding a new mentor at CBM) does not require a YAML edit or a Configure run — the user is created in the CRM UI and assigned to roles and teams in the CRM UI.
after_payload.rationale: Matches the prevailing IAM industry pattern (policy as code, identity membership as runtime state). For CBM's operational reality (mentors onboarded continuously as recruitment progresses), the alternative would require a YAML commit, push, and Configure run for every new mentor — operationally heavier than the structural benefit it provides. The structure of the security posture is fully described in the YAML (which roles exist, what each can do, which teams exist); the membership state lives in the CRM where it changes most frequently and where the CRM UI already provides the management surface. Decouples the deployment artifact's churn rate from the user population's churn rate.
after_payload.title: YAML security scope: definitions only, not user assignments

after_payload.alternatives_considered: **Option A — narrow v1.2.** Ship the existing plan (Parts A–D as originally drafted) without scope-level or teams. Scope-level access and teams remain Manual Configuration line items. Rejected because the deployment artifact cannot describe a meaningful security posture without scope-level access — the Mentor role cannot be expressed at all (every role would either see all entities or none). The cost of waiting on field-level to ship anything at all is too high. **Option B — expanded v1.2.** Ship the existing plan plus scope-level access plus teams in a single v1.2 release. Defensible but doubles the v1.2 surface area, delays delivery of either piece, and forces field-level into a release before the additional pilot input the original plan flagged as needed has accrued. Rejected on delivery speed and on the principle that field-level deserves more pilot data before commitment.
after_payload.consequences: Gap-analysis Section 6 amended end-to-end (commit eb1d7e7) — Q1, Q2, Q3 amended; Q7 and Q8 added; five v1.2 parts (A–E) and a deferred-to-v1.3 section. UPDATE-PROMPT for the next conversation authored at PRDs/product/UPDATE-PROMPT-yaml-schema-v1.2-category-6.md (commit f152181; SHA fix at 4ff2e43) directing the drafting of app-yaml-schema.md v1.2 sections covering Parts A–E. The v1.2 implementation prompt series for Claude Code (analogous to v1.1's archived A–H series at PRDs/_archive/yaml-schema-prompts/) is the downstream artifact for the conversation after that — covering loader extensions, validator extensions, role_manager.py and team_manager.py, and API client extensions for /api/v1/Role and /api/v1/Team. The v1.3 field-level + presets work is queued behind the v1.2 implementation series. MR-pilot items MR-MC-AC-001/002 remain on the MANUAL CONFIGURATION list through v1.2 release.
after_payload.context: The existing Category 6 plan in PRDs/product/yaml-schema-gap-analysis-MR-pilot.md targeted YAML schema v1.2 for the entire Role-Based Access Control category — Part A (roles declaration), Part B (field-level permissions, the original target driven by MR-MC-AC-001/002), Part C (permission presets), and Part D (panel and layout role-aware visibility, added 05-03-26). Verification against the actual codebase in this conversation confirmed: (1) the loader at espo_impl/core/config_loader.py recognizes only version, description, content_version, entities, and relationships — no security-related top-level keys; (2) validate_program() applies no top-level key whitelist, so an unknown security: block parses as a silent no-op; (3) no role manager, team manager, or API client wrappers exist; (4) the EspoCRM REST API exposes Role and Team as first-class CRUD entities at /api/v1/Role and /api/v1/Team. The strawman drafted earlier in the conversation covered scope-level entity access (CRUD per role at all/team/own/no), teams, and system permissions — none of which appeared in the existing v1.2 plan. The existing v1.2 plan covered field-level access; the strawman did not. The two scopes are complementary, not competing.
after_payload.decision: **Adopt Option C — scope-level + teams as v1.2; field-level shifts to v1.3.** Category 6 splits across two schema versions. v1.2 ships five parts: Part A — Roles declaration (kept from original plan); Part B — Teams declaration (NEW); Part C — Scope-level entity access with whitelist semantics (NEW); Part D — System permissions including assignment_permission, user_permission, export, mass_update, audit_log (NEW); Part E — Panel and layout role-aware visibility (renumbered from original Part D, content preserved). v1.3 ships the deferred field-level read/edit permissions (was original Part B) and named permission presets (was original Part C). Dedicated security file is renamed from programs/roles.yaml to programs/security.yaml to reflect the roles + teams scope.
after_payload.rationale: Scope-level access and teams are universal to every role in every domain (every role needs entity-level CRUD bounds; every team-scope access level requires a team to exist) and translate directly to target-CRM role and team primitives without needing condition-expression infrastructure. Field-level access is the harder problem (state-dependent permissions, asymmetric per-role per-field overrides, cross-CRM mapping ambiguity, condition-expression dependency) and the original plan's own commentary acknowledged that field-level 'deserves careful design across at least one more pilot domain before commitment.' Reordering ships meaningful security capability now (the Mentor role can be expressed in YAML for the first time, even if imperfectly) instead of waiting on the full field-level design. The MR-pilot's two field-level items (MR-MC-AC-001/002 — ten admin-only fields and ten state-dependent mentor-editable fields) remain Manual Configuration through v1.2 and ship at v1.3.
after_payload.title: Category 6 reordering: v1.2 ships scope-level access + teams (Parts A–E); v1.3 ships field-level + presets

after_payload.artifacts_produced: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/deposit_event.md (initial draft v1.0); PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-build-planning-kickoff.md (initial draft v1.0); six decisions DEC-155 through DEC-160; seven references (six decided_in plus one is_about to PI-022); zero new planning items.
