after_payload.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Design the `deposit_event` entity type schema for V2's storage layer. Drive a structured architectural discussion that produces one deliverable: `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/deposit_event.md` — the complete schema specification per the template in `governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md`. Cadence matches the methodology workstream's schema-design conversations and the user-interface planning conversations: structured architectural discussion driven one decision at a time, building toward the specification section by section. At conversation close: decisions written via direct API as decision records; any deferred items written as planning items; one session record written by Doug through the V2 desktop New Session dialog per the session-record-at-close pattern. This conversation also produces one additional artifact: the kickoff prompt for the build-planning conversation that closes the workstream."

Structured summary of the architectural discussion:

The conversation drove six architectural decisions, one at a time, with full discussion at each step before the user's terse approval. The six decisions and their resolutions:

(1) DEC-155 — Identifier prefix and format. Adopted DEP-NNN zero-padded to three digits. Three-letter form on the strength of the dominant three-letter precedent (DEC, REF, COP). Pairs naturally with COP for the family-3 deposit-bucket reading. Alternatives DEPT, DEPOS, EVT, APPLY rejected for disambiguation, length, or semantic mismatch.

(2) DEC-156 — Lifecycle shape. The primary architectural decision of the conversation. The user pushed back on the initial recommendation (born-terminal append-only) by asking about continuous-status visibility for failure diagnosis. Discussion surfaced three real failure scenarios (clean failure handled equally by both options; unclean crash where workflow-staged would capture stuck-running state; long apply where continuous status would help). A third synthesis option emerged: born-terminal append-only with diagnostic log file capture via a deposit_event_log_file_path field. The user adopted the synthesis. Establishes a new cross-spec precedent: born-terminal append-only with creation as the event-recording moment — a third lifecycle category alongside workflow-shaped and documentary.

(3) DEC-157 — Back-reference mechanism. Adopted references-table edges with one new generic-verb kind deposit_event_wrote_record spanning four target types (session, decision, planning_item, reference) in one vocabulary entry. Rejected per-target-type variants (A′), JSON column (B), dedicated back-reference table (C), target-side columns (D) on grounds of references-edge precedent, established generic-verb naming pattern, and minimum-viable schema growth. Settled cardinality, 409-SKIP semantics (skipped records not back-referenced), back-reference scope (substantive content layer only, no recursive back-references).

(4) DEC-158 — Re-apply semantics. Adopted multi-event-per-payload: a close_out_payload may carry zero or more inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edges, one per apply attempt. Establishes a second new cross-spec precedent: born-terminal append-only entities admit multi-event-per-target-record. Relaxes the close_out_payload spec's at-most-one default (which that spec section 3.8.3 explicitly deferred to this conversation). Surfaced as a cross-spec consistency finding for the build-planning conversation's section 7.2 reconciliation. The close_out_payload's ready → applied transition fires on the first inbound DEP with outcome=success; subsequent DEPs (failures, re-confirmations) don't move the close_out_payload's status.

(5) DEC-159 — Field inventory. Captured the nine-column shape (identifier, title, description, outcome, records_summary, error_info, apply_context, log_file_path, created_at). Adopted _outcome over _status for semantic precision (born-terminal fact vs. workflow state). Declined _notes (incompatible with immutable record), _updated_at and _deleted_at (born-terminal append-only deviation), denormalized total-records-written count (derivable). JSON shape for records_summary, error_info, apply_context documented in spec but not SQL-enforced.

(6) DEC-160 — API surface, UI, soft-delete posture, acceptance criteria. Reduced API surface (POST + GET only). Read-only UI audit log with no Create/Edit/Delete dialogs, Outcome filter combo in master-pane toolbar, master pane sorts identifier descending (audit-log deviation), right-click context menu reduced to two read affordances. Plain-text log_file_path display (no in-app viewer). Strict no-Create-dialog (backfill via PI-022 implementation script). Fifteen acceptance criteria captured.

After all six decisions settled, the spec was drafted end-to-end at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/deposit_event.md (approximately 290 lines following the spec guide template). The build-planning conversation's kickoff prompt was drafted at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-build-planning-kickoff.md (the additional deliverable required at this conversation's close per the kickoff prompt's framing).

The conversation surfaced no new planning items. PI-022 (authored by SES-046) continues to cover retroactive backfill of governance entity records, refined by the build-planning conversation per the deposit_event spec section 3.8.2 working assumptions. One cross-spec consistency finding surfaced for the build-planning conversation's section 7.2 reconciliation: the close_out_payload spec's at-most-one inbound edge default needs revision to zero-or-more.

after_payload.alternatives_considered: DEPT (four letters) — rejected for visual collision with 'Department' in unrelated contexts. DEPOS (five letters) — rejected for length-without-disambiguation; no parallel five-letter precedent exists in governance or methodology. EVT (three letters) — rejected for reading as generic 'event'; loses the deposit-bucket family connotation. APPLY (five letters) — rejected for reading as a verb rather than entity name; collides with the apply script and apply-close-out prompt series naming. DP (two letters) — not seriously considered (too short; reads as initials).
after_payload.consequences: DEP becomes the canonical identifier prefix for the new entity type. Format DEP-NNN matches every other governance prefixed identifier in V2. No vocabulary or naming conflicts surface in the cross-spec consistency check. The COP/DEP pairing makes the family-3 deposit-bucket reading visually obvious in audit queries and database snapshots.
after_payload.context: The deposit_event entity is the sixth and final governance entity type designed in the governance-entity schema-design workstream. Per the spec guide section 6 cross-spec consistency conventions, the identifier prefix is 2-to-5 uppercase letters, zero-padded to three digits, and must not collide with the existing prefix list (DEC, SES, RSK, PI, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT, PROC, CRMC, ENG, WS, CONV, RB, WT, COP). The kickoff prompt named DEP as the working assumption with DEPOS, DEPT, EVT, APPLY as alternatives raised.
after_payload.decision: Adopt DEP as the identifier prefix with format DEP-NNN zero-padded to three digits (e.g., DEP-001, DEP-054).
after_payload.rationale: Three-letter form matches the dominant three-letter precedent (DEC, REF, COP, plus the two-letter exceptions WS, WT, RB and the four-letter PROC). Pairs naturally with COP for the family-3 deposit-bucket reading: a COP-NNN payload is applied by DEP-NNN-or-other events. Survives the collision check against the current prefix list. Per DEC-123's affirmation that each downstream conversation makes its own prefix-length call within the 2-to-5 letter range, three letters is appropriate here.
after_payload.title: deposit_event identifier prefix and format: DEP-NNN

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option B — workflow-staged multi-status (running → success/failure/aborted, with per-status timestamps, PUT/PATCH admitted during running, edit dialogs for the status transition). Rejected on the grounds that (a) the 'running' state lasts ~3 seconds and is essentially never observed in operation, (b) PUT/PATCH endpoints and editable dialogs contradict the fact-about-an-event intuition the entity is meant to capture, (c) the unclean-crash visibility benefit it would provide is small relative to the cost (apply runs interactively in ~3 seconds; crashes are rare and the terminal output is right there), (d) it would reopen the precedent the predecessor specs anticipated. Option A — born-terminal append-only without log capture. Rejected after the user's pushback as too thin on diagnostic detail: a crashed-mid-flight apply would leave no governance record and no log file to consult; the operator would have only the (potentially-closed) terminal output. The Option C synthesis (born-terminal append-only WITH log capture) addresses both concerns.
after_payload.consequences: Establishes a new cross-spec precedent: born-terminal append-only with creation as the event-recording moment. A third lifecycle category for governance entities. Cascading effects: deposit_event has _outcome (not _status), single _created_at timestamp (no per-status timestamps), POST + GET only API surface, no PUT/PATCH/DELETE, no soft-delete, read-only UI with no Create/Edit/Delete dialogs. The apply script (apply_close_out.py) requires modification per Slice D of the build-planning conversation: write stdout to a log file during apply, POST the deposit_event record at end with outcome and all diagnostic fields populated. A new directory (PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/deposit-event-logs/) is established as the canonical log file home; whether files there are git-tracked or gitignored is a build-planning policy decision.
after_payload.context: The primary architectural decision of the conversation. The kickoff prompt named a four-state workflow lifecycle (running, success, failure, aborted) as a working assumption while explicitly noting that 'the deposit_event entity is expected to introduce the append-only-with-one-timestamp precedent' per the close_out_payload spec section 3.4.6 and work_ticket spec section 3.4.3 anticipations. The choice cascades into status/outcome field semantics, timestamp count, API surface (PUT/PATCH admitted or not), and UI shape (read-only audit log versus editable workflow panel). The user pushed back on the initial recommendation by asking about continuous-status visibility for failure diagnosis, prompting a synthesis option to be put on the table.
after_payload.decision: Adopt born-terminal append-only with diagnostic log capture. The deposit_event record is POSTed by the apply script at apply completion with outcome and all diagnostic fields populated; no intermediate 'running' state; no PUT, no PATCH, no DELETE; single timestamp (deposit_event_created_at) carrying the event moment. A new deposit_event_log_file_path field (repo-relative path per the file-pointer precedent from reference_book/work_ticket/close_out_payload) points at a stdout transcript captured during apply, providing durable diagnostic detail without status-transition machinery.
after_payload.rationale: Born-terminal append-only realizes the precedent anticipated by predecessor specs (close_out_payload section 3.4.6, work_ticket section 3.4.3), matches the operational shape of apply (creation IS the event), and yields the smallest possible API and UI surface for an entity whose purpose is durable auditing rather than workflow tracking. The 'running' intermediate state has no operational meaning for a 3-second apply and would introduce edit machinery incompatible with the fact-recording intent. The log file synthesis addresses the user's continuous-status concern by capturing diagnostic detail in a separate durable artifact (the log file) rather than forcing the entity itself into a workflow lifecycle. The synthesis preserves the structural simplicity of born-terminal append-only while addressing the legitimate diagnostic visibility need that motivated the user's pushback. Establishes a new cross-spec precedent — born-terminal append-only with creation as the event-recording moment — a third lifecycle category alongside workflow-shaped (workstream, conversation, close_out_payload, work_ticket) and documentary (reference_book).
after_payload.title: deposit_event lifecycle shape: born-terminal append-only with diagnostic log capture (new cross-spec precedent)

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Per-target-type kinds within references-table (deposit_event_wrote_session, deposit_event_wrote_decision, etc., four kinds, four pair clauses) — rejected for unnecessary vocabulary growth when a single conceptual relationship is being modeled. JSON column on deposit_events table (deposit_event_records_written array of target_type/target_id pairs) — rejected for bypassing V2's relationship-vocabulary discipline, making the reverse query JSON-search-bound rather than a direct refs-table query. Dedicated back-reference table (new deposit_event_records table with deposit_event_identifier, target_type, target_id columns) — rejected for introducing a parallel relationship-storage mechanism outside the references-table discipline without compensating benefit. Target-side back-reference columns (written_by_deposit_event_identifier on each of the five target tables) — rejected for inverting the references-edge precedent, requiring migrations on established methodology/governance entity tables, and making the forward query a fan-out across all target tables.
after_payload.consequences: One new vocabulary entry in REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS: deposit_event_wrote_record. One new _kinds_for_pair clause: source_type='deposit_event' and target_type in ('session', 'decision', 'planning_item', 'reference') admits the kind. Plus the parent-linkage kind deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload from DEC-159's field inventory. Apply script flow at end-of-apply: step 5 creates the deposit_event record, step 6 emits one back-reference edge per HTTP-200/201-created record (409-SKIPped records are not back-referenced). Cardinality: one DEP → many records (zero or more); one record → zero or one DEP (zero if created outside the apply path, one if created by apply). The back-reference edges themselves are NOT recursively back-referenced (the recursion stops at the substantive content layer). Future expansion to additional target entity types is one-line additions to the _kinds_for_pair clause.
after_payload.context: Each apply attempt creates zero or more records in the database (a session, decisions, planning_items, references, possibly other governance entity types in the future). The deposit_event record needs to point at those records so 'what did this apply create?' and 'which apply created this decision?' are first-class governance queries. The kickoff prompt named four alternative mechanisms: references-table edges (the working assumption per the established DEC-124 references-edge cross-spec precedent), JSON column on the deposit_events table, dedicated back-reference table, or target-side columns. The references-table option further admits a sub-choice between a single generic kind and per-target-type kinds.
after_payload.decision: Adopt references-table edges with one new generic-verb cross-cutting kind, deposit_event_wrote_record, spanning four target types (session, decision, planning_item, reference) in a single vocabulary entry and a single _kinds_for_pair clause covering all four pairs.
after_payload.rationale: Continues the DEC-124 references-edge-over-foreign-key cross-spec precedent locked by SES-048. Adds the minimum viable schema growth (one vocab entry, one pair rule). Supports both directional queries via the existing references-table machinery: 'which records did DEP-001 write?' (forward) and 'which DEP wrote DEC-117?' (reverse) are both single refs-table queries. Single generic kind chosen over per-target-type variants on the strength of (a) cross-cutting nature — the relationship intentionally spans multiple target types and a single conceptual kind captures that intent; (b) established generic-verb naming pattern in the existing vocabulary (references, is_about, supersedes, decided_in all use generic-verb naming without target-type binding); (c) minimum-viable schema growth. Per DEC-133's frequency-justified test, the generic single-kind variant is the right choice for this cross-cutting cross-target-type relationship.
after_payload.title: deposit_event back-reference mechanism: references-table edges with one new generic-verb kind deposit_event_wrote_record

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option B — at-most-one (one DEP per COP, single-success rule). The apply script checks for an existing successful DEP and refuses to create a second one. Rejected because it forces re-apply attempts into ghost runs with no governance record, contradicting the audit purpose of the entity and the close_out_payload spec section 3.8.3's invitation to relax. Option C — at-most-one-success-but-multiple-failures. First successful apply creates DEP-1; subsequent successful re-applies refused; failed applies admitted. Rejected because the distinction between 'successful re-apply' and 'failed re-apply' has no operational meaning at the access layer (a successful re-confirmation IS valuable governance information; refusing to record it is artificial).
after_payload.consequences: Establishes a new cross-spec precedent: born-terminal append-only entities admit multi-event-per-target-record. Surfaces a cross-spec consistency finding for the build-planning conversation's section 7.2 reconciliation: the close_out_payload spec section 3.3.2 and section 3.4.3 carry at-most-one language that needs revision to zero-or-more. Per the kickoff's no-inline-amendment rule, this conversation does not edit close_out_payload.md; the revision is handled at the build-planning conversation's consistency gate. The deposit_events table grows linearly with apply attempts (not exponentially); most COPs will have exactly one DEP, some debugged ones will have a small number. The access layer's atomic transaction on POST /deposit-events with outcome=success against a ready COP transitions the COP atomically; POSTs against an already-applied COP succeed without COP transition; POSTs with outcome=failure leave the COP unchanged regardless of pre-state.
after_payload.context: The close_out_payload spec section 3.8.3 adopted at-most-one inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edge as the safe default and explicitly deferred the call to this conversation. DEC-156's born-terminal append-only shape forces the question into the open: each apply attempt either creates a new immutable DEP record (multi-event), or the apply script refuses to create a second one (at-most-one). The apply script is already idempotent on re-run (HTTP 409 SKIPs for already-present records), and re-apply is an operationally meaningful pattern (verification, retry-after-partial-failure, debugging). The choice affects edge cardinality, the close_out_payload's status transition rule, and what audit history the deposit_events table captures.
after_payload.decision: Adopt multi-event-per-payload. Each apply attempt creates a new deposit_event record. A close_out_payload may carry zero or more inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edges, one per apply attempt. The close_out_payload's ready → applied transition fires on the first inbound DEP with outcome=success; subsequent DEPs (failures or re-confirmations) do not move the close_out_payload's status (it's already at applied and terminal per the truly-terminal precedent). Failed DEPs against a ready close_out_payload create a governance record of the failed attempt but do not transition the close_out_payload.
after_payload.rationale: Naturally pairs with born-terminal append-only (DEC-156): each attempt is a fact about an event; multiple attempts are multiple facts. Aligns with the operational reality of re-apply (verification, retry, redeploy). Captures the full apply history of a payload as a governance-queryable artifact. The at-most-one alternative would force re-apply attempts into ghost runs with no governance record, contradicting the audit-log purpose of the deposit_events table. Establishes a new cross-spec precedent — born-terminal append-only entities admit multi-event-per-target-record — useful for any future workstream that revisits this producer/consumer pair shape.
after_payload.title: deposit_event re-apply semantics: multi-event-per-payload (new cross-spec precedent)

after_payload.alternatives_considered: _status field name (vocabulary consistency with workstream/conversation/close_out_payload/work_ticket) — rejected because the field's semantic shape is permanent fact, not workflow state; naming should follow semantics. Include _notes field — rejected because consultant scratchpad pattern is incompatible with immutable record. SQL-level JSON shape enforcement via SQLite's JSON1 extension — rejected for portability and engine-specific complexity. Retain base-schema _updated_at and _deleted_at columns locked to null/created — admissible at implementation but the spec declares no semantics (build-planning chooses implementation path). Denormalize total-records-written as a separate column — rejected as premature optimization; derivable from records_summary or edge count.
after_payload.consequences: The deposit_events table has nine columns total. The records_summary JSON shape is canonically {sessions: int, decisions: int, planning_items: int, references: int} with additive extension admitted. The error_info JSON shape carries kind, message, step, optional http_status, optional traceback when outcome=failure; null when outcome=success (access-layer cross-check). The apply_context JSON shape carries apply_script_version, invocation, runner. The log_file_path stores repo-relative paths under the canonical pattern PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/deposit-event-logs/dep_NNN.log. Field-naming convention break (_outcome rather than _status) is documented in the spec as a deliberate deviation; build-planning's section 7.2 consistency check confirms acceptance as the new precedent rather than violation.
after_payload.context: Field inventory is mostly cascading from prior decisions (DEC-156 born-terminal append-only fixes timestamp shape; DEC-157 fixes relationship modeling — no foreign-key columns). The conversation surfaced four sub-choices warranting explicit settlement: outcome field name (semantic precision vs. vocabulary consistency); inclusion of title/description fields for parity with predecessor entities; JSON shape lock-in for the three diagnostic JSON fields; base-schema timestamp deviation under full append-only.
after_payload.decision: Adopt the nine-column shape: deposit_event_identifier, deposit_event_title (auto-generated by apply script), deposit_event_description (auto-generated), deposit_event_outcome (enum success|failure), deposit_event_records_summary (JSON), deposit_event_error_info (JSON, conditional null/non-null on outcome), deposit_event_apply_context (JSON), deposit_event_log_file_path (repo-relative), deposit_event_created_at. Adopt _outcome over _status for semantic precision. Document the three JSON shapes (records_summary, error_info, apply_context) in spec language but not at the SQL constraint layer; Python helper module shares the shape between apply script and access layer. Deviate from base-schema timestamps: no _updated_at (record is immutable), no _deleted_at (no soft-delete; full append-only). Decline _notes (incompatible with immutable record), denormalized total-records-written count (derivable from records_summary sum or wrote_record edge count).
after_payload.rationale: Identity and content fields (identifier, title, description) provide parity with predecessor entities and avoid runtime computation in master-pane list rendering; auto-generation by the apply script removes consultant authoring overhead. _outcome field name reflects the semantic shape — born-terminal fact about an event rather than workflow state implying transitions; the naming distinction is the precedent being established. Three JSON diagnostic fields capture the rich detail of an apply attempt without committing to a fixed column schema that would need migration for shape evolution; SQL-level shape enforcement adds engine-specific complexity without compensating benefit (Python validator pattern is the canonical enforcement elsewhere in V2). Base-schema deviation on _updated_at and _deleted_at is the precedent being established — born-terminal append-only entities omit update and delete semantics. Field declines (_notes, denormalized count) follow from the entity nature (immutable record, derivable summary).
after_payload.title: deposit_event field inventory: nine columns including three diagnostic JSON fields; _outcome over _status; declines _notes, _updated_at, _deleted_at, denormalized total count

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Master-pane sort ascending (cross-panel consistency) — rejected for working against the audit-log read pattern. 'View log' button in detail pane opening the file in the platform's default viewer — rejected as introducing filesystem-access machinery that doesn't earn its complexity for a 30-record-lifetime entity; future-add if real friction emerges. UI Create dialog used exclusively for backfill — rejected as muddying the 'apply script is the only authoring path' intuition; the backfill script approach follows established precedent (apply_ses_011_planning_records.py).
after_payload.consequences: deposit_events route table registers POST and GET endpoints only. PUT, PATCH, DELETE, /restore return HTTP 405 (or HTTP 404 if framework auto-resolves; implementation choice for build-planning). Detail pane wires no Create/Edit/Delete/Restore dialogs and disables the references-create dialog's 'Add reference' affordance. Right-click context menu offers two read affordances. Master pane default sort is identifier descending. Outcome filter combo and badge styling for outcome are wired. Fifteen acceptance criteria establish the build-planning conversation's per-slice acceptance list aggregation baseline. PI-022 backfill is Slice E of the build-planning conversation's implementation plan, executed as a one-off script after the build slices land.
after_payload.context: API surface and UI shape cascade from prior decisions (DEC-156 born-terminal append-only forces POST + GET only; the read-only nature forces no Create/Edit/Delete dialogs). The conversation surfaced three sub-choices warranting explicit settlement: master-pane sort order (ascending consistency vs. descending audit-log nature); log-file viewer affordance (plain-text path vs. in-app button); backfill authoring path (strict no-Create-dialog vs. admit dialog for PI-022 backfill).
after_payload.decision: Adopt reduced API surface (POST + GET only, no PUT, no PATCH, no DELETE, no /restore). Adopt read-only UI audit log with no Create/Edit/Delete/Restore dialogs. Master pane: ListDetailPanel with columns identifier, title, outcome (badge-styled), created_at; Outcome filter combo (All/Success/Failure) in the master-pane toolbar; default sort identifier descending (audit-log deviation from V2's default ascending). Detail pane: vertical read-only layout rendering all fields plus the references section with parent-linkage edge prominent at top and wrote_record edges grouped by target_type with count badges; 'Add reference' affordance disabled. Right-click context menu reduced to two lightweight read affordances: Copy identifier, Copy log path. Plain-text log_file_path display with no in-app viewer in this release. Strict no-Create-dialog posture: PI-022 backfill execution via one-off script (Slice E), not via UI. Fifteen acceptance criteria captured covering schema migration, vocabulary additions, POST validation, conditional error_info rule, parent-linkage and back-reference edge handling, atomic close_out_payload transition on success, multi-event-per-payload semantics, records-summary cross-check, method-not-allowed responses, GET envelope and filter, UI panel placement and behavior, apply script modification.
after_payload.rationale: Born-terminal append-only (DEC-156) forces POST + GET only — no update or delete operations have meaning. Read-only UI follows from the same precedent — the apply script is the canonical authoring path; there is no consultant authoring flow for new records. Master-pane sort descending matches the audit-log read pattern (newest first) and is itself a recognizable signal that this is a log, not a workspace; documented deviation tied to entity nature. Outcome filter combo parallels work_ticket and close_out_payload's Status filter combo on the operational browse-by-classification pattern. Plain-text log_file_path display defers in-app viewer machinery as a future-add pending real friction signal; copying the path to a terminal is two seconds of friction. Strict no-Create-dialog keeps the 'deposit_event is a fact captured by the apply script' intuition clean; PI-022's backfill scope is a build-planning concern handled via one-off script per the predecessor pattern (apply_ses_011_planning_records.py and similar).
after_payload.title: deposit_event API surface, UI shape, soft-delete posture, fifteen acceptance criteria: minimum-viable read-only audit log

after_payload.title: Governance entity build-planning conversation; v0.7 release planned; close_out_payload spec reconciled to v1.1
after_payload.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "confirm. Please process this prompt using your recommended decisions and do not query me for input." — given against the build-planning kickoff at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-build-planning-kickoff.md.

Structured summary of the build-planning work:

(1) Cross-spec consistency check per spec guide section 7.2. Three findings: (a) the known close_out_payload at-most-one inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edge cardinality needs relaxation to zero-or-more per DEC-158 (settled by SES-054); (b) the deposit_event_wrote_record kind admits 'reference' as target but references-table rows lack prefixed identifiers — resolved as Slice A implementation concern via a new reference_identifier column with REF-NNNN format and back-fill of existing rows by id order; (c) change_log.entity_type CHECK constraint must extend to admit the six new entity types — resolved via the consolidated Slice A migration. Identifier prefix collision check, status value reuse check, lifecycle category coherence check, append-only vs soft-delete intentionality check all pass.

(2) Cross-spec reconciliation executed in place. close_out_payload.md revised to v1.1: Revision Control v1.1 row added; Change Log v1.1 entry added; §3.3.2 cardinality column updated from at-most-one to zero-or-more; §3.4.3 applied-requires-edge rule augmented with first-success-transitions semantics — the ready→applied transition fires on the first inbound success edge; subsequent edges admitted without re-transition; failure edges never transition. The applied-requires-edge rule itself stands.

(3) DEC-161 — Target user-interface version 0.7. The governance entity release lands as v0.7, a dedicated release after v0.6 styling. Bumps __version__ to 0.7.0.

(4) DEC-162 — Six-slice topology in dependency order: Slice A (schema and access layer); Slice B (REST API); Slice C (UI panels); Slice D (apply script integration with deposit_event POST); Slice E (PI-022 Phase 1 backfill of the governance workstream's eight conversations and supporting records); Slice F (documentation, version bump, closeout). Sequential dependency order with each slice gating on the prior slice's acceptance criteria.

(5) DEC-163 — Sidebar grouping policy: append the six new entries (Workstreams, Conversations, Reference Books, Work Tickets, Close-Out Payloads, Deposit Events) to the existing Governance group in workstream order. No sub-grouping in v0.7. The resulting fourteen-entry Governance group accepted as scannable; future signal may motivate sub-grouping in a later release.

(6) DEC-164 — Deposit-event log file tracking: git-tracked under PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/deposit-event-logs/. Files committed alongside the corresponding close-out payload commit. The diagnostic value of durable log capture earns the modest repo-size cost (~5KB per apply attempt).

(7) DEC-165 — Cross-spec consistency reconciliation: close_out_payload.md v1.1 admits zero-or-more inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edges per DEC-158. First-success-transitions semantics documented. In-place revision of close_out_payload.md executed during this conversation; the reconciliation is the build-planning equivalent of a v1.1 patch.

(8) DEC-166 — PI-022 refinement: phased backfill execution plan. Phase 1 in Slice E backfills the governance entity schema-design workstream's eight conversations, one workstream, eight close_out_payloads, eight deposit_events, eight work_tickets, and ten reference_books (with two RBs carrying two version rows each). Phases 2 through 4 (prior workstreams, prior conversations, prior payload/deposit history) deferred to follow-on planning items PI-023 through PI-025 authored at v0.7 build closeout, not here. PI-022 itself remains Open at v0.7 ship; discharge waits on completion of all phases.

(9) Four primary deliverables created end-to-end: governance-entity-PRD-v0.1.md (7-section PRD targeting v0.7); governance-entity-implementation-plan.md (six-slice plan with per-slice deliverables, acceptance criteria, cross-cutting concerns); six per-slice Claude Code prompts under prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.7-{A,B,C,D,E,F}-*.md; this close-out payload at close-out-payloads/ses_055.json plus the apply prompt at prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-055.md. Eleven files total committed and pushed.

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Bundle with the next multi-tenancy-routing-fix slice — rejected because the routing fix is parallel methodology work with no entity-type changes; bundling adds risk to both. Bundle with a hypothetical v0.7 'governance + routing' combined release — rejected for the same reason. Defer to v0.8 to bundle with as-yet-unnamed parallel work — rejected because the governance entities are ready to ship now and delay incurs governance-coverage debt.
after_payload.consequences: __version__ = '0.7.0' set in Slice F. README.md and crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md updated in Slice F. About dialog reflects v0.7 automatically via the version constant. The release tag v0.7.0 is pushed at Slice F close. v0.6 styling remains the baseline state until Slice A's migration applies; v0.7 lands additively without back-port concerns.
after_payload.context: The governance entity schema-design workstream produced six entity-type schema specifications across SES-048 through SES-054. The build-planning conversation must set the target user-interface version for the release that ships these entities. The active version sequence has v0.6 (styling) most recently shipped (__version__ = 0.6.0 in crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/__init__.py at this conversation's authoring time). The kickoff prompt named the target version as open at this writing.
after_payload.decision: Target user-interface version 0.7 for the governance entity release. The release is dedicated to the six new entity types (workstream, conversation, reference_book, work_ticket, close_out_payload, deposit_event) and their supporting infrastructure (vocabulary additions, apply-path integration, retroactive backfill of the governance workstream itself). __version__ bumps to 0.7.0 in Slice F.
after_payload.rationale: Dedicated release reads cleanly in the version history: v0.4 methodology entities, v0.5 engagement management, v0.6 styling, v0.7 governance entities. Bundling with later work (e.g., multi-tenancy routing fix slices in flight, Cleveland Business Mentors Planning Item 001) would muddy the entity-type release narrative and make per-slice testing harder. The six new entity types plus the apply-path integration plus the PI-022 Phase 1 backfill is already a substantial release; no need to dilute it.
after_payload.title: Target user-interface version 0.7 for the governance entity release

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Five-slice topology bundling Slice D with Slice E — rejected because the apply-script modification is independently valuable. Seven-slice topology splitting Slice A into two (schema migrations alone, then access layer) — rejected as over-decomposition; the migration plus the access layer that exercises it benefits from being authored together. Eight-slice topology with one entity type per slice — rejected because the entity types share infrastructure (vocab, migrations, panel base classes); per-entity slicing duplicates effort across slices.
after_payload.consequences: Six Claude Code execution prompts authored at this conversation's close (committed in this push). Doug executes them sequentially via Claude Code; each slice's acceptance criteria reviewed before the next slice opens. The total v0.7 effort is bounded by Slice A's complexity (largest by line count) and Slice E's data-volume risk (idempotency must be solid). Build closeout session lands at the end of Slice F.
after_payload.context: Six new entity types plus apply-path integration plus retroactive backfill plus documentation updates is too much for one slice; needs decomposition. The methodology-entity v0.4 release used a slice-per-entity topology (A foundation, B-E one entity each, F closeout); the v0.5 engagement-management used an A-E topology with the schema-and-API in Slice B; the v0.6 styling used an A-F topology with retrofit slices. The governance-entity release shares more structurally with v0.4 (multiple new entity types) but adds two complications: the apply-path modification (Slice D) and the retroactive backfill (Slice E).
after_payload.decision: Six-slice topology in strict sequential dependency order: Slice A (schema migrations, vocab.py update, ORM models, all six repository modules); Slice B (REST API routers and envelope handling); Slice C (desktop UI panels, sidebar integration, CRUD dialogs); Slice D (apply_close_out.py modifications, log file capture, atomic deposit_event POST); Slice E (PI-022 Phase 1 backfill via one-off script); Slice F (documentation updates, About-dialog version bump to 0.7.0, build closeout session, follow-on planning items). Each slice has its own Claude Code prompt under prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.7-{A..F}-*.md. Each slice gates on the prior slice's acceptance criteria passing.
after_payload.rationale: Six slices is the smallest decomposition where each slice has a coherent scope and an independent acceptance gate. Bundling A with B is feasible but inflates the slice; the API routers benefit from being authored with the access layer already shipped (clearer test boundary). Bundling D with E is feasible but the apply-script modification is a generally-useful change independent of the backfill, and bundling delays the routine close-out improvements until Phase 1 backfill is also done. Six independently revertable slices is the safest topology: a failure in any slice can be rolled back without affecting prior slices, and the application remains in a coherent state (e.g., if Slice C ships and Slice D fails, the API and UI work even though the apply path is still on the v0.6 behavior).
after_payload.title: Six-slice execution topology for v0.7 in sequential dependency order

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Sub-grouping into 'Governance — content' (charter, status, decisions, sessions, risks, planning items, topics, references) and 'Governance — workflow' (workstreams, conversations, reference_books, work_tickets, close_out_payloads, deposit_events) — rejected as over-decomposition for this release; deferred to signal. Reordering to alphabetize the combined group — rejected because alphabetical order obscures the workstream-order semantic. Introducing a divider between the existing eight and the new six — rejected as a low-value UI affordance.
after_payload.consequences: Slice C appends the six new sidebar entries to the existing Governance group in the named order. The sidebar configuration file (likely a Python dictionary or JSON config) gains six entries; no new sub-group code paths needed. If future user signal supports sub-grouping, the change is mechanical: introduce a sub-group rendering pattern, move six entries underneath. Until then, the fourteen-entry Governance group is the production layout.
after_payload.context: The existing Governance sidebar group has eight entries (charter, status, decisions, sessions, risks, planning items, topics, references). Adding the six new governance entity panels (Workstreams, Conversations, Reference Books, Work Tickets, Close-Out Payloads, Deposit Events) makes the group fourteen entries deep. The workstream-establishing conversation flagged this as an open question deferred to build-planning. Two reasonable approaches: append the new six at the end of the existing group with no sub-grouping (Option A); introduce a 'Governance — workflow' sub-group for the six new entries (Option B); reorder the existing group to bury or promote certain entries (Option C).
after_payload.decision: Append the six new entries to the existing Governance group in workstream order (Workstreams, Conversations, Reference Books, Work Tickets, Close-Out Payloads, Deposit Events). No sub-grouping in v0.7. The resulting fourteen-entry group accepted as scannable.
after_payload.rationale: Sub-grouping (Option B) introduces a new UI pattern that doesn't exist elsewhere in the application; the methodology sidebar group has four entries and the v0.5 engagement-management work didn't introduce sub-groups. Adopting sub-groups here sets a precedent that bleeds across other sidebar groups for inconsistency. The fourteen-entry Governance group is at the threshold of scannable; if signal emerges that users have difficulty finding entries, sub-grouping can be introduced in a future release as a sidebar refactoring. Workstream-order placement is the natural reading order: workstream → conversation → reference_book → work_ticket → close_out_payload → deposit_event mirrors the schema-design conversation sequence and the dependency order of the entities. Reordering the existing group (Option C) breaks muscle memory for existing users.
after_payload.title: Governance sidebar grouping for v0.7: append the six new entries in workstream order with no sub-grouping

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Gitignore the log files; store the path in deposit_event_log_file_path but never commit the file — rejected because the file value is wasted (the deposit_event record points at a file that may or may not exist on any given workstation). Track only failure-case logs; gitignore success-case logs — rejected as introducing per-file decision logic at commit time; failure cases are rare so the storage cost is dominated by success cases, and consistent tracking is simpler. Compress logs at commit time — rejected as premature optimization; raw text is searchable in git history without decompression.
after_payload.consequences: Slice D creates the deposit-event-logs/ directory with .gitkeep and README.md. Slice D's apply script modification writes the log file at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/deposit-event-logs/dep_NNN.log; the apply's caller (typically Claude Code or Doug running locally) commits the log file alongside the close-out payload commit. Slice E's backfill creates placeholder log files (dep_NNN-historical.log) for the eight historical applies that pre-date the entity type; each is a one-line file noting the log was not captured at apply time. The directory grows by one file per apply attempt going forward.
after_payload.context: The deposit_event schema (DEC-159) introduced a deposit_event_log_file_path column carrying the repo-relative path to a captured stdout log written by the apply script during each apply run. The deposit_event spec section 3.8.1 deferred to build-planning whether these log files are git-tracked (committed alongside the corresponding close-out payload commit) or gitignored (treated as ephemeral local artifacts with the path recorded but the file uncommitted).
after_payload.decision: Deposit-event log files are git-tracked under PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/deposit-event-logs/dep_NNN.log. Files are committed alongside the close-out payload commit (typically by the apply-script's caller). The directory carries a README.md and a .gitkeep to ensure the directory is present in fresh clones. No .gitignore patterns added for the log files themselves.
after_payload.rationale: The log file is the diagnostic record of an apply attempt. Failure-case logs especially are valuable: they capture the apply script's stdout up to the point of failure, including the HTTP request bodies and the API response payloads. Without git tracking, these logs are only available on the workstation that ran the apply — useless for code review or for retrospective debugging by anyone else. The cost is modest: ~5KB per apply attempt (the apply script's stdout for a typical close-out payload is a few dozen lines), and apply attempts are infrequent (one per close-out conversation, plus the occasional re-apply for verification). The total directory size after a year of v0.7 operation is on the order of a few hundred KB. Git tracking parallels the close-out payload files themselves (close-out-payloads/ses_NNN.json), which are also committed; the deposit-event log is the matching diagnostic artifact for the apply.
after_payload.title: Deposit-event log file tracking policy: git-tracked under PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/deposit-event-logs/

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Defer the reconciliation to a separate conversation — rejected because the inconsistency would block Slice A's repository implementation; the access-layer logic at close_out_payloads.py needs the settled cardinality. Issue a v1.0.1 patch rather than v1.1 — rejected because the spec guide doesn't recognize a three-segment versioning convention; v1.1 is the natural label for the post-audit revision (paralleling reference_book.md's v1.1 audit-correction pattern). Re-author the close_out_payload spec from scratch — rejected as wasteful; the substantive content is correct, only two paragraphs need revision.
after_payload.consequences: close_out_payload.md committed at v1.1. The Slice A repository implementation at close_out_payloads.py implements the first-success-transitions logic per the revised §3.4.3. The deposit_event-side repository at deposit_events.py drives the close_out_payload transition atomically via the access layer per the deposit_event spec §3.4.3. The reconciliation is the build-planning equivalent of a v1.1 patch and serves as the precedent for any future cross-spec consistency findings that surface late in a workstream's lifecycle.
after_payload.context: The deposit_event spec section 3.8.1 surfaced a cross-spec consistency finding for the build-planning conversation's section 7.2 reconciliation: the close_out_payload spec sections 3.3.2 and 3.4.3 carried at-most-one inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edge language that DEC-158 (SES-054 deposit_event Decision 2 — multi-event-per-payload precedent) supersedes. Section 3.8.3 of close_out_payload.md v1.0 explicitly invited the relaxation. The deposit_event-side spec settled the cardinality at zero-or-more; the close_out_payload-side spec needed in-place text revision to reflect the settlement.
after_payload.decision: Execute the cross-spec consistency reconciliation by revising close_out_payload.md in place to v1.1. Substantive changes: §3.3.2 cardinality column language updated from at-most-one to zero-or-more with first-success-transitions clarification; §3.4.3 applied-requires-edge rule augmented with the full first-success-transitions semantics — the ready→applied transition fires on the first inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edge from a deposit_event record with outcome=success; subsequent inbound edges (failures, re-confirmations from re-applies) admitted without further close_out_payload state change; failure edges never drive the transition. The applied-requires-edge rule itself stands — setting status to applied still requires at least one inbound success edge. Revision Control table gains a v1.1 row; Change Log gains a v1.1 entry; Last Updated and Status fields updated.
after_payload.rationale: The reconciliation is required for cross-spec coherence — the deposit_event spec describes multi-event-per-payload semantics that the close_out_payload spec must admit at the cardinality and state-transition levels. In-place revision is the natural mechanism per the spec guide section 8's revision-in-place posture for cross-spec consistency findings (as opposed to a new conversation). The first-success-transitions semantics make the state machine deterministic: only success drives the transition, only the first success drives it, subsequent edges (success or failure) admit without re-triggering. This matches the apply script's behavior (per the Slice D plan) and the access layer's intended atomic POST behavior.
after_payload.title: Cross-spec consistency reconciliation executed: close_out_payload.md v1.1 admits zero-or-more inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edges

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Single-phase full backfill in Slice E covering everything — rejected because the scope inflates Slice E (~200 records vs ~50 for Phase 1), increases risk of partial-backfill states, and delays v0.7 ship without delivering proportional value. Go-forward only (no backfill) — rejected because the governance workstream's own records are the proof of concept; not backfilling them leaves the application without a single workstream record to display in the new panels at v0.7 ship. Selective backfill (only workstream and conversation records, skipping work_ticket and reference_book) — rejected because the supporting records (kickoffs as work_tickets, schema specs as reference_books) are needed for the cross-entity navigation patterns to demonstrate.
after_payload.consequences: Slice E executes Phase 1 via the one-off script crmbuilder-v2/scripts/backfill_governance_phase_1.py per implementation plan §2.5. At Slice F closeout, three follow-on planning items authored: PI-023 (Phase 2 — prior workstreams), PI-024 (Phase 3 — prior conversations), PI-025 (Phase 4 — historical applies as deposit_events). Each follow-on PI references PI-022 via is_about edge. PI-022's status remains Open at v0.7 ship; the planning items panel shows it alongside the three new children. Future operational decisions about when to run Phases 2 through 4 are made on a per-phase basis; each phase is its own scoped effort.
after_payload.context: PI-022 (authored by SES-046, status Open) tracks retroactive migration of governance entity records for sessions, decisions, planning items, references, and prior workstreams already in the database. The planning item's description outlines six categories of backfill candidates and three resolution paths (go-forward only, selective backfill, full backfill with reconstructed outcomes). Refinement is the build-planning conversation's responsibility per the workstream plan section 5.4.
after_payload.decision: Refine PI-022 into a phased execution plan. Phase 1 executes in Slice E of v0.7: backfill the governance entity schema-design workstream itself (1 workstream, 8 conversations, 8 close_out_payloads, 8 deposit_events, 8 work_tickets, 10 reference_books with ~14 version rows, ~70 reference edges). Phases 2 through 4 deferred to follow-on planning items authored at the v0.7 build closeout session: Phase 2 (PI-023) — prior workstreams (methodology entity schema-design, v0.5 engagement management, v0.6 styling, multi-tenancy routing fix, Cleveland Business Mentors paper test, catalog ingestion); Phase 3 (PI-024) — prior conversations SES-001 through SES-045 and their kickoffs as work_tickets where reconstructable; Phase 4 (PI-025) — historical applies as deposit_events for the ~38 prior close-out payload files. PI-022 itself remains Open at v0.7 ship; discharge waits on completion of all phases.
after_payload.rationale: Phase 1 is the proof-of-end-to-end test that the new entity types work against real content — and the content closest to hand is the workstream that designed them. The eight conversations (SES-047 through SES-052, SES-054, plus this conversation SES-055) plus their supporting records exercises every new entity type, every new relationship kind, and every access-layer rule. Failures in Phase 1 surface immediately and block Slice F's closeout — exactly the right gating. Phases 2 through 4 are operationally valuable but lower-stakes: they backfill records that are already operating without governance entities (the conversations happened, the payloads applied, the project moved forward); discovering issues in Phase 1 first means Phases 2 through 4 can run against a hardened implementation. Each subsequent phase is scoped at its own time; phases need not run sequentially and can be parallelized across workstreams. PI-022 stays Open as the parent tracker; the three follow-on planning items (PI-023, PI-024, PI-025) are the discharge children.
after_payload.title: PI-022 refinement: phased retroactive backfill plan; Phase 1 in Slice E covers the governance entity schema-design workstream itself

after_payload.summary: v0.7 ships. 24 slice commits plus 1 amend, every slice's tests green at slice close. The transaction-correctness fix is the most consequential engineering decision: every governance edge-rule failure now rolls back cleanly. The full access+api suite runs faster under explicit transactions (107s vs slower prior). WS-001 transitions to complete as the final step. Phases 2-4 of PI-022 are authored as PI-024 (prior workstreams), PI-025 (prior conversations), PI-026 (historical applies as deposit_events) for future execution.
after_payload.title: v0.7 governance entity release shipped
after_payload.topics_covered: v0.7 governance entity release executed across six slices: Slice A (schema migrations, vocab.py, ORM models, six repositories, ~55 access-layer tests); Slice B (six routers + GET /references filter fix + ~9 API tests; uncovered and fixed the pysqlite SAVEPOINT autocommit bug that left orphan rows on edge-rule validation failure — explicit BEGIN IMMEDIATE + busy_timeout per the SQLAlchemy SQLite recipe; full access + api suite green at 628 tests); Slice C (StorageClient gained 47 governance methods, six panels, five CRUD dialogs with edge-aware create flows for conversation/close_out_payload, sidebar appended with six entries, 14 UI smoke tests); Slice D (apply_close_out.py tees stdout to deposit-event-logs/dep_NNN.log and POSTs a deposit_event atomically as its last step; 3 script tests); Slice E (PI-022 Phase 1 backfill executed live against the dogfood CRMBUILDER.db — 1 workstream, 8 conversations, 10 reference books with 12 version rows, 8 work_tickets, 8 close_out_payloads, 8 deposit_events, ~70 reference edges; idempotent on re-run; exporter updated to snapshot the seven governance tables); Slice F (version bump to 0.7.0, README + CLAUDE.md updates, this closeout). The release closes the gap between the v2 governance database's stated single-source-of-truth role and its actual coverage of the planning-and-execution machinery itself.

after_payload.alternatives_considered: (a) Restructure every governance repository to validate edges before the autoassign insert (impossible because edges reference the not-yet-assigned identifier). (b) Replace the SAVEPOINT pattern with a single explicit BEGIN-then-INSERT-with-retry-on-IntegrityError without nesting (works but rewrites the well-precedented autoassign across every repo). (c) Add a 'remove orphan on rollback' compensating action in every repository (fragile, can't fully reverse side effects). (d) Accept the orphan rows and document the limitation (violates the access layer's transaction contract and would surface as data corruption under any real edge-rule failure in production). Option (e), the SQLAlchemy SQLite recipe, addresses the root cause at the engine layer and is what the bug actually warrants.
after_payload.consequences: Every transaction begins with BEGIN IMMEDIATE; the connection is in non-autocommit mode (controlled by SQLAlchemy). Concurrent writers wait up to 5s on the lock before erroring. Existing tests stay green (no regressions across the 628-test suite). Future engine work in the per-engagement DB inherits the same correctness. If a real performance problem surfaces under heavy concurrent read load (extremely unlikely for the project's workload), the busy_timeout can be tuned or BEGIN switched to DEFERRED with the deadlock risk re-evaluated.
after_payload.context: Slice B testing uncovered that a 422 failure on a governance create left an orphaned partial row (verified: a conversation POST missing the workstream-membership edge persisted CONV-001 despite the API returning 422 and the session_scope context manager calling session.rollback()). Root cause: the governance repositories use the v0.4 SAVEPOINT-based autoassign pattern (begin_nested + savepoint.commit for collision-safe identifier assignment) followed by post-insert edge-rule validation. Under pysqlite's default autocommit emulation, RELEASE SAVEPOINT durably commits to disk because no outer BEGIN was ever issued; a later session.rollback() then has nothing to undo. The methodology repos never exercised the pattern because they validate before insert, so the bug was latent until the governance entities' insert-then-validate-edges shape exposed it.
after_payload.decision: Apply the standard SQLAlchemy SQLite transaction-control recipe in access/db.py: in the connect handler, set dbapi_conn.isolation_level=None to disable pysqlite's autocommit emulation and add PRAGMA busy_timeout=5000 so contended writers queue rather than fail fast; in a new 'begin' event handler, emit 'BEGIN IMMEDIATE' explicitly so concurrent writers acquire the RESERVED lock at transaction start (avoiding the deferred-then-upgrade deadlock the eight-thread concurrent-POST tests would otherwise hit).
after_payload.rationale: The fix is the SQLAlchemy-documented recipe for SQLite plus explicit transactions, applies one foundational change (the engine in access/db.py) that makes the entire access layer's transaction semantics correct, and lets every governance repository's validate-after-insert pattern work as written. The methodology repos get the same correctness for free even though they never exposed the bug. The full access+api suite runs faster under explicit transactions (~107s vs ~7min for access alone previously) because the explicit transactions are more consistent for SQLAlchemy's flush ordering. The trade-off is BEGIN IMMEDIATE acquires the RESERVED lock at transaction start even for read-only sessions, mildly serializing reads against writes; for the project's desktop/single-user/test loads this is operationally invisible and is what the busy_timeout exists to absorb.
after_payload.title: SQLite transaction-control fix — explicit BEGIN IMMEDIATE + busy_timeout for proper rollback

after_payload.description:
DEC-117 through DEC-120 (later expanded through DEC-160 across the seven-conversation governance entity schema-design workstream, SES-046 through SES-054) introduced six new entity types: workstream, conversation, reference book, work ticket, close-out payload, deposit event. The v0.7 governance entity release (SES-055 build-planning conversation; built across Slices A through F per `governance-entity-implementation-plan.md`; closed at SES-056) shipped these entity types live in the dogfood CRMBUILDER database. The retroactive-migration question this planning item originally posed was resolved by DEC-166 (SES-055) as: yes, phased backfill, executed per child planning item scope at operational discretion.

**Phase 1: Complete (v0.7 Slice E).** The governance entity schema-design workstream's own records backfilled live against CRMBUILDER.db via `crmbuilder-v2/scripts/backfill_governance_phase_1.py`: 1 workstream (WS-001), 8 conversations (CONV-001..008 covering SES-047 through SES-055), 10 reference books (RB-001..010, with RB-005 and RB-007 each carrying two version rows), 8 work tickets (WT-001..008 for the eight kickoff prompts), 8 close-out payloads (COP-001..008), 8 deposit events (DEP-001..008 historical reconstructions, plus DEP-009 from the SES-056 closeout apply), and ~70 reference edges connecting them. The backfill script is idempotent on re-run.

**Phases 2 through 4: Tracked in child planning items, deferred to operational signal.** PI-024 — Phase 2: backfill prior workstreams (methodology entity schema-design, v0.5 engagement management, v0.6 styling, multi-tenancy routing fix, Cleveland Business Mentors paper test, catalog ingestion). PI-025 — Phase 3: backfill prior conversations SES-001 through SES-045 and their kickoffs as work_tickets where reconstructable. PI-026 — Phase 4: backfill historical applies as deposit_events for the ~38 pre-v0.7 close-out payload files. Each child carries an is_about reference edge to this parent (created at SES-056 closeout).

**Status posture.** This planning item remains Open as the parent tracker; discharge waits on completion of all three child phases. The original question this item posed is resolved; what remains is the per-phase execution work scoped under the child items. When all three phases ship, this parent's status moves to Resolved with resolution_reference pointing at the conversation that closes the final phase.
after_payload.title: Retroactive migration: whether to backfill governance entity records for sessions, decisions, planning items, references, and prior workstreams already in the database

before_payload.description:
DEC-117 through DEC-120 introduce six new entity types (workstream, conversation, reference book, work ticket, close-out payload, deposit event). After the schema-design workstream lands the entities and a build implements them in the database, the question is whether to retroactively populate records for pre-existing project state, or only go forward from the moment the entities ship.

Scope of the retroactive question:

(a) **Workstream records for completed workstreams.** Examples: the methodology entity schema-design workstream (SES-011..015 plus the SES-017 build-planning conversation plus the SES-019..024 v0.4 build conversations); the v0.5 engagement management workstream (SES-026..032 spanning multiple conversations); the v0.6 styling workstream (SES-036..043 spanning conversation 1, conversation 2, and seven build-execution conversations); the multi-tenancy routing fix workstream (SES-044..045 plus slice B); the catalog ingestion workstream (SES-016 or wherever it landed). Each has a clear identity, member conversations, and an outcome.

(b) **Conversation records for completed sessions.** All 45 existing sessions (SES-001 through SES-045) had a conversation behind them; each conversation has metadata (purpose, position-in-workstream where applicable, kickoff prompt path where one existed, predecessor and successor).

(c) **Reference book records for existing reference documents.** Workstream master plans, Product Requirements Documents (v0.1 through v0.6), implementation plans, methodology guides, schema specifications (the four methodology entity specs plus the soon-to-arrive six governance entity specs), the session-startup entry document (`crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md`), the apply script (`crmbuilder-v2/scripts/apply_close_out.py`).

(d) **Work ticket records for existing kickoff and execution prompts.** The four methodology schema-design kickoffs (domain, entity, process, crm_candidate), the methodology workstream-establishing planning prompt, the v0.4 through v0.6 planning prompts, all per-slice Claude Code execution prompts under `prompts/`.

(e) **Close-out payload records for existing payloads.** Every `close-out-payloads/ses_NNN.json` file (there are ~38 of them).

(f) **Deposit event records for past applies.** Reconstructable from commit history (git log of the relevant payload-apply commits), but the apply outcomes (success/partial/failure) are not in the commit log; outcome reconstruction would be assumptive (assume success unless commit message indicates otherwise).

Resolution paths to consider at the workstream-establishing conversation or at the per-entity schema-design conversations:

1. **Go-forward only.** No backfill. Record entities exist only for work that happens after the entities ship. Pre-existing project state has no governance entity records. Queries about pre-shipped state continue to work via the existing free-text mentions in session records.

2. **Selective backfill.** Backfill the entity types where reconstruction is high-fidelity (workstream, conversation, reference book, work ticket, close-out payload) but skip deposit event because reconstructing outcomes is assumptive.

3. **Full backfill with reconstructed outcomes.** Backfill all six entity types including deposit events; outcome reconstruction tags assumptive entries explicitly.

Resolution is not blocking for the workstream itself — the schema design proceeds with the entities defined for go-forward use; backfill is a follow-on decision at the build-planning conversation or later.

Discharge: closed when a decision is reached and (if backfill is chosen) the backfill is executed.
before_payload.title: Retroactive migration: whether to backfill governance entity records for sessions, decisions, planning items, references, and prior workstreams already in the database
