after_payload.title: audit-v1.2 planning resolved: §9 open questions and security.yaml placement decided, planning doc v1.1 through v1.3 authored; rebased from SES-059 to SES-060 to clear PI-024 collision
after_payload.topics_covered:
Seed: Doug uploaded audit-v1.2-planning.md v1.0 (the kickoff product of SES-058) for review and continuation. No accompanying user message text; the project's standing system prompt set ARCHITECTURE mode and provided the close-out protocol.

Structured summary of the resolution conversation:

(1) Initial review surfaced three architectural concerns not captured in v1.0. First: Prompt H referenced 'Alembic-style migration' for the new role and team client-schema tables. The project does not use Alembic; the actual mechanism is a homegrown versioned migration runner at automation/db/migrations.py with a schema_version table and existing _client_v1 through _client_v3 migrations. The fix is a new _client_v4 migration following the existing pattern. Second: §4.1 established security.yaml as a distinct artifact separate from per-entity YAMLs but did not pin where in a program file's directory structure it lives — root alongside per-entity YAMLs versus a security/ subdirectory. This affects both audit emission (Prompt H) and loader discovery (Prompt A). Third: §8.5 named the 11-prompt series-size risk but proposed no mitigation. Option worth considering: pause at Prompt E for a deploy-side validation milestone before starting the audit-side work in F through K. Doug approved working through the §9 open questions one at a time, with the three concerns flagged for incorporation.

(2) §9.3 — Default for include_security and include_filtered_tabs. Passed the two-part test (operator-facing impact; two viable defaults). Concrete example: Doug's next CBM audit after v1.2 ships, opening the dialog and seeing the new checkboxes in either checked or unchecked state. Options: default-on (consistent with existing AuditOptions pattern where every category-wide option defaults True) versus default-off (new capabilities opt-in to avoid surprising operators). Recommendation: default-on, on the rationale that the audit's identity is capture-everything and a default-off new capability is a quiet inconsistency in that identity with a bad failure mode (operators who never notice the new checkboxes get half-audits indefinitely). The v1.0 provisional default-False was overcautious for the actual operator situation: Doug is effectively the sole operator and will read the v1.2 release notes. Doug approved default-on. This is a revision from v1.0's provisional answer.

(3) §9.4 — Backward compatibility for existing audit YAML. Passed the two-part test (real risk of losing prior audit output; three genuinely different options). Concrete example: Doug audited CBM under v1.1 four weeks ago and got 8 per-entity YAMLs in audits/cbm-source/. What happens when v1.2 runs against the same source? Options: overwrite (latest snapshot replaces prior), alongside (versioned suffix or dated subdirectory, both versions coexist), merge (parse existing files and diff). Recommendation: overwrite with a one-line UX guard — the Audit dialog displays a pre-run confirmation when the output directory contains files matching the audit emission pattern. The merge option was rejected on maintenance grounds (diffing structural YAML is fragile across schema evolution); the alongside option was rejected because two YAML versions of the same entity coexisting in the same program root creates real deploy ambiguity. Doug approved overwrite with the confirmation guard.

(4) §9.1 and §9.2 resolved as routine decisions in a single turn (neither passed the two-part test). §9.1 persona cross-reference validation: kept as documentation metadata only, no validation. Cross-checking would require a source-of-truth list of Master PRD personas, which doesn't live anywhere the loader has access to and would couple the loader to client-specific PRD content. The failure mode of a typo'd persona name is documentation-reader-briefly-confused, not severe enough to justify the infrastructure or coupling. §9.2 empty scope_access: emit informational warning in the audit log when a captured role has no per-entity access. Matches existing audit log pattern for analogous edge cases (e.g., entity with no custom fields); gives operators visibility into roles that grant nothing on the source. Doug approved both.

(5) Planning doc v1.1 authored and pushed (commit 315801b). v1.1 rewrites §9 from open-question subsections to resolved-decision subsections, adds the Alembic→_client_v4 correction to Prompt H and threads the same migration version into Prompt I (one _client_v4 migration covers all v1.2 audit schema additions rather than separate versions per prompt), wires the §9.4 overwrite-confirmation dialog into §4.1 operator flow and Prompt J, and updates §10 to record that §9 is resolved while surfacing two workflow items (series-size mitigation and security.yaml placement) that were not yet decided.

(6) security.yaml file placement decision raised separately as the one remaining design question affecting Prompt A. Passed the two-part test (real loader-discovery and future-proofing impact; two genuinely different options). Concrete example: Doug audits CBM source under v1.2 with security enabled — output ends up either flat (audits/cbm-source/security.yaml alongside per-entity YAMLs at root) or grouped (audits/cbm-source/security/security.yaml under a dedicated subdirectory). Options: root (flat program directory, loader scans *.yaml at root) versus security/ subdirectory (loader scans root and security/ for *.yaml, future security-adjacent files anchor in the same folder). Recommendation: subdirectory, on the rationale that §7 already names v1.4 deferred work (Section 12.7 field-level permissions and permission presets) that will produce additional security-related files; doing it now costs a small loader convention; doing it later costs an output migration for any program previously audited under v1.2. Doug approved subdirectory.

(7) Planning doc v1.2 authored and pushed (commit c9ebcda). v1.2 threads the security/ subdirectory placement through §4.1 step 8 (emission path), §4.1 step 5 and §9.4 consequence (overwrite-confirmation trigger now matches *.yaml at root OR security/*.yaml under the subdirectory), Prompt A's loader scan (root plus security/), Prompt H's emission target, and Prompt J's overwrite-trigger pattern. §10 is trimmed to remove the resolved placement bullet, leaving only the series-size workflow question as deliberately uncaptured in the doc.

(8) Identifier collision discovered and rebased. After v1.2 pushed (15:28 UTC), the close-out payload was authored as ses_059.json with DEC-175..179. On the next pull-rebase before push, origin/main had advanced to commit 44182d1 (Doug pushed at 15:32 UTC) — the PI-024 prior-workstreams backfill conversation had run concurrently in another sandbox and pushed its own SES-059 close-out first, with DEC-175 through DEC-177 covering inventory, lifecycle-date strategy, and master-plan reference_book bundling. The pull-rebase silently overwrote this conversation's ses_059.json with PI-024's version. Resolution: rebase this conversation's records to SES-060 with DEC-178..182, re-author ses_060.json with content identical to the lost ses_059.json apart from identifier shifts, and bump the planning doc to v1.3 to fix the §10 reference 'SES-059 (DEC-175 through DEC-179)' to 'SES-060 (DEC-178 through DEC-182)'. The v1.3 bump is a pure rebase with no design content change.

(9) Series-size mitigation (§8.5) deliberately not resolved. Whether to pause at Prompt E for a deploy-side validation milestone before starting Prompts F through K is a workflow choice for Doug to make at execution time, not a design decision that belongs in the planning document. The doc neither requires nor forbids the pause. Surfaced in §10 for visibility but left to Doug.

(10) Next-conversation kickoff. The next conversation opens against the planning document at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-automation-PRD/audit-v1.2-planning.md (now at v1.3) and produces CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-audit-v1.2-A-roles-teams-recognition.md as the PI-034 deliverable.

(11) Lesson learned about concurrent sandbox identifier allocation. The next-available-identifier check against the engagement's db-export snapshot is necessary but not sufficient when multiple Claude.ai sandboxes are running concurrently. Both this conversation and the PI-024 backfill conversation observed the same head (SES-058 from the snapshot, with SES-058's apply not yet run locally, so functionally SES-057 was the latest applied head) and independently allocated SES-059. The collision was only detected at push time via pull-rebase. Out of scope for this conversation but flagged as a methodology issue worth tracking — the planning kickoff for the Code Change Lifecycle workstream (SES-057, PI-027 through PI-033) may be the natural home for this concern since it owns the commit-and-deposit-event entity work.

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option A — validate against a configured list of known personas, warning on unknown values. Rejected: introduces a configuration surface (where does the list live? per-client? in the program YAML itself?) and a coupling between the loader and client-specific PRD content for marginal benefit. Option B — validate against a list inferred from other YAML content. Rejected as too clever; persona: is metadata, not a structural reference.
after_payload.consequences: No persona-validation logic in config_loader.py. No new dependency from the loader on any client-specific document list. Prompts A, B, and D treat persona: as opaque metadata. If a future feature wants persona-aware validation (for example, a CRM Methodology Auditor that checks YAML against Master PRD content), it can build that as a separate validator outside the deploy-engine loader.
after_payload.context: Schema v1.3 Section 12.1 defines a persona: field on RoleDefinition as 'documentation metadata — the loader does not cross-check the identifier against any source.' The audit-v1.2 workstream needs to decide whether to honor this schema specification verbatim or to optionally extend the loader to validate persona: values against a known list (such as Master PRD personas for the client implementation) and warn on unknown values. v1.0 of audit-v1.2-planning.md carried this as §9.1 with a provisional answer of no validation. SES-060 formalizes the resolution.
after_payload.decision: No validation. The loader treats persona: as opaque documentation metadata. The audit captures whatever value the source instance carries and emits it as-is. No cross-checking against any list, no warnings on unrecognized values, no new dependency from the loader on client-specific document content.
after_payload.rationale: Schema Section 12.1 is explicit that persona: is metadata. Cross-checking it would require a source-of-truth list of Master PRD personas, which doesn't live anywhere the loader currently has access to and would couple the loader to client-specific PRD content (a coupling the rest of the loader carefully avoids). The failure mode of a typo'd persona name is 'documentation reader briefly confused' — minor and self-correcting. The validation infrastructure and the loader-to-PRD coupling are not justified by that failure mode.
after_payload.title: RoleDefinition.persona is documentation metadata only — no loader validation

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option A — silent capture, no log line. Rejected: operator visibility into degenerate cases is cheap and useful. Option B — warning emitted as a YAML comment in the output. Rejected: intrusive on every redeploy. Option C — elevate to warning severity that surfaces in the audit summary panel. Rejected: implies a problem where there isn't one.
after_payload.consequences: Prompt H's _write_yaml_files() emits the informational log line for every captured role with empty scope_access. The validator, the loader, and the deploy side are unaffected — they handle empty scope_access roles identically to non-empty ones. The audit progress dialog's summary line at end of run mentions the warning count if any informational warnings were emitted.
after_payload.context: If a source EspoCRM instance has a role with no per-entity access — for example, a placeholder role kept for documentation purposes or a role that was created and never had scope_access wired up — the v1.2 audit captures it as a role with an empty scope_access: map. The schema allows this construct (an empty mapping is valid). The audit-v1.2-planning.md v1.0 §9.2 carried the open question: should the audit surface this case to the operator, and if so, how? v1.0's provisional answer was yes-as-informational-log-warning. SES-060 formalizes the resolution.
after_payload.decision: Yes, informational warning in the audit log, not in the YAML output. When the audit captures a role with empty scope_access, the audit log records a line of the form 'Role X has no scope_access; this role grants no entity access on the source instance.' The YAML output itself is unaffected — the role is emitted with whatever its source state was, including the empty scope_access mapping. The warning level is informational, not error or warning-severity.
after_payload.rationale: Matches the existing audit log pattern for analogous edge cases (e.g., entity with no custom fields, layout with no rows). Gives operators visibility into 'this role grants nothing on the source and probably wants cleanup' without altering the audited content. Putting the warning in the YAML output would be intrusive (operators would have to remove a YAML comment or warning block on every redeploy), and elevating the severity to warning or error would imply a deploy-blocking condition where there isn't one — an empty scope_access role is valid YAML that deploys cleanly.
after_payload.title: Captured role with empty scope_access produces an informational audit-log warning

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option A — default False per v1.0 provisional answer. Rejected on grounds above. Option B — split default (one True, one False, e.g., filtered tabs default True because lower behavioral footprint, security default False because larger). Rejected as arbitrary; both new options sit at the same conceptual level as existing options, and splitting defaults invites confusion. Option C — prompt operator on first v1.2 run to choose defaults. Rejected as over-engineered.
after_payload.consequences: Prompts H, I, and J use default=True for the new AuditOptions booleans. Prompt K's documentation includes a release-notes mention of the new defaults so operators know what to expect on the first v1.2 run. First v1.2 audit run after the workstream lands produces security.yaml (under security/) and filteredTabs: blocks automatically. Operators who specifically don't want this output uncheck the boxes once and the AuditOptions persistence preserves that choice on subsequent runs (where AuditOptions persistence already exists; if not, that's a separate feature).
after_payload.context: Audit-v1.2 adds two new category-wide AuditOptions booleans: include_security (gates the role and team discovery added in Prompt H) and include_filtered_tabs (gates the filtered-tab discovery added in Prompt I). The Audit dialog exposes these as checkboxes. v1.0 of audit-v1.2-planning.md §9.3 carried the open question: should these new checkboxes default to checked or unchecked? v1.0's provisional answer was default False, on the rationale of avoiding surprising operators on the first v1.2 run. SES-060 revises this.
after_payload.decision: Both new AuditOptions booleans default to True. The Audit dialog's Filtered tabs and Security checkboxes start checked when the dialog opens. Operators opt out by unchecking. This matches the existing pattern — every current AuditOptions category-wide boolean defaults True.
after_payload.rationale: The audit's product identity is 'capture the full configuration of a source instance for round-trip deploy.' Every existing AuditOptions boolean defaults True, expressing that identity in code. A default-off new capability is a quiet inconsistency in that identity and has a bad failure mode: operators who never notice the new checkboxes get half-audits indefinitely without realizing what's missing. The 'default-off avoids surprising operators' reasoning that produced v1.0's provisional answer was overcautious for the actual operator situation — Doug is effectively the sole operator at this stage and will read the v1.2 release notes. The one-time surprise of new artifacts appearing on the first v1.2 run is preferable to silent half-audits in perpetuity.
after_payload.title: AuditOptions.include_security and include_filtered_tabs default to True

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option A — overwrite without any guard. Rejected: minor UX cost, real safety benefit. Option B — alongside output (versioned suffix or dated subdirectory). Rejected: file proliferation and deploy ambiguity. Option C — merge (parse existing, diff, emit merged output). Rejected on maintenance grounds.
after_payload.consequences: Prompt J implements the confirmation dialog as part of the entity-picker UI work. The dialog's trigger condition uniformly matches *.yaml at root and security/*.yaml under the subdirectory. Operators who want to preserve prior audit output have two options: (a) cancel the dialog and manually rename or move the output directory before re-running, (b) point the audit at a different output directory. Neither is automated by the audit; both are operator responsibility.
after_payload.context: v1.2 audit runs against a source instance whose output directory may already contain artifacts from a prior v1.1 audit run — per-entity YAMLs without filteredTabs: blocks, no security.yaml, possibly with operator edits. v1.0 of audit-v1.2-planning.md §9.4 carried the open question: what happens to the existing files? Three options were framed: overwrite (latest snapshot replaces prior), alongside (versioned suffix or dated subdirectory, both versions coexist in the same root), merge (parse existing files and emit files merging new blocks into existing content). v1.0's provisional answer was overwrite. SES-060 formalizes the resolution and adds a UX guard.
after_payload.decision: Overwrite, with a pre-run confirmation guard. When the operator clicks Run Audit and the output directory contains files matching the audit emission pattern (any file matching *.yaml at the output directory root OR security/*.yaml under the subdirectory), the Audit dialog displays an overwrite-confirmation prompt: 'Output directory contains N existing audit YAML files; running this audit will overwrite them. Proceed?' The dialog defaults focus to Cancel; the operator must explicitly click Proceed to continue. Empty output directories run without the prompt.
after_payload.rationale: Audited YAML is a snapshot, not source code. An audit run is the operator's explicit request to capture the current state of the source instance. Hand-editing audit output is an anti-pattern; the right place to fork audit output is into a separate working directory under the operator's control. But the audit itself doesn't enforce or document that, and there is a real failure mode of losing operator edits on a subsequent audit run. The confirmation guard catches that failure mode for a one-line UX cost. The merge option was rejected on maintenance grounds (diffing structural YAML is fragile across schema evolution, renamed fields, reordered blocks); the alongside option was rejected because two YAML versions of the same entity coexisting in the same program root creates real deploy ambiguity.
after_payload.title: Existing audit output is overwritten with a pre-run confirmation guard

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option A — root placement, security.yaml alongside per-entity YAMLs. Rejected on future-proofing grounds detailed above. Option B — per-entity security blocks embedded in the entity YAML rather than a separate file. Rejected because roles and teams are not entity-scoped; the data shape doesn't fit. Option C — named subdirectories per security category (security/roles/, security/teams/) anticipating v1.4. Rejected as premature; v1.4 may or may not produce that structure, and over-anticipating creates its own migration burden.
after_payload.consequences: Prompt A extends config_loader.py's scan to include security/*.yaml under the subdirectory. Prompt H emits to <output_dir>/security/security.yaml. Prompt J's overwrite-confirmation trigger pattern matches both root *.yaml and security/*.yaml uniformly. The user-guide update in Prompt K calls out the subdirectory convention. v1.4 work picks up the same subdirectory for any additional security-related YAML it produces. Programs audited under v1.2 that need to be hand-edited or forked to a working directory should preserve the security/ subdirectory structure to avoid loader confusion.
after_payload.context: v1.2 audit emits a new artifact, security.yaml, containing the roles: and teams: blocks captured from the source instance. The placement of this file in a program directory is consequential for two reasons: it determines where the loader looks for the file (Prompt A's config_loader.py scan), and it sets a precedent for where future security-related files land. Section 12.7 (field-level permissions and permission presets) is named in audit-v1.2-planning.md §7 as deferred v1.4 work and will likely produce additional security-related files. v1.1 of audit-v1.2-planning.md §10 surfaced this question separately from the §9 resolutions. SES-060 resolves it.
after_payload.decision: security.yaml lives in a security/ subdirectory of the program directory, not at root alongside per-entity YAMLs. The loader's program-directory scan is extended to scan both root *.yaml and security/*.yaml. The audit emits to <output_dir>/security/security.yaml, creating the subdirectory if missing. The overwrite-confirmation trigger pattern (DEC-181) matches files at either location. The subdirectory is conventional — the parser does not require any specific filename within it; any YAML in security/ is parsed by the same loader logic as root YAML.
after_payload.rationale: v1.4 deferred work (Section 12.7) will produce more security-related files (permission presets, possibly field-level permission overlays). Anchoring those files in a single subdirectory now is cheaper than migrating v1.2 outputs when v1.4 lands. The cost is a small loader convention (operators learn that security YAMLs live in security/, not at root like entity YAMLs) and one extra scan target in config_loader.py. The alternative — flat placement at root — would either (a) clutter v1.4 program directories with mixed entity and security files, or (b) require migrating all v1.2-audited outputs when v1.4 ships. Both are worse than the small loader convention being adopted now.
after_payload.title: security.yaml lives in a security/ subdirectory of the program directory

after_payload.deposit_event_description: Applied COP-060. Outcome: success. 5 decisions, 1 sessions.

after_payload.artifacts_produced: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md v1.0 (the design authority for the seven-stage Code Change Lifecycle chain); PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-commit.md (work_ticket seeding the PI-028 commit entity schema-design conversation); PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_061.json (this payload); PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-061.md (apply prompt). Eight decisions DEC-183 through DEC-190. Zero new planning items (PI-028 through PI-033 already exist from SES-057). Eleven references: eight decided_in linking each decision to SES-061, one is_about from SES-061 to PI-028 (next planning item in the workstream sequence), one is_about from SES-061 to the methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md reference_book (note: that reference_book record will be authored by PI-033 backfill, not in this conversation; the reference here is forward-declared by identifier — see in-flight note), one supersedes from this conversation to SES-057 (this conversation succeeds SES-057 in the workstream's conversation chain).
after_payload.conversation_reference: Claude.ai planning conversation. Opened against the kickoff document at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/code-change-lifecycle-workstream-establishing-kickoff.md, authored by predecessor SES-057. The kickoff established the Code Change Lifecycle workstream (per DEC-168 adopting Option III, first-class commit entities) and named eight design decisions deferred for settlement in this conversation: commit identifier strategy, granularity of resolves edges, commit ingestion trigger, commit record fields, multi-repo and multi-engagement scoping, typed addresses relationship_kind, work_ticket authoring rule scope, and direction/name of the blocks relationship_kind. The conversation ran in ARCHITECTURE mode (project default) and worked through the eight decisions in sequence using the consequential decision template, with each decision recommendation grounded in the actual state of the dogfood database (db-export snapshots queried via Python directly; no MCP server in this sandbox). Five of eight decisions ran full 8-element templates (#1 #2 #3 #5 #8); three were decide-and-announce because the options did not pass part-two of the two-part test (#4 commit fields, #6 addresses typed kind, #7 work_ticket scope rule — though #7 was settled with strong reference to the existing work_ticket spec's pre-authored canonical examples). Three artifacts produced: the methodology document, the PI-028 commit schema kickoff, and the close-out payload itself. The PI-027 resolution posture was settled in passing as option (a) — defer to PI-033 retroactive resolve — to keep PI-027 honest as a normative-but-not-yet-implemented planning item, preserving the diagnostic signal of 'planning items in Resolved status without resolves edges are data-integrity violations.'
after_payload.in_flight_at_end: Code Change Lifecycle workstream now in its second phase: methodology-authored, schema-spec-pending. The next conversation opens against PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-commit.md to produce PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/commit.md per the seven-section governance schema spec format. PI-028 stays Open at the end of that conversation (same payload-format-gap reason as PI-027). After PI-028 closes, PI-029 schema migration work begins as a Claude Code execution series; the first prompt CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-pi-029-A-commits-table-and-vocab.md is authored by PI-028's close-out or by a fresh planning conversation depending on PI-028's time-and-context budget. Subsequent planning items PI-030 (close-out payload extension and apply script update and enumerate_commits helper), PI-031 (Commits panel UI), PI-032 (methodology rollout including CLAUDE.md updates), PI-033 (historical backfill including this PI-027 retroactive resolve) execute in strict dependency order per DEC-170. Out-of-scope items deferred per methodology section 1: workstreams that produce no commits stay outside the lifecycle's audit-chain scope; cross-engagement commit queries beyond the per-engagement boundary stay deferred unless a use case surfaces.
after_payload.title: Code Change Lifecycle methodology drafted: eight deferred design decisions settled, methodology document v1.0 authored, PI-028 commit entity schema kickoff produced
after_payload.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: kickoff document at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/code-change-lifecycle-workstream-establishing-kickoff.md, opening this conversation against PI-027 to draft methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md and settle eight deferred design decisions, with the PI-028 commit entity schema kickoff as the closing deliverable.

Structured summary of the methodology drafting conversation:

(1) Identifier collision and three-step renumbering. The kickoff was authored by SES-057 anticipating SES-058 and DEC-171..178 for this conversation. The first rebase came when a parallel sandbox completed the audit feature v1.2 planning workstream and queued ses_058.json (claiming SES-058 + DEC-171..174 + PI-034..044); this conversation rebased to SES-059 + DEC-175..182. The second rebase came on push attempt — a parallel sandbox completed the PI-024 prior-workstreams backfill planning workstream and pushed first as ses_059.json (claiming SES-059 + DEC-175..177); this conversation rebased to SES-060 + DEC-178..185. The third rebase came on the next push attempt — yet another parallel sandbox completed the audit-v1.2 §9 resolutions conversation and pushed ses_060.json (claiming SES-060 + DEC-178..182); this conversation rebased to SES-061 + DEC-183..190. The final identifier assignments survived push verification, and applies must run in this order: SES-058 audit feature; SES-059 PI-024 backfill; SES-060 audit-v1.2 resolutions; SES-061 this conversation. Each apply requires its predecessors. The renumbering experience itself surfaces a methodology question for PI-032 to address: parallel-sandbox conversations cannot reserve identifier ranges in advance; reserve-at-apply-time is the only collision-free option. Doug acknowledged each rebase by proceeding.

(2) Decision 1 — commit identifier strategy. Three options framed: (a) CM-NNN sequence with SHA stored separately, (b) SHA-as-identifier with full 40-char SHA as primary key, (c) both — CM-NNN as identifier with SHA as unique secondary key plus /by-sha/{sha} lookup endpoint. Recommended option (a) refined to four-digit padding (CM-NNNN matching REF-NNNN's high-volume pattern) with the /by-sha/{sha} convenience endpoint borrowed from option (c). Rationale: uniformity with the rest of the governance vocabulary (SES-NNN, DEC-NNN, PI-NNN, WT-NNN, WS-NNN, REF-NNNN) beats direct git navigability; the convenience endpoint takes the friction out of the only direction (SHA-to-CM-NNNN) that matters operationally. Doug confirmed.

(3) Decision 2 — granularity of resolves edges. Three options framed: (a) per-commit only, (b) per-conversation only, (c) both. Recommended option (b): single resolves kind for conversation-to-planning_item, with multi-conversation work handled via a separate addresses kind (extended to conversation-to-planning_item as well as work_ticket-to-planning_item in Decision 6). Rationale: per-commit grain forces an authoring judgment Doug should not have to make at close-out time ('did this kickoff commit really contribute or just the methodology commit?'); per-conversation grain matches the natural unit at which resolution is decided and at which planning_item.status transitions. The two-join SQL for 'commits that resolved PI-NNN' is operationally fine. Unique constraint on (target_type, target_id, relationship_kind='resolves') ensures a planning_item is resolved by at most one conversation. Doug confirmed.

(4) Decision 3 — commit ingestion trigger. Four options framed: (a) manual payload enumeration, (b) git post-commit hook, (c) background polling, (d) apply-time git scan. Surfaced a fifth option (a-prime): payload-declared with mechanical enumeration helper at close-out authoring time — combines auditability of (a) with no-miss quality of (d). Recommended (a-prime): the close-out payload contains a commits array enumerating each commit by SHA; a helper command (tools/governance/enumerate_commits.py or inline 'git log --pretty' snippet) runs at close-out authoring time to populate the section from 'git log <last-applied-sha>..HEAD' per touched repo. No hooks, no polling, no apply-time scanning. The 'last applied SHA' is derived per repo by querying GET /commits?commit_repository=<name>&sort=commit_committed_at:desc&limit=1; no separate state table needed. Apply-script idempotency: UNIQUE on commit_sha rejects duplicates with HTTP 409. Doug confirmed.

(5) Decision 4 — commit record fields. Did not pass two-part test (impact real but per-field options are 'include or defer'; outcomes don't materially differ). Decided and announced as a batch: v0.8 fields are commit_identifier, commit_sha (UNIQUE), commit_message_first_line, commit_message_full, commit_author_name, commit_author_email, commit_committed_at (from %cI), commit_repository, commit_branch, commit_parent_shas (JSON array — replaces kickoff's singular 'parent_sha' to handle merge commits cleanly), commit_files_changed_count, commit_conversation_id (FK), plus governance-standard timestamps. Deferred to v0.9: commit_files_changed_paths, commit_signed_by, commit_committer_name/email, commit_message_trailers. The 'parent_shas as JSON array' refinement of the kickoff's singular 'parent_sha' was a side recommendation accepted in the same turn. Doug confirmed.

(6) Decision 5 — multi-repo and multi-engagement scoping. Four options framed: (a) repo-scoped commits with cross-engagement references, (a-prime) conversation-scoped commits (all live in the conversation's engagement DB with repository column distinguishing physical repo), (b) per-engagement child records, (c) shared meta-DB. Recommended (a-prime). Rationale: engagements are accounting units; every commit lives with its conversation; no cross-engagement references for commits; no new shared-DB pattern. Edge case named: a CBM-engagement conversation that incidentally commits in crmbuilder (rare — dogfood scope but possible) lives in CBM engagement DB regardless. PI-033 back-fill scope: each historical commit assigned to its historical conversation, which lives in one engagement; the commit goes in that engagement. A cross-engagement 'where is this SHA?' lookup utility is cheap (iterate the commits tables of each engagement DB) but deferred until a use case surfaces. Doug confirmed.

(7) Decision 6 — typed addresses relationship_kind. Did not pass two-part test (the functional outcomes of 'addresses' vs 'is_about with type filters' don't materially differ; only the query-clarity reading differs). Decided and announced. Option (a): add addresses as a typed relationship_kind admitting two source/target pairs — (work_ticket, planning_item) (the kickoff's narrow case) and (conversation, planning_item) (the multi-conversation case from Decision 2). Many-to-many cardinality on both sides. Existing v0.7 backfill's eight work_ticket-to-planning_item is_about edges migrate to addresses as part of PI-033. DEC-133's frequency-justified deferral applies to speculative vocabulary; addresses has named query patterns and a committed use site in this workstream, so deferral test does not trigger. Doug confirmed.

(8) Decision 7 — work_ticket authoring rule scope. Three options framed: (a) narrow (Claude Code prompts only, ~168 historical), (b) middle (Claude Code plus kickoff/planning-prompt files, ~195 historical), (c) broad (every single-use seed file including SESSION-PROMPT-* and UPDATE-PROMPT-*, ~221 historical). Recommended (c) broad. Rationale: the work_ticket spec's work_ticket_kind closed enum has values kickoff_prompt, claude_code_prompt, ad_hoc_prompt, other — designed for all three categories; the spec's canonical-examples narrative names files from all three categories; the spec author intent was already broad. Filename pattern classifies: CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-*.md → claude_code_prompt; *-kickoff*.md and *-planning-prompt*.md → kickoff_prompt; SESSION-PROMPT-* and UPDATE-PROMPT-* → ad_hoc_prompt; other → other. Back-fill cost: ~213 net new records under (c) versus ~160 under (a) — half-day to one-day difference; not material. Going-forward enforcement is documented in PI-032's methodology rollout. Doug confirmed.

(9) Decision 8 — direction and name of the blocks relationship_kind. Three options framed: (a) keep blocks with 'source blocks target = target waits for source' convention (natural English; existing two rows must be swapped to match), (b) keep blocks with inverted convention (existing rows match as-is; future authors learn the inverted convention), (c) rename blocks to blocked_by (existing rows keep source/target endpoints; only kind value migrates; semantics match natural English with no convention to memorize). Recommended (c) blocked_by. Rationale: a self-documenting kind name removes the need for future readers to consult the methodology to interpret an edge; conventions that disagree with natural English get violated in the long run. The two existing rows REF-0357 (source=PI-025 target=PI-024) and REF-0358 (source=PI-026 target=PI-025) keep endpoints; their kind value migrates from 'blocks' to 'blocked_by'. Folded into PI-029's Alembic migration. Doug confirmed.

(10) PI-027 resolution posture. Per kickoff section 'Close-out expectations,' two options for handling this conversation's own resolution given the payload format gap: (a) defer (leave PI-027 Open in this close-out; PI-033 resolves it retroactively once resolves_planning_items payload section ships in PI-030), or (b) hand-flip PI-027's status via direct API after apply. Methodology section 9 adopts (a). Rationale: the honesty of 'the methodology specifies a model the schema does not yet support' matters more than the visual neatness of a flipped status; the diagnostic that motivated the workstream ('PI-022 is the only Resolved planning item, and it has no resolves edge') would be impossible to detect under (b); adopting (a) preserves the diagnostic signal through this conversation's tail.

(11) Methodology document authored. PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md v1.0 (this conversation) is the design authority for the seven-stage Code Change Lifecycle chain. Nine sections: Purpose and Scope; the Seven-Stage Lifecycle (planning item → planning conversation → execution conversation opens against work_ticket → execution conversation produces commits → close-out records resolves/addresses/work_tickets/commits → apply transaction lands records → downstream queries walk chain); Vocabulary Additions (commit entity type, resolves and addresses kinds, blocks→blocked_by rename); Close-out Payload Format Extension (four new sections: commits, work_tickets, resolves_planning_items, addresses_planning_items; fixed apply order); Authoring Rules (when work_ticket is born/consumed/superseded; when planning_item is resolved; commit ingestion at close-out time; attribution rule for manual commits between close-outs); Audit Query Patterns (five canonical SQL examples covering PI→commits, commit→PIs, workstream→commits, indeterminate-state detection, work_ticket→PIs); Migration and Back-fill Posture (PI-033 scope: ~221 historical work_tickets, several hundred commits, retroactive resolves edges, two is_about-to-addresses migrations, two blocks-to-blocked_by rows); Known Gaps and Downstream Dependencies (PI-028 through PI-033 mapped to deliverables; methodology is normative-but-not-yet-implemented until PI-030 ships); PI-027 Resolution Posture (option a — defer).

(12) PI-028 commit entity schema kickoff authored. PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-commit.md is the work_ticket seeding the PI-028 schema-design conversation. The kickoff intentionally defers most architectural questions to the methodology document and surfaces only the rendering-into-schema-spec-format questions: lifecycle posture (documentary), cross-spec precedent contributions (three candidates: by-natural-key lookup pattern, JSON array column for variable-cardinality scalars, conversation-scoped accounting unit principle), field naming convention reaffirmation (parent-prefix per DEC-046), validation rules for natural-key columns (commit_sha 40-hex-lowercase, commit_repository non-empty), relationship rendering (commit_conversation_id as FK only, no typed refs kind per DEC-133 deferral test), acceptance criteria specific to commit, and derived endpoint candidates. Read list prioritizes methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md as authoritative; references work_ticket.md and reference_book.md as closest precedents; cross-references the spec-design template. Close-out expectations for PI-028 named: 3-6 decisions expected, PI-028 stays Open per the same PI-027 logic, and authoring the PI-029 Prompt A is named as an in-flight follow-on.

(13) Sandbox commit and push. The methodology document, PI-028 kickoff, ses_061.json close-out payload, and CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-061.md apply prompt are committed and pushed together in one commit per the sandbox push convention.

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option (b) SHA-as-identifier rejected — breaks the NNN-prefix uniformity for marginal gain; SHA ordering is hash-random not chronological; identifier-comparable queries (e.g., 'CM-101 came before CM-102') would not work. Option (c) full both-canonical rejected — having two equally-canonical references to one record forces every downstream code site to pick which is canonical for its use, multiplying complexity. Three-digit CM-NNN rejected on the volume argument; even a single moderately active workstream produces dozens of commits.
after_payload.consequences: Every commit record carries a four-digit CM-NNNN identifier assigned client-side at apply time. Audit-chain queries return CM-NNNN values; downstream code refers to commits by CM-NNNN in prose. The /by-sha/{sha} endpoint covers SHA-first callers (notably the git-log ingestion helper specified in DEC-185 and the future commits-panel UI). The high-volume nature of commits (versus other governance entities in the low hundreds) is acknowledged in the schema by choosing four-digit padding from inception.
after_payload.context: The Code Change Lifecycle workstream introduces the commit entity as a first-class governance record. The kickoff named three identifier-strategy options: (a) CM-NNN sequence-style identifier with SHA stored as a separate field, (b) SHA-as-identifier with the full or stable-prefix SHA as the primary key, (c) both — CM-NNN as identifier per the prefixed-identifier convention plus SHA as a unique secondary key with a /commits/by-sha/{sha} lookup endpoint. The decision shapes how every future query, prose discussion, apply-prompt reference, and audit-chain walk refers to commits.
after_payload.decision: Option (a) refined to four-digit padding plus the /by-sha/{sha} convenience endpoint from option (c). Commit identifier is CM-NNNN (four-digit zero-padded sequence; matches REF-NNNN's high-volume four-digit pattern; will not need bumping for at least 10,000 commits). The full 40-character SHA is stored in a separate commit_sha column with UNIQUE INDEX. A GET /commits/by-sha/{sha} convenience endpoint handles full or prefix-SHA lookups; prefix ambiguity returns HTTP 409 with the candidate list. Client-side identifier assignment per the existing prefixed-identifier convention (crmbuilder CLAUDE.md line 62).
after_payload.rationale: Uniformity with the rest of the governance vocabulary (SES-NNN, DEC-NNN, PI-NNN, WT-NNN, WS-NNN, REF-NNNN) beats direct git navigability. Every other governance entity uses the same NNN-prefixed shape; breaking the pattern for one entity type asks every reader to remember which entity gets which identifier shape. The /by-sha/{sha} convenience endpoint takes the friction out of the only direction (SHA-to-CM-NNNN) that matters operationally; routine audit-chain queries join through the CM-NNNN identifier and never see the SHA-translation hop. The four-digit choice (over three-digit CM-NNN) acknowledges that commits will be the highest-volume governance entity in the system and the four-digit padding aligns with REF-NNNN's already-established precedent.
after_payload.title: Commit entity identifier strategy: CM-NNNN with full SHA in a separate commit_sha field plus /by-sha/{sha} convenience lookup

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Per-commit only — accepts the authoring discipline cost in exchange for direct SQL; rejected on the discipline-burden argument and the resolved-status-overstating problem (10 contributing commits would suggest 10 'resolutions' when only one delivery happened). Both grains — accepts doubled edge volume for fastest SQL on both query directions; rejected as redundant since both directions are queryable under the per-conversation model with one extra join.
after_payload.consequences: Apply transaction generates exactly one resolves edge per resolved planning_item and atomically flips planning_item.status from Open to Resolved in the same transaction. Manual status flips outside the resolves-edge mechanism are forbidden by methodology (per methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md section 5.4). The diagnostic 'planning_items in Resolved status without a resolves edge' becomes a data-integrity violation signal that the diagnostic script flags. Multi-conversation contribution is captured via addresses edges (DEC-188) without doubling commit-level edge volume.
after_payload.context: A conversation that delivers a planning item commits possibly N times to deliver it. The resolution-declaration edge can live at commit grain (commit-to-planning_item), conversation grain (conversation-to-planning_item), or both. The choice shapes payload authoring discipline, edge volume, and the meaning of planning_item.status='Resolved'.
after_payload.decision: Per-conversation only. Single resolves relationship_kind admitting the pair (conversation, planning_item). Multi-conversation work — where work on a planning_item spans multiple conversations and only the final one resolves — uses a separate addresses kind (per DEC-188) admitting (conversation, planning_item) for the non-terminal contributing conversations. The resolves edge is unique per planning_item (UNIQUE on target_type+target_id filtered by relationship_kind='resolves'); a planning_item can be resolved by at most one conversation. The 'commits that contributed to PI-NNN' query walks refs(relationship_kind IN ('resolves','addresses') AND target=PI-NNN AND source_type='conversation') then joins to commits via the commit_conversation_id FK.
after_payload.rationale: Per-conversation grain matches the natural unit at which resolution is decided in a close-out payload, matches how planning_item.status transitions (one event per planning_item, not N), and avoids an authoring discipline judgment ('did this kickoff commit really contribute to PI-NNN or just the methodology commit?') that should not be required at close-out time. Per-commit grain forces that judgment; both-grain doubles edge volume for marginal gain. The two-join SQL for 'commits that resolved PI-NNN' is operationally fine.
after_payload.title: Granularity of resolves edges: per-conversation only, with multi-conversation contribution via addresses

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option (a) raw manual enumeration — rejected on forgotten-commit silent-gap failure mode. Option (b) git hook — rejected on commit-time API-coupling and sandbox-unhookability of sandbox commits. Option (c) polling — rejected on latency, polling-state, and close-out-timing awkwardness. Option (d) apply-time git scan without payload declaration — rejected because apply non-determinism if repo state diverges between conversation close and apply time (rebase, force-push) silently changes what gets ingested.
after_payload.consequences: Every close-out turn ends with a helper invocation that emits the commits payload section. The helper is implemented in PI-030's scope along with the close-out payload schema extension and apply script update. Until PI-030 ships, conversation close-outs continue to use the v0.7 payload format without the commits section. The helper specification includes per-repo bracketing, accommodating the conversation-scoped multi-repo case from DEC-187. Attribution rule: every commit in the enumerated range is attributed to the close-out's owning conversation, regardless of physical author identity; manual commits Doug made between close-outs are rolled into the next conversation's set. If attribution discipline becomes a problem, a future v0.9 adds an optional commit_author_kind field.
after_payload.context: Commits exist in git first; their governance records are created somewhere in the close-out lifecycle. The kickoff named four options: (a) manual payload enumeration, (b) git post-commit hook, (c) background polling against git log, (d) apply-time git scan. A fifth option (a-prime) surfaced during the decision: payload-declared like (a), but enumerated mechanically via a helper command at close-out authoring time — combines (a)'s auditability with (d)'s no-miss quality.
after_payload.decision: Option (a-prime). The close-out payload contains a top-level commits array. The conversation's close-out turn runs a helper (tools/governance/enumerate_commits.py or an inline 'git log --pretty' snippet) to populate the array from 'git log <last-applied-sha-per-repo>..HEAD' for each repo the conversation touched. The 'last-applied SHA per repo' is derived at helper-run time by querying GET /commits?commit_repository=<name>&sort=commit_committed_at:desc&limit=1 — no separate state table. apply_close_out.py POSTs each commits-array entry as a commit record. The commit's commit_conversation_id is set to the close-out's owning conversation by the apply script; no explicit field needed in the payload entry. Idempotency is enforced by the UNIQUE constraint on commit_sha (HTTP 409 on duplicate; per-record error tolerance allows apply to continue).
after_payload.rationale: Combining auditability of (a) with no-miss quality of (d) eliminates the failure modes of each: forgotten-enumeration of pure (a), API-down or sandbox-unhookable of (b), latency-and-cursor-state of (c), repo-state-divergence-at-apply of pure (d). The cost is one mechanical step at close-out authoring time and ~150 bytes of payload per commit. The 'last applied SHA' state lives implicitly in the commits table itself, requiring no new state machinery.
after_payload.title: Commit ingestion trigger: payload-declared, helper-enumerated via git log at close-out authoring time

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Including commit_files_changed_paths in v0.8 — rejected for migration cost without query-pattern motivation. Including commit_committer_name/email — rejected because the values are almost always identical to author in this workflow. Including commit_signed_by — rejected because Doug's commits aren't currently signed and the field would be null on every row. Keeping the kickoff's singular parent_sha — rejected because merge commits have two parents and a JSON array handles 0/1/2 uniformly.
after_payload.consequences: PI-029 ships the Alembic migration creating the commits table with these thirteen plus three columns. The enumerate_commits.py helper emits exactly the v0.8 set; payload entries match the column set. Adding any deferred field is a future v0.8.x or v0.9 migration. The parent_shas JSON-array column is the first JSON-array column on a governance entity table; if it works cleanly, future schemas needing variable-cardinality scalar lists may adopt the same pattern (named as a candidate cross-spec precedent for PI-028 to elevate in the commit schema spec section 7).
after_payload.context: The kickoff named a minimum field set (sha, message, author, author_email, committed_at, repository, branch, parent_sha) and an optional candidate set (files_changed_count, files_changed_paths, signed_by, committer, merge_parent_sha). The decision settles which optional fields are in v0.8 versus deferred to v0.9.
after_payload.decision: v0.8 commit record carries: commit_identifier (CM-NNNN, client-side), commit_sha (TEXT UNIQUE), commit_message_first_line (TEXT), commit_message_full (TEXT), commit_author_name (TEXT), commit_author_email (TEXT), commit_committed_at (TIMESTAMP from %cI committer date), commit_repository (TEXT), commit_branch (TEXT), commit_parent_shas (JSON array; 0 entries for initial commit, 1 for normal, 2 for merge — replaces kickoff's singular parent_sha to handle merges cleanly), commit_files_changed_count (INTEGER from git diff-tree), commit_conversation_id (FK to conversation.conversation_identifier), plus the three governance-standard timestamps commit_created_at, commit_updated_at, commit_deleted_at. Deferred to v0.9 or later: commit_files_changed_paths (separate normalized table when needed), commit_signed_by (Doug's commits aren't signed), commit_committer_name and commit_committer_email (practically identical to author in this workflow), commit_message_trailers (parsed Co-authored-by/Signed-off-by — not used today).
after_payload.rationale: The chosen set covers every audit-chain query named in the methodology document section 6 without unnecessary columns. The kickoff's singular parent_sha was replaced with the JSON-array parent_shas because merge commits have two parents; a JSON array handles 0/1/2 uniformly without a separate merge_parent_sha column. Deferred fields are speculative — commit_files_changed_paths has no current query pattern (git show <sha> provides on demand) and would either need a large JSON blob or a separate normalized table (more migration than the value justifies); the rest are either null on every row in this workflow or duplicate fields already covered by author_name and author_email.
after_payload.title: Commit record fields for v0.8: thirteen-column shape with parent_shas as JSON array; four candidate fields deferred to v0.9

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option (a) repo-scoped with cross-engagement references — rejected because it requires introducing cross-DB reference mechanics not currently used; cross-engagement references add persistent complexity at every query site for marginal gain. Option (b) per-engagement child records — rejected because parent-child conversations across DBs make conversation completeness ambiguous and double or triple conversation records per cross-repo work. Option (c) shared meta-DB — rejected because it introduces a new shared-DB pattern that doesn't exist elsewhere in V2; the per-engagement isolation is the established model.
after_payload.consequences: Every engagement DB's commits table tracks commits across all repos its conversations touched. The repository column distinguishes physical repo. PI-033 back-fill assigns each historical commit to its historical conversation, which lives in one engagement; most historical commits are crmbuilder dogfood work and go to the CRMBUILDER engagement DB; CBM-engagement-only commits go to CBM. The 'conversation as accounting unit' principle is the candidate cross-spec precedent the PI-028 schema spec can elevate for future external-artifact entity types (any entity representing artifacts physically outside the engagement DB inherits the principle).
after_payload.context: V2 engagement databases are per-client (CRMBUILDER dogfood DB, CBM client DB). A conversation may produce commits in multiple repos in a single session — for example, a dogfood conversation that fixes a deploy-engine bug in crmbuilder and also commits a YAML update in ClevelandBusinessMentoring to validate the fix. The kickoff named three options: (a) repo-scoped commits per engagement DB (each engagement tracks its own repo's commits) with cross-engagement references, (b) per-engagement child conversation records (parent in one DB, mirror in each engagement), (c) shared meta-DB for commits across all engagements.
after_payload.decision: Conversation-scoped commits (option a-prime, surfaced during the decision). Every commit lives in the same engagement DB as the conversation that produced it, regardless of which physical repo holds the SHA. The repository column on each commit row (TEXT, no enum) records which physical repo holds the SHA. Cross-engagement reference mechanics for commits are not introduced. A future cross-engagement 'where is this SHA?' lookup utility is cheap to add (iterate the commits tables of each engagement DB) but deferred until a use case surfaces.
after_payload.rationale: Engagements are accounting units; every record produced by a conversation belongs to that conversation's engagement. The per-engagement DB isolation pattern stays self-contained. Audit-chain queries inside an engagement stay one-DB; no cross-DB walks needed. The edge case 'a CBM-engagement conversation incidentally commits in crmbuilder' (rare — dogfood scope, but possible) is handled: the commit lives in CBM engagement DB with repository='crmbuilder'. The cost — CBM engagement DB never sees CBM-repo commits made under a CRMBUILDER-engagement conversation — is acceptable because such cross-engagement commit queries are not on the horizon; engagement-scoped audit queries are the only operational pattern.
after_payload.title: Multi-repo and multi-engagement scoping: conversation-scoped commits with repository column distinguishing physical repo

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option (b) stay with is_about — works functionally (queries filter is_about by source_type and target_type) but loses the semantic explicitness that addresses provides; future readers of an is_about edge from a work_ticket to a planning_item have to know the convention 'work_ticket is_about planning_item means addresses'. Adding addresses removes the convention-memorization burden.
after_payload.consequences: vocab.py REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS adds 'addresses'; _kinds_for_pair adds 'addresses' to the allowed kinds for (work_ticket, planning_item) and (conversation, planning_item). The Alembic migration in PI-029's scope adds 'addresses' to the refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint. The v0.7 backfill authored 8 work_ticket-to-planning_item is_about edges; PI-033 migrates those edges from kind='is_about' to kind='addresses'. The conversation-to-planning_item addresses pair is brand new and has no backfill obligation — future multi-conversation workstreams use it from inception.
after_payload.context: Work_tickets are born to address planning items; today this is captured as a generic is_about edge with no semantic specificity. DEC-184's per-conversation resolves model also requires a non-terminal contribution edge for multi-conversation work where a conversation contributes but doesn't resolve. Both use sites share the same semantic: 'source is doing work toward target without necessarily completing it.' The kickoff named (a) add addresses as a typed kind versus (b) stay with is_about and rely on type filters for queries.
after_payload.decision: Option (a) — add addresses as a typed relationship_kind. Admissible source/target pairs: (work_ticket, planning_item) for the work_ticket-is-born-to-address case (work_ticket spec already anticipates this via the addresses_planning_item field on the work_ticket payload entry), and (conversation, planning_item) for the multi-conversation non-terminal contribution case (DEC-184). Many-to-many cardinality on both sides: a work_ticket can address multiple planning_items (cross-cutting tickets like the methodology document that informs PI-028 through PI-033); a planning_item can be addressed by multiple work_tickets (decomposed work); a conversation can address multiple planning_items; a planning_item can be addressed by multiple non-terminal conversations.
after_payload.rationale: The query pattern 'all work_tickets created to address PI-NNN' is central to the audit-chain motivation in DEC-168, and DEC-184 already committed addresses to the conversation-side for multi-conversation resolution. DEC-133's frequency-justified deferral applies to speculative vocabulary; addresses has two named use sites in this workstream and concrete query patterns, so the deferral test does not trigger. The query-clarity win (relationship_kind='addresses' reads obviously versus is_about filtered by source/target types) justifies the small vocabulary cost.
after_payload.title: Add addresses as a typed relationship_kind admitting (work_ticket, planning_item) and (conversation, planning_item)

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option (a) narrow rejected on the unused-enum-value and audit-chain-completeness arguments. Option (b) middle rejected as inconsistent — if kickoffs are work_tickets, session prompts and update prompts (structurally identical single-use seeds) should be too; drawing the line at filename patterns is arbitrary.
after_payload.consequences: PI-033 back-fill authors ~221 work_ticket records: ~168 claude_code_prompt-kind, ~27 kickoff_prompt-kind, ~26 ad_hoc_prompt-kind. Each linked to its consuming conversation via conversation_opens_against_work_ticket where derivable from filename conventions; some require per-record judgment. PI-032 methodology rollout adds the authoring rule to the close-out checklist (every conversation that commits a seed file authors a work_ticket record in its close-out payload). Going forward, the close-out payload's work_tickets array becomes routine; the apply script processes it before the planning_items array per the fixed apply order in methodology section 4.
after_payload.context: The work_ticket spec's work_ticket_kind enum has four values: kickoff_prompt, claude_code_prompt, ad_hoc_prompt, other. The kickoff named three options for the authoring rule's scope: (a) narrow — only CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-*.md files become work_tickets (~168 historical), (b) middle — Claude Code prompts plus all kickoff/planning-prompt files (~195), (c) broad — every single-use seed including SESSION-PROMPT-* and UPDATE-PROMPT-* (~221). The work_ticket spec's canonical-examples narrative names files from all three categories, indicating the spec author's intent was already broad.
after_payload.decision: Option (c) broad. Every single-use seed document committed to either repo is born as a work_ticket record in the same close-out payload that produces the commit. Filename pattern classifies by kind: CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-*.md → claude_code_prompt; *-kickoff*.md and *-planning-prompt*.md → kickoff_prompt; SESSION-PROMPT-* and UPDATE-PROMPT-* → ad_hoc_prompt; anything else qualifying as a single-use seed → other (escape hatch). The work_ticket's addresses_planning_item (when known) names the planning item the conversation will address; for workstream-establishing kickoffs the addresses edge may be omitted and added later when the workstream's planning items are authored.
after_payload.rationale: The work_ticket spec's kind enum was authored with kickoff_prompt as a first-class value precisely because the spec author intended kickoffs to be governed as work_tickets; option (a) would leave that enum value mostly unused for new work. The audit-chain motivation in DEC-168 wants to walk from a planning item through the discrete units of work that addressed it; excluding Claude.ai kickoff conversations from work_ticket governance creates an arbitrary boundary that omits most of the project's decision-making work. Back-fill cost difference between (c) and (a) is ~50 records — a half-day to one-day difference, not material.
after_payload.title: Work_ticket authoring rule: every single-use seed document committed to either repo is a work_ticket, classified by the four-value kind enum

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option (a) keep blocks with natural-English convention plus row swap — rejected because keeping the ambiguous 'blocks' name preserves future confusion. Option (b) keep blocks with inverted convention — rejected because future authors must memorize the inverted convention; conventions that fight natural English get violated. The alternative kind name 'depends_on' was also considered (more generic, slightly less specific to planning-workflow semantics); blocked_by was preferred because it directly evokes the dependency lifecycle (the dependent thing is the source, waiting on a prerequisite).
after_payload.consequences: PI-029's Alembic migration drops the existing refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint, adds 'resolves' and 'addresses' and 'blocked_by' to the allowed set, removes 'blocks' from the allowed set, re-adds the CHECK constraint, then UPDATE refs SET relationship_kind='blocked_by' WHERE relationship_kind='blocks'. vocab.py REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS replaces 'blocks' with 'blocked_by'; _kinds_for_pair for (planning_item, planning_item) admits 'blocked_by' (typed) plus 'supersedes' (same-type rule) plus the generic kinds. Two existing rows REF-0357 and REF-0358 see their kind value flip in the migration; their source and target endpoints stay exactly as they are now, and they now read correctly as 'PI-025 is blocked_by PI-024' and 'PI-026 is blocked_by PI-025.'
after_payload.context: The v0.7 backfill created two blocks edges in the dogfood database: REF-0357 (source=PI-025 target=PI-024) and REF-0358 (source=PI-026 target=PI-025). PI-024 is Phase 2 backfill of prior workstreams, PI-025 is Phase 3 backfill of prior conversations, PI-026 is Phase 4 backfill of historical applies. Natural ordering: PI-024 must finish before PI-025, which must finish before PI-026. The kickoff named three options: (a) keep blocks with natural-English convention 'source blocks target = target waits for source' (existing rows must be swapped to match), (b) keep blocks with inverted convention 'source is blocked by target' (existing rows match as-is; future authors learn the inverted convention), (c) rename blocks to blocked_by (existing rows keep source/target endpoints; only the kind name migrates; semantics match natural English directly).
after_payload.decision: Option (c) — rename blocks to blocked_by. Admissible pair: (planning_item, planning_item). Semantics: source planning_item cannot proceed until target planning_item is resolved. Reads natively: 'PI-Y is blocked_by PI-X' means PI-Y is the dependent, PI-X is the prerequisite. The two existing rows REF-0357 and REF-0358 keep their source/target endpoints unchanged; only the relationship_kind value migrates from 'blocks' to 'blocked_by'. The migration is one UPDATE statement folded into PI-029's consolidated Alembic migration alongside the resolves and addresses kind additions.
after_payload.rationale: A self-documenting kind name removes the need for future readers to consult the methodology to interpret an edge. Conventions that disagree with natural English (option b) get violated in the long run; swapping the existing rows (option a) creates a one-time correction obligation and keeps the ambiguous 'blocks' name forever. blocked_by reads directly in both authoring ('declare PI-Y blocked_by PI-X') and querying ('list every planning_item that is blocked_by something'). Two existing rows is a negligible migration cost.
after_payload.title: Rename relationship_kind blocks to blocked_by; existing two rows keep source/target endpoints and migrate kind value only

after_payload.deposit_event_description: Applied COP-061. Outcome: success. 8 decisions, 1 sessions.
